In the meantime Sumantra makes his appearance in the
gynaeceum and, extolling the Emperor, reminds him of
his intention to install Sri Rama as Prince Regent
and eventually leaves the gynaeceum in
order to summon Sri Rama at the
instance of the Emperor
content: As dawn broke, the sun rising and the auspicious hour arriving, Sage Vasistha entered Ayodhya with his disciples, bearing the items for Rama's coronation. The city was decorated with colorful banners, its streets clean and filled with joyful people. Markets overflowed with goods, and the air was fragrant with incense. Ayodhya, bustling with anticipation for Rama's installation, rivaled the splendor of Indra's celestial city.  Vasistha observed the palace, adorned with flags and teeming with citizens and rural folk, graced by Brahmins and attendants. Reaching the palace, the joyful sage and his companions bypassed the crowd at the gate, where he encountered Sumantra, Dasaratha's charioteer and minister.  Vasistha informed Sumantra of his arrival and presented the ceremonial items: sacred waters, a wooden throne, various seeds and herbs, gemstones, honey, curds, ghee, grains, grass, flowers, milk, eight maidens, an elephant, a chariot, a sword, a bow, a palanquin, an umbrella, chowries, a bull, a lion, a horse, another throne, a tiger skin, fire-starting sticks, musical instruments, adorned women, Brahmins, cows, animals, birds, prominent citizens, merchants, and their followers. All these, along with numerous well-wishers, eagerly awaited Rama's installation.  Urging the king to act swiftly, Vasistha instructed Sumantra to inform Dasaratha that the auspicious moment had arrived for Rama's coronation.  Sumantra, having received Vasistha's message, re-entered the palace, praising King Dasaratha. The king's attendants, respectful and eager to please, did not hinder Sumantra, recognizing his seniority and prior permission to see the king. Unaware of Dasaratha's distress, Sumantra approached him, intending to offer words of praise.  He began by comparing the king's joy to the ocean illuminated by the rising sun and urged him to share his happiness.  He then likened his role to that of the Vedas and other branches of knowledge guiding Brahma in creation, and to the sun and moon awakening the earth, encouraging Dasaratha to fulfill his duty.  Finally, Sumantra implored the king to rise, adorned and radiant, like the sun ascending Mount Meru.

summary:
Canto XIV

Citing other instances of the Emperor's fidelity to truth and threatening to lay
down her life in the event of his not carrying out her wishes, Kaikeyi
insists on his sending Sri Rama into exile at once and does not
desist from her purpose even when railed at by her husband.

In the meantime Sumantra makes his appearance in the
gynaeceum and, extolling the Emperor, reminds him of
his intention to install Sri Rama as Prince Regent
and eventually leaves the gynaeceum in
order to summon Sri Rama at the
instance of the Emperor
content: Sumantra, preparing for Rama's coronation, presented the assembled items to King Dasaratha, including various provisions, animals, dignitaries, and well-wishers.  He urged the king to proceed with the ceremony, emphasizing its importance. However, finding Dasaratha grief-stricken and unable to speak, Sumantra attempted to comfort him, comparing the king's absence to a shepherdless flock or an army without a general.  Kaikeyi intervened, falsely claiming the king was asleep due to excitement and exhaustion over Rama's imminent coronation.  She then instructed Sumantra to fetch Rama without delay.

summary:
Canto XIV

Citing other instances of the Emperor's fidelity to truth and threatening to lay
down her life in the event of his not carrying out her wishes, Kaikeyi
insists on his sending Sri Rama into exile at once and does not
desist from her purpose even when railed at by her husband.

In the meantime Sumantra makes his appearance in the
gynaeceum and, extolling the Emperor, reminds him of
his intention to install Sri Rama as Prince Regent
and eventually leaves the gynaeceum in
order to summon Sri Rama at the
instance of the Emperor
content: Sumantra, praising King Dasaratha, likened his absence to situations lacking leadership: a shepherdless flock, an army without a general, a moonless night, and cows without a bull.  Despite Sumantra's attempts to comfort him, the king remained grief-stricken. He felt Sumantra's well-intentioned words only deepened his pain.  Seeing the king's distress, Sumantra respectfully withdrew. Kaikeyi, however, insisted Sumantra bring Rama quickly, seemingly eager for the coronation.  Sumantra, believing the king's fatigue was preventing him from attending the ceremony, hurried to fetch Rama.  He left the palace and found the entrance crowded with people bearing gifts, ready to celebrate the coronation.

summary:
Canto XIV

Citing other instances of the Emperor's fidelity to truth and threatening to lay
down her life in the event of his not carrying out her wishes, Kaikeyi
insists on his sending Sri Rama into exile at once and does not
desist from her purpose even when railed at by her husband.

In the meantime Sumantra makes his appearance in the
gynaeceum and, extolling the Emperor, reminds him of
his intention to install Sri Rama as Prince Regent
and eventually leaves the gynaeceum in
order to summon Sri Rama at the
instance of the Emperor
content: Sumantra, observing King Dasaratha's distress, respectfully withdrew.  Kaikeyi then addressed Sumantra, falsely explaining that the king was asleep, exhausted from the excitement of Rama's imminent coronation. She urged Sumantra to quickly bring Rama to the king.  Dasaratha, overhearing this, expressed his own desire to see Rama.  Sumantra, believing he was facilitating the coronation, happily and swiftly departed to fetch Rama.  Kaikeyi again emphasized the need for haste.  Sumantra, convinced he was acting on the king's wishes and motivated by the impending coronation, left the palace. He emerged to find the entrance thronged with people bearing gifts in anticipation of the celebration.

summary:
Canto XV
While going out of the gynaeceum to bring Sri Rama, Sumantra sees Vasistha
and others as well as a number of kings waiting at the gate and hastens

back to announce their presence to the king. He is, however, sent back
to fetch Sri Rama and forthwith enters the latter’s apartments
content: Brahmins learned in the Vedas, along with royal priests, gathered at the palace gate for Rama's installation ceremony.  Ministers, military leaders, and merchants also assembled joyfully for the event.  With the sun in Cancer and the Pusya star visible, as at Rama's birth, preparations were complete.  Golden pitchers filled with water, a decorated seat, a chariot with a tiger-skin cover, and water from the Ganga and Yamuna's confluence were ready.  Water from other sacred rivers, pools, and seas was also collected.  Honey, curds, ghee, grains, kusha grass, flowers, eight young women, a majestic elephant, gold and silver pitchers with lotuses and lilies, white chowries, and a white umbrella were all prepared.  A white bull and horse stood ready. Musical instruments, panegyrists, and bards were present.  The assembled Brahmins and others, having brought the necessary items for the installation, wondered who would inform the king of their arrival, as he was not present despite the sun having risen.

summary:
Canto XV
While going out of the gynaeceum to bring Sri Rama, Sumantra sees Vasistha
and others as well as a number of kings waiting at the gate and hastens

back to announce their presence to the king. He is, however, sent back
to fetch Sri Rama and forthwith enters the latter’s apartments
content: Sumantra returned to the palace gate and informed the assembled Brahmins, officials, and kings that he was sent by the king to fetch Rama for the ceremony.  He assured them he would inquire about the king's well-being and the reason for his delayed appearance.  Re-entering the palace, Sumantra praised the king's lineage and approached the royal bedchamber.  He offered blessings to Dasaratha, wishing him victory and acknowledging the arrival of the new day. He urged the king to awaken and attend to the waiting dignitaries, emphasizing their eagerness to see Rama.  Dasaratha, recognizing Sumantra's voice, questioned why his earlier command to bring Rama had not been obeyed, stating he was not asleep. He again instructed Sumantra to bring Rama quickly.

summary:
Canto XV
While going out of the gynaeceum to bring Sri Rama, Sumantra sees Vasistha
and others as well as a number of kings waiting at the gate and hastens

back to announce their presence to the king. He is, however, sent back
to fetch Sri Rama and forthwith enters the latter’s apartments
content: Sumantra, following the king's renewed command, left the king's chambers and proceeded towards Rama's palace.  He traveled along the main road, adorned with festive banners and flags.  The anticipation of Rama's imminent installation filled Sumantra with joy, a sentiment echoed by the conversations he overheard among the people.  He soon arrived at Rama's magnificent palace.  Its splendor was comparable to Mount Kailasa and Indra's celestial abode.  The palace, secured by imposing doors and embellished with numerous balconies, featured a grand facade adorned with gold and a gateway of gems and coral. Its brilliance resembled a luminous autumn cloud.  The palace was richly decorated with gems, pearls, sandalwood, and aloe, emitting a delightful fragrance like the aromatic slopes of Mount Dardura.  The palace grounds were graced by the presence of cranes and peacocks.  Intricate carvings of wolves and other artistic details further enhanced its beauty, captivating the eyes and minds of all who beheld it.  The palace, radiant as the sun and moon, rivaled the opulence of Kubera's palace and teemed with a variety of birds. Sumantra observed the palace, a majestic structure akin to Mount Meru, surrounded by a crowd of people with hands respectfully clasped, many of whom had brought gifts in anticipation of Rama's installation.

summary:
Canto XV
While going out of the gynaeceum to bring Sri Rama, Sumantra sees Vasistha
and others as well as a number of kings waiting at the gate and hastens

back to announce their presence to the king. He is, however, sent back
to fetch Sri Rama and forthwith enters the latter’s apartments
content: Sumantra arrived at Rama's palace, a grand structure shining like the sun and moon, as magnificent as Kubera's palace and bustling with various birds.  It resembled Mount Meru, surrounded by people with clasped hands, many bearing gifts for Rama's upcoming installation as prince regent. The palace, resplendent and cloud-like, was adorned with gems and attended by servants.  Sumantra, driving his chariot, approached the palace, bringing joy to the city's inhabitants. He was thrilled to arrive at the opulent palace, teeming with deer and peacocks, reminiscent of Indra's abode.  Passing through the ornate gates, which rivaled Mount Kailasa and the celestial realm, Sumantra navigated past Rama's followers and reached the inner chambers. There, he heard the joyful pronouncements of those preparing for Rama's installation and the people's enthusiastic blessings. The palace shone brightly, akin to Mount Meru's peak, and housed various creatures.  The entrance was crowded with people carrying gifts, their vehicles parked nearby. Sumantra then observed the impressive elephant, Satrughnaya, as tall as a mountain, which would carry Rama. He also noted the presence of adorned ministers, arriving on horseback, elephant, or chariot. Making his way past them, Sumantra entered the richly appointed inner chambers of the palace, which resembled a cloud-capped mountain with mansions like aerial cars, containing a wealth of gems.

summary:
Canto XV
While going out of the gynaeceum to bring Sri Rama, Sumantra sees Vasistha
and others as well as a number of kings waiting at the gate and hastens

back to announce their presence to the king. He is, however, sent back
to fetch Sri Rama and forthwith enters the latter’s apartments
content: Sumantra, unhindered, entered Rama's private chambers. The palace interior, with its towering mansions resembling celestial vehicles, was as radiant as a cloud-wreathed mountain peak, filled with a wealth of precious gems.

summary: No summary available
content: Sumantra, entering a gate guarded by loyal men, proceeded to another secured by devoted guards.  He requested them to inform Rama of his arrival. The guards promptly relayed the message to Rama, who was with Sita.  Rama graciously welcomed Sumantra into his private chamber.  Sumantra found Rama, radiant and adorned, seated on a golden couch.  Sita stood beside him. Sumantra respectfully greeted Rama. He then conveyed Dasaratha's wish, along with Kaikeyi's, for Rama to see them. Rama, pleased,  conjectured to Sita that his parents' summons likely pertained to his imminent appointment as Prince Regent.

summary: No summary available
content: Sumantra informed Rama of his father Dasaratha's and Queen Kaikeyi's desire to see him.  Overjoyed, Rama shared with Sita his belief that this summons likely concerned his appointment as Prince Regent. He expressed his confidence that Kaikeyi, perceptive and eager to please Dasaratha, was likely advocating for him.  Rama saw Sumantra's arrival as a positive sign, further solidifying his expectation of imminent good news.  After bidding a loving farewell to Sita, who expressed her wishes to see him installed and offered prayers for his protection, Rama left his residence with Sumantra.  As he departed, he encountered Lakshmana waiting respectfully at the gate.

summary: No summary available
content: As Rama departed the palace, blessings were invoked for his protection.  He then emerged like a lion from its mountain lair and saw Lakshmana waiting respectfully. After exchanging greetings with friends and relatives gathered at the middle gate, Rama boarded his magnificent chariot, radiant as fire.  The chariot, adorned with gold and jewels, was drawn by powerful horses and sped forth like Indra's own.  Lakshmana followed closely, holding a ceremonial fan.  The crowd erupted in cheers as Rama's procession, including elephants and warriors, moved through the city.  Musicians played, bards sang praises, and women showered flowers from balconies, expressing their joy and offering blessings.  They anticipated Kausalya's happiness at seeing Rama's impending coronation. They praised Sita's virtue, comparing her fortunate union with Rama to Rohini's with the moon god.  Amidst the celebratory clamor, Rama heard the people's enthusiastic discussions about his lineage and their hopes for his reign. They believed his rule would bring prosperity and happiness to the kingdom.  The procession continued, led by those proclaiming blessings and reciting Rama's lineage, accompanied by musicians.  Rama observed the bustling streets, filled with people, animals, chariots, and shops overflowing with goods.

summary: No summary available
content: As Rama's chariot, resplendent and swift, departed the palace, Lakshmana attended him with a ceremonial fan.  The crowd's joyous clamor filled the air as the procession, complete with horses, elephants, and warriors, moved through the city. Musicians and bards contributed to the celebratory atmosphere, while women showered flowers upon Rama from the balconies.  They expressed their anticipation of Kausalya's joy and praised Sita's virtuous character, comparing her union with Rama to that of Rohini and the moon god.  The people enthusiastically discussed Rama's lineage and the prosperity they expected from his reign.  The procession advanced, led by those offering blessings and reciting Rama's lineage, accompanied by musicians.  Rama observed the bustling streets filled with people, animals, chariots, and shops brimming with goods.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama, joyous amongst his friends and family, rode his chariot through Ayodhya.  The city was decorated with flags and banners, fragrant with incense, and filled with people.  He proceeded down the main road, lined with white houses that resembled clouds.  Like Indra, king of the gods, Rama surveyed the route, clear of any danger and bustling with shops overflowing with sandalwood, aloe, silks, pearls, crystals, flowers, and food.  The crossroads were adorned with offerings.  As he passed, people called out blessings and praises, which Rama received graciously.  They spoke of the righteous path of his ancestors and expressed their hopes for even greater prosperity under his rule, surpassing even the golden age of his father, Dasaratha.  They declared that nothing would bring them more joy than Rama's coronation.  Though the crowds lauded him, Rama remained humble, yet no one could tear their gaze away from him as he passed.  Those who missed seeing him felt a pang of regret.  Rama, compassionate to all, regardless of their social standing, earned the devotion of the people.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama, continuing his procession, heard the praises of his friends and family, though he remained unaffected by the adulation.  Even as he passed, people couldn't look away, captivated by his presence.  Those who didn't see him felt a sense of loss. Rama, compassionate to all people, earned their devotion. He respectfully drove past crossroads, temples, and sacred places.  He then entered the magnificent royal palace, which was adorned with beautiful attics and pleasure-houses, a structure comparable to Indra's celestial abode.  After passing through the first three gates in his chariot, Rama proceeded on foot through the remaining two, dismissing his entourage before entering the private chambers to meet his father. The waiting crowd joyfully anticipated his return, like the ocean awaiting the moonrise.

summary:
Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods
content: Rama approached his father, King Dasaratha, who sat dejectedly with Kaikeyi.  Rama bowed respectfully to both. Dasaratha, tearful and distressed, could barely acknowledge Rama's presence.  Observing his father's unusual state, Rama became apprehensive, like one who accidentally steps on a snake. He saw the king emaciated with grief, sighing, and looking like a turbulent ocean, a sun in eclipse, or a sage who has uttered falsehood.  Concerned, Rama wondered why his father, usually so affectionate, was unresponsive. He questioned Kaikeyi, asking if he had unknowingly offended his father and pleading with her to intervene on his behalf. He expressed his deep concern for Dasaratha's well-being, hoping he was not suffering from physical or mental anguish, and anxiously inquired if he had offended Bharata, Shatrughna, or any of his mothers. Rama declared that he could not bear to live if he had displeased his father in any way.

summary:
Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods
content: Rama, deeply concerned by his father's silence and distress, questioned Kaikeyi about the cause.  He expressed his worry, hoping no physical or mental ailment troubled the king, and wondered if he had unknowingly offended anyone, including Bharata, Shatrughna, or any of his mothers.  Rama affirmed his devotion to his father, emphasizing the impossibility of living if he had caused displeasure. He wondered if Kaikeyi had perhaps spoken harshly to Dasaratha, causing him distress.  In response, Kaikeyi claimed the king was neither angry nor in anguish but held something back for fear of hurting Rama. She insisted that Rama must fulfill a promise Dasaratha made to her, a boon the king now regretted granting.  Kaikeyi implied this undisclosed promise was difficult for Dasaratha to honor and warned that the king might forsake truth due to his anger towards her for Rama's sake. She agreed to reveal the matter only if Rama pledged to obey the king's command, whatever it may be.

summary:
Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods
content: Kaikeyi continued to manipulate Rama, asserting that Dasaratha, bound by truth and piety, was tormented by the promise he made to her. She pressed Rama to swear obedience to his father's command before she would reveal it, insinuating that the king's distress stemmed from his reluctance to burden Rama with this difficult request.  Rama, distraught by the implication of his father's unhappiness, readily agreed, declaring his unwavering devotion and willingness to obey any command, even if it meant self-sacrifice. He implored Kaikeyi to disclose the king's wish.  Kaikeyi then revealed her demand: that Rama be exiled to the Dandaka forest for fourteen years while Bharata be crowned king. She claimed these were the boons Dasaratha had granted her long ago, for saving his life in battle.  She insisted that honoring this promise was the only way for both Dasaratha and Rama to uphold their integrity. She portrayed the king as heartbroken over his predicament, unable even to look at Rama due to his grief over this forced exile.

summary:
Canto XVIII

Questioned by Sri Rama as to what preyed on his father’s mind, Kaikeyi
tells him all that had happened in the meantime and sternly
urges him to depart for the woods
content: Kaikeyi commands Rama to relinquish his claim to the throne and instead allow Bharata to be crowned king, using the resources gathered for Rama's own coronation. She dictates that Rama must spend fourteen years in the Dandaka forest, wearing matted hair and bark clothing, while Bharata rules the prosperous kingdom.  Kaikeyi manipulates Rama by claiming that Dasaratha is overwhelmed with grief over this situation, brought about by boons he had previously granted her. She asserts that Dasaratha is unable to face Rama due to his sorrow and that fulfilling her request is the only way to uphold both his and Rama's honor.  Kaikeyi urges Rama to obey his father's supposed wish and release him from this supposed suffering.  Despite Kaikeyi's harsh words, Rama remains composed, while Dasaratha, though strong-willed, is deeply distressed by the impending separation from his son.

summary:
Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya
content: Rama, unfazed by the harsh decree, calmly accepted his exile to the forest, honoring his father's promise. He questioned Kaikeyi, seeking to understand the king's sudden change in demeanor.  Rama assured her that he bore no ill will and would willingly obey, even relinquishing Sita and his own life if commanded. He emphasized his eagerness to fulfill his father's wishes, especially if it pleased Kaikeyi.  Concerned by the king's sadness, Rama urged that messengers be sent to retrieve Bharata.  He affirmed his resolve to depart swiftly for the Dandaka forest, adhering to his father's command without question.  Pleased with his compliance, Kaikeyi hastened his departure, promising to summon Bharata. She dismissed Rama's concerns about the king's silence, attributing it to shyness and urging him to disregard it.

summary:
Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya
content: Rama, accepting his exile, reassured Kaikeyi of his obedience.  He urged her to quickly dispatch messengers to retrieve Bharata. Kaikeyi, pleased with his compliance, pressed him to leave immediately, attributing the king's silence to shyness.  Dasaratha, heartbroken by Rama's words, collapsed onto his couch, overcome with grief.  Rama, urged by Kaikeyi, prepared to leave for the forest.  He responded to Kaikeyi's harsh words by stating his disinterest in worldly possessions and his commitment to righteousness.  He affirmed his duty to his father and agreed to his fourteen-year exile. He then requested permission to bid farewell to his mother, Kausalya, and Sita before departing for the Dandaka forest.  He reminded Kaikeyi of her duty to ensure Bharata's proper governance of the kingdom and care for their father. Dasaratha, unable to speak, wept aloud in his anguish.

summary:
Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya
content: Rama, addressing Kaikeyi, questioned her perception of his virtues, given her seemingly trivial request to his father, despite her significant influence over him.  He asked for her patience while he informed his mother, Kausalya, and Sita of his departure for the Dandaka forest.  He reminded Kaikeyi of her responsibility to ensure Bharata's proper governance and care for their father.  Overwhelmed by grief, Dasaratha wept uncontrollably.  Rama, after paying his respects to his father and Kaikeyi, departed.  Leaving the inner quarters, he met his friends.  A visibly upset Lakshmana, followed close behind.  Rama, bypassing the consecration preparations, maintained his composure despite the loss of his kingship.  He dismissed his attendants and, controlling his emotions, entered his mother's chambers to convey the unwelcome news. Despite the circumstances, Rama's outward appearance remained unchanged, showing no visible sign of distress.  He maintained his usual joyful demeanor and respectful manner as he entered his mother's presence, followed by Lakshmana, who also struggled to contain his sorrow.

summary:
Canto XIX

Having agreed to leave for the forest, Sri Rama proceeds
to take leave of his mother Kausalya
content: Rama, maintaining his composure and usual joyful demeanor, entered his mother Kausalya's palace.  His appearance remained unchanged, betraying no sign of the inner turmoil he felt about his imminent exile.  He greeted everyone with his usual respectful and pleasant speech. Lakshmana, equally distressed by the turn of events but mirroring Rama's outward calm, followed closely behind.  Even upon entering Kausalya's palace, which was decorated in anticipation of Rama's coronation, and realizing the stark contrast between the joyous preparations and his own misfortune, Rama did not outwardly express his sorrow, mindful of the impact such a display might have on his loved ones.

summary:
Canto XX

Even as Sri Rama issued forth from the palace of Queen Kaikeyi, the inmates
of the gynaeceum burst into a piteous wail, extolling the prince’s virtues.
Mother Kausalya embraces and pronounces her benedictions on Sri
Rama as the latter approaches her and falls at her feet. On being
apprised of the circumstances that had brought him there, she
falls to the ground overwhelmed with grief, and weeps
bitterly expressing her deep sorrow
content: As Rama left Kaikeyi's palace, the women inside cried out, lamenting his imminent departure. They praised his virtues, recalling how he cared for them, protected them, and resolved their disputes peacefully.  Meanwhile, King Dasaratha, overcome with shame and sorrow at his decision, hid himself.  Rama, though deeply saddened by the grief of his loved ones, proceeded with Lakshmana to his mother Kausalya's quarters.  He passed the gatekeepers, who greeted him with cheers, then the Brahmins at the second gate, and finally the women and guards at the third gate.  The women joyfully informed Kausalya of Rama's arrival.  Kausalya, who had spent the night in prayer and was continuing her rituals at daybreak, offering oblations to Vishnu, was unaware of the events that had transpired.

summary:
Canto XX

Even as Sri Rama issued forth from the palace of Queen Kaikeyi, the inmates
of the gynaeceum burst into a piteous wail, extolling the prince’s virtues.
Mother Kausalya embraces and pronounces her benedictions on Sri
Rama as the latter approaches her and falls at her feet. On being
apprised of the circumstances that had brought him there, she
falls to the ground overwhelmed with grief, and weeps
bitterly expressing her deep sorrow
content: At the third gate of Kausalya's palace, Rama saw women, young girls, and elderly men guarding the entrance.  The women, excited about Rama's supposed appointment as crown prince, rushed inside to share the good news with his mother.  Meanwhile, Kausalya, unaware of the recent events, had spent the night in prayer and continued her morning rituals, offering oblations to Vishnu. Dressed in silk and dedicated to religious practices, she oversaw the priests as they chanted sacred texts and poured offerings into the fire.  Rama entered his mother's chambers and saw the preparations for the divine worship: curds, rice, ghee, sweetmeats, parched paddy, white flowers, a rice pudding, sesame rice, sacrificial wood, and water-filled jars. He observed his mother, pale from fasting, as she performed the rites.  Kausalya, overjoyed to see her son after a long time, rushed to greet him as a mare greets her foal.  Rama embraced his mother.

summary:
Canto XX

Even as Sri Rama issued forth from the palace of Queen Kaikeyi, the inmates
of the gynaeceum burst into a piteous wail, extolling the prince’s virtues.
Mother Kausalya embraces and pronounces her benedictions on Sri
Rama as the latter approaches her and falls at her feet. On being
apprised of the circumstances that had brought him there, she
falls to the ground overwhelmed with grief, and weeps
bitterly expressing her deep sorrow
content: Queen Kausalya, initially unaware of Rama's exile, was informed by her excited handmaidens of his imminent appointment as crown prince.  Rama, however, gently corrected this misunderstanding, revealing his impending fourteen-year exile to the Dandaka forest. He explained that he would live like an ascetic, subsisting on roots, fruits, and other forest fare, while Bharata would be crowned Prince Regent.  Kausalya, devastated by this news, collapsed like a felled tree.  Rama comforted his grieving mother, brushing the dust from her clothes.  Kausalya lamented her fate, expressing that the pain of separation from her son was far greater than the sorrow of childlessness. She further worried about the unkind words she would endure from her co-wives due to Rama's exile.

summary:
Canto XX

Even as Sri Rama issued forth from the palace of Queen Kaikeyi, the inmates
of the gynaeceum burst into a piteous wail, extolling the prince’s virtues.
Mother Kausalya embraces and pronounces her benedictions on Sri
Rama as the latter approaches her and falls at her feet. On being
apprised of the circumstances that had brought him there, she
falls to the ground overwhelmed with grief, and weeps
bitterly expressing her deep sorrow
content: Kausalya, overwhelmed with grief, laments Rama's exile. She expresses that the pain of his departure is worse than the sorrow of childlessness, explaining that a barren woman's only anguish is the lack of a child, while she must endure the greater agony of separation from her grown son.  She anticipates unkind remarks from her co-wives due to Rama's banishment and feels her position in the palace will be further diminished. Kausalya mourns that despite her efforts in raising Rama, she is now treated with disdain, a situation she expects will worsen in his absence.  She questions her own resilience, wondering why death does not claim her despite her immense sorrow.  Kausalya feels as though her heart is made of stone, unbreakable despite the weight of her grief.

summary:
Canto XX

Even as Sri Rama issued forth from the palace of Queen Kaikeyi, the inmates
of the gynaeceum burst into a piteous wail, extolling the prince’s virtues.
Mother Kausalya embraces and pronounces her benedictions on Sri
Rama as the latter approaches her and falls at her feet. On being
apprised of the circumstances that had brought him there, she
falls to the ground overwhelmed with grief, and weeps
bitterly expressing her deep sorrow
content: Kausalya continues to lament, expressing the futility of her past religious practices if they couldn't prevent this tragedy. She believes her heart is unbreakable, as it doesn't shatter from the immense grief. She wishes for death but feels it evades her, much like a lion ignoring a crying deer.  Even if death doesn't come, life is meaningless without Rama.  Like a cow yearning for its calf, she declares her intention to follow him into exile, despite her frailty.  Observing Rama constrained by duty, Kausalya's anguish deepens, and she weeps like a Kinnara whose child is captured.

summary:
Canto XXI

Consoling Kausalya in her grief over the impending exile of Sri Rama, Prince
Laksmana opposes the idea of Sri Rama’s leaving for the forest and,
censuring Dasaratha, makes up his mind to accompany his
eldest brother. Kausalya too deters Sri Rama from going
into exile, branding Kaikeyi’s command as unjust.

Sri Rama, however, justifies the command on the
ground of its being countenanced by the
Emperor and requests his mother to grant
him leave and perform auspicious
rites connected with his departure
content: Lakshmana, distressed by the situation, addressed the weeping Kausalya. He argued against Rama's exile, questioning Dasaratha's decision to banish his eldest son due to Kaikeyi's influence. Lakshmana couldn't fathom any wrongdoing on Rama's part that merited such punishment, asserting that no one, not even Rama's enemies, could find fault with him.  He criticized Dasaratha for succumbing to Kaikeyi's will, likening the king's behavior to that of a senile man driven by desire.  Lakshmana questioned how any virtuous person could exile such a noble son. He then proposed that Rama seize control of the kingdom before the news of the exile spread, offering his unwavering support and protection. Lakshmana declared he would eliminate anyone who opposed Rama, including those loyal to Bharata.  He even suggested that Dasaratha should face consequences if he sided with Kaikeyi against Rama.  Finally, Lakshmana challenged Dasaratha's authority to bestow the kingdom upon Bharata, questioning his right to create such conflict.

summary:
Canto XXI

Consoling Kausalya in her grief over the impending exile of Sri Rama, Prince
Laksmana opposes the idea of Sri Rama’s leaving for the forest and,
censuring Dasaratha, makes up his mind to accompany his
eldest brother. Kausalya too deters Sri Rama from going
into exile, branding Kaikeyi’s command as unjust.

Sri Rama, however, justifies the command on the
ground of its being countenanced by the
Emperor and requests his mother to grant
him leave and perform auspicious
rites connected with his departure
content:
Lakshmana continued his impassioned plea against Rama's exile, suggesting drastic measures against Dasaratha if he persisted in his decision.  He questioned Dasaratha's authority to give the kingdom to Bharata, emphasizing Rama's rightful claim. Lakshmana swore his unwavering loyalty to Rama, vowing to follow him into fire or forest and to avenge this injustice. He promised Kausalya he would resolve the situation with his strength.

Kausalya, deeply saddened, then appealed to Rama, asking him to consider Lakshmana's words.  She urged him to defy Kaikeyi's unjust command and remain in Ayodhya.  Kausalya argued that his filial duty to her superseded any other obligation.  She pleaded with him, emphasizing her deep attachment and declaring she would not survive his departure.

summary:
Canto XXI

Consoling Kausalya in her grief over the impending exile of Sri Rama, Prince
Laksmana opposes the idea of Sri Rama’s leaving for the forest and,

eldest brother. Kausalya too deters Sri Rama from going
into exile, branding Kaikeyi’s command as unjust.

Sri Rama, however, justifies the command on the
ground of its being countenanced by the
Emperor and requests his mother to grant
him leave and perform auspicious
rites connected with his departure
content:
Kausalya, invoking examples of devoted sons, implored Rama to reconsider his exile, arguing that his duty to her outweighed any other. She declared her life meaningless without him and threatened to fast to death if he left, warning him of dire spiritual consequences for causing her such grief.

Rama, however, gently but firmly explained his decision. Citing examples of legendary figures like Kasyapa, Kandu, Sagara's sons, and Parasurama, he emphasized the importance of obeying one's father, even if the command seemed difficult or unjust.  He assured Kausalya that he wasn't establishing a new precedent but following a well-trodden path of filial duty, one that ultimately brought blessings, not curses.  He asserted that fulfilling his father's wish was the most righteous action he could take in this situation. Finally, acknowledging Lakshmana's fierce loyalty and strength, Rama addressed him, preparing to respond to his brother's impassioned pleas.

content: Rama continues to explain to his mother, Kausalya, that he is not creating a new tradition by obeying his father's command to go into exile, but following an established path of filial piety.  He argues that fulfilling his father's wish, even if it seems unjust, is the most righteous action he can take.  Acknowledging Lakshmana’s devotion and strength, Rama addresses his brother's concerns regarding the exile. He reminds Lakshmana of his unsurpassed love and prowess, while expressing concern for Kausalya’s grief.  Rama emphasizes the importance of adhering to righteousness and truthfulness, highlighting that his father's command, although painful, aligns with these principles. He reminds Lakshmana that promises made to parents, particularly in the context of duty, must be kept.  Rama explains his commitment to fulfilling the exile due to Kaikeyi's command and requests Kausalya to bless his journey with auspicious rites, assuring her of his return. He urges her to restrain her grief and accept his decision as a necessary fulfillment of his father’s command.  Finally, having calmed his mother with his steadfast resolve and righteous words, Kausalya regains her composure and prepares to speak again.

content: Rama consoles his grieving mother, Kausalya, assuring her of his eventual return from exile after fulfilling his father's command. He emphasizes that obedience to his father is an eternal moral law, binding not only himself but also Sita, Lakshmana, and Sumitra.  He declares his resolve to proceed with the exile, urging Kausalya to set aside her grief and support his decision by performing auspicious rites for his journey. Kausalya, deeply moved, expresses her profound love for Rama, questioning the purpose of her life without him.  She pleads with him not to leave her. Witnessing his mother's anguish, Rama becomes even more determined to adhere to his dharma. He addresses both his distraught mother and Lakshmana, reminding Lakshmana of his unwavering devotion and strength, and gently chiding him for adding to Kausalya's distress.  Rama explains that true happiness stems from pursuing righteousness above material gain or worldly pleasure. He emphasizes the importance of honoring a father's command, especially when that father is also a king and preceptor.  Rama reaffirms his commitment to his father's pledge and explains that King Dasaratha is their ultimate protector and guide. He questions how Kausalya could leave Ayodhya while her virtuous husband, the king, still lives.  Finally, Rama implores Kausalya to bless his journey into exile and perform the necessary rites, assuring her of his return, just as King Yayati returned to heaven through truthfulness. He declares his refusal to compromise his honor and righteousness for the sake of worldly power.

content: Rama continues to console his mother Kausalya, explaining that he must fulfill his father's pledge. He reminds her that his father, Dasaratha, is their rightful authority and the source of their well-being.  He questions how Kausalya could possibly leave Ayodhya while her husband, the king, still lives there. Rama then asks Kausalya to bless his journey into exile and perform the customary rituals for a safe passage. He assures her of his eventual return, drawing a parallel to King Yayati's return from exile.  Finally, Rama reiterates his commitment to righteousness, stating that he will not compromise his honor for worldly power, particularly the kingship, as life is too short to live dishonorably.  Having explained his position and instructed Lakshmana, Rama respectfully takes his leave of Kausalya.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama, seeing his brother Lakshmana’s distress and anger over his exile, calmly addressed him.  Rama urged Lakshmana to cast aside his anger towards their father and Kaikeyi, and instead embrace the situation with joy, seeing it as an opportunity to uphold their father's promise and secure his place in heaven.  He requested Lakshmana to redirect the preparations from the coronation to the exile, ensuring the same level of diligence.  Mindful of Kaikeyi’s feelings, Rama urged Lakshmana to reassure her and allay any fears she might have about his reaction to the exile.  Rama affirmed his respect and love for his parents, stating that he had never intentionally or unintentionally offended them. He expressed his desire to honor his father’s promise, freeing him from any guilt or concern. Rama believed his exile was destined and would bring peace to Kaikeyi. He emphasized that he had no desire to defy fate and was ready to depart for the forest without delay.  He concluded by stating that Providence alone was responsible for both his exile and his potential return to power.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama continued to counsel Lakshmana, advising him to accept the situation. He suggested that Kaikeyi would find peace once Rama, adorned in ascetic garb, departed for the forest.  Rama stated his intention to honor his father's promise and not resist fate, believing destiny orchestrated his exile. He questioned how Kaikeyi, a woman of noble character, could act in such a way unless influenced by a higher power. Rama reiterated that unforeseen events are the work of Providence and cannot be averted.  He reminded Lakshmana that destiny governs joy and sorrow, gain and loss, even influencing the actions of learned sages.  Unexpected occurrences, especially those interrupting planned events, are also attributed to destiny.  Rama affirmed his own composure despite the disrupted coronation, urging Lakshmana to abandon his grief and halt the coronation preparations. He proposed using the consecrated water gathered for the ceremony for his purification rites before entering exile.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama continued to console Lakshmana, explaining that unforeseen events are often the work of a higher power. He remained unperturbed by the sudden change of plans, accepting it as destiny.  He urged Lakshmana to cease the coronation preparations and instead use the consecrated water for his purification rituals before embarking on his exile.  Rama dismissed the importance of the royal items intended for the coronation, stating that simple water would suffice for his ascetic vows. He advised Lakshmana against grieving the loss of kingship, emphasizing that exile held greater significance for him.  Finally, Rama defended both Kaikeyi and his father, attributing the situation to divine influence beyond their control.

summary:
Canto XXIII

Enraged to hear the exhortation of Sri Rama, Laksmana urges in reply that
the word of their father was worth ignoring inasmuch as it was divorced
from righteousness and, further emphasizing the predominance
of personal effort over destiny, persuades Sri Rama to take
up arms against those who interfere with his installation
and occupy the throne of Ayodhya by force. Sri Rama,
however, pacifies Laksmana and impresses on him
the imperative necessity of their carrying out
the command of their father
content: Lakshmana, distressed by Rama's acceptance of exile, swung between despair and admiration for his brother's righteousness.  His anger manifested physically: he frowned, hissed like a cobra, and his face became fierce like a lion's.  He then vehemently challenged Rama's decision, arguing that his father's command, influenced by Kaikeyi, was unjust and should be defied. Lakshmana questioned Rama's concern for public opinion and his passive acceptance of what he perceived as a deceitful plot. He emphasized Rama's capability to reclaim his rightful position through action and condemned Dasaratha and Kaikeyi's manipulation.  He further asserted that such adherence to a flawed sense of duty was detrimental and disapproved of by the people. Lakshmana expressed his own inability to tolerate anyone but Rama as king and criticized the misplaced piety that led Rama to this predicament. He declared his disapproval even if the situation were divinely ordained, urging Rama to disregard the unjust decree.

summary:

the word of their father was worth ignoring inasmuch as it was divorced
from righteousness and, further emphasizing the predominance

and occupy the throne of Ayodhya by force. Sri Rama,

content: Lakshmana continues his impassioned plea to Rama, questioning how Rama can passively accept this unjust exile when he has the power to rectify the situation. He criticizes Rama's adherence to a flawed sense of duty, arguing that it's viewed negatively by the people and serves only the interests of their enemies, Dasaratha and Kaikeyi. Lakshmana rejects the idea that this situation is divinely ordained and insists that true strength lies in defying fate, not submitting to it.  He vows to use his own power to overturn the exile, asserting that neither gods nor men can prevent Rama's rightful coronation.  Lakshmana declares his intent to thwart Dasaratha and Kaikeyi's plans and promises to inflict suffering on anyone who opposes Rama. He sarcastically suggests that Rama should only consider a forest retreat after thousands of years of rule, after entrusting his sons with the kingdom, as was the tradition of ancient sage-kings.  Finally, Lakshmana dismisses any concerns Rama might have about causing unrest by claiming the throne, assuring him there's no need for such apprehension.

summary:

the word of their father was worth ignoring inasmuch as it was divorced
from righteousness and, further emphasizing the predominance

up arms against those who interfere with his installation

content: Lakshmana boasts of his strength and declares that his might surpasses even destiny, promising suffering to anyone who opposes him. He ironically suggests that Rama should consider a forest retreat only after ruling for thousands of years, following the tradition of ancient kings who passed the kingdom to their sons.  Lakshmana then dismisses Rama’s potential concerns about causing unrest by claiming the throne, pledging to protect both Rama and the kingdom as the shore protects the ocean. He urges Rama to proceed with the coronation, confident that he alone can repel any opposition.  Lakshmana emphasizes that his weapons are not for show but for crushing enemies, asserting he wouldn't hesitate to use them against anyone, even Indra.  He vividly describes the battlefield carnage he will unleash, filling the earth with the bodies of elephants, horses, and warriors, felled by his sword. He questions how any man could stand against him in battle.  Lakshmana boasts of his archery skills and promises to inflict devastating losses on the enemy. He concludes by declaring that his actions today will expose Dasaratha’s powerlessness and establish Rama’s sovereignty, affirming that his powerful arms, fit for adornment and generosity, will be used to eliminate those obstructing Rama’s coronation.

content: Lakshmana continues his impassioned plea to Rama, boasting of his martial prowess. He declares his ability to overwhelm opponents with arrows, striking down individuals and masses alike. He asserts that his actions will not only demonstrate his own strength but also expose King Dasaratha's inability to prevent Rama's rightful ascension.  Lakshmana emphasizes the duality of his arms – capable of both adornment and generosity, yet also ready to eliminate any obstacles to Rama's coronation.  He implores Rama to command him, offering to vanquish any enemy and secure the kingdom for his brother. Finally, Rama, deeply moved, comforts Lakshmana and gently reminds him of his unwavering commitment to obeying their parents' wishes, highlighting this obedience as the righteous path.

summary:
Canto XXIV

Finding Sri Rama firm in his obedience to the command of his parents,
Kausalya (Sri Rama’s mother) urges him to take her alongwith him.
On being told, however, that it was incumbent on a matron
whose husband was alive to remain with the latter
and serve him, she consents to Sri Rama’s
departure to the forest
content: Kausalya, overwhelmed with grief, questioned how Rama, raised in luxury and kindness, could endure the hardships of the forest. She lamented his exile, expressing disbelief that such a fate could befall the beloved prince.  Kausalya declared her intention to accompany Rama, likening herself to a cow following her calf.  Rama gently responded, urging her to remain and care for their father, Dasaratha, as abandoning him would be cruel and against dharma (righteous conduct).  He emphasized the importance of serving their father as long as he lived.  Kausalya, pleased by Rama's wisdom and dutiful nature, accepted his advice.  Despite this resolution, Rama continued to console his grieving mother.

summary:
Canto XXIV

Finding Sri Rama firm in his obedience to the command of his parents,
Kausalya (Sri Rama’s mother) urges him to take her alongwith him.
On being told, however, that it was incumbent on a matron
whose husband was alive to remain with the latter
and serve him, she consents to Sri Rama’s
departure to the forest
content: Rama explains to his grieving mother, Kausalya, that abandoning her husband, King Dasaratha, would be a grave transgression against dharma.  He reminds her that their father, as king, deserves their utmost respect and service. Kausalya, moved by Rama's wisdom and dutiful words, accepts his reasoning. However, Rama continues to console her, assuring her that he will return after fulfilling his father's command and spending the stipulated time in the forest. He reminds Kausalya that a wife's duty is to serve her husband, and that this is the path to righteousness.  He urges her to maintain her devotion to the king, observing religious practices and praying for his well-being during Rama's absence.  Kausalya, though still heartbroken, acknowledges Rama's unwavering resolve.  She tearfully expresses her hope to see him return safely from the forest, clad in ascetic garb. Recognizing the inevitability of his departure, Kausalya offers blessings and prayers for his safe return.

summary:
Canto XXIV

Kausalya (Sri Rama’s mother) urges him to take her alongwith him.
On being told, however, that it was incumbent on a matron

and serve him, she consents to Sri Rama’s
departure to the forest
content: Queen Kausalya, accepting the inevitability of Rama's exile, advises him on his duties and expresses her hopes for his safe return.  She reminds Rama that the kingdom is not leaderless, as his brother Bharata will serve as regent.  She urges Rama to be mindful of King Dasaratha's grief, advising him to offer support and comfort to his aging father.  Kausalya counsels Rama to maintain his devotion to the king during his absence. She emphasizes a wife's duty to her husband, highlighting the spiritual merit gained through such service. Kausalya requests Rama to ensure that religious rites and offerings are continued in his absence. She then expresses her own intention to lead a disciplined life, dedicated to serving the king and awaiting Rama's return.  Overcome with sorrow, she acknowledges the power of destiny and bids farewell to Rama, offering blessings for his safe journey and successful completion of his exile. Kausalya finds solace in the hope that Rama will return safely, bringing her joy and comfort.

summary:
Canto XXIV

Finding Sri Rama firm in his obedience to the command of his parents,
Kausalya (Sri Rama’s mother) urges him to take her alongwith him.
