and others, but being unable to walk
begins to totter and stops short
on the road yielding to the
remonstrances of his
wise ministers
content: King Dasaratha, witnessing the collective grief of his people, collapsed like a felled tree.  A cry arose from the crowd following Rama’s chariot, some calling out to Rama, others to his mother Kausalya.  Rama, looking back, saw his father’s despair and his mother’s anguish.  Bound by his duty, he avoided their gaze and instructed Sumantra to hasten their departure. He noted his parents were on foot, though deserving of comfort and a chariot.  Like an elephant driven forward by goads, Rama felt unable to bear the distressing sight. Kausalya, like a cow whose calf is tethered, ran after Rama's chariot, weeping and calling out to Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana. Rama repeatedly looked back at his grieving mother, who followed as if in a trance.  Dasaratha cried out for the chariot to stop, while Rama urged Sumantra to proceed.  Torn, Sumantra received further instructions from Rama: ignore the king's pleas and continue, for prolonging this painful scene would be disastrous.  Sumantra obeyed, urging the horses onward.  The king’s men, unable to keep up with the chariot, returned to Dasaratha, having symbolically accompanied Rama in their minds.  The ministers advised the king against following any further, saying it was unwise to pursue someone you wished to see return.  Overwhelmed with grief and exhaustion, Dasaratha finally stopped, watching his son depart with a heavy heart, accompanied by his equally distressed wives.

summary:
Canto XL

Going clockwise round DaSaratha (as a token of respect), Sita, Rama and
Laksmana greet him. Accompanied by Sita, Sri Rama salutes Kausalya.
Laksmana too hails Kausalya first and then his own mother, Sumitra.
Sumitra for her part tenders salutary advice to her son. The princes
and the princess having mounted the chariot, Sumantra flicks
the horses. The citizens that had assembled, closely follow
the chariot; but unable to keep pace with its swift
movement, they return desolate. Dasaratha too
runs after the chariot alongwith Kausalya
and others, but being unable to walk
begins to totter and stops short
on the road yielding to the
remonstrances of his
wise ministers
content: Sumantra, the charioteer, followed Rama's command and urged the horses to greater speed, leaving behind the crowd that was struggling to keep up.  The king's men, unable to match the chariot's pace, returned to Dasaratha, having symbolically accompanied Rama in their hearts. The citizens, however, remained, their minds fixed on Rama. Upon their return, the ministers advised Dasaratha against pursuing Rama further, arguing that one should not follow someone they wish to see return.  Hearing this counsel, the distressed Dasaratha, his body perspiring and his face etched with sorrow, finally halted, his wives beside him, watching his son Rama disappear from view.

summary:
Canto XLI

The ladies of the royal gynaeceum bewail the exile of Sri Rama and others and
the citizens of Ayodhya too are reduced to a miserable plight
content: The women of Ayodhya cried out in lament as Rama departed.  They mourned the loss of their protector, a prince who was kind and compassionate to all, regardless of their station.  They questioned the king's decision, feeling abandoned and vulnerable.  The queens, heartbroken, wailed like cows separated from their calves.  Overwhelmed by the cries of his people, King Dasaratha was consumed by grief.  Normal life in Ayodhya ceased.  People neglected their duties and routines, and even nature seemed to reflect the city’s despair.  Omens appeared in the sky, and a dark cloud enveloped the city, symbolizing the sorrow that had befallen Ayodhya with Rama's exile.

summary:
Canto XLI

The ladies of the royal gynaeceum bewail the exile of Sri Rama and others and
the citizens of Ayodhya too are reduced to a miserable plight
content: The celestial bodies dimmed, their light fading as if mirroring the city's despair.  A storm gathered, dark clouds swirling like a churning ocean, as Ayodhya trembled with the news of Rama's exile.  Darkness enveloped the city, both literally and figuratively.  The people of Ayodhya were plunged into grief, neglecting their daily lives and lamenting the king's decision.  They openly expressed their sorrow, tears streaming down their faces as they walked the streets.  The natural world seemed to reflect their despair: the breeze vanished, the moon appeared troubled, and the sun's warmth couldn't reach the grieving populace. Family bonds were strained as everyone focused solely on Rama's absence.  Rama's friends, particularly, were overwhelmed with sorrow, unable to find solace in sleep.  The city itself, deprived of its beloved prince, felt as unstable as the earth without its guardian deity, its cries of lament echoing through the streets.

summary:
Canto XLII

Sri Rama having departed for the forest in an exceptionally swift-going
chariot, Dasaratha vainly seeks to cover the intervening distance on foot.
The very dust raised by the chariot having disappeared after a while,
the Emperor feels doubly disconsolate and drops to the ground.
When Kaikeyi comes forward to support him, he scolds her and
asks her not to touch his person. Kausalya then lifts him up
and persuades him to return. His personal attendants
take him to the latter's apartments and, seeing him
plunged in grief, Kausalya seats herself beside
him and begins to lament in various ways
content: King Dasaratha continued to watch the dust trail of Rama's departing chariot as long as it was visible.  As the dust disappeared, marking Rama's increasing distance, the King collapsed to the ground in despair.  Kausalya, his chief wife, rushed to his right side to help him up, while Kaikeyi approached his left.  Seeing Kaikeyi, Dasaratha, overcome with grief and anger, rebuked her, forbidding her from touching him and disowning her as his wife and kin. He declared that he renounced any connection to her or those who served her, blaming her selfish desires and lack of virtue for Rama's exile.  He even disavowed any future benefit from their marriage rituals and declared that any offerings Bharata might make in the afterlife on his behalf should not reach him. Kausalya then lifted the dust-covered king and escorted him back to the palace.  Dasaratha, tormented by the thought of Rama's exile, felt intense remorse, like someone who had committed a terrible act. He repeatedly looked back at the path Rama's chariot had taken, his appearance as diminished as a sun in eclipse.  Anguished, the king lamented the loss of his beloved son.  Upon learning that Rama had reached the city's outskirts, he cried out that while the hoofprints of the chariot horses were still visible, Rama himself was gone from sight.

summary:
Canto XLII

Sri Rama having departed for the forest in an exceptionally swift-going
chariot, Dasaratha vainly seeks to cover the intervening distance on foot.
The very dust raised by the chariot having disappeared after a while,
the Emperor feels doubly disconsolate and drops to the ground.
When Kaikeyi comes forward to support him, he scolds her and
asks her not to touch his person. Kausalya then lifts him up
and persuades him to return. His personal attendants
take him to the latter's apartments and, seeing him
plunged in grief, Kausalya seats herself beside
him and begins to lament in various ways
content: Dasaratha, heartbroken and inconsolable, repeatedly looked back at the path Rama's chariot had taken, his face as pale as a sun in eclipse.  He lamented the loss of his beloved son, his grief intensifying as he realized Rama had reached the city's outskirts. He cried out in anguish, noting that while the hoofprints of the chariot horses were still visible, Rama himself was gone.  The king imagined Rama's current hardship, contrasting it with his former comfort: how the prince, once accustomed to soft cushions, sandal paste, and gentle breezes, would now be sleeping on the bare ground, under a tree, his head resting on a rough piece of wood or stone. He pictured Rama rising at dawn, covered in dust, sighing deeply. He envisioned forest dwellers seeing the exiled prince, the protector of the world, wandering as a forlorn figure.  Dasaratha also lamented Sita's plight, imagining her delicate feet injured by thorns as she walked through the forest, and her fear upon hearing the roars of wild animals.  Turning his anger towards Kaikeyi, he blamed her for his misery, wishing her a widow's life in the kingdom he now found unbearable without Rama.  Surrounded by his people, the grieving king returned to his palace, now as desolate as a pool emptied of its life.  He looked upon the deserted city, its empty streets and closed shops, a reflection of his own despair.  Entering the palace, which felt empty without Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, he feebly requested his attendants to take him to Kausalya, Rama's mother, seeking solace in her presence.

summary:
Canto XLII

Sri Rama having departed for the forest in an exceptionally swift-going
chariot, Dasaratha vainly seeks to cover the intervening distance on foot.
The very dust raised by the chariot having disappeared after a while,
the Emperor feels doubly disconsolate and drops to the ground.
When Kaikeyi comes forward to support him, he scolds her and
asks her not to touch his person. Kausalya then lifts him up
and persuades him to return. His personal attendants
take him to the latter's apartments and, seeing him
plunged in grief, Kausalya seats herself beside
him and begins to lament in various ways
content: Dasaratha, overcome with grief, entered the deserted city.  The normally bustling streets and marketplaces were empty, reflecting the king's despair.  He mourned for Rama, his thoughts consumed by his son's absence. The palace felt empty and lifeless without Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana.  He weakly asked to be taken to Kausalya, Rama's mother, desperately seeking comfort. Though in her presence, his mind remained agitated, the palace seeming as bleak as a starless, moonless night.  He cried out in anguish, lamenting Rama's departure and the uncertainty of his return. He expressed his sorrow for those who would not live to see Rama again, emphasizing the joy that awaited those who would witness his eventual homecoming. As the night deepened, a night that felt like the end of his own world, Dasaratha, lost in his grief, reached out to Kausalya. He could not see her, his vision still fixed on the path Rama had taken.  He asked her to touch him, a small gesture of comfort in his immense despair. Kausalya, witnessing her husband's profound sorrow, sat beside him and wept.

summary:
Canto XLIII

The Lament
content: Queen Kausalya, heartbroken by Rama's exile, laments to King Dasaratha. She condemns Kaikeyi's actions, comparing her to a venomous serpent, freed from its old skin and now free to inflict further pain. Kausalya expresses her anguish, stating that even a life of poverty in Ayodhya would be preferable to Rama's exile, or even servitude to Kaikeyi. She criticizes Dasaratha for unjustly banishing Rama, likening it to a sacred offering being given to demons. Kausalya paints a vivid picture of Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana entering the forest, and worries about their well-being, forced to endure hardships they've never known.  She yearns for the day Rama will return, envisioning Ayodhya's joyous celebration upon their arrival.  She imagines the city adorned with banners, the crowds rejoicing, and the princes being showered with flowers and fruits upon their return.

summary:
Canto XLIII

The Lament
content: Kausalya continues to lament Rama's exile. She wonders when Rama will return to Ayodhya, victorious, with Sita by his side.  She longs for the day the city will celebrate their return, showering the princes with flowers and fruits as they process through the streets.  Kausalya questions her past actions, believing she must have committed some terrible deed to deserve this suffering.  She likens herself to a cow separated from her calf, blaming Kaikeyi for this forced separation.  Overwhelmed with grief, she declares she cannot live without her son, Rama, and Lakshmana.  Her sorrow burns within her like the scorching summer sun.

summary:
Canto XLIV

Establishing the greatness of Sri Rama, Sumitra, who is a pastmaster in
eloquence, assuages Kausalya’s grief
content: Sumitra consoles the grieving Queen Kausalya, reminding her of Rama's virtues. She emphasizes Rama's nobility in upholding his father's word and adhering to dharma, the righteous path.  Sumitra highlights Lakshmana's unwavering devotion and Sita's willing companionship in Rama's exile. She extols Rama's widespread fame and inherent goodness, suggesting that nature itself will protect him. Sumitra further recounts Rama's strength, recalling his defeat of the demon Subahu and his mastery of celestial weapons. She expresses confidence in Rama's ability to thrive in the forest and ultimately regain his rightful throne, emphasizing his inherent qualities of splendor, valor, and benevolent strength as guarantees of his future success.  Sumitra concludes by declaring Rama's transcendent nature, likening him to the source and essence of light, rule, glory, and forbearance.

summary:
Canto XLIV

Establishing the greatness of Sri Rama, Sumitra, who is a pastmaster in
eloquence, assuages Kausalya’s grief
content: Sumitra continues her consoling words to Kausalya, emphasizing Rama's strength and invincibility. She recounts Viswamitra's bestowal of celestial weapons upon Rama and his subsequent defeat of the demon Subahu.  Sumitra asserts that Rama will remain undeterred in the forest, as comfortable as he would be in his own palace. She questions how the earth could disobey Rama, whose arrows easily vanquish his foes.  Sumitra further emphasizes Rama's inherent splendor, valor, and benevolent strength, assuring Kausalya that these qualities guarantee his return to the throne. She proclaims Rama's transcendent nature, comparing him to fundamental forces like the sun and fire, and the embodiment of rule, glory, and forbearance.  Sumitra questions what challenges could possibly hinder a being of such divine stature, whether in the forest or in the city. She affirms that Rama will soon be rightfully restored to his throne, accompanied by Sita and the blessings of fortune. Sumitra describes the widespread grief in Ayodhya upon Rama's departure, noting Sita's unwavering devotion in accompanying him, as well as Lakshmana's armed vigilance.  She reiterates her confidence in Rama's triumphant return, urging Kausalya to abandon her grief and despair. Sumitra paints a vivid picture of Rama's eventual return, bowing before his mother, prompting tears of joy. Finally, she implores Kausalya to relinquish her sorrow, reminding her of Rama's unwavering righteousness and suggesting that her grief should be directed towards comforting others who are also suffering from Rama's absence.  She assures Kausalya that she will soon see her son return, surrounded by his companions, and her joy will be abundant.

summary:
Canto XLIV

Establishing the greatness of Sri Rama, Sumitra, who is a pastmaster in
eloquence, assuages Kausalya’s grief
content: Sumitra consoles Kausalya, assuring her that Rama will return safe and sound, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana.  She gently chides Kausalya for her excessive grief, reminding her that others also mourn Rama's absence and need her comfort.  Sumitra emphasizes Rama's unwavering righteousness and virtuous nature, stating that no one is more committed to the right path.  She paints a vivid picture of Rama's homecoming: he will greet his mother respectfully, prompting tears of joy, and will tenderly touch her feet with his hands.  Sumitra's eloquent words of comfort finally bring her to a pause.  Upon hearing Sumitra's consoling speech, Kausalya's grief dissipates like a light autumn cloud.

summary:
Canto XLV

When the citizens that followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest
refuse to return even when pleaded by Sri Rama in many ways,
unable as they were to bear separation from him, Sri Rama, with

Sita and Laksmana, gets down from his chariot and begins to
walk. The citizens try to deflect him from his course and
persuade him to return, but in vain. At the close of
the day they all reach the bank of the Tamasa
content: The people of Ayodhya, deeply devoted to Rama, followed him into exile.  Even when King Dasharatha returned to the city, bound by custom to only accompany Rama a short distance, the citizens refused to leave Rama's side. Rama, renowned for his virtues, was beloved by the people of Ayodhya like the full moon. Though the people pleaded with him, Rama, honoring his father's promise, continued towards the forest.  Looking at them with affection, Rama addressed the citizens, urging them to transfer their love and respect to Bharata. He assured them that Bharata, though young, possessed wisdom and noble qualities and would rule wisely, allaying their fears. Rama emphasized Bharata's suitability as a leader and urged the citizens to obey him and their father, King Dasharatha, so that the king wouldn't grieve during Rama's exile.  The more Rama adhered to his father's wishes, the stronger the people's desire for him as their king became. Rama and Lakshmana, through their virtues, drew the tearful citizens with them as if bound by cords. Elderly Brahmanas, unable to keep pace with Rama's chariot, called out, urging the horses to turn back and be loyal to their master by not taking him further against the people's wishes. They reminded the horses that all creatures, especially those with ears, heard their pleas and should therefore heed them.

summary:
Canto XLV

When the citizens that followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest
refuse to return even when pleaded by Sri Rama in many ways,
unable as they were to bear separation from him, Sri Rama, with

Sita and Laksmana, gets down from his chariot and begins to
walk. The citizens try to deflect him from his course and
persuade him to return, but in vain. At the close of
the day they all reach the bank of the Tamasa
content: The Brahmanas, especially the elders wise in knowledge, age, and ascetic practice, pleaded with Rama's horses to stop, reminding them of their loyalty to their master and highlighting the people's distress. They appealed to all creatures, particularly those with ears, to hear their plea and persuade Rama to return. They extolled Rama's virtues and argued that such a noble person deserved to be taken back to the city, not further into the forest. Seeing their anguish, Rama quickly descended from his chariot and began walking with Sita and Lakshmana. This gesture allowed the elderly Brahmanas to keep pace. Rama, known for his compassion, couldn't bear to ride while they struggled on foot.  Distressed by Rama's continued departure, the Brahmanas declared their unwavering devotion and intention to follow him, pointing out that even the sacred fires were being carried along. They offered their ceremonial canopies as shade from the sun and expressed their shift in focus from Vedic studies to accompanying him. Assuring Rama of their wives' safety at home, the Brahmanas questioned who would uphold righteousness if Rama, a paragon of virtue, disregarded their pleas.  Lowering their dust-covered, white-haired heads, they implored him to return.

summary:
Canto XLV

When the citizens that followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest
refuse to return even when pleaded by Sri Rama in many ways,
unable as they were to bear separation from him, Sri Rama, with

Sita and Laksmana, gets down from his chariot and begins to
walk. The citizens try to deflect him from his course and
persuade him to return, but in vain. At the close of
the day they all reach the bank of the Tamasa
content: The Brahmanas, deeply attached to Rama, declared their unwavering resolve to follow him into exile.  Their minds, once focused on Vedic scriptures, were now fixed on accompanying him.  They assured Rama that their wives were safe and their knowledge of the Vedas secure within their memories.  They questioned how righteousness could prevail if Rama, its embodiment, ignored their pleas.  With heads bowed low, their white hair dusted from prostration, they begged him to return.  They reminded him of the unfinished sacrifices that depended on his presence.  Even the inanimate and animate world, they asserted, yearned for his return, symbolized by the rustling leaves of trees that seemed to cry out and the motionless, hungry birds perched on branches. As this heartfelt plea continued, they reached the Tamasa River, its presence seemingly a further attempt to impede Rama's journey.  Meanwhile, Sumantra, Rama's charioteer, unyoked the weary horses, allowing them to drink, bathe, and graze near the riverbank.

summary:
Canto XLVI

Having reached the bank of the Tamasa and thinking of the plight of the
people of Ayodhya, Sri Rama lays himself down on a bed of leaves
bewailing the lot of his parents and feeling reassured by the thought
of Bharata’s noble qualities; while Laksmana opens his dialogue
with Sumantra on the divine excellences of Sri Rama. Waking
up in the meantime, Sri Rama urges the charioteer-minister to
drive the chariot in such a way as to put the citizens, that
had accompanied them, off the scent and lead them to
think that the chariot had turned back towards
Ayodhya instead of proceeding towards the forest.

He then mounts the chariot alongwith Sita and
Laksmana and presses on to the forest
content: On the banks of the Tamasa river, Rama, looking at Sita, addressed Lakshmana.  He reminded Lakshmana that this was their first night of exile and urged him not to worry about those left behind in Ayodhya.  Rama observed the quiet forest, noting the animals and birds seeking shelter, and lamented that the city of Ayodhya, his father's capital, would be mourning their departure. He acknowledged the people's affection for himself, Lakshmana, Bharata, and Shatrughna. Rama expressed concern for his parents, fearing their grief might overwhelm them. However, he took comfort in Bharata's kind nature, believing he would console their parents. Rama then thanked Lakshmana for accompanying him, stating it relieved him of the burden of seeking aid to protect Sita. He declared his intention to fast that night, subsisting only on water despite the availability of wild fruits.  Rama then instructed Sumantra to attend to the horses. After Sumantra had seen to the horses’ needs, he returned to Rama.  As night fell, Sumantra and Lakshmana prepared a bed of leaves for Rama. Rama and Sita then retired for the night on the riverbank. While Rama slept, Lakshmana spoke with Sumantra about Rama's many virtues.

summary:
Canto XLVI

Having reached the bank of the Tamasa and thinking of the plight of the
people of Ayodhya, Sri Rama lays himself down on a bed of leaves
bewailing the lot of his parents and feeling reassured by the thought
of Bharata’s noble qualities; while Laksmana opens his dialogue
with Sumantra on the divine excellences of Sri Rama. Waking
up in the meantime, Sri Rama urges the charioteer-minister to
drive the chariot in such a way as to put the citizens, that
had accompanied them, off the scent and lead them to
think that the chariot had turned back towards
Ayodhya instead of proceeding towards the forest.

He then mounts the chariot alongwith Sita and
Laksmana and presses on to the forest
content: After Sumantra attended to the horses, he returned to Rama. Sumantra and Lakshmana then prepared a bed of leaves for Rama and Sita on the banks of the Tamasa river.  Rama and Sita rested there for the night.  As Rama slept, Lakshmana discussed Rama's virtues with Sumantra.  They stayed awake all night, and as the sun rose, Rama awoke to find the citizens of Ayodhya still camped nearby. He observed their devotion and instructed Lakshmana to prepare for their departure quickly while the citizens slept, choosing a safe and secluded route to minimize further hardship for the devoted people of Ayodhya. Lakshmana agreed, and Rama instructed Sumantra to prepare the chariot. Sumantra promptly readied the chariot and informed Rama, urging him to board with Sita and Lakshmana.

summary:
Canto XLVI

Having reached the bank of the Tamasa and thinking of the plight of the
people of Ayodhya, Sri Rama lays himself down on a bed of leaves
bewailing the lot of his parents and feeling reassured by the thought
of Bharata’s noble qualities; while Laksmana opens his dialogue
with Sumantra on the divine excellences of Sri Rama. Waking
up in the meantime, Sri Rama urges the charioteer-minister to
drive the chariot in such a way as to put the citizens, that
had accompanied them, off the scent and lead them to
think that the chariot had turned back towards
Ayodhya instead of proceeding towards the forest.

He then mounts the chariot alongwith Sita and
Laksmana and presses on to the forest
content: Lakshmana agreed with Rama's suggestion to depart quickly.  Rama then instructed Sumantra to prepare the chariot for their journey into the forest.  Sumantra quickly prepared the chariot and informed Rama that it was ready.  Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana boarded the chariot, and they swiftly crossed the Tamasa River.  After crossing the river, they traveled on a smooth, safe road.  To mislead the citizens of Ayodhya who had followed them, Rama instructed Sumantra to drive the chariot north for a short distance and then return discreetly, creating the impression that they were heading back to Ayodhya.  Sumantra followed Rama's instructions and reported back. Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana then settled comfortably in the chariot, and Sumantra drove them towards a forest suitable for ascetic life, initially heading north due to favorable omens.

summary:
Canto XLVII

The citizens that had followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest woke
to find Sri Rama and his party gone and begin to reproach
themselves. Overcome with grief they hunt up the
tracks of his chariot; but unable to find them,
they helplessly return to Ayodhya
in utter despondency
content: The citizens who had followed Rama awoke at dawn to find him gone. Grief-stricken and tearful, they searched for him but could not even catch a glimpse. Dejected and bewildered, they lamented, "Woe to the sleep that kept us from noticing Rama's departure! How could he, whose actions are always purposeful, abandon us, his devoted followers, for a life of asceticism in the forest? He was like a father to us! Should we end our lives here or journey north to die?  What good is life without him?  We could light a funeral pyre and perish together. How can we face those in Ayodhya and tell them that we let Rama, the kind and generous, go?  The city will be devastated.  Having accompanied him, how can we return without him?"  They searched for his chariot tracks for a while, but the trail disappeared.  Disheartened and feeling abandoned by fate, they retraced their steps back to Ayodhya, where the citizens were already in despair.

summary:
Canto XLVII

The citizens that had followed Sri Rama in his journey to the forest woke
to find Sri Rama and his party gone and begin to reproach
themselves. Overcome with grief they hunt up the
tracks of his chariot; but unable to find them,
they helplessly return to Ayodhya
in utter despondency
content: The citizens, heartbroken like cows who have lost their calves, continued to lament Rama's departure. Following the chariot tracks for a short distance, they were dismayed when the trail vanished, realizing Rama had taken a different route back.  Discouraged and feeling abandoned by fate, they questioned what they should do, lamenting their misfortune.  With heavy hearts, they returned to Ayodhya, finding the city and its people already gripped by despair.  The city, without Rama, appeared desolate, like a river stripped of its serpents or the sky without the moon, bringing the citizens no joy.  They entered their opulent homes, yet their sorrow was so profound they could barely recognize their own families, their happiness completely gone.

summary: No summary available
content: The citizens of Ayodhya, heartbroken by Rama's departure, returned home grieving.  Their sorrow was palpable, their tears flowed freely.  Upon reaching their homes, they were met by their families, and the collective weeping intensified.  Joy vanished from the city. Merchants ceased their trade, households stopped cooking, and even the return of lost wealth or the birth of a son brought no solace.  The women of Ayodhya, distraught at Rama's absence, reproached their husbands, questioning the value of home, family, and possessions without him.  They praised Lakshmana for his unwavering loyalty in accompanying Rama and Sita.  They envied the rivers, ponds, and lakes that Rama would grace with his presence, the forests and mountains that would adorn his path.  Nature itself, they believed, would honor Rama, offering him flowers, fruits, and clear waters.  They declared that where Rama resided, fear and humiliation could not exist.

summary: No summary available
content: The women of Ayodhya continued lamenting Rama's exile.  They envisioned the beautiful flora and refreshing waters that would greet Rama on his journey, believing that nature itself would honor him.  They affirmed that wherever Rama resided, fear and disgrace could not exist.  Hope briefly flickered as they expressed a desire to follow Rama, seeking the protection and solace of his presence.  Their grief then turned to anger directed at Kaikeyi. They declared their unwillingness to live under her rule, fearing her unjust and destructive influence on the kingdom. They predicted the demise of the king and the ruin of the kingdom in Rama's absence, urging their husbands to choose death, exile with Rama, or flight to a place untouched by Kaikeyi's name, rather than endure her reign.

summary: No summary available
content: The women of Ayodhya continued to mourn Rama's exile. They questioned how anyone could find happiness under the rule of Kaikeyi, who orchestrated Rama's banishment. They lamented that the kingdom was rudderless and predicted its imminent ruin due to Kaikeyi's actions.  In their despair, they contemplated drastic measures – poison, following Rama into exile, or fleeing to a place untouched by Kaikeyi's influence.  They felt unjustly bound to Bharata's rule, comparing themselves to livestock awaiting slaughter.  Amidst their grief, they described Rama's noble qualities – his strength, his truthfulness, his gentle nature, and his striking appearance.  Their sorrow intensified as night fell, and Ayodhya, once vibrant, became shrouded in darkness and silence.  The cessation of daily activities and the absence of joy transformed the city into a desolate wasteland, mirroring the emptiness in their hearts.  Overwhelmed by grief, they wept and fainted, their love for Rama surpassing even their love for their own sons. The once joyous city became a bleak shadow of its former self, devoid of music, celebration, and prosperity.

summary: No summary available
content: The women of Ayodhya, heartbroken over Rama's exile, grieved as if their own sons or brothers had been banished.  Their sorrow was so profound that Rama was dearer to them than their own children.  The city of Ayodhya, once vibrant with music, dance, and celebration, now fell silent.  Its joy vanished, and its bustling trade ceased.  The once-thriving city became a desolate wasteland, like an ocean drained of its water.

summary:
Canto XLIX
Having covered a long distance in the meantime, Sri Rama finishes his
morning bath and devotions and, having crossed the Vedasruti,

Gomati and Syandika rivers, presses forward
talking with Sumantra
content: Rama, obeying his father's order, journeyed a long distance through the night.  As he traveled, the pleasant night passed, and after morning prayers, he continued onward, passing through numerous regions.  He observed well-cultivated villages and blossoming forests as he rode, seemingly at a slow pace but actually covering ground quickly, thanks to his excellent horses.  He overheard the conversations of villagers, who lamented Dasaratha's decision, influenced by desire, to exile Rama. They criticized Kaikeyi for her harshness and improper actions in orchestrating Rama's banishment.  They expressed concern for Sita, accustomed to comfort, now facing hardship, and questioned Dasaratha's lack of affection in abandoning his blameless son. Rama continued his journey, passing out of Kosala, crossing the Vedasruti river, and then the Gomati, a river with cool waters and cows on its banks.  He also crossed the Syandika river, filled with the sounds of peacocks and swans.  He pointed out to Sita the prosperous land of Kosala, gifted long ago by Manu to Ikshvaku, and discussed with his charioteer Sumantra his longing to return to Ayodhya and hunt in the forests with his parents.

summary:
Canto XLIX
Having covered a long distance in the meantime, Sri Rama finishes his
morning bath and devotions and, having crossed the Vedasruti,

Gomati and Syandika rivers, presses forward
talking with Sumantra
content: Rama crossed the Gomati River and then the Syandika, a river lively with the sounds of peacocks and swans.  He showed Sita the prosperous land of Kosala, a gift from the ancient king Manu to his son Ikshvaku.  Speaking to his charioteer, Sumantra, Rama expressed his longing to return to Ayodhya and hunt in the forests along the Sarayu River with his parents.  While acknowledging that hunting is a favored pastime of royal sages, Rama insisted that he did not yearn for it excessively. He continued his journey, conversing with Sumantra.

summary:
Canto L

Standing with his face turned towards Ayodhya, Sri Rama bids farewell to
his birth-place and, sending back the people hailing from the countryside,
who had come to see him, and crossing the frontiers of Kosala, Sri
Rama reaches the bank of the holy Ganga. Alighting from the
chariot under an Ingudi tree standing on the bank, he goes
forward to meet Guha, the chief of the Nisadas, who
had come to meet him. Nay, worshipping the evening

content: Rama, bidding farewell to Ayodhya, addressed the city, expressing his gratitude to its protectors and promising to return after his exile.  He then tearfully addressed the people who had accompanied him, requesting them to return to their homes.  The people, grieving, paid their respects and watched as Rama departed.  He journeyed through the prosperous lands of Kosala, passing through villages filled with resources, charitable people, and places of worship. He then entered a territory of contented people and abundant livestock, marked by the sounds of Vedic chants.  Continuing his journey, Rama reached the banks of the sacred river Ganga, a threefold river frequented by sages and celestial beings, adorned with hermitages and graced by the presence of various divine beings. Its pools, overflowing with water, served as a haven for celestial nymphs and its banks as a playground for gods.

content: Rama reached the divine Ganga river.  The threefold river flowed through the heavens, earth, and the underworld. Its cool, clear waters were frequented by sages.  The riverbanks, adorned with hermitages, were visited by celestial nymphs and various divine beings. The river flowed through celestial gardens and contained divine lotuses.  Its waters, sometimes calm, sometimes turbulent, created a variety of sounds.  The river, teeming with life, was graced by the presence of swans, cranes, and other birds. Its banks were lined with trees and flowers.  Purified by the touch of Vishnu's feet, the river cleansed those who bathed in or drank its waters.

content: Rama, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana, arrived at the Ganga river. The river was described as pure, its waters capable of washing away sins. Its banks were lively with various creatures, from Indra's elephants to common birds.  Rama, noticing a large Ingudi tree, decided to rest there for the night.  He, Sita, and Lakshmana disembarked from the chariot. Sumantra unyoked the horses.  Guha, the local chieftain and a friend of Rama, arrived to greet them. He offered Rama his kingdom and provisions for the night, expressing his honor at having such a distinguished guest.

summary:
Canto L

content: Guha, deeply saddened by Rama's ascetic attire, embraced him and offered his kingdom of Sragaverapura, along with various foods and provisions. He expressed his honor at hosting such an esteemed guest. Rama, in turn, acknowledged Guha's affection and hospitality, but declined the offerings, explaining his commitment to a life of asceticism, sustained by fruits, roots, and the bark of trees. He requested only fodder for his horses, emphasizing their importance as his father's beloved animals.  Guha immediately ordered his men to provide the horses with proper feed and drink. Rama, after performing evening rituals, consumed only water, observing a fast. Lakshmana washed Rama and Sita's feet before retiring to a nearby tree.  Guha, along with Sumantra, kept watch over the sleeping Rama, discussing his virtues.  Thus, the night passed for Rama, who, accustomed to comfort, now lay on the bare ground.

summary:
Canto L

content: Rama, clad in barkcloth, performed his evening rituals facing west and broke his fast with only water brought by Lakshmana, a gesture of reverence for the sacred Ganga.  After washing Rama and Sita's feet, Lakshmana retired to a nearby tree.  Guha, along with the charioteer Sumantra, kept watch over the sleeping Rama, discussing his noble qualities.  Thus, the night passed slowly for Rama, who, accustomed to a life of ease and comfort, was now resting on the bare earth.

summary:
Canto LI

Expressing his readiness to guard the Crown prince and his consort,
keeping awake the whole night, Guha importunes Laksmana to repose.
Reminding Guha of Sri Rama’s greatness, Laksmana, however, tells
him that even though the duty of guarding his princely brother and
his consort could as well be entrusted to Guha, he felt that he
did not deserve to lie down in the presence of his elder
brother and sister-in-law and preferred to remain awake.
Expressing grief for his royal father and loving mothers
he therefore spends the night talking with Guha
content: Guha, witnessing Rama and Sita sleeping on the ground, offered Lakshmana a comfortable bed, assuring him of his loyalty and vigilance.  He declared his deep affection for Rama and pledged to protect him and Sita with his kinsmen.  Guha emphasized his familiarity with the forest and his ability to defend them against any threat.  Lakshmana declined the offer, explaining his inability to rest while his brother and sister-in-law slept on the ground.  He lamented their exile and expressed his concern for his father, King Dasaratha, and mothers, fearing they might not survive the grief. He pictured Ayodhya, once filled with cries of anguish, now fallen silent with exhaustion. He worried most about Queen Kausalya, believing his own mother, Sumitra, might find solace in the hope of seeing her other son, Shatrughna.

summary:
Canto LI

Expressing his readiness to guard the Crown prince and his consort,
keeping awake the whole night, Guha importunes Laksmana to repose.
Reminding Guha of Sri Rama’s greatness, Laksmana, however, tells
him that even though the duty of guarding his princely brother and
his consort could as well be entrusted to Guha, he felt that he
did not deserve to lie down in the presence of his elder
brother and sister-in-law and preferred to remain awake.
Expressing grief for his royal father and loving mothers
he therefore spends the night talking with Guha
content: Lakshmana, deeply troubled, reflected on the dire consequences of Rama's exile. He feared that his father, King Dasaratha, weakened by grief, would not survive the night.  He imagined the lamentations of the women in the palace, now silenced by exhaustion. Lakshmana worried about Queen Kausalya, Rama's mother, and his own mother, Sumitra, though he believed Sumitra might find some comfort in the hope of seeing her other son, Shatrughna. He envisioned Ayodhya, once joyful and prosperous, now desolate and grieving.  He questioned how his father could endure the absence of his eldest son. Lakshmana predicted that if Dasaratha perished, Kausalya and then Sumitra would follow. He lamented that his father would die without seeing Rama ascend the throne.  He pictured the funeral rites, performed by those fortunate enough to survive.  Lakshmana then shifted to a more hopeful vision of Ayodhya, thriving and bustling with activity, should his father live. He expressed his wish that he and Rama would safely return from exile to this prosperous city.  Overwhelmed by grief and worry, Lakshmana continued to lament throughout the night. Witnessing Lakshmana's deep sorrow, Guha, too, wept.

summary:
Canto LI

Expressing his readiness to guard the Crown prince and his consort,
keeping awake the whole night, Guha importunes Laksmana to repose.
Reminding Guha of Sri Rama’s greatness, Laksmana, however, tells
him that even though the duty of guarding his princely brother and
his consort could as well be entrusted to Guha, he felt that he
did not deserve to lie down in the presence of his elder
brother and sister-in-law and preferred to remain awake.
Expressing grief for his royal father and loving mothers
he therefore spends the night talking with Guha
content: Lakshmana expressed hope that his father, King Dasaratha, would survive Rama's exile, allowing them to return and see him again. He wished for a safe return to Ayodhya with Rama after their exile ended. Lakshmana's night was spent in deep grief and worry.  Observing Lakshmana's sorrow, Guha, overwhelmed by the situation, also wept.

summary:
Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,
procured by Guha, Sri Rama and Laksmana with Sita
get into the boat. On reaching the middle of the
stream, Sita offers prayers to Mother Ganga
(the deity presiding over the stream) and
after crossing the river all the three halt

for the night at the foot of a tree
content: Sri Rama, at daybreak, addressed Lakshmana, noting the sunrise and the sounds of cuckoos and peacocks, and proposed they cross the Ganga river. Lakshmana informed Guha, who promptly ordered a boat.  A minister quickly brought a suitable vessel to the riverbank and informed Guha. Guha, with folded hands, offered the boat to Rama, inquiring if he could offer further assistance.  Rama accepted, requesting everything be loaded quickly.  Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita, armed and armored, then proceeded towards the river.  The charioteer, Sumantra, asked Rama for further instructions.  Rama, touching Sumantra's hand, instructed him to return to Ayodhya and remain composed, explaining that they would continue their journey into the Dandaka forest on foot. Sumantra, distressed at being dismissed, made a final plea to Rama.

summary:
Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,
procured by Guha, Sri Rama and Laksmana with Sita
get into the boat. On reaching the middle of the
stream, Sita offers prayers to Mother Ganga
(the deity presiding over the stream) and
after crossing the river all the three halt

for the night at the foot of a tree
content: Sumantra, the charioteer, overcome with grief, questioned Rama about his own future now that Rama was leaving.  Rama instructed Sumantra to return swiftly to Ayodhya and remain composed. He reiterated his intent to journey to Dandaka forest on foot.  Sumantra, distraught, lamented Rama's exile, comparing it to the life of a common man. He questioned the value of piety and learning if even Rama could face such adversity.  He declared that despite the exile, Rama, accompanied by Sita and Lakshmana, would ultimately achieve glory. Sumantra then expressed his own despair at being left behind, fearing the influence of Kaikeyi.  Overwhelmed, Sumantra wept. Once composed, Rama consoled Sumantra, urging him to comfort Dasaratha and prevent him from grieving. He emphasized Sumantra’s importance as a friend to the Ikshvaku dynasty and instructed him to obey Dasaratha's commands, particularly those related to Kaikeyi, explaining that a king's purpose is to ensure his will is carried out.  Rama's final instruction was for Sumantra to respectfully convey a message to Dasaratha upon his return.

summary:
Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,
procured by Guha, Sri Rama and Laksmana with Sita
get into the boat. On reaching the middle of the
stream, Sita offers prayers to Mother Ganga
(the deity presiding over the stream) and
after crossing the river all the three halt

for the night at the foot of a tree
content: Rama continued to instruct Sumantra, emphasizing the importance of obeying Dasaratha, particularly in fulfilling Kaikeyi’s wishes. He explained that kings rule to ensure their commands are followed.  Rama urged Sumantra to prevent Dasaratha from succumbing to grief.  He then dictated a message for Sumantra to convey: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita were not sorrowful about their exile.  They would return after fourteen years. Sumantra was to offer greetings to Kausalya and the other queens on their behalf. Rama also instructed Sumantra to tell Dasaratha to quickly recall Bharata and install him as the heir. He reassured Sumantra that once Bharata was installed, his grief would lessen.  Furthermore, Rama advised that Bharata should be told to treat all his mothers with respect, especially Kausalya. By accepting the role offered by their father, Bharata would find happiness and prosperity. Sumantra, moved by Rama’s words, responded that his boldness in speaking was motivated by affection and loyalty.  He expressed his anguish at returning to Ayodhya without Rama.

summary:
Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,
procured by Guha, Sri Rama and Laksmana with Sita

content: Sumantra, deeply saddened by Rama's exile, expressed his unwavering devotion and questioned how he could return to Ayodhya without him. He predicted the city's despair at seeing the empty chariot, comparing it to an army seeing its leader's chariot without him.  He worried about the people grieving Rama's absence. Sumantra recalled the city's distress at Rama's departure and foresaw an even greater lament upon his return alone. He wrestled with the dilemma of telling Kausalya a comforting lie versus the painful truth. Sumantra declared his inability to return without Rama, his horses refusing to carry anyone else, and requested permission to join Rama's exile. He threatened self-immolation if abandoned and vowed to protect Rama during his austerities using the chariot. He expressed his hope to find solace in serving Rama in the forest.

summary:
Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,
procured by Guha, Sri Rama and Laksmana with Sita

stream, Sita offers prayers to Mother Ganga
(the deity presiding over the stream) and
after crossing the river all the three halt

for the night at the foot of a tree
content: Sumantra, overwhelmed with grief, pleaded with Rama to allow him to join his exile. He declared his inability to return to Ayodhya without him, likening the city to being inaccessible as heaven to a sinner.  Sumantra vowed to serve Rama, protect him during his austerities, and even offered to use the chariot for defense against wild creatures. He expressed hope that serving Rama in the forest would bring him solace, and reminded Rama of his fondness for his servants. Sumantra further argued that the fourteen years of exile would feel like moments in Rama's presence, but an eternity apart.  Rama, compassionate towards his devoted servant, explained his decision to send him back.  He reasoned that Sumantra's return would assure Kaikeyi of his departure, preventing her from suspecting King Dasaratha of deceit and ensuring she received the kingdom protected by Bharata.

summary:
Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,

after crossing the river all the three halt
