content: Rama, expressing his deep affection for Sumantra, explained his reasons for sending him back to Ayodhya.  He wanted Kaikeyi to believe he had truly left for the forest, preventing her from wrongly accusing King Dasaratha of deception. Rama's priority was to ensure Kaikeyi received the kingdom, safeguarding it under Bharata's rule.  He then instructed Sumantra to return to Ayodhya and relay his messages as directed.  Turning to Guha, Rama explained his need to reside in a more secluded hermitage, fitting for his exile. He requested the sap of a banyan tree, which he used to mat his and Lakshmana's hair, adopting the ascetic appearance.  Rama then advised Guha to remain vigilant in protecting his kingdom, emphasizing the challenges of governance.  Finally, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana departed, reaching the riverbank where they found a boat. Rama directed Lakshmana to secure the boat and help Sita aboard.

summary:
Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,
procured by Guha, Sri Rama and Laksmana with Sita
get into the boat. On reaching the middle of the
stream, Sita offers prayers to Mother Ganga
(the deity presiding over the stream) and
after crossing the river all the three halt

for the night at the foot of a tree
content: Upon reaching the riverbank, Rama advised Guha on the complexities of governance, stressing the importance of vigilance concerning the army, treasury, fortifications, and citizens.  After this counsel, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana took their leave of Guha and proceeded towards the river.  Spotting a boat, Rama instructed Lakshmana to steady it and assist Sita in boarding, followed by himself. Lakshmana dutifully followed his brother's instructions, helping Sita into the boat before boarding himself.  Rama then embarked, after which Guha directed his kinsmen to row. Once aboard, Rama offered prayers. He and Sita respectfully sipped water from the Ganga and bowed to the river, a gesture Lakshmana also repeated. After bidding farewell to Guha and Sumantra, Rama signaled the boatmen to begin their journey.  Propelled by the oarsmen and guided by the helmsman, the boat swiftly traversed the river.  Upon reaching the middle of the Ganga, Sita offered a prayer, asking the river goddess to protect Rama as he fulfilled his exile.  She vowed to return and offer further worship upon their safe return after fourteen years.

summary:
Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,
procured by Guha, Sri Rama and Laksmana with Sita
get into the boat. On reaching the middle of the
stream, Sita offers prayers to Mother Ganga
(the deity presiding over the stream) and
after crossing the river all the three halt

for the night at the foot of a tree
content: Sita prayed to Ganga, promising to return and offer grand worship after their fourteen-year exile. She extolled the river's divine nature, its presence in all realms, and its connection to Brahma and the Ocean-king.  She vowed to offer cows, clothing, food, and other precious items to Brahmans upon Rama's safe return to Ayodhya. She further promised to worship all deities and sacred sites along the riverbanks. Sita concluded her prayer with a plea for Rama, Lakshmana, and herself to safely return from exile. Upon reaching the southern bank, Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita disembarked and continued their journey. Rama instructed Lakshmana to lead the way, with Sita following behind him, while Rama himself would protect them both from the rear. He emphasized the importance of their mutual protection, especially considering the hardships Sita would face entering the untamed forest.

summary:
Canto LII

While about to step into the boat brought by Guha’s men and asked by Guha
if he could be of any further use to the prince, Sri Rama enjoins him to
obey the Emperor. Importuned by Sumantra to take him as a personal
attendant to the forest, the prince declines his loving offer and,
expostulating with him, sends him back to Ayodhya. Entangling
their locks into a thick mass with the milk of a banyan tree,
procured by Guha, Sri Rama and Laksmana with Sita
get into the boat. On reaching the middle of the
stream, Sita offers prayers to Mother Ganga
(the deity presiding over the stream) and
after crossing the river all the three halt

for the night at the foot of a tree
content: Rama instructed Lakshmana to lead the way, followed by Sita, while he would protect them from behind. He acknowledged the difficulties ahead, particularly for Sita, who was accustomed to palace life and would now experience the hardships of the forest.  She was entering a wild, untamed area, devoid of human activity and comforts.  Lakshmana, Sita, and Rama proceeded in this formation.  Meanwhile, Sumantra, still watching from the northern bank, wept as they disappeared into the distance.  Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita then journeyed through the fertile Vatsa region, between the Ganga and Yamuna rivers.  The brothers hunted four large deer – a Varaha, Rishya, Prishata, and Maharuru – and gathered edible fruits before seeking a suitable tree for their night's rest.

summary:
Canto LIII

Apprehending trouble for Kausalya and others at the hands of Kaikeyi, Sri
Rama, who was seated at the foot of a banyan tree and was a past
master in the art of persuasion, persuades Laksmana to the best
of his ability to return to Ayodhya. Laksmana, however, pleads
inability to survive in his absence and does not budge an
inch from his resolution to stay with his eldest brother.

Sri Rama, therefore, yields and sets his seal to

his continuing with him during his exile
content: Rama, having arrived at the foot of a tree and performed evening prayers, addressed Lakshmana.  He observed that this was their first night outside their homeland, away from Sumantra, urging Lakshmana not to grieve.  He emphasized the need for vigilance in the coming nights for Sita's safety and well-being.  Rama suggested they make the best of the situation, finding materials to create makeshift beds.  He lamented the king's likely discomfort and contrasted it with Kaikeyi's presumed satisfaction at achieving her aims.  Rama worried about his father's vulnerability to Kaikeyi's influence, especially given his age and isolation. He reflected on the power of desire, questioning why anyone would abandon a loyal son for a woman, as his father had seemingly done.  He mused on Bharata's fortunate position, poised to rule Kosala unopposed.  Rama concluded that Kaikeyi's motives were threefold: to cause Dasaratha's demise, orchestrate Rama's exile, and secure the throne for Bharata. He expressed concern that Kaikeyi's arrogance might lead her to persecute Kausalya and Sumitra.

summary:
Canto LIII

Apprehending trouble for Kausalya and others at the hands of Kaikeyi, Sri
Rama, who was seated at the foot of a banyan tree and was a past
master in the art of persuasion, persuades Laksmana to the best
of his ability to return to Ayodhya. Laksmana, however, pleads
inability to survive in his absence and does not budge an
inch from his resolution to stay with his eldest brother.

Sri Rama, therefore, yields and sets his seal to

his continuing with him during his exile
content: Rama, lamenting his exile, considers the implications for his family. He believes Bharata will enjoy the full benefits of the kingdom now that Dasaratha is old and Rama is in the forest.  He criticizes Dasaratha for prioritizing pleasure over virtue and wealth, predicting his downfall.  Rama suspects Kaikeyi orchestrated the entire situation: Dasaratha's decline, Rama's exile, and Bharata's ascension.  He fears Kaikeyi, empowered by her success, might now target Kausalya and Sumitra out of spite.  Concerned for his mother Sumitra's well-being, Rama urges Lakshmana to return to Ayodhya and protect her, as Kausalya will be vulnerable after Dasaratha's passing. Rama intends to go to the Dandaka forest with Sita alone.  He even entertains the dark thought that Kaikeyi might harm his mothers.  Rama blames himself for his mother's suffering, feeling he has failed her despite her years of devoted care. He laments being a source of sorrow to his mother, comparing himself unfavorably to her pet myna bird, which displays fierce loyalty.  He feels useless in the face of his mother's grief, describing her as heartbroken and desolate. While Rama claims he could conquer Ayodhya and the entire world with his prowess, he refrains from violence out of fear of sin and its consequences in the afterlife.  He chooses to honor his father's commitment to Kaikeyi's boons instead.  Overwhelmed by these thoughts, Rama weeps through the night in the desolate forest.

summary:
Canto LIII

Apprehending trouble for Kausalya and others at the hands of Kaikeyi, Sri
Rama, who was seated at the foot of a banyan tree and was a past
master in the art of persuasion, persuades Laksmana to the best
of his ability to return to Ayodhya. Laksmana, however, pleads
inability to survive in his absence and does not budge an
inch from his resolution to stay with his eldest brother.

Sri Rama, therefore, yields and sets his seal to

his continuing with him during his exile
content: Rama, deeply saddened by his exile, laments his mother Kausalya's grief, picturing her overwhelmed with sorrow.  He asserts his capability to conquer Ayodhya and the world but chooses to refrain from violence due to fear of sin and the repercussions in the afterlife.  Instead, he accepts his exile to honor his father's promise.  Rama sits through the night, weeping in the lonely forest.  Lakshmana consoles the grieving Rama, comparing Ayodhya without him to a moonless night.  Lakshmana declares his inability to live without Rama, expressing his unwillingness to return to Ayodhya even to see his own family.  Finding a prepared bed under a banyan tree, Rama and Sita rest. Moved by Lakshmana's unwavering loyalty and devotion, Rama agrees for Lakshmana to accompany him and Sita on their exile.  From that point on, the two brothers, strong and fearless, resided in the forest without any fear, like lions on a mountain peak.

summary:
Canto LIV

Set out on his journey for the Dandaka forest with Sita and Laksmana, Sri
Rama reaches at dusk the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja in the vicinity
of the confluence of the holy Ganga and Yamuna rivers. Paying
due honours to Sri Rama and his party, the sage recommends
Citrakita as the fittest place for him to sojourn in.

Spending the night in discourses on various
topics with him, the sage grants him
leave early next morning to
depart for Citrakita
content: Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, after spending a night under a large tree, continued their journey towards the confluence of the Ganga and Yamuna rivers at dawn.  They traveled through a vast and beautiful forest, observing various landscapes. As the day ended, Rama pointed out the fragrant smoke rising near Prayaga, indicating the presence of Sage Bharadwaja's hermitage. The sound of the converging rivers confirmed their proximity.  Signs of human activity, such as chopped wood and cut trees, were visible within the hermitage.  The two brothers approached the sage's dwelling as the sun set.  Upon reaching the hermitage, Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita respectfully waited at a distance.  They then entered the sage’s hut and greeted the revered Bharadwaja, who was surrounded by his disciples. Rama introduced himself, Sita, and Lakshmana, explaining they were exiled to the forest and Lakshmana chose to accompany them.

summary:
Canto LIV

Set out on his journey for the Dandaka forest with Sita and Laksmana, Sri
Rama reaches at dusk the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja in the vicinity
of the confluence of the holy Ganga and Yamuna rivers. Paying
due honours to Sri Rama and his party, the sage recommends
Citrakita as the fittest place for him to sojourn in.

Spending the night in discourses on various
topics with him, the sage grants him
leave early next morning to
depart for Citrakita
content: Upon receiving permission, Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana entered Sage Bharadwaja's hut.  They found the wise sage, surrounded by his disciples, having just completed his fire offering.  Rama introduced himself and his family, explaining their exile and Lakshmana's voluntary accompaniment. He informed the sage of their intention to live a virtuous life in the forest, sustaining themselves on roots and fruits.  Bharadwaja welcomed them warmly, offering them refreshments and arranging for their comfort.  After they were settled, the sage addressed Rama, acknowledging his awareness of Rama’s unjust exile. He suggested that they stay at the hermitage, located in a peaceful and beautiful setting at the confluence of two great rivers. However, Rama respectfully declined, expressing concern that their presence so close to Ayodhya might attract unwanted visitors, disturbing the peace of the hermitage.  He requested the sage to suggest a more secluded location where Sita could live comfortably.

summary:
Canto LIV

Set out on his journey for the Dandaka forest with Sita and Laksmana, Sri
Rama reaches at dusk the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja in the vicinity
of the confluence of the holy Ganga and Yamuna rivers. Paying
due honours to Sri Rama and his party, the sage recommends
Citrakita as the fittest place for him to sojourn in.

Spending the night in discourses on various
topics with him, the sage grants him
leave early next morning to
depart for Citrakita
content: Rama, concerned about the proximity of the hermitage to Ayodhya, explained to Sage Bharadwaja that his presence might attract unwanted attention from citizens and disrupt the tranquility of the ashram.  He expressed his desire for a more secluded dwelling where Sita could reside in peace and comfort.  In response, Bharadwaja suggested Citrakūṭa, a sacred mountain sixty miles away, inhabited by sages and renowned for its beauty.  He described it as a place frequented by monkeys and bears, resembling the Gandhamādana mountain.  Bharadwaja extolled the virtues of Citrakūṭa, claiming that merely gazing upon its peaks inspired virtuous thoughts and discouraged sin. He recounted how many ascetics had attained enlightenment there after years of dedicated austerities.  As an alternative, Bharadwaja offered Rama the option of remaining at his hermitage for the duration of their exile.  The sage then extended his hospitality to Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, providing them with all they needed. They spent the night engaged in insightful conversations.  The following morning, after a restful night, Rama requested permission from Bharadwaja to depart for their chosen destination. The sage graciously granted his leave, advising them to journey to Citrakūṭa, a place abundant in honey, roots, and fruits.

summary:
Canto LIV

Set out on his journey for the Dandaka forest with Sita and Laksmana, Sri
Rama reaches at dusk the hermitage of Sage Bharadwaja in the vicinity
of the confluence of the holy Ganga and Yamuna rivers. Paying
due honours to Sri Rama and his party, the sage recommends
Citrakita as the fittest place for him to sojourn in.

Spending the night in discourses on various
topics with him, the sage grants him
leave early next morning to
depart for Citrakita
content: Rama, having spent the night at Bharadwaja's hermitage, respectfully requested the sage's permission to depart for their designated place of exile. Bharadwaja granted his leave, directing them towards Citrakūṭa, a mountain abundant in fruits, roots, and honey.  He described Citrakūṭa as an ideal dwelling place, a sacred and beautiful mountain teeming with diverse trees and inhabited by Kinnaras and Nagas. The calls of peacocks and the presence of elephants added to its charm.  Bharadwaja assured Rama that the mountain's natural beauty, including rivers, waterfalls, mountain peaks, caves, and streams, would bring him and Sita joy during their wanderings in the surrounding forests, which were also home to herds of deer and elephants.  He encouraged them to settle on Citrakūṭa, where the pleasant songs of birds would further enhance their experience.

summary:
Canto LV

Sage Bharadwaja tells Sri Rama and Laksmana, even as they set out
on their journey to CitrakUta, the route by which they should
proceed. Accompanied by Sita, Sri Rama and Laksmana
cross the Yamuna on a raft prepared by themselves.

In the evening they halt on the bank of the
Yamuna alongwith Sita, who is rejoiced
to get fruits and blossoms of her liking
content: After spending the night at Sage Bharadwaja's hermitage, Rama and Lakshmana bid farewell to the sage and prepared to journey towards Citrakūṭa.  The sage blessed them for a safe journey, offering guidance on their route. He advised them to follow the Yamuna River westward, then find a frequented crossing point where they could build a raft.  He further instructed them to locate a large banyan tree where Sita could offer prayers for their safe return to Ayodhya.  Continuing, they should travel two miles to Nilavana, a pleasant path free of forest fires, which would lead them to Citrakūṭa.  Rama expressed gratitude for the sage's kindness, acknowledging their past good deeds.  Following the sage's directions, Rama and Lakshmana, with Sita leading the way, proceeded towards the Yamuna. Upon reaching the swiftly flowing river, they contemplated how to cross. Resourcefully, the brothers constructed a large raft using wooden logs, dry bamboos, and fragrant Usira roots. Lakshmana thoughtfully fashioned a comfortable seat for Sita using cane shoots and rose-apple twigs.

summary:
Canto LV

Sage Bharadwaja tells Sri Rama and Laksmana, even as they set out
on their journey to CitrakUta, the route by which they should
proceed. Accompanied by Sita, Sri Rama and Laksmana
cross the Yamuna on a raft prepared by themselves.

In the evening they halt on the bank of the
Yamuna alongwith Sita, who is rejoiced
to get fruits and blossoms of her liking
content: Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita reached the Yamuna River.  Concerned about the swift current, they built a sturdy raft from logs, bamboo, and fragrant roots. Lakshmana fashioned a comfortable seat for Sita on the raft.  Rama carefully helped Sita, along with her belongings, onto the raft.  The brothers then began to row across the river.  Midstream, Sita prayed to the river deity for a safe crossing and for the successful completion of Rama's exile.  She vowed to offer a grand sacrifice upon their return to Ayodhya.  Reaching the southern bank, they disembarked and left the raft. They continued to a nearby banyan tree, where Sita prayed for the fulfillment of Rama's vow and their safe return to see their mothers, Kausalya and Sumitra.  Observing Sita's prayer, Rama instructed Lakshmana to escort Sita ahead while he followed, armed, behind them.

summary:
Canto LV

Sage Bharadwaja tells Sri Rama and Laksmana, even as they set out
on their journey to CitrakUta, the route by which they should
proceed. Accompanied by Sita, Sri Rama and Laksmana
cross the Yamuna on a raft prepared by themselves.

In the evening they halt on the bank of the
Yamuna alongwith Sita, who is rejoiced
to get fruits and blossoms of her liking
content: Upon reaching the Yamuna's bank, Sita circled a banyan tree, praying for the successful completion of Rama's exile and their safe return to Ayodhya to see their mothers, Kausalya and Sumitra.  Observing Sita's devotion, Rama instructed Lakshmana to escort her while he followed, armed, close behind.  Rama further instructed Lakshmana to provide Sita with any fruits or flowers she desired.  Sita, captivated by the unfamiliar flora, inquired about the various trees, shrubs, and blossoming creepers.  Lakshmana, eager to fulfill her requests, gathered an array of beautiful flowering branches. Sita also found great joy in observing the river, its sands and waters vibrant, its air filled with the calls of swans and cranes.  Journeying a short distance further, Rama and Lakshmana hunted permissible game in the forest along the Yamuna's banks.  They found a suitable resting place amidst a lively forest teeming with peacocks, monkeys, and elephants, near the river's edge.

summary:
Canto LVI

Moving further next morning and rejoicing on the way to see the loveliness
of the forest, the party reaches CitrakUta and enters the hermitage of
Valmiki. Making up his mind to sojourn there with the permission
of the sage, Sri Rama gets Laksmana to erect a hut of
leaves for themselves and, worshipping the deities
presiding over the structure, they solemnly enter

their abode at a propitious hour
content: As the night passed, Rama awoke Laksmana, pointing out the sweet songs of the forest birds and urging them to continue their journey to Citrakuta.  Laksmana quickly roused himself, and after bathing in the Yamuna, they proceeded on their path.  Along the way, Rama pointed out to Sita the beauty of the flowering trees, the abundance of fruit, and the plentiful honeycombs.  He noted the calls of the birds and the impressive peaks of Citrakuta mountain, suggesting they could live there comfortably.  Upon reaching the mountain, Rama remarked to Laksmana on its beauty, the plentiful food and water, and its suitability as a dwelling place, especially given the presence of other ascetics. He proposed they make their home there.

summary:
Canto LVI

Moving further next morning and rejoicing on the way to see the loveliness
of the forest, the party reaches CitrakUta and enters the hermitage of
Valmiki. Making up his mind to sojourn there with the permission
of the sage, Sri Rama gets Laksmana to erect a hut of
leaves for themselves and, worshipping the deities
presiding over the structure, they solemnly enter

their abode at a propitious hour
content: Upon reaching the desirable Citrakuta mountain, Rama, Sita, and Laksmana found it a beautiful and resourceful place, teeming with birds, fruits, roots, and fresh water.  Rama declared it a suitable dwelling place, particularly noting the presence of other ascetics.  They then entered the hermitage of the sage Valmiki, who welcomed them warmly.  After proper introductions, Rama instructed Laksmana to build a hut of strong wood. Laksmana promptly obeyed, constructing a well-made and attractive dwelling.  Rama then directed Laksmana to retrieve a Gajakanda bulb to perform a ritual for consecrating the new hut, emphasizing the importance of observing scriptural procedures.  Laksmana retrieved the bulb and, following Rama's further instructions, roasted it in the fire as an offering.

summary:
Canto LVI

Moving further next morning and rejoicing on the way to see the loveliness
of the forest, the party reaches CitrakUta and enters the hermitage of
Valmiki. Making up his mind to sojourn there with the permission
of the sage, Sri Rama gets Laksmana to erect a hut of
leaves for themselves and, worshipping the deities
presiding over the structure, they solemnly enter

their abode at a propitious hour
content: Having received Rama's instructions, Laksmana prepared the Gajakanda bulb.  Rama then instructed him to roast the bulb, emphasizing the auspiciousness of the time. Laksmana then placed the bulb into the fire.  Once roasted, Laksmana informed Rama that the offering was ready. Rama, well-versed in ritual practices, performed the necessary rites to consecrate the hut. He then entered the dwelling, finding contentment in its completion. Following this, Rama performed further ceremonies, including offerings to various deities and the consecration of altars within the hermitage.  Finally, the three exiles – Rama, Sita, and Laksmana – entered their new home, a charming leaf-thatched hut, finding respite from their earlier hardships.  Their arrival at Citrakuta, with its abundant resources and natural beauty, brought Rama relief from the pain of exile.

summary:
Canto LVII

Having been told of Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakuta by the spies of Guha
and taking leave of the latter, Sumantra drives back to Ayodhya. Entering
the royal gynaeceum, he submits to the Emperor what he reported
earlier to the citizens who followed his chariot. Dasaratha
and Kausalya fall into a swoon to hear about
Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakita and
all the inmates of the gynaeceum
burst into a wail from agony
content: Sumantra, grieving after Rama's departure, left Guha and returned to Ayodhya.  His journey back, passing woodlands, rivers, and towns, was swift.  Arriving in Ayodhya at dusk, he found the city eerily quiet.  Concerned, he worried that the city was suffering from Rama’s absence. As he entered the city, crowds of people rushed to him, desperate for news of Rama. Sumantra sadly informed them of Rama's departure across the Ganga.  The news was met with grief and despair. People lamented their loss, expressing how much they valued Rama’s presence in their city, recalling his role in their celebrations and his fatherly care. Sumantra also heard the sorrowful cries of women from the windows of their homes as he passed through the city streets.

summary:
Canto LVII

Having been told of Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakuta by the spies of Guha
and taking leave of the latter, Sumantra drives back to Ayodhya. Entering
the royal gynaeceum, he submits to the Emperor what he reported
earlier to the citizens who followed his chariot. Dasaratha
and Kausalya fall into a swoon to hear about
Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakita and
all the inmates of the gynaeceum
burst into a wail from agony
content: As Sumantra journeyed through Ayodhya, he heard the city's residents lamenting Rama's absence. They mourned their beloved prince, expressing their despair at not seeing him in their daily life, at festivals, and ceremonies.  He heard women grieving from their windows, heartbroken by Rama’s exile.  Sumantra, his face veiled in sorrow, proceeded towards the palace.  He passed through the palace gates, witnessing the shared grief of the palace staff.  The women of the palace, having learned of Sumantra's return without Rama, cried out in anguish.  Their eyes, filled with tears, reflected their deep sorrow.  Sumantra overheard the queen's anxieties about how he would convey the news to Kausalya, Rama's mother. They marveled at Kausalya's strength in enduring her son's departure. Overwhelmed by the collective grief, Sumantra entered the palace.  He found Dasaratha, weak and distressed, consumed by sorrow for his son.  Sumantra relayed Rama's message, upon which the king fainted from grief.  The news of Dasaratha’s collapse spread through the palace, intensifying the existing sorrow, and the women wailed in despair.

summary:
Canto LVII

Having been told of Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakuta by the spies of Guha
and taking leave of the latter, Sumantra drives back to Ayodhya. Entering
the royal gynaeceum, he submits to the Emperor what he reported
earlier to the citizens who followed his chariot. Dasaratha
and Kausalya fall into a swoon to hear about
Sri Rama’s departure for Citrakita and
all the inmates of the gynaeceum
burst into a wail from agony
content: Upon reaching the eighth gateway, Sumantra found King Dasaratha within the white chamber, grief-stricken and weakened by his son's exile.  Sumantra approached the seated king, offered his respects, and faithfully relayed Rama's message.  The king listened in silence, his mind in turmoil.  Overwhelmed by sorrow, he collapsed unconscious to the floor.  The news of the king's collapse quickly reached the women's quarters, causing great distress. As the king fell, the women cried out, raising their arms in anguish.  Queen Kausalya, aided by Sumitra, lifted her fallen husband. She questioned why he hadn't responded to Sumantra, who had completed the difficult task of accompanying Rama. Kausalya implored Dasaratha to rise, reminding him of his virtue and urging him not to succumb to despair. She reassured him that Kaikeyi, whose influence had led to this situation, was not present, so he could speak freely.  Overcome by her own grief, Kausalya then collapsed, weeping. Seeing Kausalya and the king in such states, the other women surrounding them began to wail. This collective cry of sorrow spread throughout the palace, drawing the attention of men and women, young and old, from all corners of Ayodhya, plunging the city once again into a state of turmoil.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Bewailing the lot of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana, who did not in anyway
deserve the hardships they were undergoing in the forest, the Emperor

urges Sumantra to deliver their parting message and the charioteer
proceeds to tell him what they said
content: King Dasaratha, regaining consciousness after swooning, summoned Sumantra the charioteer. Sumantra, distressed and grieving for Rama, approached the king, who was overwhelmed with sorrow. Dasaratha lamented Rama's hardships, questioning how his son, accustomed to comfort, could endure living under a tree in the forest. He wondered how Rama, once followed by an entourage, could bear the isolation, and how he, Lakshmana, and Sita could reside in a place inhabited by wild animals.  The king questioned how the princes and Sita, accustomed to royal comforts, could travel on foot. He praised Sumantra for witnessing Rama's departure and urged him to recount every detail of Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita's words and actions in the forest, clinging to these details for solace.  Sumantra, deeply moved, began his narration, describing how Rama respectfully instructed him to convey his obeisance to his father.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Bewailing the lot of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana, who did not in anyway
deserve the hardships they were undergoing in the forest, the Emperor

urges Sumantra to deliver their parting message and the charioteer
proceeds to tell him what they said
content: King Dasaratha, desperate for news of his exiled sons, implored Sumantra to recount every detail of their time in the forest.  Sumantra, heartbroken and struggling to speak through his tears, began to relay Rama's instructions. Rama, ever dutiful, requested that Sumantra convey his respects to his father, Dasaratha.  He also asked that his greetings be conveyed to the entire royal household, with special attention to his mother Kausalya's well-being.  He urged Kausalya to remain steadfast in her devotion and to treat Kaikeyi with the utmost respect.  Addressing Bharata, Rama emphasized the importance of filial duty, advising him to serve their father respectfully, regardless of the king's advanced age, and to treat Kausalya as his own mother.  Overcome with emotion, Rama wept as he delivered these messages. Lakshmana, filled with anger, questioned the injustice of Rama's exile, blaming Kaikeyi's influence and the king's hasty decision for their suffering.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Bewailing the lot of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana, who did not in anyway
deserve the hardships they were undergoing in the forest, the Emperor

urges Sumantra to deliver their parting message and the charioteer
proceeds to tell him what they said
content: Rama, weeping, reiterated his request to Bharata to consider Kausalya as his own mother.  Lakshmana, enraged, questioned the justification for Rama's exile. He criticized the king's hasty decision, influenced by Kaikeyi, declaring their suffering undeserved.  He questioned whether greed or a granted boon motivated the exile, asserting that regardless, it was a grave misstep.  Lakshmana professed his deep devotion to Rama, considering him a brother, master, kinsman, and even a father figure. He lamented the king's decision to exile Rama, the beloved of all, questioning how the populace could be pleased.  Meanwhile, Sita stood silently, overcome with grief, as if possessed by a spirit. She wept, unable to speak, her gaze fixed on Rama. As Sumantra prepared to leave, Sita, her face pale, burst into tears, her eyes fixed on her departing husband. Rama, with tear-streaked face and joined palms, stood supported by Lakshmana, as Sita continued to weep, watching the royal chariot depart.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Bewailing the lot of Sri Rama, Sita and Laksmana, who did not in anyway
deserve the hardships they were undergoing in the forest, the Emperor

urges Sumantra to deliver their parting message and the charioteer
proceeds to tell him what they said
content: Sita, her face pale and drawn, watched her husband as Sumantra prepared to leave for Ayodhya.  Overcome with grief, she suddenly burst into tears.  Simultaneously, Rama, his face streaked with tears and his palms pressed together in a gesture of respect, stood supported by Lakshmana. As the royal chariot began its departure, Sita, deeply sorrowful, continued to weep, her gaze fixed on Rama and the departing chariot.

summary: No summary available
content: Sumantra informs King Dasaratha of the sorrowful departure of Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita.  He describes his own grief and the reluctance of his horses to leave Rama.  Sumantra waited at Guha's residence for days, hoping Rama might summon him back. He then describes the widespread despair in the kingdom, marked by withered trees, dried-up rivers and ponds, and the stillness of the forest creatures.  The flowers lack fragrance, the fruits are less vibrant, and the gardens in Ayodhya are desolate.  His return to the city was met with silence and sighs, as the people mourned Rama's absence. Women wept from their homes upon seeing the chariot return without Rama, their faces streaked with tears.  Sumantra concludes by observing that everyone, regardless of their relationship to Rama, is equally consumed by grief.

summary: No summary available
content: Sumantra returns to Ayodhya and finds the city plunged into grief over Rama's exile.  Women weep openly, lamenting their inability to see Rama. Their tears streak their faces, blurring their carefully applied kohl.  Sumantra observes that everyone, regardless of their relationship with Rama – friend, foe, or neutral – is equally distressed. The city itself seems to mirror the people's sorrow; the animals are listless, the sounds of the city are replaced by sighs and cries of despair, and a sense of joylessness pervades everything.  He reports this desolate scene to Dasaratha, who responds with anguish.  Dasaratha blames himself for rashly agreeing to Rama's exile, manipulated by Kaikeyi and without consulting his advisors.  He wonders if this tragedy is divine punishment for some unknown sin.  He begs Sumantra to take him to Rama, his senses yearning for one last glimpse of his son. He desperately commands that someone bring Rama back, declaring he cannot live without him, even for an hour. He pleads with Sumantra to take him to Rama, no matter how far he has traveled. He longs to see his son, with his pearl-like teeth and mighty bow, alongside Sita.  Dasaratha declares that he will surely die if he cannot see Rama. Overwhelmed by grief and the pain of separation, the King compares his sorrow to a vast, turbulent ocean, fueled by Rama's and Sita's absence, with waves of tears and the cries of his people.  He laments his misfortune at not being able to see Rama in his current state of distress, and cries out to Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita, lamenting that they are unaware of his profound suffering.

summary: No summary available
content: King Dasaratha, overwhelmed by grief, cries out, longing to see his son Rama. He believes he will die if he cannot see Rama, who has pearl-like teeth and a mighty bow, along with Sita. He laments his inability to see Rama in his current state, adorned with gem earrings. Dasaratha compares his sorrow to a vast ocean, difficult to navigate.  The force of the ocean is Rama's grief, its other shore is Sita's absence. Tears form the turbulent waters, while his flailing arms mimic fish leaping within.  Loud wails represent the ocean's roar, and his disheveled hair resembles seaweed. Kaikeyi is likened to submarine fire, Manthara's words to alligators, the boons granted to the coastline, and the term of exile to the ocean's vastness. The king collapses onto his couch, lamenting his inability to see Rama and Lakshmana. Queen Kausalya, hearing his cries, is gripped by renewed fear.

summary:
Canto LX

Sumantra does not succeed in soothing the agony of Kausalya, who tossed
about on the floor due to excess of grief over her separation from
Sri Rama, even though he consoles her by telling her that
Sri Rama, being resolute and high-minded, was
living in the forest free from agony
content: Sumantra tried to console the grieving Queen Kausalya, who was distraught over Rama's exile. He told her that Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana were living peacefully in the forest.  He described how Lakshmana dutifully served Rama, and how Sita, though in a lonely forest, remained composed and found joy as if she were at home. Sita’s spirits were high, finding pleasure in the natural surroundings and inquiring about the various sights they encountered.  Sumantra added that Sita felt as if she were merely on a short trip from Ayodhya when Rama or Lakshmana were by her side. He briefly mentioned a comment Sita had made about Kaikeyi but quickly moved on to more comforting topics.

summary:
Canto LX

Sumantra does not succeed in soothing the agony of Kausalya, who tossed
about on the floor due to excess of grief over her separation from
Sri Rama, even though he consoles her by telling her that
Sri Rama, being resolute and high-minded, was
living in the forest free from agony
content: Sumantra continued his attempts to comfort Queen Kausalya by describing Sita's resilience and positive attitude in the forest.  He related how Sita, ever curious, inquired about the villages, cities, rivers, and trees they passed.  Her spirits remained high, and she felt as though she were on a mere outing from Ayodhya when Rama or Lakshmana were by her side. Sumantra briefly recalled a comment Sita had made about Kaikeyi, but quickly dismissed it as an unimportant aside. He emphasized Sita's enduring beauty, undiminished by the hardships of the journey.  Her face, he said, remained radiant, and her feet, though no longer adorned with alta, retained their reddish hue.  Sita continued to wear her jewelry, finding pleasure in it, and her graceful movements were reminiscent of the elegant gait of swans.  Despite the wild surroundings, she showed no fear of the forest's creatures, trusting in Rama's protection.  Sumantra concluded his description by reassuring Kausalya that Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana were not to be pitied, and that their story would become legendary.  He reminded her that they were fulfilling their father's promise and finding contentment in their simple forest life.  Despite Sumantra's comforting words, Queen Kausalya remained inconsolable, continuing to cry out for her beloved son, Rama.

summary:
Canto LXI

Apprehending danger to the king from his inability to bear the grief caused
by separation from Sri Rama, Kausalya, though foremost among
devoted wives, twits Dasaratha
content: Queen Kausalya, grieving the departure of Rama, rebukes King Dasaratha.  She questions how he could send Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, raised in luxury, to endure the hardships of the forest. Kausalya worries how the delicate Sita will cope with the harsh weather, simple food, and frightening sounds of the forest, contrasting them with her accustomed comforts.  She laments Rama's absence, wondering where he sleeps now, deprived of his palace bed. Kausalya expresses her deep sorrow, comparing her steadfast heart to adamant. She condemns Dasaratha's decision as cruel, forcing her loved ones into an impoverished state.  Kausalya doubts Bharata will relinquish the throne upon Rama's return, comparing the situation to Brahmins refusing leftover food after a ritual. She questions why Rama, the rightful heir, would accept a kingdom already ruled by his younger brother.

summary:
Canto LXI

Apprehending danger to the king from his inability to bear the grief caused
by separation from Sri Rama, Kausalya, though foremost among
devoted wives, twits Dasaratha
content: Kausalya continues her lament, using analogies to illustrate the impossibility of Rama accepting the kingdom after Bharata's rule.  She compares it to Brahmins refusing leftover food, even if offered by other Brahmins, and to a tiger refusing to eat prey killed by another.  She also likens it to the reuse of ritualistic items, which are considered unsuitable for a second ceremony.  Kausalya asserts that Rama would find the kingdom, having been ruled by Bharata, as unappealing as spoiled wine or leftover Soma. She emphasizes Rama's strength and unwillingness to tolerate such an insult, comparing him to a mighty tiger that wouldn't allow its tail to be twisted.  Kausalya declares that even if all the worlds opposed him, Rama wouldn't be intimidated, but his adherence to righteousness prevents him from seizing the throne by force.  She portrays Rama's exile as a devastating blow dealt by his own father, comparing it to a fish devouring its own offspring.  Kausalya then questions Dasaratha's sense of righteousness, given his treatment of Rama. She lists a woman's sources of support – husband, son, and family – and declares that Dasaratha, controlled by Kaikeyi, is no longer a support to her, and Rama has been banished.  She feels utterly destroyed.  Kausalya concludes by declaring that Dasaratha has ruined the kingdom, his counselors, his citizens, herself, and Rama, leaving only Bharata and Kaikeyi pleased. Overwhelmed by Kausalya's harsh words, Dasaratha faints, crying out for Rama, and remembers his ill-fated promise.

summary:
Canto LXI

Apprehending danger to the king from his inability to bear the grief caused
by separation from Sri Rama, Kausalya, though foremost among
devoted wives, twits Dasaratha
content: Queen Kausalya continues her condemnation of King Dasaratha. She states that a woman relies on her husband, son, and family for support, but she has none.  Her husband is controlled by Kaikeyi, and her son, Rama, has been exiled. Kausalya is left without support and expresses her devastation, refusing to follow Rama into the forest as she cannot bear separation from the king. She accuses Dasaratha of ruining the kingdom, its people, their counselors, herself, and Rama. The only ones happy, she asserts, are Bharata and Kaikeyi. Overwhelmed by her harsh words, Dasaratha faints, crying out for Rama and remembering his ill-fated promise.

summary:
Canto LXII

When reproached in harsh words by Kausalya, though already feeling
disconsolate through separation from his sons and daughter-in-law,
DaSgaratha falls into a swoon recalling his past sin in the form of
killing a hermit boy, which was responsible for his present
misfortune. On waking from his swoon he tries with folded
hands to conciliate Kausalya and on the latter reciprocating
his sentiments, the Emperor is lulled into a nap
content: Overwhelmed by Kausalya's harsh words, King Dasaratha, already heartbroken over Rama's exile, fainted. Upon regaining consciousness, he saw Kausalya beside him and was overcome with remorse, remembering a past sin: the accidental killing of a young hermit.  Consumed by both this old guilt and his current grief, he trembled and pleaded with Kausalya for forgiveness, acknowledging her compassionate nature and emphasizing a wife's duty to her husband. Kausalya, deeply moved by his distress, wept and begged *his* forgiveness for her harsh words, attributing her outburst to the overwhelming grief of losing her son.  She acknowledged the impropriety of her actions, emphasizing the high regard she held for her husband.

summary:
Canto LXII

When reproached in harsh words by Kausalya, though already feeling
disconsolate through separation from his sons and daughter-in-law,
DaSgaratha falls into a swoon recalling his past sin in the form of

content: Kausalya, prostrate at Dasaratha's feet, begged his forgiveness. She lamented that her grief-stricken outburst had caused her husband, a man deserving of respect, to plead with *her*.  Acknowledging her lapse in propriety, she confessed that her words were spoken out of turn, driven by the anguish of Rama's exile. Kausalya described grief as an insidious enemy that destroys reason and patience, making even small sorrows unbearable. For her, the mere five days of Rama's absence felt like five years.  Her sorrow, like a vast ocean fed by swift rivers, deepened with every thought of her son. As Kausalya spoke these conciliatory words, night fell, and the king, comforted by her remorse and exhausted by his own grief, drifted into sleep.

summary:
Canto LXIII

Waking from sleep after a while and recalling his sinful deed, which spelt
death to him, Dasaratha proceeds to tell Kausalya how, while he was Prince
Regent of Ayodhya, he went out a-hunting in the forest one day and
heard during the last watch of the night the sound of a hermit boy filling
his pitcher with water by submerging into Sarayu river. Mistaking the
gurgling sound for the trumpeting of an elephant, DaSaratha hit the
boy with an arrow, which dug deep into his body and mortally
wounded him. On approaching his quarry he discovered the
fatal blunder and tendered his heartfelt apology to the hermit
boy, who asked him to extract the arrow from his body and
report the matter to his aged parents. The boy died
immediately the arrow was taken out of his body
content: King Daśaratha, overwhelmed by grief after Rama and Lakshmana's exile, is consumed with anxiety.  The king, once mighty like Indra, is now shadowed by sorrow.  Remembering a past misdeed he believes responsible for his current suffering, Daśaratha decides to confess to Kausalya.  On the sixth night of Rama's exile, the king recalls his sin.  He acknowledges that one reaps what one sows, lamenting that a fool fails to consider the consequences of his actions.  Daśaratha uses analogies, comparing himself to someone who destroys a mango grove to cultivate less fruitful Palāśa trees, and to someone who tends to a Kimśuka tree, unaware of its undesirable outcome. He confesses to having made a grave error, driven by youthful folly. Daśaratha explains that, as a young prince skilled in archery, he earned the title of "sound-hitter," able to strike unseen targets by their sound alone. He now realizes this skill led to a terrible mistake, likening it to a child innocently consuming poison. Just as someone might be deceived by the Palāśa's beautiful flowers, Daśaratha was misled by his archery prowess. He sets the scene: Kausalya was not yet his wife, he was the prince regent, and the monsoon season, which fueled his desire to hunt, had arrived. The sun, having absorbed the earth's moisture and scorched the world, was now traversing the southern quarter, a region associated with spirits and fear.

summary:

immediately the arrow was taken out of his body
content: In the darkness, Daśaratha heard a sound like an elephant filling its trunk at the river.  Driven by his hunting instincts, he shot an arrow towards the sound, hoping to kill a large game animal. As dawn broke, a cry of pain pierced the air, followed by a human voice.  A forest-dweller, wounded and dying, questioned why he, a harmless ascetic, had been attacked. He lamented his fate, explaining he had only come to the secluded riverbank at night to collect water.  He wondered who shot him and what wrong he could possibly have committed to deserve such a fate.

content: Daśaratha recounts the tragic tale of the accidental killing. The dying ascetic cried out, questioning why he, a harmless, unarmed forest-dweller collecting water at night, was shot.  He lamented his fate and wondered who the archer was and what wrong he had done to deserve this. The ascetic further questioned how anyone could justify attacking a peaceful, hermit-like individual clad in bark and deerskin. He worried about the devastating impact of his death on his elderly, dependent parents.  He then described his failing condition and instructed Daśaratha to inform his father of the incident, providing directions to his hermitage. The ascetic reassured Daśaratha that his father’s anger, though intense, would not consume him if he confessed his guilt.  Despite being near death, the ascetic attempted to maintain composure and console Daśaratha by revealing he wasn't a Brahmin, thus lessening the karmic weight of the deed.

summary:
Canto LXIII

Waking from sleep after a while and recalling his sinful deed, which spelt
death to him, Dasaratha proceeds to tell Kausalya how, while he was Prince
Regent of Ayodhya, he went out a-hunting in the forest one day and
heard during the last watch of the night the sound of a hermit boy filling

immediately the arrow was taken out of his body
content: Dasaratha continues his tragic account.  The dying ascetic expressed concern for his elderly parents, who depended entirely on him. He wondered how they would survive without him. He lamented being killed by an unknown person while innocently collecting water, questioning what wrong he had committed to deserve such a fate. Daśaratha, deeply distressed by the young man's suffering, dropped his bow and arrow. Overwhelmed with grief and confusion, he rushed to the ascetic's side.  He found the young man lying by the Sarayu River, wounded by the arrow, his matted hair disheveled and his water pitcher emptied.  The ascetic, observing Daśaratha’s distress, questioned the king’s actions, emphasizing his own innocence and the devastating impact this would have on his blind, elderly parents who were waiting for him. The ascetic directed Daśaratha to inform his father of the tragedy, assuring him that his father’s anger, while justifiable, would not consume him if Daśaratha confessed. Then, noticing Daśaratha's hesitation to remove the arrow, fearing he would hasten the young ascetic’s death, the ascetic addressed the king’s concern, urging him to extract the arrow despite the inevitable outcome.  Daśaratha concluded this part of the narrative by describing the ascetic's labored breathing and eventual death on the riverbank.

Canto LXIII

Waking from sleep after a while and recalling his sinful deed, which spelt
death to him, Dasaratha proceeds to tell Kausalya how, while he was Prince
Regent of Ayodhya, he went out a-hunting in the forest one day and

immediately the arrow was taken out of his body
content: Dasaratha, filled with remorse, recounts how he approached the dying ascetic youth, begging his forgiveness. The young man, suffering intensely, requested Dasaratha to remove the arrow, comparing its agonizing effect to a river eroding a sandbank.  Dasaratha hesitated, realizing that removing the arrow would likely hasten the youth's death.  The ascetic, sensing Dasaratha's inner turmoil, reassured him, urging him to remove the arrow despite the inevitable outcome.  Composing himself with remarkable fortitude despite his failing strength, the ascetic comforted Dasaratha further, revealing he was not a Brahmin but the son of a Vaisya and a Sudra woman, thereby lessening the gravity of Dasaratha's offense. As the young man’s condition worsened, Dasaratha finally removed the arrow. The ascetic, his gaze fixed on Dasaratha, then passed away, leaving the king heartbroken and filled with regret as he watched the lifeless body on the riverbank.

summary:
Canto LXIV

DaSaratha continues to tell Kausalya how, having sought the presence of
the aged parents of the deceased, he apprised them of the sad incident
and escorted them to the river bank, where their son lay dead; how,
clasping the boy to their bosom, they both piteously wailed and
offered libations of water to his spirit for its benefit; how, invested
with an ethereal body, the spirit ascended to heaven while
consoling the aged couple and finally how, having cursed
the king that he too would meet his death in his agony of
separation from his son, the ascetic couple gave up
the ghost and attained the Supreme. Having thus
related the story of his imprecation and loudly
lamenting, the Emperor breathes his last with
the thought of Sri Rama foremost in his mind
content: Dasaratha, stricken with grief, recounted to Kausalya how he mistakenly killed the hermit boy.  He described his journey to the boy's hermitage, finding the boy's elderly, blind parents waiting expectantly for their son's return. Dasaratha's heart was heavy with dread as he approached them.  He explained how the old hermit, unaware of his son's death, called out for him. The hermit urged his son to return quickly, reminding him of his anxious mother and reassuring him that any perceived offense would be forgiven. The hermit's words, emphasizing the boy's importance as their eyes and support, intensified Dasaratha's guilt.  Dasaratha then confessed his true identity to the hermit, revealing he was a Kshatriya king and not the boy's son. He detailed how, while hunting near the river, he mistook the sound of the boy filling a water pitcher for an animal and shot him with an arrow.

summary:
Canto LXIV

DaSaratha continues to tell Kausalya how, having sought the presence of
the aged parents of the deceased, he apprised them of the sad incident
and escorted them to the river bank, where their son lay dead; how,

content: Dasaratha, struggling to control his fear, confessed his true identity and the circumstances of the boy's death to the boy's father. He explained that, mistaking the sound of the boy filling a water pitcher for an animal, he had shot him with an arrow. He described finding the boy near death on the riverbank and how, after removing the arrow, the boy ascended to heaven. Dasaratha pleaded with the hermit for guidance on what to do next.  The hermit, despite his grief and fury, acknowledged Dasaratha's unintentional act. He explained that had Dasaratha not confessed, he would have faced dire consequences.  The hermit then asked Dasaratha to lead him and his wife to their deceased son. He expressed their desire to see their son one last time, describing the heartbreaking image of their son lying lifeless, covered in blood, and with his deerskin clothing scattered around him.

Canto LXIV

content: King Dasaratha recounts the hermit's lament over his dead son. The hermit questioned why his son lay lifeless, bathed in blood, his deerskin clothes scattered around him. He wondered who would now perform his morning rituals and console him in his grief. He worried about how he would survive without his son's support, particularly given his wife's advanced age and blindness.  Despairing, the hermit expressed his intention to join his son in death, asking Yama, the god of death, to allow his son to remain with them.  He pleaded with Yama, appealing to his piety, to grant him this one boon and ease his suffering.

content: Dasaratha continued his narration to Kausalya, describing how the hermit grieved for his deceased son.  The old man pleaded with his son's spirit not to depart for the afterlife yet, promising to join him soon along with his wife. He lamented their helpless state in the forest and envisioned himself beseeching Yama, the god of death, to allow his son to remain and care for them. The hermit argued his piety and asked for this single boon. He then urged his son to ascend to the heavenly realms reserved for warriors slain in battle, comparing his fate to that of noble kings and those who lived virtuous lives. The hermit declared that no one born in their lineage could face an evil destiny, implying that the one responsible for his son's death would suffer such a fate.  He then performed the last rites for his son. Dasaratha then described how the young man, now possessing a celestial form, ascended to heaven accompanied by Indra. The hermit’s son consoled his parents, assuring them they would soon rejoin him in the afterlife before departing in a magnificent aerial chariot.  He then turned to Dasaratha, who stood respectfully nearby.

Canto LXIV

content: Dasaratha recounted how the hermit’s son, after conversing with his parents and Indra, ascended to heaven in a celestial chariot.  He consoled his parents, promising they would soon join him.  The hermit, having performed the last rites, turned to Dasaratha and cursed him. He lamented that Dasaratha had rendered him childless and asked the king to end his life as well.  He declared that Dasaratha would also suffer the agony of losing a son. Although acknowledging Dasaratha's unintentional error, the hermit pronounced a severe curse, emphasizing the pain of his loss. He explained that the consequence of the king's action would inevitably come, just as sickness follows unhealthy food. After the curse, the hermit couple ascended to heaven. Dasaratha then lamented to Kausalya, recognizing the curse's fulfillment. He cried out in grief for Rama, his beloved son and protector, feeling overwhelmed by the approaching sorrow.

summary:
Canto LXIV

DaSaratha continues to tell Kausalya how, having sought the presence of
the aged parents of the deceased, he apprised them of the sad incident

content: King Dasaratha, overcome with grief and fear, told Kausalya that the sage's curse was upon him.  He lamented his impending death due to his separation from Rama, saying he could no longer even see her.  He wished Rama could touch him or that he could regain his wealth and position, believing these might save him.  Dasaratha acknowledged the injustice he had inflicted upon Rama and the nobility Rama had shown in accepting his exile. He questioned how any father could banish his son, and how any son wouldn't resent such treatment.  His memory fading, Dasaratha felt the messengers of death urging him onward. He expressed his anguish at dying without seeing Rama again.  Dasaratha described the unbearable grief of Rama's absence, comparing it to a scorching sun drying up a small amount of water. He declared that those who would see Rama's return after fourteen years would be blessed. He described Rama's beauty – his earrings, face, teeth, nose – and envisioned his return to Ayodhya.  His heart sinking, Dasaratha described his failing senses, unable to perceive sound, touch, or taste, his mind overwhelmed with sorrow.  He continued to lament in the presence of Kausalya and Sumitra, ultimately succumbing to his grief and passing away in the middle of the night.

Canto LXIV

DaSaratha continues to tell Kausalya how, having sought the presence of
the aged parents of the deceased, he apprised them of the sad incident

content: Dasaratha's lament continued, his senses failing as his grief intensified. He mourned his inability to see Rama, whose absence felt like a scorching sun drying up his very life force. He envied those who would witness Rama's return, describing his son's beauty and the joy his arrival would bring.  Dasaratha's heart sank, his mind clouded with sorrow.  He felt the messengers of death closing in, urging him to his end.  He cried out in anguish, unable to see or hear, consumed by the pain of Rama's exile.  The king then succumbed to his grief, passing away in the middle of the night in the presence of Kausalya and Sumitra.

summary: No summary available
content: The king's retinue, including bards and musicians, gathered at the royal residence early the next morning.  They sang praises and played instruments, filling the palace with music and echoing benedictions.  Birds, both free and caged, joined the chorus with their own songs.  Attendants, primarily women and eunuchs, stood ready to serve the king.  Water scented with sandalwood, along with auspicious items like holy water and fresh garments, were prepared for the king's bath and attire. As the sun rose, the retinue grew anxious, wondering why the king hadn't yet emerged. The ladies-in-waiting near his bedside attempted to rouse him gently, but found no response.  Touching him with care and reverence, they detected no signs of life, noticing the absence of a heartbeat or pulse.  Fearful and trembling, they began to suspect the worst, realizing the king had passed away.

summary: No summary available
content: The king's secondary wives approached him gently, attempting to wake him.  Despite their respectful efforts, they found no signs of life in the king.  Recognizing the stillness, they became fearful, trembling like reeds in a current.  Their suspicion that the king had died became a certainty.  Meanwhile, Kausalya and Sumitra remained deeply asleep, overcome by grief from their sons' absence. Kausalya, dimmed by sorrow, appeared like a star hidden by darkness.  Neither she nor Sumitra, whose face was tear-stained, showed any outward awareness of the king's condition.  Observing the sleeping queens and the lifeless king, the palace women concluded King Dasaratha had passed away in his sleep.  They began to wail loudly, their cries echoing through the palace like elephants mourning a lost leader. The noise startled Kausalya and Sumitra awake.  They too, upon seeing and touching the king, cried out in grief and collapsed.  Kausalya, covered in dust, lay on the floor. The other women watched in despair as Kausalya, overcome by grief, fell to the ground.  Kaikeyi and the king's other wives, also overwhelmed with sorrow, wept and fainted.  The palace filled with the sound of their combined lamentations.

summary: No summary available
content: The king's wives, including Kaikeyi, overwhelmed by grief, fainted. Their cries intensified the already deafening wails emanating from the women's quarters, amplifying the sorrowful sounds. The palace, once a place of joy, was now filled with fear, confusion, and the cries of distraught relatives mourning King Dasaratha's passing.  The king's wives, deeply saddened, wept and clung to his lifeless body, lamenting their loss like helpless women.

summary: No summary available
content: Queen Kausalya, overcome with grief, mourned the passing of King Dasaratha and the exile of Rama.  She lamented to Kaikeyi, blaming her for the king's demise and Rama's banishment. Kausalya expressed her despair, feeling unable to live without her husband and son. She condemned Kaikeyi's actions, claiming they stemmed from greed and led to the ruin of their family.  Kausalya worried about Sita's well-being in the forest and feared that the news of Rama's exile would devastate her father, King Janaka.  Overwhelmed by sorrow, she declared her intention to join her husband in death by self-immolation. The ministers, witnessing her profound grief, gently separated her from the king's body and prepared it for preservation, knowing that the funeral rites could not be performed without a son present.

summary: No summary available
content: The ministers gently removed the grieving Queen Kausalya from King Dasaratha's body.  They then placed the king in an oil-filled trough for preservation, following the instructions of Vasistha and the other priests.  Aware of the proper funeral rites, the ministers knew they could not proceed without a son present. The queens, learning of the king’s placement in the oil-filled trough, lamented his death. Overwhelmed with grief and crying, they questioned why the king had abandoned them, especially after Rama's exile.  They expressed their despair at facing the future without their husband and sons, and in the presence of Kaikeyi. The queens mourned the loss of their protector and lord, Rama, who had left for the forest. They wondered how they could endure life without the king and Rama, subjected to Kaikeyi’s presence.  The queens lamented, highlighting Kaikeyi's role in their abandonment. Filled with sorrow, they wept for Dasaratha.  Ayodhya, without its king, was likened to a starless night or a woman without her husband. The city, once vibrant, was now filled with the sounds of mourning.  As the king passed away and his queens grieved, the sun set and darkness fell. The king's friends and relatives, gathered there, decided against cremation without a son present, and so they left the king in the oil-filled trough.

summary: No summary available
content: Ayodhya mourned King Dasaratha.  Without its king, the city felt as empty as a starless night or a woman without her husband.  Its streets and houses were desolate, its people weeping openly. With the king gone and his queens grieving, the sun set, symbolizing the descent of darkness upon the kingdom.  The king's friends and relatives decided against cremation in the absence of a son, placing his body in an oil-filled trough for preservation.  The city, without Dasaratha, was like the sky without the sun or a night without stars, its crossroads filled with grieving people.  Throughout Ayodhya, groups of people gathered, expressing their sorrow and blaming Kaikeyi for the tragic events.

summary:
Canto LXVII

Attributing the rise and fall of a kingdom to the presence and
disappearance of its ruler, Markandeya and other great sages,
who attended the king’s court the following day, urge
Vasistha to install any of the princes
on the throne immediately
content: The city of Ayodhya mourned through a long, sorrowful night.  At daybreak, the Brahmanas who had assumed the king's duties gathered at court.  Along with the ministers, respected sages like Markandeya, Maudgalya, Vamadeva, Kasyapa, Katyayana, Gautama, and Jabali addressed Vasistha, the royal priest. They lamented the king's passing due to grief over Rama and Lakshmana's absence. With the king gone, Rama and Lakshmana in the forest, and Bharata and Satrughna away in Kekaya, the sages stressed the urgent need for a new king.  They argued that a kingdom without a ruler quickly descends into chaos.  They cited the lack of rain, the reluctance to cultivate crops, the breakdown of social order, the absence of security, and the cessation of public works, religious ceremonies, and festivals as consequences of a kingless state.  They urged Vasistha to appoint a new king from the house of Ikshvaku immediately to avert disaster.

summary:
Canto LXVII

Attributing the rise and fall of a kingdom to the presence and
disappearance of its ruler, Markandeya and other great sages,
who attended the king’s court the following day, urge
Vasistha to install any of the princes
on the throne immediately
content: The sages continued to emphasize the critical need for a king.  Without a ruler, they argued, Ayodhya would descend into chaos. Public projects like assembly halls, gardens, and temples would be abandoned.  Religious ceremonies and sacrifices, especially the elaborate "satras," would cease. Even wealthy Brahmanas would hesitate to offer substantial donations, fearing they might become targets for thieves.  Public festivals and gatherings would dwindle, and legal disputes would remain unresolved.  Young women would be afraid to venture out, even in groups. Farmers and herders, despite having guards, would feel insecure.  Travel for pleasure or trade would be dangerous.  Ascetics would struggle to find food and shelter. The army would weaken, leaving the kingdom vulnerable to attack.  Scholars would not be able to meet and debate freely.  Even simple acts of worship would become difficult.  In short, without a king, there would be no prosperity, no security, and no joy.

summary:
Canto LXVII

Attributing the rise and fall of a kingdom to the presence and
disappearance of its ruler, Markandeya and other great sages,
who attended the king’s court the following day, urge
Vasistha to install any of the princes
on the throne immediately
content: The sages continued their impassioned plea for the immediate installation of a king. They described a kingdom without a ruler as vulnerable and chaotic.  Without a king, the army would be weak and unable to defend against enemies. People would not feel safe to travel or display their wealth.  Scholars would be unable to gather and discuss important matters.  Religious ceremonies and offerings would cease.  Even princes would lose their prominence and vibrancy.  A kingdom without a leader is like a river without water, a forest without grass, or cattle without a herder. They mourned the passing of King Dasaratha, who had been their guiding light, comparing him to the standard of a chariot or smoke indicating a fire. His absence left a void, and they feared that without a new king, people would turn on each other like predatory fish. They warned that the absence of law and order would embolden those who disregard societal norms and religious principles.  Just as an eye guides the body, a king guides the kingdom, embodying truth and righteousness. The king, they argued, is the source of stability and prosperity, a protector like a parent, and even surpasses powerful deities in his role. Without a king to distinguish right from wrong, the world would be plunged into darkness.  Reaffirming their loyalty and obedience to Vasistha, the sages implored him to choose and crown a successor from the Ikshvaku dynasty, or anyone suitable, to restore order and prevent further decline.

summary:
Canto LXVII

Attributing the rise and fall of a kingdom to the presence and
disappearance of its ruler, Markandeya and other great sages,
who attended the king’s court the following day, urge
Vasistha to install any of the princes
on the throne immediately
content: The sages emphasized the vital role of a king in maintaining order and differentiating between good and evil.  Without a king, they argued, the world would descend into chaos and darkness.  They reiterated their unwavering obedience to Vasistha, comparing their adherence to his commands to the oceans respecting the coastline.  Recognizing the kingdom's current state of vulnerability and disorder due to the king's absence, they implored Vasistha to select and crown a new king immediately, suggesting a prince of the Ikshvaku dynasty or any other suitable candidate.

summary:
Canto LXVIII
With the concurrence of Markandeya and other sages, Vasistha despatches
messengers to call back Bharata and Satrughna from their maternal

grandfather's. They leave forthwith for the capital of Kekaya
and quickly enter that city
content: Vasistha, after hearing the counsel of the sages, addressed the ministers and Brahmins, explaining that Bharata, who was given the kingship, was living happily with Satrughna in his maternal grandfather's kingdom.  He proposed sending swift messengers to retrieve them.  The assembly agreed, and Vasistha summoned Siddhartha, Vijaya, Jayanta, Asoka, and Nandana, instructing them to travel quickly to Rajagrha and inform Bharata of urgent business, without mentioning Rama's exile, Dasaratha's death, or any potential threat to the Raghu dynasty. They were also instructed to take gifts of fine clothes and ornaments for Bharata and his grandfather.  The messengers prepared for their journey, gathering provisions and mounting swift horses.  After receiving Vasistha's permission, they departed, traveling past the Malini River between the Aparatala and Pralamba mountains. They then crossed the Ganga at Hastinapura, passing through the Panchala territory and Kurujangala, observing the lakes and rivers along the way.  Finally, they quickly crossed the Saradanda River, a beautiful waterway teeming with birds and clear water.

summary:
Canto LXVIII
With the concurrence of Markandeya and other sages, Vasistha despatches
messengers to call back Bharata and Satrughna from their maternal

grandfather's. They leave forthwith for the capital of Kekaya
and quickly enter that city
content: The messengers continued their journey, passing the Malini River between the Aparatala and Pralamba mountains.  They then crossed the Ganga at Hastinapura, traveling westward through Kurujangala and the Panchala territory. They observed the flourishing lakes and clear rivers along their route.  They quickly crossed the Saradanda River, a beautiful, clear waterway frequented by various birds.  After respectfully circling a sacred tree called Satyopayacana on the riverbank, they entered the city of Kulinga.  Continuing on, they passed through the village of Tejobhibhavana and then Abhikala, crossing the Iksumati River. They traveled through the Bahlika kingdom, past the Sudama mountain, observing Brahmins dedicated to their spiritual practices. They saw the rivers Vipasa and Salmali, along with wells, ponds, and lakes.  They also observed various animals, including lions, tigers, deer, and elephants.  Despite their horses' fatigue, they efficiently traveled a long, peaceful road to Girivraja, the capital of the Kekaya kingdom. Driven by their loyalty to Vasistha, their concern for the people's welfare, and their desire to uphold their king's honor, the messengers entered the city that night.

summary:
Canto LXIX

Finding Bharata oppressed and sad, his friends try to beguile him by means of
music and narration of stories. But when the prince does not return to his
normal mood even then, they inquire of him as to what makes
him pensive and Bharata tells them how he saw
a bad dream the previous night
content: Bharata had a disturbing dream the night messengers arrived in Girivraja.  He awoke troubled near dawn.  His friends tried to comfort him with music, dancing, and storytelling.  However, Bharata remained despondent. When asked about his sadness, Bharata described his dream: his father falling from a mountain into a pool of cow dung, swimming in it, drinking oil, and then eating rice covered in sesame oil before submerging himself in oil. He also saw a dried-up ocean, the moon fallen to earth, the world shrouded in darkness and plagued by demons, a broken elephant tusk, extinguished fires, a cracked earth with withered trees and smoking, crumbling mountains.  He then saw his father, dressed in black and seated on an iron throne, being attacked by dark-complexioned women.  Finally, he saw his father, wearing a red garland and smeared with red paste, riding south in a chariot pulled by donkeys.

summary:
Canto LXIX

Finding Bharata oppressed and sad, his friends try to beguile him by means of
music and narration of stories. But when the prince does not return to his
normal mood even then, they inquire of him as to what makes
him pensive and Bharata tells them how he saw
a bad dream the previous night
content: Bharata's unsettling dream continued. He saw the king's elephant's tusk shattered, and ceremonial fires abruptly extinguished. The earth was cracked, the trees withered, and the mountains crumbled and smoked.  He witnessed his father, dressed in black and seated on an iron throne, being harassed by dark-complexioned women.  His father then appeared wearing a red garland and red paste, riding south in a donkey-drawn chariot. A grotesque, red-clad ogress mocked and pulled the king.  Bharata feared this dream foretold the death of himself, Rama, his father, or Lakshmana. He recalled the ill omen of riding a donkey-drawn chariot in a dream, signifying an impending funeral pyre.  He admitted his distress to his friends, explaining his parched throat, unease, and inexplicable self-loathing. Although he couldn't pinpoint the source of his fear, he felt a deep sense of foreboding, especially as he dwelled on his father's uncertain fate.

summary: No summary available
content: Bharata, while recounting a dream to his friends in Rajagrha, was visited by messengers from Ayodhya.  The messengers, having completed their arduous journey, were respectfully received by the King of Kekaya and his son.  They delivered Sage Vasistha's message to Bharata, assuring him of everyone's well-being and urging his swift return to Ayodhya for an important matter. The messengers also presented Bharata with valuable gifts of robes and jewels to be given to his maternal grandfather and uncle.  Twenty crores worth of gifts were designated for the king and ten crores for the uncle. Bharata, known for his affection for family, dutifully accepted and distributed the gifts.  He then inquired about the well-being of his father, King Dasaratha, his brothers Rama and Lakshmana, and his mothers Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi.  The messengers reassured Bharata of their good health and prompted him to prepare for his journey, indicating that fortune favored him.  Bharata agreed to depart after seeking permission from his grandfather.  He assured his grandfather of his return in the future.

summary: No summary available
content: Bharata, assured of his family's well-being, prepared for his return to Ayodhya.  He informed the messengers of his intention to seek his grandfather's permission.  He respectfully addressed his grandfather, expressing his desire to leave and promising to visit again.  His grandfather affectionately granted him permission, conveying his blessings to Kaikeyi and requesting Bharata to send his regards to his parents, Sage Vasistha, other Brahmanas, Rama, and Lakshmana. The king of Kekaya generously bestowed gifts upon Bharata, including elephants, blankets, deerskins, gold, large dogs, gold coins, and horses.  King Aswapati provided esteemed ministers as escorts, and Bharata's uncle, Yudhajit, gifted him elephants and mules.  Despite these generous offerings, Bharata felt anxious due to the messengers' urgency and a disturbing dream he had experienced, and he prioritized his departure.  He returned to his residence, then departed, traveling along a bustling main road filled with people, elephants, and horses. He then proceeded to the palace’s inner chambers, entering unhindered.

summary: No summary available
content: Bharata, despite receiving lavish gifts from the king of Kekaya, was preoccupied with his imminent departure for Ayodhya.  A disturbing dream and the messengers' urgency fueled his anxiety.  He briefly returned to his residence before venturing onto the bustling main road, crowded with people, elephants, and horses.  He then proceeded to the palace's inner chambers, entering without hindrance.  After bidding farewell to his maternal grandparents and uncle Yudhajit, Bharata, accompanied by Satrughna, embarked on his journey.  A retinue of one hundred chariots provided by King Aswapati, along with camels, bullocks, horses, mules, and servants, followed Bharata.  Under the protection of his grandfather's guards and accompanied by esteemed ministers, Bharata, known for his strength and lack of enemies, departed with Satrughna, much like a celestial being leaving the heavens.

summary: No summary available
content: Bharata journeyed from Rajagrha, crossing the Sudama and Hradini rivers. He then crossed the westward-flowing Satadru river and another at Ailadhana.  He continued through the Aparaparvata region and a river said to petrify objects, reaching the Salyakarsana area. After purifying himself in the Silavaha river, he crossed the MahaSaila hills towards the Caitraratha forest. He proceeded past the Saraswati river where it meets the Ganga, through Viramatsya, and into the Bharunda forest. After crossing the Kulinga river and reaching the Yamuna, Bharata's party rested.  He saw to the horses' needs before continuing. Bharata swiftly crossed a large, unfrequented forest.  He then approached the Ganga river near the village of Arnsudhana, aiming to cross at Pragvata.
