Trisanku recounted his ordeal: his desire to ascend to heaven bodily, the failed sacrifices, the rejection by Vasistha and his sons, and the subsequent curse. He lamented his misfortune despite his righteous conduct, expressing his belief in the power of fate over human effort. Trisanku pleaded with Viswamitra for help, believing himself a victim of cruel destiny. He asserted that fate had thwarted his efforts and that he had no other recourse but to seek Viswamitra's aid. He declared his intention to seek no other refuge, emphasizing his complete reliance on the sage's intervention.

summary:
Canto LVIII

Having been repulsed by Vasistha’s sons as well, Trisanku gets ready
to seek another priest, whereupon he is degraded by the curse of
Vasistha’s sons to the position of a Candala and approaches
Viswamitra with the same request
content: Trisanku, abandoned and transformed by the curse, implored Viswamitra for assistance, declaring that he would seek no other refuge and placing his complete trust in the sage's power to reverse his ill fate.

summary:
Canto LIX

Viswamitra assures TriSanku of his help, sends word to a number of sages,
inviting them to conduct a sacrifice for Trisanku, and pronounces
a curse on those who decline to come
content: Viswamitra reassured the fallen king Trisanku, welcoming him and promising his protection.  He declared his intention to summon all the great sages to assist in a sacrifice that would allow Trisanku to ascend to heaven in his current form.  Viswamitra then instructed his sons to prepare for the sacrifice and sent messengers to gather the sages, including Vasistha's sons, emphasizing that any disrespectful remarks should be reported.  The messengers returned, informing Viswamitra that while most sages were coming, Vasistha and his sons refused, questioning how they could participate in a sacrifice for someone of Trisanku's degraded status.  Enraged by their insults, Viswamitra cursed Vasistha's sons to be reborn for seven hundred generations as outcasts, forced to live off the flesh of dogs and perform degrading tasks.  He further cursed Mahodaya to become a Nisada, doomed to a life of violence and cruelty.

summary:
Canto LIX

Viswamitra assures TriSanku of his help, sends word to a number of sages,
inviting them to conduct a sacrifice for Trisanku, and pronounces
a curse on those who decline to come
content: Viswamitra's messengers returned, reporting that while many sages accepted the invitation to Trisanku's sacrifice, Vasistha, his sons, and Mahodaya refused.  They questioned the propriety of conducting such a ritual for a fallen king, expressing doubt that deities would accept offerings from such a ceremony and suggesting that any Brahmin partaking in the sacrificial meal would be defiled.  They further mocked Viswamitra's involvement. Upon hearing these disparaging remarks, Viswamitra, enraged, cursed Vasistha's sons to be reborn for seven hundred generations as outcasts, subsisting on the flesh of dogs and forced to live degrading lives.  He also cursed Mahodaya to become a Nisada, destined to a life of violence and without compassion.

summary:
Canto LX

At the instance of Viswamitra the sages assembled at his hermitage
commence a sacrifice; on the gods failing to appear at the
sacrifice to accept the offerings, Viswamitra sends
TriSanku bodily to heaven by dint of his own
spiritual power, but the latter is expelled by
Indra, on which Viswamitra stops him in
the air and proceeds to create a new
heaven and desists from his purpose
only on the gods granting his desire
content: Viswamitra, having used his yogic power to witness the downfall of Vasistha's sons and Mahodaya, addressed the assembled sages. He introduced Trisanku, a descendant of Ikswaku, known for his piety and generosity, who had sought Viswamitra's help to ascend to heaven in his current physical form. Viswamitra requested the sages to commence a sacrifice for this purpose.  The sages, recognizing Viswamitra's formidable power and fearing his wrath, agreed to proceed with the ritual, despite their reservations.  They appointed Viswamitra as the chief priest, with other knowledgeable priests assisting in their respective roles, performing the ceremonies according to prescribed procedures.  However, despite Viswamitra's prolonged invocation, none of the gods appeared to accept their share of the offerings, enraging the sage. He then raised his sacrificial ladle, ready to act on his own.

summary:
Canto LX

At the instance of Viswamitra the sages assembled at his hermitage
commence a sacrifice; on the gods failing to appear at the
sacrifice to accept the offerings, Viswamitra sends
TriSanku bodily to heaven by dint of his own
spiritual power, but the latter is expelled by
Indra, on which Viswamitra stops him in
the air and proceeds to create a new
heaven and desists from his purpose
only on the gods granting his desire
content: Enraged, Viswamitra lifted his sacrificial ladle and declared to Trisanku that he would use his spiritual power to send him to heaven in his physical body.  He commanded Trisanku to ascend, claiming his austerities would ensure his success. As Trisanku rose towards heaven, Indra and the gods rejected him, citing a preceptor's curse and commanding him to return to earth.  Trisanku plummeted back to earth, crying out to Viswamitra for help.  Furious at this defiance, Viswamitra declared he would create a new heaven and new gods. He began by creating another set of constellations and a new lunar system in the southern sky.  Alarmed by Viswamitra's power and actions, the gods, demons, and sages pleaded with him, arguing that Trisanku, cursed by his teacher, did not deserve to ascend to heaven in his physical form. Viswamitra responded that he had given his word and would not break his promise to Trisanku.

summary:
Canto LX

At the instance of Viswamitra the sages assembled at his hermitage
commence a sacrifice; on the gods failing to appear at the
sacrifice to accept the offerings, Viswamitra sends
TriSanku bodily to heaven by dint of his own
spiritual power, but the latter is expelled by
Indra, on which Viswamitra stops him in
the air and proceeds to create a new
heaven and desists from his purpose
only on the gods granting his desire
content: Viswamitra's actions caused great concern among the gods, demons, and sages. They appealed to him, arguing that Trisanku, cursed by his preceptor, was unworthy of ascending to heaven in his physical body.  Viswamitra, however, stood firm, stating that he had made a promise and would not break it.  He declared that Trisanku would reside in heaven in bodily form and that the new constellations he had created would remain in the sky. The gods ultimately conceded to Viswamitra's demands. Trisanku was placed in the heavens, head downward, among the new constellations, which would orbit him.  The gods and sages then departed, leaving Viswamitra praised and his will accomplished.

summary:
Canto LXI

The animal to be sacrificed at the sacrificial performance of King Ambarisa
having been stolen away by Indra appearing in disguise, the arch-priest
enjoined the king to secure a human beast as a substitute. Wandering

in search of such a beast, Ambarisa saw the sage Rcika at
Bhrgutunga and requested him to hand over his juvenile
son in exchange for a hundred thousand cows. On the
parents declining to part with their eldest and youngest
sons, the middle one, Sunahsepa by name, voluntarily
offered himself for being sold and the king
returned with him to his capital
content: Viswamitra, witnessing the departure of the sages from his hermitage, addressed them, suggesting they move to the western region near the Puskara lakes to continue their ascetic practices due to an impediment in the southern quarter.  He then proceeded to perform rigorous austerities, subsisting on roots and fruits.  Concurrently, King Ambarisa of Ayodhya commenced a horse-sacrifice.  However, the sacrificial animal disappeared, prompting the officiating priest to inform the king that a substitute, either the original animal or a human, was required to proceed with the ritual. King Ambarisa, accompanied by a large number of cows for trade, then embarked on a search for a replacement. His quest led him to Mount Bhrgutunga, where he encountered Sage Rcika, his wife, and sons.  After exchanging pleasantries, Ambarisa proposed to purchase one of Rcika’s sons for a hundred thousand cows to serve as a sacrificial offering, explaining his predicament and the urgency of the situation.

summary:
Canto LXI

The animal to be sacrificed at the sacrificial performance of King Ambarisa
having been stolen away by Indra appearing in disguise, the arch-priest
enjoined the king to secure a human beast as a substitute. Wandering

in search of such a beast, Ambarisa saw the sage Rcika at
Bhrgutunga and requested him to hand over his juvenile
son in exchange for a hundred thousand cows. On the
parents declining to part with their eldest and youngest
sons, the middle one, Sunahsepa by name, voluntarily
offered himself for being sold and the king
returned with him to his capital
content: King Ambarisa, after respectfully greeting Sage Rcika and inquiring about his well-being, offered him a hundred thousand cows in exchange for one of his sons to be used as a sacrificial offering, explaining his failed search for the original sacrificial animal. Rcika declined to sell his eldest son.  His wife then declared their youngest son equally precious and unavailable.  Sunahsepa, the middle son, upon hearing his parents’ pronouncements, offered himself to the king.  King Ambarisa, pleased with this turn of events, gave the sage a vast sum of gold, silver, jewels, and the promised cows in exchange for Sunahsepa, then promptly departed for his capital with the boy in his chariot.

summary:
Canto LXI

The animal to be sacrificed at the sacrificial performance of King Ambarisa
having been stolen away by Indra appearing in disguise, the arch-priest
enjoined the king to secure a human beast as a substitute. Wandering

in search of such a beast, Ambarisa saw the sage Rcika at
Bhrgutunga and requested him to hand over his juvenile
son in exchange for a hundred thousand cows. On the
parents declining to part with their eldest and youngest
sons, the middle one, Sunahsepa by name, voluntarily
offered himself for being sold and the king
returned with him to his capital
content: King Ambarisha, a renowned and righteous ruler, swiftly placed Sunahsepa into his chariot and hastened back to his kingdom.

summary:
Canto LXII

On reaching Puskara, while Ambarisa is taking rest, Sunahsepa approaches
Viswamitra, who was practising austerities there, and inquires of him
a means of escaping death without meeting with the king’s
opposition, whereupon Viswamitra teaches him a prayer
each for propitiating Indra and Lord Visnu, respectively,
by reciting which the boy secures release from
Ambarisa’s bondage and the latter gets the
reward of the sacrifice without
sacrificing him
content: King Ambarisa, accompanied by Sunahsepa, rested at Puskara.  While the king rested, Sunahsepa approached his maternal uncle, Viswamitra, who was meditating there with other sages.  Distressed and fatigued, Sunahsepa pleaded with Viswamitra for help, saying he had no family and begged the sage to protect him from his impending sacrifice. Sunahsepa wished for King Ambarisa's sacrifice to be successful but also for his own life to be spared, hoping to live long and perform austerities.  Viswamitra, moved by his nephew’s plea, asked his sons to take Sunahsepa's place as the sacrifice, believing it would benefit them in the afterlife. He reasoned that Sunahsepa, a helpless child of a hermit, deserved their compassion.  Viswamitra urged his sons to act virtuously and fulfill their duty, assuring them that this act would appease the gods and fulfill his own promise of protection to Sunahsepa.  However, Viswamitra’s sons, led by Madhucchanda, arrogantly rejected their father's request, questioning his decision to prioritize another's child over his own. They considered his proposal as inappropriate as consuming contaminated food.  This disrespectful response enraged Viswamitra.

summary:
Canto LXII

On reaching Puskara, while Ambarisa is taking rest, Sunahsepa approaches
Viswamitra, who was practising austerities there, and inquires of him
a means of escaping death without meeting with the king’s
opposition, whereupon Viswamitra teaches him a prayer
each for propitiating Indra and Lord Visnu, respectively,
by reciting which the boy secures release from
Ambarisa’s bondage and the latter gets the
reward of the sacrifice without
sacrificing him
content: At Puskara, Sunahsepa appealed to Viswamitra, highlighting the successful completion of Ambarisa's sacrifice and the fulfillment of Viswamitra's promise of protection.  Viswamitra's sons, led by Madhucchanda, arrogantly questioned their father's decision to save Sunahsepa, deeming it inappropriate.  Enraged, Viswamitra cursed his sons to live as outcasts for a thousand years.  He then instructed Sunahsepa to recite hymns to Indra and Vishnu during the sacrifice to secure his release. Sunahsepa, following Viswamitra's instructions, urged King Ambarisa to proceed with the sacrifice.  The king, joyful at Sunahsepa's compliance, quickly went to the sacrificial grounds.  Ambarisa then bound and prepared Sunahsepa for the sacrifice.  Bound to the sacrificial post, Sunahsepa sang hymns praising Indra and Vishnu.  Pleased with the hymns, Indra granted Sunahsepa a long life. King Ambarisa also successfully completed his sacrifice, receiving blessings due to Indra's favor.

summary:
Canto LXII

On reaching Puskara, while Ambarisa is taking rest, Sunahsepa approaches
Viswamitra, who was practising austerities there, and inquires of him
a means of escaping death without meeting with the king’s
opposition, whereupon Viswamitra teaches him a prayer
each for propitiating Indra and Lord Visnu, respectively,
by reciting which the boy secures release from
Ambarisa’s bondage and the latter gets the
reward of the sacrifice without
sacrificing him
content: King Ambarisa, following the guidance of the priests, formally prepared Sunahsepa for the sacrifice. He dressed him in red and bound him to the sacrificial post.  Even while bound, the young Sunahsepa sang hymns praising Indra and Vishnu.  Impressed by these sacred songs, Indra granted Sunahsepa a long life. King Ambarisa also successfully completed his sacrifice, receiving abundant blessings thanks to Indra's favor. Afterward, Viswamitra resumed his intense meditation at Puskara.

summary:
Canto LXIII

Brahma pays a visit to Viswamitra at Puskara and rewards him with
the status of a Rsi (seer). There he feels enamoured of Menaka,
a celestial nymph deputed by Indra, and enjoys life with
her for ten years. At last he realizes his folly and
retires to the Himalayas, where he renews his
austerities and is rewarded with the status of
a Maharsi by Brahma. Still dissatisfied,
he prosecutes his austerities with
renewed vigour
content: Viswamitra, after a thousand years of austerities, was visited by the gods, led by Brahma, who bestowed upon him the title of Rishi.  Brahma returned to his celestial abode, and Viswamitra resumed his ascetic practices.  Later, the celestial nymph Menaka, sent by Indra, arrived at Puskara to bathe.  Viswamitra, captivated by her beauty, invited her to stay at his hermitage.  Overcome by infatuation, he spent ten years with her, unaware of the passage of time.  Eventually, Viswamitra realized that his involvement with Menaka was a divine scheme to disrupt his austerities, causing him deep remorse and regret. He felt ashamed and distressed at the lost time and the interruption to his spiritual pursuits.

summary:
Canto LXIII

Brahma pays a visit to Viswamitra at Puskara and rewards him with
the status of a Rsi (seer). There he feels enamoured of Menaka,
a celestial nymph deputed by Indra, and enjoys life with
her for ten years. At last he realizes his folly and
retires to the Himalayas, where he renews his
austerities and is rewarded with the status of
a Maharsi by Brahma. Still dissatisfied,
he prosecutes his austerities with
renewed vigour
content: Viswamitra, realizing that his ten-year relationship with Menaka was a divinely orchestrated plot to disrupt his spiritual progress, was filled with remorse and regret. He recognized that the gods had manipulated him into losing precious time and diverting him from his ascetic pursuits. Viswamitra then dismissed Menaka and journeyed to the Himalayas, determined to regain his spiritual focus.  He committed to lifelong celibacy and engaged in intense austerities on the banks of the Kausiki river for thousands of years, causing fear among the gods.  They appealed to Brahma, who acknowledged Viswamitra's efforts and offered him the title of Maharsi.  However, Viswamitra desired the title of Brahmarsi, a rank signifying complete control over his senses.  Brahma informed him he had not yet reached that level of mastery and encouraged him to continue his efforts. Viswamitra then resumed his even more arduous austerities, standing with arms raised, living on air, enduring extreme heat and cold, causing great concern among the gods, including Indra.

summary:
Canto LXIII

Brahma pays a visit to Viswamitra at Puskara and rewards him with
the status of a Rsi (seer). There he feels enamoured of Menaka,
a celestial nymph deputed by Indra, and enjoys life with
her for ten years. At last he realizes his folly and
retires to the Himalayas, where he renews his
austerities and is rewarded with the status of
a Maharsi by Brahma. Still dissatisfied,
he prosecutes his austerities with
renewed vigour
content: Viswamitra, after Brahma's departure, continued his rigorous austerities. He stood with his arms raised, surviving on air alone, without shelter.  He endured scorching summers surrounded by five fires, exposed himself to the open sky during monsoons, and stood in icy water throughout winters. He maintained this incredibly demanding practice for a thousand years.  His intense asceticism caused considerable anxiety among the gods, including Indra.

summary:
Canto LXIV
Petrifying by means of a curse Rambha, the celestial nymph
sent by Indra to wean him from his austerities,

Viswamitra resolves upon a further
course of austerities
content: Indra, the king of the gods, tasked the celestial nymph Rambha with disrupting Viswamitra's intense meditation by enticing him. Rambha expressed her apprehension to Indra, fearing the sage's wrath.  Reassuring her, Indra promised to accompany her disguised as a cuckoo.  Rambha, adorned with captivating beauty, approached Viswamitra. The sage, hearing the cuckoo's song and noticing Rambha's presence, grew suspicious. Recognizing Indra's scheme, Viswamitra, enraged, cursed Rambha to become a stone statue for ten thousand years, prophesying her eventual release by a powerful Brahmana. Immediately, Rambha turned to stone, while Indra and Kamadeva, the god of love, fled.

summary:
Canto LXIV
Petrifying by means of a curse Rambha, the celestial nymph
sent by Indra to wean him from his austerities,

Viswamitra resolves upon a further
course of austerities
content: Viswamitra declared that Rambha, for attempting to seduce him, a sage striving to conquer his base desires, would be turned to stone for ten thousand years. He then prophesied that a powerful Brahmana, possessing immense spiritual energy gained through austerities, would eventually free her.  Overcome with remorse for succumbing to anger and depleting the spiritual merit earned through his arduous penance, Viswamitra lamented his lack of control over his senses.  As a consequence of his curse, Rambha instantly turned into stone, while Indra and Kamadeva fled.  Viswamitra resolved to never again yield to anger or speak unnecessarily, even vowing to abstain from breathing and eating for centuries to regain control over his senses and attain the ultimate state of Brahmanhood. He pledged to continue his austerities until he reached this goal, declaring that his physical body would not deteriorate while he remained dedicated to his spiritual practice.

summary:
Canto LXV

On Viswamitra not ceasing from his austerities even on his being
conferred the title of a Brahmarsi by Brahma, Vasistha, as
urged by gods, recognizes it and here ends the narration

of Satananda. Having worshipped Vigwamitra,
King Janaka then returns to his palace
content: Viswamitra, leaving the Himalayas, traveled east and began intense austerities. He took a vow of silence for a thousand years, performing incredibly difficult and unparalleled ascetic practices.  He gave all his food to a Brahmin, remaining without any himself, yet upheld his vow of silence without complaint. Even after a thousand years of austerities and facing many obstacles, Viswamitra remained resolute and unyielding, his anger contained. When he finally broke his fast, Indra, disguised as a Brahmin, appeared and requested the food. Viswamitra, again, held his breath for another thousand years. Smoke emanated from his head, causing distress and confusion throughout the three worlds. Alarmed by his power and the disruption it caused, the gods, sages, and other celestial beings appealed to Brahma, concerned that Viswamitra's growing ascetic power, fueled by various temptations and provocations, posed a threat to the universe. They highlighted his flawless discipline and warned that if his desires remained unfulfilled, his austerities could destroy all creation. The cosmos reflected this turmoil, with oceans raging, mountains crumbling, earth quaking, and violent winds blowing.  The gods expressed their inability to find a solution and lamented the growing disbelief and confusion among the people of the three worlds.

summary:
Canto LXV

On Viswamitra not ceasing from his austerities even on his being
conferred the title of a Brahmarsi by Brahma, Vasistha, as
urged by gods, recognizes it and here ends the narration

of Satananda. Having worshipped Vigwamitra,
King Janaka then returns to his palace
content: The gods, deeply troubled by Viswamitra's escalating ascetic power, pleaded with Brahma. They described the widespread chaos caused by his austerities—turbulent oceans, crumbling mountains, a quaking earth, and violent winds.  Fearing that Viswamitra's unfulfilled desires would lead him to destroy the universe, they urged Brahma to intervene.  The sun appeared dimmed by Viswamitra's radiance.  The gods implored Brahma to appease the sage before he destroyed everything.  They conceded to grant Viswamitra any boon, even dominion over the gods themselves.  Brahma, along with the other gods, approached Viswamitra, hailing him as a Brahmarishi and expressing their satisfaction with his asceticism.  They granted him longevity.  Viswamitra, in turn, requested that the sacred syllable OM, the word 'Vasat,' and the Vedas come to him of their own accord. He also desired that Vasistha, a renowned sage and Brahma's son, acknowledge him as a Brahmarishi. Satisfied with the gods' promise, he bid them farewell.  Vasistha, urged by the gods, befriended Viswamitra, acknowledging his status as a Brahmarishi and confirming that all his desires had been fulfilled. The gods then returned to their celestial abodes. Viswamitra, having achieved the esteemed status of a Brahmana, honored Vasistha.

summary:
Canto LXV

On Viswamitra not ceasing from his austerities even on his being
conferred the title of a Brahmarsi by Brahma, Vasistha, as
urged by gods, recognizes it and here ends the narration

of Satananda. Having worshipped Vigwamitra,
King Janaka then returns to his palace
content: Viswamitra, having received the Brahmarishi title, was addressed by Brahma and the gods.  Viswamitra, pleased by their acknowledgement, expressed his remaining desires: possession of the sacred syllable OM, the word 'Vasat,' and the Vedas, and formal recognition from Vasistha.  The gods agreed, and Vasistha, compelled by the gods, accepted Viswamitra's elevated status.  The gods then returned to their heavenly dwellings. Viswamitra, having achieved his goal, honored Vasistha.  He continued his ascetic practices, traveling the world.  Satananda concluded his narration by praising Viswamitra's power and virtue. King Janaka, deeply impressed by the story, expressed gratitude to Viswamitra for attending the sacrifice. He acknowledged the benefits gained from hearing about Viswamitra's austerities. Janaka, noting the approaching evening rituals, requested to meet Viswamitra again the following morning and took his leave after respectfully circumambulating the sage. Viswamitra granted Janaka permission to depart.

summary:
Canto LXV

On Viswamitra not ceasing from his austerities even on his being
conferred the title of a Brahmarsi by Brahma, Vasistha, as
urged by gods, recognizes it and here ends the narration

of Satananda. Having worshipped Vigwamitra,
King Janaka then returns to his palace
content: King Janaka, still captivated by Viswamitra's stories, expressed that he wished to hear more. However, recognizing the approaching sunset and the time for evening rituals, he announced his intention to depart.  He requested to meet Viswamitra again the next morning.  Viswamitra, pleased by the king's respect and eagerness, happily granted him permission to leave.  Janaka, accompanied by his priest and relatives, respectfully circumambulated the sage before departing.  Viswamitra, along with Rama and Lakshmana, then returned to his own dwelling, receiving continued reverence from those present.

summary:
Canto LXVI
On Viswamitra apprising Janaka of the eagerness of Sri Rama and
Laksmana to see his famous bow, Janaka reveals to him its

glory and tells him how he got it as well as Sita and
also of his pledge to give her in marriage to

Sri Rama if he
content: King Janaka welcomed Sage Viswamitra, Rama, and Lakshmana.  Janaka offered his services to the sage. Viswamitra explained that Rama and Lakshmana wished to see the famed bow in Janaka's possession. Janaka agreed to show them the bow and began to narrate its history.  The bow belonged to the gods and was entrusted to his ancestor, Devarata.  He recounted how Lord Shiva, angered by the gods' refusal to share sacrificial offerings, threatened them with the bow.  The gods appeased Shiva, who then returned the bow.  Janaka further revealed that while preparing land for a sacrifice, he found a baby girl who emerged from the earth.  He named her Sita and raised her as his own daughter.

summary:
Canto LXVI
On Viswamitra apprising Janaka of the eagerness of Sri Rama and
Laksmana to see his famous bow, Janaka reveals to him its

glory and tells him how he got it as well as Sita and
also of his pledge to give her in marriage to

Sri Rama if he
content: Janaka continued his story, explaining that the gods, satisfied with his penance, granted him a powerful army.  With this army, he defeated the kings who had besieged Mithila, forcing them to flee.  Janaka then declared his intention to show the divine bow to Rama and Lakshmana. He pledged to give Sita to Rama in marriage if he could string the bow.

summary:
Canto LXVI
On Viswamitra apprising Janaka of the eagerness of Sri Rama and
Laksmana to see his famous bow, Janaka reveals to him its

glory and tells him how he got it as well as Sita and
also of his pledge to give her in marriage to

Sri Rama if he
content: The gods, pleased with Janaka's devotion, granted him a complete army, including chariots, elephants, horses, and infantry.  Defeated by this powerful force, the enemy kings and their advisors fled in all directions. Janaka then reiterated his intention to display the magnificent bow to Rama and Lakshmana.  He declared that if Rama could string the bow, he would give Sita, his daughter born not of woman, to him in marriage.

summary:
Canto LXVII

Urged by Viswamitra, Sri Rama bends the bow, sent by Janaka to
the sacrificial hall, in order to string it and in the process
breaks it, whereupon Janaka with the concurrence of
VisSwamitra sends his counsellors to Ayodhya
to invite Emperor Dasaratha for the wedding
content: Viswamitra requested King Janaka to show the bow to Rama.  Janaka ordered his counselors to bring the celestial bow, adorned with sandalwood paste and flowers.  The counselors, possessing immense strength, retrieved the large, heavy bow from the city, requiring five thousand men to transport the chest containing it.  Presenting the iron chest to Janaka, the counselors remarked that this revered bow, worshipped by all kings, was now ready for Rama and Lakshmana's viewing. Janaka, addressing Viswamitra, Rama, and Lakshmana, explained the bow's history, stating that despite its worship by the Janaka dynasty and attempts by powerful kings, gods, demons, and other celestial beings, none had been able to string it. He then invited Viswamitra to show the bow to the princes.  Viswamitra instructed Rama to look at the bow.  Rama, opening the chest at Viswamitra's behest, declared his intention to not only hold and examine the bow but also to attempt stringing it.  Both the king and Viswamitra readily agreed, and Rama effortlessly grasped the bow.

summary:
Canto LXVII

Urged by Viswamitra, Sri Rama bends the bow, sent by Janaka to
the sacrificial hall, in order to string it and in the process
breaks it, whereupon Janaka with the concurrence of
VisSwamitra sends his counsellors to Ayodhya
to invite Emperor Dasaratha for the wedding
content: At Viswamitra's urging, Rama looked at the bow.  Rama declared his intention to not just examine it, but also to string it.  The king and Viswamitra agreed.  Rama easily lifted the bow and, in full view of the crowd, strung it as if it were a child's toy.  As he bent the mighty bow, it snapped in two with a deafening roar that shook the earth and knocked everyone present, except for Viswamitra, King Janaka, Rama, and Lakshmana, to the ground.  When the crowd recovered, Janaka, relieved and impressed, praised Rama's incredible feat of strength to Viswamitra.  He declared that Sita would bring honor to their family by marrying Rama.  Janaka reaffirmed his vow that Sita could only be won through a feat of strength, and that she would now be given to Rama. He requested Viswamitra's permission to send his counselors to Ayodhya to invite Dasaratha to the wedding, instructing them to politely explain the events to the emperor and bring him back to Mithila. Viswamitra agreed, and the king dispatched his counselors with detailed instructions to recount the events and escort Dasaratha back.

summary:
Canto LXVII

Urged by Viswamitra, Sri Rama bends the bow, sent by Janaka to
the sacrificial hall, in order to string it and in the process
breaks it, whereupon Janaka with the concurrence of
VisSwamitra sends his counsellors to Ayodhya
to invite Emperor Dasaratha for the wedding
content: King Janaka sought Viswamitra's blessing to send his counselors to Ayodhya.  He instructed them to travel swiftly by chariot and respectfully invite Dasaratha to Mithila. They were to fully explain how Rama had won Sita's hand in marriage through his remarkable feat of strength.  The counselors were also to inform Dasaratha that Rama and Lakshmana had been under Viswamitra's care and protection.  Finally, they were to escort the delighted emperor back to Mithila for the wedding. Both Viswamitra and Janaka addressed the counselors, emphasizing the importance of accurately conveying the events and ensuring Dasaratha's return.

summary:
Canto LXVIII

Reaching Ayodhya, the counsellors of Janaka tell Dasaratha how Sri Rama
broke the bow of Lord Siva in Janaka’s custody and won the hand of
his daughter, and convey to him their master’s invitation for the
wedding, on which Dasaratha makes up his mind in consultation
with Vasistha to depart for Mithila at an early date
content: King Janaka's envoys, having rested for three days after their tiring journey, arrived in Ayodhya and entered the royal palace.  There they met the venerable King Dasaratha.  Reassured by the king's kind demeanor, the envoys delivered their message from Janaka.  They conveyed Janaka's polite greetings and inquiries about Dasaratha's well-being, as well as that of his advisors and priests.  Speaking with Janaka's and Viswamitra's approval, the envoys relayed Janaka's message.  They reminded Dasaratha of the challenge of the bow, and how Sita had been promised to whoever could string it.  They recounted how Rama not only strung the bow, but broke it, and thus won Sita's hand. Janaka, honoring his pledge, requested Dasaratha's permission to proceed with the marriage. He urged Dasaratha to come to Mithila with his advisors and witness the joy of his sons, Rama and Lakshmana.  The envoys concluded by emphasizing the kind and respectful nature of Janaka's message, which was endorsed by both Viswamitra and Satananda.  Upon hearing this news, King Dasaratha was overjoyed and addressed his own advisors, Vasistha and Vamadeva. He mentioned Rama and Lakshmana’s stay in Videha, under Viswamitra’s care.

summary:
Canto LXVIII

Reaching Ayodhya, the counsellors of Janaka tell Dasaratha how Sri Rama
broke the bow of Lord Siva in Janaka’s custody and won the hand of
his daughter, and convey to him their master’s invitation for the
wedding, on which Dasaratha makes up his mind in consultation
with Vasistha to depart for Mithila at an early date
content: King Dasaratha, overjoyed by the news, confirms that Rama and Lakshmana are indeed in Videha under Viswamitra's care.  He relates how Janaka, impressed by Rama's prowess, now wishes to give his daughter Sita to him in marriage.  Dasaratha then proposes to his advisors, Vasistha and Vamadeva, and his counselors,  a swift journey to Mithila to formalize the marriage, urging them to avoid any delay. The counselors and sages unanimously agree to the proposal.  Dasaratha, pleased with their agreement, decides that the journey will commence the following day.  Janaka's envoys, honored by Dasaratha's hospitality, happily spend the night in Ayodhya, content with the king's decision.

summary:
Canto LXIX

With a large number of followers, DaSaratha proceeds to Mithila for the
wedding and is received with signal honour by Janaka and his
people and comfortably lodged
content: King Dasaratha, after spending the night with his family and advisor, instructed his minister Sumanthra to make arrangements for the journey to Mithila.  He ordered the treasury officers to gather wealth and gemstones, guarded by soldiers, and to proceed ahead. The four divisions of the army – infantry, cavalry, chariots, and elephants – were to follow, along with well-equipped palanquins and other vehicles.  He requested respected Brahmins, including Vasistha, Vamadeva, Jabali, Kashyapa, Markandeya, and Katyayana, to travel ahead.  Dasaratha also ordered his own chariot to be prepared, emphasizing the need for haste due to the envoys' urging.  Following the king's command, the army marched behind him and the Brahmins. After a four-day journey, they reached the Videha territory.  King Janaka, upon hearing of Dasaratha's arrival, began preparations to welcome him.  Janaka greeted the elderly Dasaratha with joy and extended a warm welcome.  He expressed his delight at Dasaratha’s arrival, considering it a fortunate event.  Janaka mentioned the joy of Rama and Lakshmana, and the good fortune of Vasistha and other Brahmins accompanying Dasaratha. He declared that all obstacles to Sita’s marriage had been removed, expressing his happiness at the alliance with the Raghu dynasty. He urged Dasaratha to perform the wedding ceremony the next morning, after the completion of the sacrifice. Dasaratha listened attentively and acknowledged Janaka's words, reminding him that the fulfillment of a gift rests with the giver.

summary:
Canto LXIX

With a large number of followers, DaSaratha proceeds to Mithila for the
wedding and is received with signal honour by Janaka and his
people and comfortably lodged
content: King Dasaratha assured Janaka that he would comply with his wishes regarding the wedding, stating that the fulfillment of a gift rests with the giver.  Janaka, impressed by Dasaratha's agreeable response, expressed his astonishment. The assembled sages, joyful at their reunion, spent the night contentedly.  Rama and Lakshmana, led by Viswamitra, paid respects to their father, Dasaratha.  The king, overjoyed to see his sons, was further honored by Janaka's hospitality, and retired for the night in high spirits. Janaka, after completing the sacrificial rites and ensuring the well-being of his daughters Sita and Urmila, also retired for the night.

summary:
Canto LXX

Janaka sends for his younger brother, KuSadhwaja, from Sankasya.
Invited by him, DaSaratha meets Janaka at the latter’s palace,
where at the instance of DaSaratha, Vasistha glorifies
the race of Ikswaku
content: King Janaka, after completing his sacrificial rites, instructed his priest Satananda to summon his brother, Kuśadhvaja, from Śaṅkāśyā.  Kuśadhvaja, described as virtuous and valiant, resided in the prosperous city, enjoying the waters of the Ikṣumati River. Janaka desired his brother's presence at the upcoming wedding. Messengers were dispatched and, upon receiving the message, Kuśadhvaja promptly traveled to Mithila.  He greeted Janaka and Satananda respectfully before being seated. The brothers then sent their counselor, Sudamana, to invite King Daśaratha and his entourage to Mithila. Sudamana conveyed the invitation, and Daśaratha, along with his sons, counselors, and sages, journeyed to Janaka's court. There, Daśaratha requested Sage Vasiṣṭha to recount his lineage.  Vasiṣṭha began by describing the origin of Brahma and traced the line through Marīci, Kaśyapa, Vivasvat, Manu, and Ikṣvāku, the first king of Ayodhyā, continuing through  Kuksi, Vikuksi, Bāṇa, Anaraṇya, Dhundhumāra, Yuvanāśva, Māndhātā, Susandhi, Dhruvasandhi, and Prasenajit.

summary:
Canto LXX

Janaka sends for his younger brother, KuSadhwaja, from Sankasya.
Invited by him, DaSaratha meets Janaka at the latter’s palace,
where at the instance of DaSaratha, Vasistha glorifies
the race of Ikswaku
content: King Daśaratha arrived at Janaka's court in Mithila with his sons, advisors, and sages.  Janaka, seated with his brother Kuśadhvaja, welcomed them.  Daśaratha then requested Sage Vasiṣṭha to recount his lineage to Janaka. Vasiṣṭha began by describing the creation of Brahma and traced the lineage through prominent figures like Marīci, Kaśyapa, Vivasvat, Manu, and finally to Ikṣvāku, the first king of Ayodhyā.  He continued the lineage through Kuksi, Vikuksi, Bāṇa, Anaraṇya,  and  Dhundhumāra. Vasiṣṭha then told the story of King Asita, who, after being defeated in battle, fled to the Himalayas with his pregnant wives and subsequently died.  One of the wives attempted to poison the other's unborn child.  A sage named Cyavana then encountered the poisoned queen, Kalindi, and prophesied that she would bear a powerful son, who would be named Sagara because he was born with the poison. Vasiṣṭha then continued the lineage from Sagara to Asamañja, Amśumān, Dilīpa and finally to Bhagiratha.

summary:
Canto LXX

Janaka sends for his younger brother, KuSadhwaja, from Sankasya.
Invited by him, DaSaratha meets Janaka at the latter’s palace,
where at the instance of DaSaratha, Vasistha glorifies
the race of Ikswaku
content: Vasiṣṭha continued recounting the Ikshvaku lineage. He spoke of Dhundhumāra, Yuvanāśva, Māndhātā, Susandhi, Dhruvasandhi, and Bharata.  Then came Asita, who was defeated by his enemies and fled to the Himalayas with his pregnant wives, where he died.  One wife attempted to harm the other's unborn child with poison.  The sage Cyavana encountered the poisoned queen, Kalindi, and foretold the birth of her powerful son.  Vasiṣṭha then swiftly moved the narrative through the generations:  Agnivarna, Sighraga, Maru, Prasusruka, Ambarisa, Nahusa, Yayati, Nabhaga, Aja, and finally to Daśaratha, father of Rama and Lakshmana. Vasiṣṭha concluded by formally requesting Janaka's daughters for Rama and Lakshmana, emphasizing their noble lineage and virtuous qualities.

summary:
Canto LXX

Janaka sends for his younger brother, KuSadhwaja, from Sankasya.
Invited by him, DaSaratha meets Janaka at the latter’s palace,
where at the instance of DaSaratha, Vasistha glorifies
the race of Ikswaku
content: Sage Vasistha continued his recounting of the Ikshvaku lineage.  A widowed princess, devoted to her deceased husband, gave birth to a son, Sagara, who was miraculously unharmed by poison meant for him in the womb.  Sagara's descendants included Asamañja, Amśumān, Dilīpa, and Bhagiratha.  From Bhagiratha came Kakutstha, and then Raghu, whose son Pravṛddha became known as Kalmāṣapāda due to a curse.  The lineage continued through Śaṅkhana, Sudarśana, Agnivarna, Sighraga, Maru, Prasusruka, Ambarisa, Nahusa, Yayati, and Nābhaga.  Finally, Vasiṣṭha arrived at Aja, father of Daśaratha, who in turn was the father of Rama and Lakshmana.  Vasiṣṭha formally requested Janaka to grant his daughters in marriage to Rama and Lakshmana, highlighting their noble lineage, virtuous character, and descent from the ancient and pure Ikshvaku line.

summary: No summary available
content: King Janaka, addressing Sage Vasistha, began to recount his lineage. He explained the importance of sharing one's ancestry, especially when giving a daughter in marriage.  He then detailed his family history, starting with King Nimi, renowned for his piety and deeds. The lineage continued through Mithi, the first king named Janaka, and subsequent rulers like Udavasu, Nandivardhana, Suketu, Devarata, Brhadratha, Mahavira, Sudhrti, Dhrstaketu, Haryaswa, Maru, Pratindhaka, and Kirtiratha.  The line continued through Devamidha, Vibudha, Mahidhraka, another Kirtirata, Maharoma, Swarnaroma, and Hraswaroma.  Janaka concluded by stating that Hraswaroma had two sons: himself, the elder, and his valiant brother, KuSadhwaja.  Their father, upon crowning Janaka king, entrusted KuSadhwaja to his care before retiring to the forest.  After their father's passing, Janaka diligently ruled the kingdom while affectionately caring for his brother.

summary: No summary available
content: King Janaka continued his narration to Sage Vasistha. He told of his father, Hraswaroma, born to Swarnaroma, who in turn was the son of Maharoma.  Janaka explained that he was the elder of two sons, his younger brother being the valiant KuSadhwaja.  Upon their father's retirement, Janaka inherited the throne and became KuSadhwaja's guardian. After their father's passing, Janaka ruled righteously while caring for his brother.  Sometime later, King Sudhanva of Sankasya besieged Mithila, demanding Sita's hand in marriage and Lord Shiva's bow. Janaka refused, leading to a battle where he defeated and killed Sudhanva. Following this, Janaka installed his brother KuSadhwaja as the ruler of Sankasya.  Finally, Janaka joyfully offered his daughters Sita and Urmila to Rama and Lakshmana respectively. He then advised King Dasaratha to conduct the necessary ceremonies for the weddings, including the *Samavartana and Nandisraddha rituals, suggesting the auspicious day of Uttara Phalguni for the marriage itself.

summary: No summary available
content: King Janaka, addressing King Dasaratha, declared that the current day was auspicious due to the ascendance of the Magha constellation.  He advised Dasaratha to conduct the wedding ceremony three days later when the Uttara Phalguni constellation would be ascendant.  Janaka further recommended that gifts be presented to Rama and Lakshmana for their well-being and future happiness.

summary:
Canto LXXII

Vasistha and ViSwamitra jointly ask for the hand of the two daughters
of Kusgadhwaja in favour of Bharata and Satrughna and Janaka
acquiesces in the proposal. Thereupon DaSaratha gets his
sons to perform the rite of Samavartana and himself
performs the Nandisraddha
content: Sages Viśvāmitra and Vasiṣṭha addressed King Janaka, praising the lineages of both the Ikṣvāku and Videha families.  They proposed a marriage alliance, deeming Sītā and Ūrmilā suitable matches for Rāma and Lakṣmaṇa, respectively.  Further, they suggested that King Kusadhvaja's daughters be wed to Bharata and Śatrughna.  They extolled the virtues and prowess of Daśaratha's four sons.  King Janaka, honored by the proposal, readily agreed, suggesting the second day of Phālguni as an auspicious date for the weddings. He humbly offered seats to the sages, acknowledging his servitude to them.

summary:
Canto LXXII

Vasistha and ViSwamitra jointly ask for the hand of the two daughters
of Kusgadhwaja in favour of Bharata and Satrughna and Janaka
acquiesces in the proposal. Thereupon DaSaratha gets his
sons to perform the rite of Samavartana and himself
performs the Nandisraddha
content: King Daśaratha finalized the marriage arrangements for his four sons.  The sages suggested the second day of Phālguni as an auspicious wedding date. King Janaka humbly accepted the proposal, expressing his deference to the sages and offering them honored seats.  He affirmed that Mithilā was as much Daśaratha's as Ayodhyā was his own.  Daśaratha graciously acknowledged Janaka's hospitality, mentioning the many honored guests the king had received.  He then announced his departure to perform the necessary Śrāddha rites. After taking leave of Janaka, Daśaratha returned to his lodging with the sages. The following morning, he performed the Samāvartana ceremony for his sons, which involved shaving their heads and armpits and gifting cows.  As part of this ceremony, the generous king donated a hundred thousand cows per son to the Brahmins, totaling four hundred thousand cows, along with other riches.  Surrounded by his sons, Daśaratha radiated like Brahmha amidst the guardians of the universe.

summary:
Canto LXXII

Vasistha and ViSwamitra jointly ask for the hand of the two daughters
of Kusgadhwaja in favour of Bharata and Satrughna and Janaka
acquiesces in the proposal. Thereupon DaSaratha gets his
sons to perform the rite of Samavartana and himself
performs the Nandisraddha
content: King Daśaratha, deeply devoted to his sons, generously gifted four hundred thousand recently calved cows to the Brahmins as part of the Samāvartana ceremony. These cows, adorned with gold-plated horns and accompanied by bell-metal milking vessels, were known for their abundant milk yield.  He also bestowed a vast amount of additional wealth upon the Brahmins.  Surrounded by his sons, who had completed the  ceremony, the radiant king resembled the benevolent creator god Brahmā amidst the celestial guardians.

summary: No summary available
content:
Canto LXXIII: The Wedding Preparations

As King Dasaratha performed the grand *Samavartana* ceremony, Yudhajit, the king of Kekeya and Bharata's maternal uncle, arrived in Mithila. After exchanging pleasantries, Yudhajit conveyed his father's well wishes and explained his own journey.  He had initially travelled to Ayodhya intending to see Bharata, but upon learning of the wedding party's departure for Mithila, he hastened there to witness the event.  King Dasaratha warmly welcomed his guest. The following morning, after completing his rituals, Dasaratha led the sages to the wedding pavilion.  Rama, adorned in festive attire and accompanied by his brothers, also proceeded to the pavilion at the auspicious hour of *Vijaya*.  Vasistha then addressed Janaka, informing him of Dasaratha's presence with his sons and requesting him to proceed with the wedding ceremony, highlighting the importance of both the giver and receiver in completing the sacred rite.  Janaka graciously responded, expressing his shared ownership of the kingdom with Dasaratha and welcoming him without reservation. He informed Vasistha that his daughters, radiant as flames, were already waiting at the altar.

summary: No summary available
content: King Dasaratha, accompanied by his festively dressed sons, awaited Janaka's permission to proceed with the wedding ceremony.  Vasistha emphasized the importance of both the giver (Janaka) and the receiver (Dasaratha) in fulfilling the sacred rite.  Janaka welcomed them, stating that the kingdom belonged as much to Dasaratha as to himself and that his daughters were already waiting at the altar.  Upon Janaka's invitation, Dasaratha and the sages entered the wedding pavilion. Janaka then requested Vasistha to conduct the ceremony. Vasistha, along with other sages including Viswamitra and Satananda, prepared the altar, decorating it with flowers and setting out various ritualistic items like golden plates, colorful jars, incense vessels, and containers filled with sprouts, parched grains, and turmeric-dyed rice.  After the altar was prepared and the sacred fire lit, Vasistha began the ceremony by offering oblations.  Sita, adorned in jewels, was then brought before the fire, opposite Rama.  Janaka presented Sita to Rama, declaring that she would be his faithful partner in fulfilling his duties, and instructed Rama to take her hand in marriage.  He assured Rama of Sita's unwavering devotion, comparing her to a shadow that would always follow him.

summary: No summary available
content: King Janaka requested Vasistha to conduct the wedding ceremony of Rama. Vasistha, along with other sages, prepared the altar with flowers, golden plates, colorful jars, incense, and various ritualistic items.  He lit the sacred fire and offered oblations.  Sita, adorned in jewels, was brought before the fire, facing Rama. Janaka formally gave Sita to Rama, declaring her his faithful partner and instructing Rama to take her hand in marriage. He assured Rama of Sita's unwavering devotion.  After Janaka transferred consecrated water to Rama, signifying the giving away of Sita, celestial music played and flowers rained down.  Janaka then offered Urmila to Lakshmana, Mandavi to Bharata, and Shrutakirti to Shatrughna.  The four princes took the hands of their respective brides, circled the fire, altar, King Janaka, and the sages, completing the wedding ceremony according to tradition.  Celestial nymphs danced and Gandharvas sang as the princes, with their brides, circled the sacred fire three times, finalizing their marriages.

summary: No summary available
content: The four princes, obedient to King Janaka's words and following Vasistha's guidance, took the hands of their respective brides.  They circumambulated the sacred fire, the altar, King Janaka, and the assembled sages, completing the traditional wedding rites.  A magnificent shower of blossoms fell from the sky, accompanied by the resounding beat of celestial drums and the harmonious melodies of vocal and instrumental music.  Celestial nymphs danced gracefully, and Gandharvas sang sweetly, creating a wondrous spectacle at the wedding of these descendants of Raghu.  Amidst this celestial symphony, the princes, radiant with joy, circled the sacred fire three times with their brides, finalizing their marriages.  Afterwards, the newlywed princes and their brides retired to their quarters, followed by King Janaka, the sages, and their relatives, all filled with happiness.

summary:
Canto LXXIV

Taking leave of Janaka and DaSaratha, Viswamitra returns to his own
hermitage and, accepting large wedding presents, DaSaratha too
turns back to Ayodhya alongwith his sons and their newly-
wedded brides. On the way the irascible and redoubtable
Parasurama, a sworn enemy of the Ksatriyas, suddenly
appears before them, axe in hand, and Vasistha
and the other sages accompanying the
party offer worship to him
content: After the wedding ceremonies, Sage Viswamitra departed for the northern mountains. King Dasaratha, after bidding farewell to King Janaka, began his journey back to his capital, Ayodhya.  King Janaka presented a lavish dowry, including riches, textiles, animals, and servants. As Dasaratha journeyed homeward with his sons and their brides, unsettling omens appeared. Fearful birds cried out, while deer crossed their path from the left.  Concerned, Dasaratha questioned Sage Vasistha about these signs. Vasistha explained the bird cries signified imminent danger, while the deer offered some reassurance. As they discussed this, a powerful storm suddenly arose, shaking the earth, uprooting trees, and darkening the sky.

summary:
Canto LXXIV

Taking leave of Janaka and DaSaratha, Viswamitra returns to his own
hermitage and, accepting large wedding presents, DaSaratha too
turns back to Ayodhya alongwith his sons and their newly-
wedded brides. On the way the irascible and redoubtable
Parasurama, a sworn enemy of the Ksatriyas, suddenly
appears before them, axe in hand, and Vasistha
and the other sages accompanying the
party offer worship to him
content: As Dasaratha and his entourage journeyed towards Ayodhya, unsettling omens arose, causing the king to seek Vasistha's counsel.  Vasistha interpreted the ominous bird cries as a sign of impending danger, while the presence of deer offered a glimmer of hope.  Suddenly, a violent storm erupted, plunging the world into darkness and chaos. Amidst this turmoil, a formidable figure emerged, radiating an intense aura. This was Parasurama, the legendary warrior-sage, recognizable by his matted locks and the axe resting on his shoulder. He appeared as imposing as Mount Kailasa and as fierce as the fire of cosmic destruction.  Recognizing the potential threat, Vasistha and the accompanying sages gathered, fearing Parasurama's wrath and a potential repeat of his past actions against the Kshatriya caste.  However, they hoped his previous purge had quenched his anger and grief over his father's death.  To placate the imposing figure, the sages approached Parasurama with offerings of respect, addressing him with reverence.

summary:
Canto LXXIV

Taking leave of Janaka and DaSaratha, Viswamitra returns to his own
hermitage and, accepting large wedding presents, DaSaratha too
turns back to Ayodhya alongwith his sons and their newly-
wedded brides. On the way the irascible and redoubtable
Parasurama, a sworn enemy of the Ksatriyas, suddenly
appears before them, axe in hand, and Vasistha
and the other sages accompanying the
party offer worship to him
content: Parasurama, the son of Jamadagni, accepted the respects offered by Vasistha and the other sages.  He then addressed Rama, Dasaratha's eldest son.

summary:
Canto LXXV

Turning a deaf ear to DaSaratha’s prayer, ParaSurama relates the
history of the bows belonging to Lords Siva and Visnu
and challenges Sri Rama to string the bow of
Lord Visnu in his possession
content: Parasurama, addressing Rama, recounts Rama's impressive feat of breaking Shiva's bow. He reveals he has brought another sacred bow, inherited from his father Jamadagni, and challenges Rama to string it as a display of strength.  Upon witnessing Rama stringing the bow, Parasurama promises a duel to further test Rama's valor.  King Dasaratha, distressed, pleads with Parasurama to spare his young sons. He reminds Parasurama of his ascetic life and past renunciation of violence after giving up his anger towards Kshatriyas. Dasaratha recounts how Parasurama relinquished his weapons after giving his word to Indra and gifted the earth to Sage Kasyapa before retiring to Mount Mahendra. He emphasizes that their entire family's lives depend on Rama's safety.  Ignoring Dasaratha's pleas, Parasurama continues to address Rama, describing the two divine bows revered by the world, crafted by Vishwakarma.  He explains that Rama broke the first bow, originally given to Shiva for his battle against Tripura. The second bow, wielded by Parasurama, belonged to Vishnu.  Parasurama details how he inherited this powerful bow and, after conquering the earth and giving it to Kasyapa, retreated to Mount Mahendra. Hearing of the broken bow, he returned, driven by his accumulated spiritual energy.  Finally, invoking the Kshatriya duty of accepting a righteous duel, he urges Rama to take up Vishnu's bow.

summary:
Canto LXXV

Turning a deaf ear to DaSaratha’s prayer, ParaSurama relates the
history of the bows belonging to Lords Siva and Visnu
and challenges Sri Rama to string the bow of
Lord Visnu in his possession
content: Parasurama continues his narrative, revealing the origin of the bow he holds. He explains that Rama broke Shiva's bow, originally given to him by the gods for his battle against the demon Tripura.  The bow Parasurama now presents was Vishnu's, bestowed upon him by the gods.  He relates a tale of a conflict between Shiva and Vishnu, instigated by Brahma to test their might. During the battle, Vishnu's roar rendered Shiva's bow useless and Shiva himself immobile. The gods intervened, requesting peace. Witnessing Vishnu's power, they deemed him superior, angering Shiva, who then gave his bow to King Devarata. Vishnu entrusted his bow to Rcika, who passed it down to Jamadagni, Parasurama's father.  Jamadagni, a peaceful sage, had no need for such a weapon. King Arjuna, mistaking Jamadagni's gentle nature for weakness, killed him.  Enraged by this act, Parasurama repeatedly exterminated the Kshatriya caste in revenge.

summary:
Canto LXXV

Turning a deaf ear to DaSaratha’s prayer, ParaSurama relates the
history of the bows belonging to Lords Siva and Visnu
and challenges Sri Rama to string the bow of
Lord Visnu in his possession
content: Parasurama explains that the bow he now possesses, originally Vishnu's, rivals Shiva's bow in power.  He recounts a battle between Shiva and Vishnu where Vishnu's roar disabled Shiva's bow and paralyzed him. The gods intervened, requesting peace. Impressed by Vishnu's might, they declared him superior, angering Shiva who then gave his bow to King Devarata. Vishnu gave his bow to Rcika, who passed it down to Jamadagni, Parasurama's father. King Arjuna, misjudging Jamadagni's peaceful nature, killed him.  Enraged, Parasurama repeatedly annihilated the Kshatriya caste in revenge. After conquering the earth, he gave it to Kasyapa as a sacrificial offering.  Upon hearing of Shiva's bow being broken, he returned. He now challenges Rama to string Vishnu's bow, urging him to uphold the Kshatriya duty of accepting a righteous duel.

summary:
Canto LXXVI

Fitting the arrow to the bow of Lord Visnu and declaring it as unfailing,
Sri Rama asks Parasurama to point out at whom it may be
discharged, and at the instance of the latter puts an
end to his title to the (ethereal) worlds earned
by him through his austerities. Recognizing
Sri Rama to be no other than Lord Visnu
and taking leave of him, ParaSurama
withdraws to Mount Mahendra
in order to resume
his austerities
content: Rama, no longer constrained by his father's presence, addressed Parasurama.  He acknowledged Parasurama's actions and the fulfillment of his filial duty.  However, he challenged Parasurama's perceived underestimation of him, inviting him to witness his true power.  Angered, Rama seized Parasurama's bow and arrow, stringing the bow and preparing to fire. He declared his respect for Parasurama as both a Brahmin and a kinsman of Vishwamitra, explaining his reluctance to harm him.  Instead, Rama offered an alternative – to strip Parasurama of his power or his claim to the celestial realms. He emphasized the infallibility of Vishnu's bow.  The gods, rishis, and various celestial beings gathered to observe this extraordinary confrontation. The onlookers were stunned by Rama's display of power, and a now powerless Parasurama gazed at him in wonder.  Disoriented by Rama's brilliance and his own diminished state, Parasurama quietly explained his exile from earth at night, a consequence of a past agreement with Sage Kashyapa.  He requested Rama to spare his ability to move freely and declared his intention to retreat to Mount Mahendra.

summary:
Canto LXXVI

Fitting the arrow to the bow of Lord Visnu and declaring it as unfailing,
Sri Rama asks Parasurama to point out at whom it may be
discharged, and at the instance of the latter puts an
end to his title to the (ethereal) worlds earned
by him through his austerities. Recognizing
Sri Rama to be no other than Lord Visnu
and taking leave of him, ParaSurama
withdraws to Mount Mahendra
in order to resume
his austerities
content: Parasurama, humbled and drained of power, softly addressed Rama.  He explained his nocturnal exile from Earth, a consequence of a past agreement with Sage Kashyapa. He requested Rama not to impede his movement and declared his intent to retreat to Mount Mahendra.  He urged Rama to use Vishnu's bow to destroy the celestial realms he had earned through his austerities, emphasizing the infallibility of the weapon and the urgency of the act. Parasurama recognized Rama as the imperishable Lord Vishnu, observing the ease with which Rama wielded Vishnu's bow. He noted the assembled gods witnessing the event.  He stated his lack of shame in his defeat, as it was at the hands of the Lord of the three worlds. He again urged Rama to release the arrow, after which he would depart for Mount Mahendra.  As he spoke, Rama released the arrow, destroying Parasurama's celestial rewards. Parasurama immediately departed for Mount Mahendra. The world was filled with light as the gods and rishis praised Rama.  Parasurama, after circumambulating Rama as a mark of respect, finally departed for his abode on Mount Mahendra.

summary:
Canto LXXVI

Fitting the arrow to the bow of Lord Visnu and declaring it as unfailing,
Sri Rama asks Parasurama to point out at whom it may be
discharged, and at the instance of the latter puts an
end to his title to the (ethereal) worlds earned
by him through his austerities. Recognizing
Sri Rama to be no other than Lord Visnu
and taking leave of him, ParaSurama
withdraws to Mount Mahendra
in order to resume
his austerities
content: Parasurama, after being honored by Rama, circumambulated him respectfully and then departed for his own dwelling on Mount Mahendra.

summary:
Canto LXXVII

On the departure of Paragurama, Sri Rama hands over the bow of Lord Visnu
to Varuna (the god of water) and, sending his army ahead, King Dasaratha
enters Ayodhya. After some days Prince Yudhajit, Bharata’s maternal
uncle takes away Bharata and Satrughna to his father’s capital
content: After Parasurama's departure, Rama, now calm, entrusted Viṣṇu's bow to Varuna, the god of water.  He then respectfully addressed the sages, led by Vasiṣṭha, and informed his father, Dasaratha, who was unaware of Parasurama's peaceful exit, that Parasurama had left and suggested the army return to Ayodhya.  Dasaratha, overjoyed at this news, embraced Rama. The king then led the army back to the festive capital city of Ayodhya, which was adorned with flags, filled with music, and citizens rejoicing at their king's return. Dasaratha entered his grand palace.  The queens, including Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi, prepared to welcome their daughters-in-law.  Sita, Urmila, Mandavi, and Śrutakīrti were led into the palace, where they performed appropriate rituals and ceremonies.  The princesses, dressed in fine silks, enjoyed the company of their husbands. The princes, skilled in weaponry and surrounded by friends and family, served their father.  Sometime later, Dasaratha addressed Bharata.

summary:
Canto LXXVII

On the departure of Paragurama, Sri Rama hands over the bow of Lord Visnu
to Varuna (the god of water) and, sending his army ahead, King Dasaratha
enters Ayodhya. After some days Prince Yudhajit, Bharata’s maternal
uncle takes away Bharata and Satrughna to his father’s capital
content: King Dasaratha, joyfully reunited with his family, was lavishly celebrated by his people.  Queens Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi oversaw the welcoming ceremonies for their daughters-in-law.  Sita, Urmila, Mandavi, and rutakīrti were escorted to the women's quarters, where they participated in rituals and temple worship.  Adorned in silk robes, they received blessings.  The princesses then happily settled into their married life. The princes, adept at weaponry and surrounded by loved ones, dutifully served their father.  Some time later, King Dasaratha informed Bharata of his maternal uncle Yudhajit’s arrival, who was waiting to take Bharata and Shatrughna back to his kingdom.

summary:
Canto LXXVII

On the departure of Paragurama, Sri Rama hands over the bow of Lord Visnu
to Varuna (the god of water) and, sending his army ahead, King Dasaratha
enters Ayodhya. After some days Prince Yudhajit, Bharata’s maternal
uncle takes away Bharata and Satrughna to his father’s capital
content: After the departure of Parashurama, Dasaratha returned to Ayodhya. Bharata’s maternal uncle, Yudhajit, arrived to take Bharata and Shatrughna back to his kingdom.  After bidding farewell to their father, Rama, and their mothers, Bharata and Shatrughna departed with Yudhajit. Rama and Lakshmana dutifully served their father, and Rama diligently attended to the needs of the citizens and his mothers.  He earned the respect and admiration of everyone in Ayodhya for his noble character and conduct. Rama, renowned for his valor, spent many happy months with his beloved wife, Sita, daughter of Janaka. Their love and admiration for each other deepened over time. Sita, beautiful as a goddess, understood Rama's innermost thoughts and feelings.  Their joyful union was likened to that of Vishnu and Lakshmi.

summary:
(Kiskindhakanda)

Canto I

When Sri Rama approached the Pampa lake, the sight of the lovely
surroundings intensified his pangs of separation from Sita. Put in
mind of Sita, he laments for her. Living with his ministers
on the summit of the Rsyamika hill, Sugriva catches
sight of the two princes and gets alarmed
content: Rama and Lakshmana, arriving at the Pampa Lake, found it teeming with lotuses, lilies, and fish.  The beauty of the lake initially excited Rama, but his joy quickly turned to sorrow as he was reminded of Sita. He lamented to Lakshmana, describing the lake's charm: its clear water, the abundance of flowers, and the surrounding trees. However, his grief over Sita's abduction and Bharata's plight overshadowed his appreciation for the scenery.  Despite his sorrow, Rama continued to describe the lake's beauty, noting the colorful woodland, cool water, and the abundance of flowers. He pointed out the blue and yellow turf, covered with fallen blossoms, and the treetops laden with flowers and entwined with climbers.  Rama described the pleasant breezes of the month of Caitra, a time of blossoming trees and ripening fruit. He likened the falling flowers to rain and observed how the wind playfully scattered blossoms from the trees onto the rocks below. The wind, shaking the flower-laden branches, caused the bees to buzz around, their song echoing through the air.  Rama concluded by describing the wind emerging from mountain caves, accompanied by the sweet songs of cuckoos, creating a scene of vibrant natural beauty.

summary:
(Kiskindhakanda)

Canto I

When Sri Rama approached the Pampa lake, the sight of the lovely
surroundings intensified his pangs of separation from Sita. Put in
mind of Sita, he laments for her. Living with his ministers
on the summit of the Rsyamika hill, Sugriva catches
sight of the two princes and gets alarmed
content: The wind, strong and swift, scatters blossoms from the trees onto the flat rocks below.  It plays with the falling flowers, creating a vibrant display.  Disturbing the bees from their places on the branches, the wind fills the air with their buzzing.  Emerging from the mountain caves, the wind seems to sing, accompanied by the sweet calls of cuckoos.  It weaves through the trees, making their branches intertwine as if they were strung together.  The cool, sandalwood-scented breeze brings a sense of relief. The trees, shaken by the wind, seem to sing along with the humming of the bees in the honey-fragrant groves. The mountains, adorned with flowering trees, appear especially beautiful. The wind-tossed trees, covered in blossoms and buzzing with bees, seem to be singing. The Karnikara trees, full of flowers, resemble people dressed in yellow and adorned with gold.  However, for Rama, this beautiful spring season only intensifies his grief for Sita.  The sounds of the birds deepen his sorrow.  Even the usually cheerful cuckoo's song now seems to mock his pain. The call of a water-fowl near a waterfall, once a source of shared joy with Sita, now brings him further anguish, reminding him of her absence.  He recalls how Sita used to delight in the bird's call.  He observes the birds settling on the trees, bushes, and climbers, their various calls filling the air, and notes the joyful sounds of the birds and bees as they interact with their mates.

summary:
(Kiskindhakanda)

Canto I

When Sri Rama approached the Pampa lake, the sight of the lovely
surroundings intensified his pangs of separation from Sita. Put in
mind of Sita, he laments for her. Living with his ministers
on the summit of the Rsyamika hill, Sugriva catches
sight of the two princes and gets alarmed
content: Rama’s grief for Sita is intensified by the vibrant beauty of the spring season around Pampa lake.  He laments to Lakshmana that even the joyous call of a water-fowl, which once delighted Sita, now fills him with sorrow.  He observes the birds settling around them and notes their various calls, particularly the sweet songs of the female birds and bees rejoicing with their mates. This reminds him of his own loss and separation from Sita. The blossoming trees, buzzing bees, and singing birds, which normally would bring joy, now only serve to heighten his pain.  He feels consumed by grief, comparing it to a fire fueled by the vernal season. The sight of the beautiful landscape and the sounds of nature only amplify his longing for Sita. He worries that Sita, wherever she may be, is also suffering, pining for him as he is for her.  He fears that if she is experiencing the same spring, surrounded by its beauty, it will only deepen her despair and that she may not be able to bear the anguish of their separation.

summary:
(Kiskindhakanda)

Canto I

When Sri Rama approached the Pampa lake, the sight of the lovely
surroundings intensified his pangs of separation from Sita. Put in
mind of Sita, he laments for her. Living with his ministers
on the summit of the Rsyamika hill, Sugriva catches
sight of the two princes and gets alarmed
content: Rama continues to lament Sita's absence, finding the beauty of the Pampa lake and the surrounding forest only intensifies his sorrow. The joyful sounds of birds and the vibrant colors of spring serve as a painful reminder of his lost love. He reflects on the various sights and sounds around him: the singing birds, buzzing bees, and blossoming trees, all of which heighten his despair.  He imagines Sita, wherever she may be, suffering the same pangs of separation. Rama observes peacocks dancing with their mates and notes how even these creatures experience love. He envies their togetherness while he remains alone, separated from his beloved Sita. He finds little solace in the fragrant breeze and the beautiful lotuses, as they only amplify his longing for her.  Even the presence of Kinnaras, demigods known for their beauty and joy, does nothing to alleviate his grief. He remarks to Lakshmana on the irony of the Asoka tree, a symbol of love, now seeming to mock his sorrow.  The natural world, usually a source of joy and peace, has become a constant reminder of his loss.

summary:
(Kiskindhakanda)

Canto I

When Sri Rama approached the Pampa lake, the sight of the lovely
surroundings intensified his pangs of separation from Sita. Put in
mind of Sita, he laments for her. Living with his ministers
on the summit of the Rsyamika hill, Sugriva catches

content: Rama's grief over Sita's absence deepens amidst the beauty of spring. He questions how Sita could endure the season without him, imagining her succumbing to despair. His affection for her remains unwavering, but the fragrant breeze, once a source of shared joy, now fuels his sorrow.  He interprets the crow's changed song as a positive omen, hoping it signifies Sita's return. The vibrant forest, with its blooming trees and buzzing bees, only intensifies his longing. He notes the irony of the Asoka tree, a symbol of love, now seemingly mocking his pain.  Even the beauty of the Pampa lake, with its lotuses and surrounding wildlife, fails to comfort him. He compares the lotus petals to Sita's eyes, further emphasizing his loss.  He acknowledges Cupid's cruelty in reminding him of Sita's captivating words now that she is gone. The arrival of spring, with its blossoming trees, exacerbates his grief, turning once-delightful experiences into painful reminders of her absence.  He points out to Lakshmana the beauty of the Karnikara tree and the colorful dust rising from Mount Rsyamuka, though these sights offer him no solace.

summary:
(Kiskindhakanda)

Canto I

When Sri Rama approached the Pampa lake, the sight of the lovely
surroundings intensified his pangs of separation from Sita. Put in
mind of Sita, he laments for her. Living with his ministers
on the summit of the Rsyamika hill, Sugriva catches
