content: Rama reassured Sugriva, emphasizing the gravity of breaking his promise of protection. He urged Sugriva not to worry and to challenge Vali again, promising to kill Vali within the hour. To avoid any confusion, Rama instructed Lakshmana to tie a blossoming Gajapuspi creeper around Sugriva's neck as a means of identification.  Lakshmana followed the instruction, and Sugriva, adorned with the creeper, proceeded with Rama back to Kiskindha for the rematch.

summary: No summary available
content: As Rama, Lakshmana, Sugriva, and their monkey troop journeyed towards Kiskindha, they passed a vibrant landscape.  Towering trees laden with blossoms, serene rivers flowing towards the sea, and caves nestled within the cliffs and mountain peaks caught their attention.  Ponds teeming with lotuses, frequented by various birds like ducks, cranes, swans, and Cakrawakas, added to the scenic beauty.  The group observed wild deer grazing peacefully, and solitary elephants, powerful and imposing, roaming the terrain.  Amidst this natural splendor, Rama pointed to a dense grove of trees, resembling a large cloud edged with plantain trees, and inquired of Sugriva about its nature, expressing his curiosity to learn more.

summary: No summary available
content: As Rama, Lakshmana, Sugriva, and the monkey army continued their journey, they observed the natural wonders around them. Rivers flowed serenely towards the ocean, their banks adorned with caves, cliffs, and towering peaks.  They passed shimmering ponds filled with lotuses and echoing with the calls of various birds like ducks, cranes, swans, and Cakrawakas. Wild deer grazed peacefully in the forest, while solitary elephants, mighty and imposing, roamed the land, their tusks gleaming white. The group then noticed a dense grove of trees, resembling a massive cloud fringed with plantain trees, and Rama, filled with curiosity, asked Sugriva about it.  Sugriva explained that it was a sacred hermitage, once home to seven sages known as the Saptajanas. These sages lived austere lives, performing rigorous penances and ultimately ascending to heaven after centuries of devotion. The hermitage, imbued with their spiritual energy, was now a protected place, difficult for even gods and demons to enter. Creatures that strayed inside, whether bird or beast, rarely returned.  The grove emanated an ethereal fragrance, filled with the sounds of ornaments, music, and chanting, and the smoke from the sages’ sacred fires could be seen curling around the treetops.  Sugriva urged Rama and Lakshmana to offer respectful salutations to the departed sages, assuring them that reverence for such holy figures would ward off any harm. Rama and Lakshmana then reverently bowed their heads, paying homage to the memory of the Saptajanas and the sacred power of their former home.

summary: No summary available
content: Continuing their journey, Rama, Lakshmana, and the monkey army encountered a grove of trees that appeared like a mountain range made of shimmering gemstones, their peaks wreathed in clouds and smoke from sacred fires.  Sugriva advised Rama and Lakshmana to pay their respects to the seven sages, the Saptajanas, who once resided there. He explained that showing reverence to these holy figures would protect them from harm.  Rama and Lakshmana then dutifully bowed their heads in honor of the sages.  After offering their respects, the group continued their travels, their spirits uplifted.  They eventually reached the formidable city of Kiskindha, ruled by Vali, a powerful being.  Preparing for a confrontation, Rama, Lakshmana, and the monkeys readied their weapons, their power and determination evident as they approached the city.

summary:
Canto XIV
Sri Rama and others remain hiding behind the trees of a grove in the vicinity
of Kiskindha, Sugriva implores Sri Rama to implement his vow of killing
Vali. Assuring Sugriva that this will be done, $1 Rama encourages
the latter to go and challenge his elder brother and
Sugriva does accordingly
content: Sugriva, filled with rage, roared, challenging Vali to a duel.  He reminded Rama of his promise to kill Vali, comparing the opportune moment to a fruiting creeper. Rama reassured Sugriva, pointing to the creeper Lakshmana tied around his neck as an identifying mark.  He vowed to kill Vali with a single arrow, just as he had pierced seven Sal trees, and told Sugriva to point out his brother when he appears.  Rama declared that if Vali escapes his arrow, Sugriva would be justified in blaming him.

summary:
Canto XIV
Sri Rama and others remain hiding behind the trees of a grove in the vicinity
of Kiskindha, Sugriva implores Sri Rama to implement his vow of killing
Vali. Assuring Sugriva that this will be done, $1 Rama encourages
the latter to go and challenge his elder brother and
Sugriva does accordingly
content: Rama, confident in his archery skills, urges Sugriva to challenge Vali.  He assures Sugriva that Vali will not escape his arrow, referencing his past feat of piercing seven Sal trees with a single shot.  He emphasizes his commitment to his promise, stating he has never spoken falsely and will not start now.  Rama instructs Sugriva to loudly challenge Vali, knowing his pride and history of victories will compel him to emerge. He compares his coming victory to Indra bringing rain to a field.  Sugriva, emboldened by Rama's words, unleashes a terrifying roar that frightens the animals in the vicinity, causing cows to scatter like distressed noblewomen, deer to flee like panicked warhorses, and birds to fall from the sky.

summary:
Canto XV

Angrily issuing forth for an encounter on hearing the roar of Sugriva,
Vali is discouraged with cogent reasons by his wife, Tara
content: Tara, Vali's wife, noticing his immediate rage at Sugriva's challenge, attempts to calm him.  She advises him to dismiss his anger like one discards a used garland.  She cautions against his impulsive reaction, suggesting a more measured response and advising him to confront Sugriva in the morning.  Tara expresses her suspicion of Sugriva's renewed challenge, especially given his previous defeat.  She believes Sugriva's confidence and defiant roars suggest he has found a powerful ally, someone whose strength has been thoroughly tested.  Tara promises to share important information she learned from Angada regarding this matter.

summary:
Canto XV

Angrily issuing forth for an encounter on hearing the roar of Sugriva,
Vali is discouraged with cogent reasons by his wife, Tara
content: Tara continued her impassioned plea to Vali, emphasizing Sugriva's unlikely solo challenge.  She reasoned that Sugriva's confident roars must indicate a powerful ally, someone whose strength is proven.  Tara revealed she possessed vital information learned from Angada:  During a forest excursion, Angada's spies reported the arrival of Rama and Lakshmana, sons of the emperor of Ayodhya, renowned warriors seeking to aid Sugriva.  Tara described Rama as a formidable warrior, a refuge for the virtuous, and a source of immense strength, akin to a raging fire.  She highlighted Rama's wisdom and devotion.  Tara urged Vali to reconsider his conflict with Sugriva, given Rama's alliance, advising him to seek peace and offer Sugriva the position of Prince Regent.  She emphasized the value of Sugriva's friendship and the importance of abandoning their animosity.  Tara believed that regardless of their location, Sugriva remained Vali's closest ally, unmatched by any other. She implored Vali to reconcile with Sugriva through gifts and respect, urging him to relinquish his anger and embrace his brother.

summary:
Canto XV

Angrily issuing forth for an encounter on hearing the roar of Sugriva,
Vali is discouraged with cogent reasons by his wife, Tara
content: Tara implored Vali to avoid conflict with Sugriva, his younger brother. She advised him to cultivate friendship with Rama and affection for Sugriva.  Dismissing any animosity, Tara emphasized Sugriva's worthiness of love and care.  She reminded Vali that Sugriva, regardless of his location, remained his closest ally, unparalleled in friendship.  Tara urged Vali to reconcile with Sugriva through gifts, honor, and attention, abandoning their animosity.  She stressed the importance of Sugriva's friendship for Vali's well-being and happiness.  Tara pleaded with Vali to heed her advice, given his affection for her, emphasizing the undesirability of conflict with Rama, whose power rivaled Indra's.  Despite Tara's sound and beneficial counsel, Vali, blinded by impending doom, disregarded her words.

summary:
Canto XVI
Spurning the advice of Tara and reproaching her, Vali goes forth to meet
Sugriva and there ensues a fierce encounter between the two brothers.
When, however, Sugriva is found losing ground, Vali is struck
with an arrow by Sri Rama and he falls to the ground
content: Vali rebuked Tara, dismissing her concerns and declaring his intent to confront his roaring brother, Sugriva. He believed tolerating such a challenge from a hostile sibling was worse than death, especially for a warrior like himself.  Vali was confident in his ability to defeat Sugriva, asserting that he could not tolerate Sugriva's arrogance and challenge. He also dismissed Tara's worries about Rama's intervention, claiming Rama would not harm him unjustly.  Vali urged Tara to return to the palace, emphasizing her past affection and service, while he proceeded to confront Sugriva and strip him of his pride. He assured her Sugriva would survive the encounter, intending only to subdue him with his fists and by wielding trees as weapons. Vali reiterated his determination to face Sugriva, brushing off Tara's concerns and highlighting her advice and love.  Tara, weeping, embraced Vali and performed a ritual for his well-being before returning to the palace, grief-stricken.  Following her departure, Vali left the city, enraged and seething like a serpent, his eyes scanning for Sugriva.  He finally spotted Sugriva, standing firm, radiating a fiery aura, ready for combat.

summary:
Canto XVI
Spurning the advice of Tara and reproaching her, Vali goes forth to meet
Sugriva and there ensues a fierce encounter between the two brothers.
When, however, Sugriva is found losing ground, Vali is struck
with an arrow by Sri Rama and he falls to the ground
content: After Tara's tearful departure, Vali, enraged, left Kishkindha, searching for Sugriva. He soon found him, poised for combat.  Vali tightened his loincloth, raised his fist, and advanced towards Sugriva, declaring his intent to deliver a fatal blow. Sugriva, equally defiant, challenged Vali, vowing to return the blow.  The fight began, with Vali striking Sugriva, causing him to vomit blood.  Sugriva retaliated by uprooting a sal tree and striking Vali, who staggered under the impact, like a heavily laden boat in a stormy sea.

summary:
Canto XVI
Spurning the advice of Tara and reproaching her, Vali goes forth to meet
Sugriva and there ensues a fierce encounter between the two brothers.
When, however, Sugriva is found losing ground, Vali is struck
with an arrow by Sri Rama and he falls to the ground
content: The fight between Vali and Sugriva intensified.  Like celestial bodies in the sky, they moved with incredible speed and power, seeking each other's weaknesses. Vali, fueled by rage, began to dominate the fight, pushing Sugriva back.  Sugriva, weakened and desperate, looked towards Rama for help. The battle raged on, a brutal exchange of blows using fists, feet, knees, trees, mountain peaks, and claws as sharp as diamonds. Bloodied and roaring, they fought like clashing storm clouds.  As Sugriva faltered, Rama, seeing his distress, prepared his arrow. The twang of his bow sent birds scattering and deer fleeing.  With the thunderous sound of a lightning strike, Rama’s arrow found its mark, piercing Vali’s chest.  Vali, the mighty warrior, crumpled to the ground.  He lay there lifeless, like a fallen Indra’s flag, his throat choked with tears.  The arrow, shining like fire, had brought down the powerful monkey king.

summary:
Canto XVI
Spurning the advice of Tara and reproaching her, Vali goes forth to meet
Sugriva and there ensues a fierce encounter between the two brothers.
When, however, Sugriva is found losing ground, Vali is struck
with an arrow by Sri Rama and he falls to the ground
content: Vali, struck by Rama's arrow, fell to the earth.  The mighty monkey king, once full of energy, now lay lifeless like a fallen banner. He was unconscious, his throat choked with tears. The arrow, radiant and deadly, resembled a weapon capable of ending an era.  Bathed in blood, Vali resembled a flowering Asoka tree torn down by a storm.  His fall mirrored the collapse of a flag dedicated to Indra, marking the end of his reign.

summary:
Canto XVII

Still retaining his life and vital energy by virtue of the gold chain bestowed
on him by Indra, though pierced in the breast with a deadly arrow,
Vali indignantly reproaches S1t Rama in many ways
content: Vali, struck by Rama's arrow, fell like a felled tree.  Adorned in gold, he resembled a flag of Indra cut loose and fallen to earth.  His fall dimmed the land he ruled, as if the moon had vanished from the sky.  Though fallen, Vali's life, strength, and spirit remained, preserved by Indra's golden chain.  The chain made him glow like a sunset-tinged cloud.  His necklace, body, and the embedded arrow shone brightly.  Rama's arrow, while guiding Vali to heaven, brought him ultimate peace.  Vali, son of Indra, adorned with gold and possessing a broad chest, long arms, and radiant face, lay fallen like a flameless fire, like King Yayati returned to earth, or like the sun fallen at the world's end. Rama and Lakshmana, with respect, approached the fallen Vali, who gazed intently at them.

summary:
Canto XVII

Still retaining his life and vital energy by virtue of the gold chain bestowed
on him by Indra, though pierced in the breast with a deadly arrow,
Vali indignantly reproaches S1t Rama in many ways
content: Vali, despite his grievous injury, remained alive and strong due to Indra's golden chain.  He resembled a dying fire, a fallen king, or a setting sun.  Rama and Lakshmana approached him respectfully. Vali, weak and wounded, addressed Rama with a mixture of pride and righteous indignation. He questioned Rama's honor, asking how he could claim merit for killing an opponent engaged in another battle. Vali acknowledged Rama's reputation for virtue, compassion, and strength, yet challenged the contradiction between Rama's supposed character and his act of treachery.  He listed the virtues expected of a king, implying Rama fell short. Vali emphasized his own adherence to these virtues and his honorable combat with Sugriva. He expressed disbelief that someone of Rama's lineage and apparent piety could commit such a deceitful act.  Vali highlighted his own innocence, his harmless forest-dwelling life, and the injustice of being killed while engaged with another combatant. He pointed to Rama's outward signs of piety, contrasting them with the cruelty of his action. Vali questioned how a knowledgeable Kshatriya, adorned with symbols of righteousness, could commit such a deed.

summary:
Canto XVII

Still retaining his life and vital energy by virtue of the gold chain bestowed
on him by Indra, though pierced in the breast with a deadly arrow,
Vali indignantly reproaches S1t Rama in many ways
content: Vali, clinging to life thanks to Indra's gift, continued his impassioned rebuke of Rama. He questioned Rama's justification for killing him, a harmless monkey who lived on fruits and roots, while engaged in combat with another. He highlighted the irony of Rama's virtuous appearance – a prince adorned with symbols of righteousness – committing such a treacherous act.  Vali emphasized the contradiction between Rama's reputation for piety and the cruelty of his action. He listed the expected virtues of a king – persuasion, generosity, forgiveness, righteousness, truthfulness, courage, and just punishment – implying Rama's failure to uphold them.  Vali pointed out the difference in their natures: a forest-dwelling monkey versus a human ruler, questioning what possible motive Rama could have had to interfere. He then listed actions considered sinful, including regicide, murder of a Brahmin or cow, theft, taking delight in killing, disbelief, improper marriage, informing, miserliness, betrayal of a friend, and violating a teacher's trust.  Finally, Vali noted the inedibility of monkey flesh for those who adhere to righteous practices, listing the few exceptions permitted for consumption by Brahmanas and Kshatriyas.

summary:
Canto XVII

Still retaining his life and vital energy by virtue of the gold chain bestowed
on him by Indra, though pierced in the breast with a deadly arrow,
Vali indignantly reproaches S1t Rama in many ways
content: Vali continued his condemnation of Rama, listing those destined for hell: kingslayers, Brahmin murderers, cow killers, thieves, those who revel in killing, unbelievers, and those who marry before their elder brothers.  He added informers, misers, those who kill their friends, and those who violate their teacher's wife to this list of sinners.  Vali pointed out the inedibility of his monkey flesh for those who follow righteous practices, noting that only certain five-clawed animals are permissible for consumption by Brahmanas and Kshatriyas. He emphasized that even his skin and bones are considered untouchable by the wise.  He lamented his death at Rama's hands, claiming he fell prey to it because he ignored Tara's wise counsel. He then accused Rama of leaving the earth unprotected, like a virtuous woman with a lawless husband. He questioned Rama's lineage, wondering how such a deceitful, malicious, and petty individual could be born from the noble Dasaratha.  Vali compared Rama to an elephant who has broken the chains of morality and disregarded righteousness.  He challenged Rama to justify his actions to holy men.  Vali criticized Rama's display of valor against a neutral party like himself while failing to show similar strength against true wrongdoers. He boasted that if Rama had faced him openly in battle, he would have met his death.  Finally, Vali lamented his death from a hidden position, likening it to a sleeping man bitten by a snake. He claimed that if Rama had asked, he would have retrieved Sita in a single day, solely to please Sugriva.  He even asserted he could have captured Ravana and delivered him to Rama without killing him, regardless of where Sita was imprisoned.

summary:
Canto XVII

Still retaining his life and vital energy by virtue of the gold chain bestowed
on him by Indra, though pierced in the breast with a deadly arrow,
Vali indignantly reproaches S1t Rama in many ways
content: Vali, feeling the effects of the fatal arrow, compared his unseen death to a sleeping man bitten by a snake.  He reiterated that he would have gladly retrieved Sita for Rama, even if she were hidden in the deepest ocean or underworld. He lamented that while Sugriva inheriting the kingdom was natural, his own death at Rama's hands was unjust, delivered while he was otherwise occupied. Accepting the inevitability of death, Vali nonetheless pressed Rama for a justification of his actions, seeking a satisfactory explanation for why he was targeted.  After this impassioned speech, his mouth dry and his body wracked with pain, Vali fell silent, his gaze fixed on Rama.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama, responding to Vali's accusations, defends his actions. He states that Vali's rebuke is unwarranted, given Vali's ignorance of righteousness, worldly matters, and proper conduct.  Rama asserts his authority, inherited from his ancestor Manu, to govern and dispense justice to all beings.  He highlights that his brother Bharata, a virtuous and just king, rules the earth and that Rama himself acts on Bharata's command to uphold dharma (righteousness).  Rama condemns Vali for abandoning virtue, succumbing to lust, and straying from the path of righteous kings.  He emphasizes the importance of respecting elders, teachers, and younger brothers as figures akin to fathers and sons, respectively, reminding Vali that morality dictates such conduct.  Finally, Rama notes that true dharma is subtle and known only to the Self, residing within all beings.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama continues to justify his slaying of Vali. He reiterates Vali's flawed nature, highlighting his enslavement to lust and his deviation from the righteous path of kings. Rama emphasizes the importance of respecting elders, teachers, and younger brothers as father figures and sons, respectively, contrasting this ideal with Vali's actions. He states that true dharma (righteousness) is subtle and known only to the inner self.  Rama questions Vali's understanding of dharma, given his association with impulsive monkeys.  He asserts that he is explaining the clear meaning of his actions, which Vali should not condemn. Rama plainly states the reason for killing Vali: Vali's transgression against dharma by taking his younger brother Sugriva's wife, Ruma, while Sugriva still lived.  He underscores that this action deserved punishment. Rama reaffirms his commitment to his pledge to Sugriva, made before other monkeys, to help him regain his wife and kingdom.  Rama states his belief that punishment is the only recourse for those who disregard public interest and established codes of conduct.  He adds that, as a Kshatriya (warrior), he cannot tolerate such a sin, emphasizing the prescribed punishment for such an act.  He explains that he and Lakshmana act under Bharata's authority, and Bharata's command is to punish those driven by lust and those who violate moral boundaries.  Rama explains that his alliance with Sugriva serves the purpose of recovering Sita, his own wife.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama continues his justification to Vali, explaining that Vali's transgression against dharma necessitated punishment.  He reiterates that Bharata, his brother and acting king, has commanded the punishment of those driven by lust and immorality. Rama emphasizes that his alliance with Sugriva serves his purpose of rescuing Sita. He reminds Vali of his public pledge to Sugriva to help him regain his wife and kingdom, a promise Rama feels bound to uphold. Rama insists that punishment is necessary for those who disregard public interest and established codes of conduct.  He cites scriptural authority, referencing Manu's teachings, to support his actions.  These teachings state that punishment cleanses sinners and that kings incur the sins of those they fail to punish. Rama draws a parallel between Vali's actions and a past sin punished by King Mandhata.  He notes that other rulers have also punished transgressions.  He concludes by asserting that his action was not driven by personal malice but by adherence to dharma, comparing his act to hunters legally killing deer. He assures Vali that he feels no remorse and urges Vali to understand his reasoning.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama continues his justifications to the dying Vali, explaining that even royal sages hunt. He declares that Vali, as a monkey, is fair game, much like deer hunted by men.  Rama asserts that kings, like himself, embody divine authority and can bestow blessings. Therefore, they should be respected, not reproached. He criticizes Vali for acting out of anger instead of understanding dharma.  Vali, now accepting Rama's reasoning, admits the truth in Rama's words.  He acknowledges his previous disrespectful outburst, attributing it to a mistake. He recognizes Rama's wisdom and commitment to dharma. Vali, now resigned to his fate, asks Rama for comforting words, acknowledging his own deviation from the path of virtue.  Overcome with grief, Vali laments not for himself or his wife, but for his young son, Angada, who will be left fatherless.

summary: No summary available
content: Vali, choked with tears, laments not for himself or his wife, but for his young son, Angada, who will be left fatherless and vulnerable. He implores Rama to protect and guide Angada and Sugriva, fostering a strong bond between them and offering the same care Rama shows his own brothers.  Vali urges Rama to ensure Sugriva treats Tara with respect, despite Vali's own transgressions against him.  Finally, Vali reiterates his acceptance of his fate, stating that he sought this death at Rama's hands and that kingdoms and even the world can be ruled by those who follow Rama's guidance.  He then falls silent.  Rama consoles the now enlightened Vali, assuring him he should not worry.  Rama explains that both the punisher and the punished fulfill their roles in accordance with dharma.  He states that Vali, cleansed of sin through this punishment, has returned to his true, righteous nature.  Rama advises Vali to release his grief, delusion, and fear, accepting the inevitable decree of fate.

summary: No summary available
content: Rama continues to console Vali, assuring him that his course of action was righteous and driven by affection for Vali.  He emphasizes that both the giver and receiver of punishment fulfill their dharma, and through this punishment, Vali has been cleansed of sin and returned to his true nature.  Rama urges Vali to relinquish his grief, confusion, and fear, accepting the unavoidable decree of fate.  He promises that Angada will be cared for by both Sugriva and himself.  Vali, calmed by Rama's comforting and righteous words, responds. He asks for Rama's forgiveness for any harsh words spoken while he was in pain and bewildered by the arrow.

summary:
Canto XIX
Agonized with grief to hear about the death of her husband, Tara, wife of
Vall, sallied forth from Kiskindha to arrive in the presence of Vali. Fleeing
helter-skelter through fear of Sri Rama and meeting Tara on the
way, the monkeys try to send her back inspiring fear in her and
advise her to make arrangements for the defence of the city and
install Angada on the throne. Rushing to the scene of
contest after dismissing them, Tara catches sight of Vali
lying on the ground, as well as of Sri Rama and others
content: Vali, wounded by Rama's arrow, was unable to respond.  Battered by trees and rocks, and pierced by the arrow, he fainted as his life ebbed away.  Tara, his wife, learned of Vali's death at Rama's hand during his fight with Sugriva.  Distressed, she and her son Angada left their home in the mountain cave.  Angada's monkey guards, despite their strength, panicked at the sight of Rama with his bow and fled. Tara saw them running in terror, like deer separated from their slain leader.  She approached the frightened monkeys and questioned their retreat, asking why they would abandon their king, Vali, especially if his death was caused by Sugriva's manipulation of Rama.  The monkeys replied that Rama, like Death himself, had felled Vali, explaining how Rama's arrows had countered Vali's attacks of trees and boulders.  They urged Tara to return, protect Angada, and have him installed as the new king, as the monkeys would surely follow Vali's son.  They warned that staying would be unwise, as enemy monkeys would soon occupy the city's fortifications.

summary:
Canto XIX
Agonized with grief to hear about the death of her husband, Tara, wife of
Vall, sallied forth from Kiskindha to arrive in the presence of Vali. Fleeing
helter-skelter through fear of Sri Rama and meeting Tara on the
way, the monkeys try to send her back inspiring fear in her and
advise her to make arrangements for the defence of the city and
install Angada on the throne. Rushing to the scene of
contest after dismissing them, Tara catches sight of Vali
lying on the ground, as well as of Sri Rama and others
content: The fleeing monkeys described Vali's death to Tara.  Rama's arrows, they explained, had overpowered Vali's hurled rocks and trees, striking him down like a lightning bolt. The monkey army, demoralized by the loss of their Indra-like leader, scattered in fear.  The monkeys urged Tara to return to the city, secure it, and install Angada as the new king. They warned of approaching hostile monkeys, covetous of power, and advised her to leave for her own safety.  Hearing this, Tara responded with grief, questioning the value of her son, kingdom, or even her own life now that Vali was gone. She declared her intention to go to her dying husband, felled by Rama's arrow. Crying out, she beat her head and chest in anguish.  As Tara rushed forward, she saw Vali lying on the ground, defeated like a fallen storm cloud.  She saw Rama and Lakshmana standing nearby, along with Sugriva.  Reaching her mortally wounded husband, Tara collapsed in grief, then rose and wept over him, seeing him bound by the grip of death.

summary:
Canto XIX
Agonized with grief to hear about the death of her husband, Tara, wife of
Vall, sallied forth from Kiskindha to arrive in the presence of Vali. Fleeing
helter-skelter through fear of Sri Rama and meeting Tara on the
way, the monkeys try to send her back inspiring fear in her and
advise her to make arrangements for the defence of the city and
install Angada on the throne. Rushing to the scene of
contest after dismissing them, Tara catches sight of Vali
lying on the ground, as well as of Sri Rama and others
content: Tara, still approaching the scene of battle, saw her fallen husband, Vali. He, the undefeated slayer of demons, now lay helpless.  He, who once hurled mountains like Indra's thunderbolts, who raged like a storm and thundered like massive clouds, now lay still, his Indra-like valor extinguished.  He resembled a spent storm cloud, a mighty lion felled by a tiger, a sacred shrine desecrated.  She also saw Rama and Lakshmana standing nearby, leaning on their bows, and Sugriva as well.  Reaching Vali’s side, Tara collapsed in grief, then rose and wept over her dying husband, seeing him in death's embrace.  She cried out, addressing him as “noble son.”  Seeing her anguish, and the arrival of Angada, Sugriva was overcome with sorrow.

summary: No summary available
content: Tara, overwhelmed with grief, clung to Vali's lifeless body.  She lamented his death, comparing his still form to a fallen mountain and uprooted tree.  She questioned why he, so powerful in battle, now lay silent.  Tara implored him to rise, reminding him of his status and the earth he once ruled.  She expressed her sorrow at his passing, now left alone and plunged into grief. Despite her heartbreak, Tara found strength in her unbroken spirit.  She acknowledged Vali's past actions – taking Sugriva's wife and exiling him – suggesting these deeds led to his demise.  She recalled her own disregarded counsel, offered for his benefit.  Finally, Tara contemplated Vali's fate, believing his spirit would captivate celestial nymphs.  She recognized time as the ultimate power, even over Vali, and questioned Sri Rama's lack of remorse for slaying him mid-battle.

summary: No summary available
content: Tara, consumed by grief, rebuked Vali for disregarding her advice, given for his own good. She envisioned his spirit, beautiful and youthful, captivating celestial nymphs.  She lamented that time, the ultimate conqueror, had brought Vali, once untouchable, under Sugriva's control.  Tara questioned Rama's lack of remorse for killing Vali mid-battle, deeming it a dishonorable act. She grieved her own future as a widow, lamenting a life now filled with sorrow.  Tara worried about her son, Angada, left vulnerable to Sugriva's anger.  She urged Angada to look upon his father, as this sight would soon be impossible.  Turning back to Vali, she pleaded with him to comfort their son and give his final message.  Tara acknowledged that Rama's act fulfilled his promise to Sugriva and secured his kingdom.  Addressing Sugriva, she urged him to find peace now that his enemy was gone and his wife returned.  Tara implored Vali to speak to her amidst her lamentations, gesturing towards his other wives. The wives, equally distraught, surrounded Angada, mourning Vali's departure and questioning his abandonment of his son. They lamented his untimely death, emphasizing Angada's virtues and handsome appearance.  Tara, in a final plea, begged Vali's forgiveness for any wrongdoing, offering to touch his feet in remorse.  Overwhelmed by grief, Tara, along with the other female Vanaras, resolved to mourn by Vali's side, abstaining from food and drink, awaiting death.

summary: No summary available
content: Tara, lamenting Vali's death, questioned his departure, leaving behind their son, Angada, who possessed similar virtues and a pleasing appearance.  She begged Vali's forgiveness for any past transgressions, offering to prostrate herself at his feet.  Overcome with grief, Tara, along with the other female Vanaras, resolved to mourn by Vali's side, refusing sustenance and awaiting death.

summary:
Canto XXI
Comforting Tara, who was lamenting through a variety of expressions,
Hanuman urges her to have the obsequies performed in respect of the
departed soul by Angada and attend to the earthly good of Angada.
Tara, however, refuses to flinch from her resolve and prefers
to follow her husband to the other world rather than
bestow any thought on the earthly welfare of her son
content: Hanuman consoled the grieving Tara, comparing her to a fallen star. He reminded her that everyone reaps the consequences of their actions, good or bad, and questioned the purpose of her grief, given the ephemeral nature of life.  He urged her to care for her son, Angada, and oversee the necessary rites for Vali.  Hanuman emphasized the unpredictable nature of life and death, advising her to focus on actions that benefit Vali in the afterlife rather than worldly displays of grief. He pointed out Vali's reliance on others and his just rule, suggesting that Vali had earned a place among the righteous.  Hanuman reassured Tara that she, along with Angada and all the monkeys, would protect the kingdom. He encouraged her to support Sugriva and Angada, proposing that Angada should rule. Finally, he advised her to perform the customary rites for Vali and witness Angada's coronation for her peace of mind.

summary:
Canto XXI
Comforting Tara, who was lamenting through a variety of expressions,
Hanuman urges her to have the obsequies performed in respect of the
departed soul by Angada and attend to the earthly good of Angada.
Tara, however, refuses to flinch from her resolve and prefers
to follow her husband to the other world rather than
bestow any thought on the earthly welfare of her son
content: Tara, overwhelmed by grief, rebuffs Hanuman's advice.  She declares that even a hundred sons like Angada cannot compare to the embrace of her deceased husband, Vali.  Tara acknowledges Sugriva's authority and her own lack of power concerning the kingdom or Angada's potential rule. She dismisses Hanuman's suggestion that she install Angada, asserting that a father, or in his absence an uncle (like Sugriva), is the rightful guide for a son, not a mother.  Finally, Tara expresses her unwavering desire to join Vali in death, finding solace only in sharing his final resting place.

summary:
Canto XXII
Craving the forgiveness of Sugriva, though he was younger than he, and
applauding the resolve of Tara (to follow her husband), nay, urging Sugriva
to follow the advice of Tara, look after Angada affectionately and
accomplish the purpose of Sri Rama, Vali asks Sugriva to take off
the gold chain from the former’s neck, and gives up the ghost
while tendering wholesome advice to Angada. Extolling the
wonderful exploits of Valt, hordes of monkeys lament in

many ways. Stricken with agony caused by separation

from Vali, Tara falls to the ground, embracing Vali
content: Vali, weakening, saw his brother Sugriva before him.  He addressed Sugriva, acknowledging Sugriva's victory and new position as leader. Vali urged Sugriva not to blame him for his past actions, explaining that he was driven by fate. He stated that they were not destined to share happiness and power simultaneously, which led to their conflict.  Vali declared that he was relinquishing his life, kingdom, and fortune. He implored Sugriva to fulfill his dying wish: to care for his son, Angada. Vali described Angada as deserving of comfort and though young, mature beyond his years, now grieving for his father. Vali requested Sugriva to protect and provide for Angada as his own son. He emphasized Angada's prowess and predicted his future success in battling demons. Vali also praised Tara's wisdom and urged Sugriva to heed her advice. Finally, he stressed the importance of fulfilling the promise made to Rama, warning of the consequences of failure.

summary:
Canto XXII
Craving the forgiveness of Sugriva, though he was younger than he, and
applauding the resolve of Tara (to follow her husband), nay, urging Sugriva
to follow the advice of Tara, look after Angada affectionately and
accomplish the purpose of Sri Rama, Vali asks Sugriva to take off
the gold chain from the former’s neck, and gives up the ghost
while tendering wholesome advice to Angada. Extolling the
wonderful exploits of Valt, hordes of monkeys lament in

many ways. Stricken with agony caused by separation

from Vali, Tara falls to the ground, embracing Vali
content: As Vali lay dying, he continued to advise Sugriva, emphasizing the importance of trusting Tara's judgment in all matters.  He highlighted her intelligence and ability to understand complex issues and predict future events based on various signs.  Vali then reminded Sugriva of his promise to Rama and warned of dire consequences if he failed to fulfill it.  He urged Sugriva to wear his golden chain, believing it held the goddess of victory.  Sugriva became sorrowful upon hearing Vali's final instructions. Accepting the chain and acknowledging Vali's words, Sugriva prepared to assume his new role.  Vali, turning to his son Angada, advised him to adapt to the changing circumstances, respect Sugriva's authority, and avoid extremes in his relationships with others.  Finally, overcome by the pain of the arrow, Vali passed away.  The monkeys, witnessing their leader's death, began to lament, mourning the loss of Vali and the now desolate Kiskindha.

summary:
Canto XXII
Craving the forgiveness of Sugriva, though he was younger than he, and
applauding the resolve of Tara (to follow her husband), nay, urging Sugriva
to follow the advice of Tara, look after Angada affectionately and
accomplish the purpose of Sri Rama, Vali asks Sugriva to take off
the gold chain from the former’s neck, and gives up the ghost
while tendering wholesome advice to Angada. Extolling the
wonderful exploits of Valt, hordes of monkeys lament in

many ways. Stricken with agony caused by separation

from Vali, Tara falls to the ground, embracing Vali
content: Vali, wracked with pain from the arrow, breathed his last.  His eyes rolled back, and his teeth were bared in a terrible grimace. The monkeys, witnessing their leader's death, cried out in mourning. They lamented the loss of Vali and the desolation that would now fall upon Kiskindha, its gardens, mountains, and forests.  They recalled Vali's strength and how he had protected them, keeping the woodlands lush and flowering.  They questioned how such a mighty warrior, who had battled the Gandharva Golabha for fifteen long years, could have fallen.  Like cows in a lion-infested forest who have lost their bull, the monkeys felt vulnerable and lost without their leader.  Tara, overcome with grief, collapsed to the ground, clinging to Vali's lifeless body as a vine clings to a fallen tree.

summary:
Canto XXIII
Though endeavouring, after smelling Vali’s face, to embrace him, Tara finds
herself unable to do so, bathed as he was in blood and daubed with
dust. Nila pulls out Sri Rama’s arrow from his heart and,
urged by Tara, Angada bows down at his feet
content:
Tara mourned Vali, her slain husband.  She lamented his uncomfortable resting place upon the hard ground, remarking that he seemed to embrace the earth rather than respond to her. She observed that fate, in the form of Rama's intervention, had empowered Sugriva. Tara further lamented that Vali, once a powerful leader to whom bears and monkeys flocked, now lay unresponsive to their cries and her own.  She recalled how Vali's enemies once lay defeated upon the same ground where he now rested, slain in battle. Tara grieved her widowhood, feeling abandoned and her happiness shattered.  Her grief was so profound, she felt like she was drowning in an ocean of sorrow, yet her heart remained unbroken. She questioned why her heart didn't shatter upon witnessing Vali's death, as he had been her friend, supporter, and beloved.

Tara described Vali's body lying in a pool of blood, his form obscured by dust, making it impossible for her to embrace him. She blamed Sugriva, whose fear of Rama had been dispelled by a single arrow, for Vali's death. Nila then removed Rama's arrow from Vali's body. As the arrow was withdrawn, it shone brightly like the setting sun. Blood flowed from Vali's wounds. Tara, wiping the battle dust from Vali, bathed him with her tears. She then turned to her son, Angada, and urged him to witness his father's tragic demise.  She lamented her absence from Vali's final moments, comparing his death to a sacrificial ritual where the blood from Rama's arrow served as the ceremonial water.

summary:
Canto XXIII
Though endeavouring, after smelling Vali’s face, to embrace him, Tara finds
herself unable to do so, bathed as he was in blood and daubed with
dust. Nila pulls out Sri Rama’s arrow from his heart and,
urged by Tara, Angada bows down at his feet
content: Tara, overwhelmed by grief, describes her heart as unbreakable despite witnessing her husband Vali's death. She laments her inability to embrace his dust-covered, blood-soaked body.  She blames Sugriva, whose fear of Rama led to this tragedy. Nila removes Rama's radiant arrow from Vali's heart, causing more blood to flow. Tara cleanses Vali with her tears and directs her son Angada's attention to his father's tragic state.  She tells Angada that Vali, once strong and honorable, has passed away and instructs him to pay his respects.  Angada obeys, embracing his father's feet. Tara mourns that Vali can no longer bless their son as he once did.  She compares herself and Angada to a cow and calf standing beside a slain bull.  Even in death, Vali's regal presence remains, like the setting sun's light upon a mountain.  Tara expresses her helplessness in preventing Vali's fate and laments the loss of her husband, her fortune, and her future.

summary:
Canto XXIII
Though endeavouring, after smelling Vali’s face, to embrace him, Tara finds
herself unable to do so, bathed as he was in blood and daubed with
dust. Nila pulls out Sri Rama’s arrow from his heart and,
urged by Tara, Angada bows down at his feet
content: Tara laments Vali's death, attributing it to past sins. She urges her son, Angada, to pay respects to his deceased father, whose body still radiates a sun-like brilliance.  Tara questions why Vali cannot bless Angada as he used to.  She compares their plight to a cow and calf beside a slain bull.  Tara then questions Vali's participation in a metaphorical "sacrifice" (the duel with Sugriva) without her, referencing the blood spilled by Rama's arrow.  She notices the absence of Vali's cherished gold chain, a gift from Indra. Despite death, Vali retains his regal aura, much like a mountain illuminated by the setting sun. Tara grieves her inability to prevent Vali's death and the resulting loss of her husband, her son's future, and their good fortune.

summary:
Canto XXIV
Beseeching Angada and others to conduct the search for Sita, Sugriva, whose
heart melted to hear the laments of Tara, seeks Srt Rama’s permission to
enter fire to atone for Vali’s death, making Sri Rama sad. Approaching
S1i Rama in her turn, Tara extols him with devotion and
expresses her intention to give up the ghost, on which
Sri Rama expostulates with her and urges her to
survive for the earthly good of Angada
content: Sugriva, overwhelmed with grief and guilt over Vali's death, approaches Rama.  He expresses remorse for his role in his brother's demise, despite having initially desired it due to Vali's past actions. Sugriva believes he does not deserve the kingship he has gained through this act. He laments that while Vali showed noble qualities, he himself acted out of anger and lust.  Sugriva compares his sin to that of Indra, and questions who could bear such a burden.  He declares that he is unworthy of honor, the position of prince regent, or the throne.

summary:
Canto XXIV
Beseeching Angada and others to conduct the search for Sita, Sugriva, whose
heart melted to hear the laments of Tara, seeks Srt Rama’s permission to
enter fire to atone for Vali’s death, making Sri Rama sad. Approaching
S1i Rama in her turn, Tara extols him with devotion and
expresses her intention to give up the ghost, on which
Sri Rama expostulates with her and urges her to
survive for the earthly good of Angada
content: Sugriva, consumed by guilt, continues to lament his role in Vali's death. He contrasts Vali's noble qualities with his own base motives of anger and lust. Sugriva believes his sin is immense, comparing it to Indra's slaying of ViSwarupa, and questions who could bear such a burden. He renounces any claim to honor, the regency, or the throne.  Overwhelmed by grief, Sugriva describes his sin as an all-consuming force, like a rampaging elephant. He worries that his virtue is diminishing, like dross purged from gold.  He fears for the future of the monkey kingdom, believing Angada may not survive his father’s death, and Tara, without her son, will also perish. Sugriva then declares his intention to enter fire, allowing the remaining monkey heroes to search for Sita. He asks Rama for permission to die, believing his death will ultimately serve Rama's purpose.  Overhearing Sugriva's despair, Rama is deeply saddened and moved to tears. He then notices Tara, Vali's wife, overwhelmed by grief and being consoled by Vali’s ministers.  She is forcibly separated from her husband's body and sees Rama, radiant and powerful, standing before her.

summary:
Canto XXIV
Beseeching Angada and others to conduct the search for Sita, Sugriva, whose
heart melted to hear the laments of Tara, seeks Srt Rama’s permission to
enter fire to atone for Vali’s death, making Sri Rama sad. Approaching
S1i Rama in her turn, Tara extols him with devotion and
expresses her intention to give up the ghost, on which
Sri Rama expostulates with her and urges her to
survive for the earthly good of Angada
content: Tara, Vali's wife, is overcome with grief.  She is forcibly separated from her deceased husband's body by Vali’s ministers and sees Rama. Recognizing him as the powerful warrior she had heard about from Angada, Tara approaches Rama.  She laments her loss, comparing Rama's pain at being separated from Sita to her own at being separated from Vali.  Tara implores Rama to kill her with the same arrow that felled Vali, so she can join her husband in the afterlife. She argues that Vali will not find happiness in heaven without her, and reminds Rama of the suffering one endures when separated from their beloved.  Tara even offers herself as a substitute for Vali, suggesting that if Rama is hesitant to kill a woman, he can consider her an extension of Vali himself. Finally, Tara appeals to Rama's sense of righteousness. She asserts that a wife is spiritually inseparable from her husband, and restoring her to Vali, even in death, would be a virtuous act.

summary:
Canto XXIV
Beseeching Angada and others to conduct the search for Sita, Sugriva, whose
heart melted to hear the laments of Tara, seeks Srt Rama’s permission to
enter fire to atone for Vali’s death, making Sri Rama sad. Approaching
S1i Rama in her turn, Tara extols him with devotion and
expresses her intention to give up the ghost, on which
Sri Rama expostulates with her and urges her to
survive for the earthly good of Angada
content: Tara, consumed by grief, pleads with Rama to end her life.  She argues that a man suffers greatly without his wife, and Vali will experience similar pain in the afterlife without her.  She offers herself as a substitute for Vali, suggesting Rama can kill her without the sin of killing a woman, as she is essentially an extension of Vali. Tara further argues that scriptures consider a wife and husband as one entity, making her reunion with Vali a pious act.  She declares she cannot live without Vali, emphasizing his noble qualities.  Rama, in turn, comforts Tara, advising her against despair. He reminds her that the world is governed by a divine order, encompassing both joy and sorrow. He assures her that she will find happiness with Sugriva and that her son Angada will become the crown prince.  Reassured by Rama's words, Tara ceases her lamentations.

summary:
Canto XXV
Sri Rama consoles Sugriva and others. Entrusting Sugriva with the work of
getting firewood for the cremation of Vali, Laksmana asks Tara to arrange
for a palanquin to carry Vali in state to the crematory. Placing the dead
body of Vali as commanded by Sri Rama on the palanquin brought by
Tara, Sugriva and others carry the palanquin to the riverside and,
taking it down on a sandy bank, prepare a pyre. Vanara women
comfort Tara mourning the death of Vali with his head
placed in her lap. Placing the dead body of Vali on the
pyre, Angada sets fire to it. At the end of the

cremation they all offer water to the spirit of the
deceased and return to Sri Rama’s presence
content: Rama, deeply sympathetic to the grief of Sugriva, Tara, and Angada, advised them to cease their mourning and focus on the necessary funeral rites for Vali.  He explained that excessive grief does not benefit the departed and that they should adhere to customary practices. Rama emphasized the inevitability of time and destiny, stating that Vali had reached the natural end of his life and attained a higher state of being through his actions and acceptance of his fate.  Therefore, rather than succumbing to sorrow, they should honor Vali by performing the appropriate rituals. Following Rama's counsel, Lakshmana instructed Sugriva to organize Vali's cremation with the assistance of Tara and Angada. He further advised Sugriva to arrange for firewood and sandalwood and to comfort the grieving Angada, reminding him of his responsibilities towards the kingdom of Kishkindha.

content: Lakshmana, after Rama finished speaking, gently addressed the grieving Sugriva, advising him to commence Vali's funeral rites with Tara and Angada. He instructed Sugriva to gather dry wood and sandalwood for the cremation. Lakshmana then urged Sugriva to comfort the distraught Angada, reminding him of his responsibilities to Kishkindha.  He further directed Angada to collect garlands, cloths, ghee, oil, fragrances, and other necessary items for the cremation.  Tara was instructed to promptly return with a palanquin, emphasizing the importance of swift action.  Finally, Lakshmana ordered strong and capable monkeys to prepare to carry the palanquin bearing Vali to the cremation ground. After issuing these instructions, Lakshmana quietly stood beside Rama.  Upon hearing Lakshmana’s commands, a worried Tara hurried into Kishkindha to retrieve a palanquin. He quickly returned with an elaborate palanquin, carried by strong monkeys. The palanquin was described as heavenly, chariot-like, adorned with carvings of birds and trees, lattices, and colorful flowers, and furnished with a comfortable seat.  Upon seeing the palanquin, Rama urged Lakshmana to proceed with Vali's cremation without delay.

getting firewood for the cremation of Vali, Laksmana asks Tara to arrange

placed in her lap. Placing the dead body of Vali on the
pyre, Angada sets fire to it. At the end of the

cremation they all offer water to the spirit of the
deceased and return to Sri Rama’s presence
content: Lakshmana and Rama oversaw the preparation of Vali's elaborate palanquin, described as a heavenly chariot adorned with carvings, lattices, and flowers.  Sugriva, weeping, placed Vali's adorned body upon the palanquin and directed the funeral procession to commence, with monkeys scattering jewels before the elaborate conveyance.  The monkeys were instructed to conduct the rites with the same grandeur afforded to a king.  Tara and other female Vanaras lamented Vali's death, expressing their grief and recalling his vibrant presence.  Angada, overcome with sorrow, placed his father's body on the pyre and performed the cremation rites.  Finally, the monkeys proceeded to the river to offer water to Vali's departed spirit.

Canto XXV
Sri Rama consoles Sugriva and others. Entrusting Sugriva with the work of
getting firewood for the cremation of Vali, Laksmana asks Tara to arrange
for a palanquin to carry Vali in state to the crematory. Placing the dead
body of Vali as commanded by Sri Rama on the palanquin brought by
Tara, Sugriva and others carry the palanquin to the riverside and,

pyre, Angada sets fire to it. At the end of the

cremation they all offer water to the spirit of the
deceased and return to Sri Rama’s presence
content: The Vanara people, grieving the loss of Vali, processed towards the cremation grounds.  The female Vanaras, led by Tara, wept and cried out for their beloved leader. Their cries echoed through the forest, as if the very trees and hills lamented with them.  At the riverside, the Vanaras prepared the funeral pyre.  Lowering Vali's adorned palanquin, they gathered around in mourning.  Tara, heartbroken, cradled Vali's head and lamented his death, recalling his kindness, strength, and vibrant presence. She questioned why he no longer looked upon her in her grief.  She spoke of his serene countenance, even in death, and questioned how fate, in the form of Rama, could have brought about this tragedy. Tara also acknowledged the other wives and companions grieving alongside her, reminding Vali of their devotion.  Finally, the other grieving Vanaras consoled Tara as she continued to mourn.  Sugriva, deeply saddened, oversaw the cremation rites alongside Rama and Lakshmana. After the cremation, Sugriva approached Rama.

Canto XXV
Sri Rama consoles Sugriva and others. Entrusting Sugriva with the work of
getting firewood for the cremation of Vali, Laksmana asks Tara to arrange
for a palanquin to carry Vali in state to the crematory. Placing the dead
body of Vali as commanded by Sri Rama on the palanquin brought by
Tara, Sugriva and others carry the palanquin to the riverside and,

pyre, Angada sets fire to it. At the end of the

cremation they all offer water to the spirit of the
deceased and return to Sri Rama’s presence
content: The Vanara women, overcome with grief, consoled and helped Tara, Vali's wife, who was deeply distraught by her husband's death.  With Sugriva's assistance, Angada, Vali's son, tearfully placed his father's body upon the funeral pyre.  Following tradition, Angada performed the cremation rites and circled the pyre before his father's spirit embarked on its final journey.  The Vanaras then proceeded to the Tungabhadra River to perform the customary water offering for the deceased. Led by Sugriva and Tara, with Angada at the forefront, they offered water to Vali's departed spirit.  Sugriva remained mournful throughout, and Rama, sharing in his sorrow, ensured the proper completion of the funeral rites.  After the cremation of the renowned Vali, Sugriva approached Rama and Lakshmana.

summary:
Canto XXVI
Requested by Hanuman to visit Kiskindha, Sri Rama declines to do so on the
ground of his having been prohibited by his father to enter the limits of
a village or town and, directing that Sugriva may be installed on the
throne of Kiskindha and Angada may assume the office of
Prince Regent, he himself takes a vow to live in a cave till
the end of the monsoon and leaves instructions to
initiate a search for Sita after the monsoon
content:
Sugriva, overcome with grief after Vali's cremation, was surrounded by his chief advisors.  They approached Rama, who stood ready for action.  Hanuman, radiant and strong, spoke to Rama, requesting him to enter Kiskindha, the monkey kingdom, so that the newly appointed king, Sugriva, could formally install him and perform the customary rituals. Hanuman urged Rama to unite the monkeys under his leadership.

Rama, ever respectful of his father's decree, declined Hanuman's request. He explained that he was bound by a vow not to enter any village or city for fourteen years. He instructed Sugriva to formally assume the throne of Kiskindha and appoint Angada, Vali's noble and capable son, as Prince Regent.  Observing that the monsoon season had begun, Rama declared it an unsuitable time for the search for Sita. He told Sugriva to return to Kiskindha while he and Lakshmana would remain in the mountains.

Canto XXVI

ground of his having been prohibited by his father to enter the limits of
