content: Sugriva instructs Vinata, a powerful monkey general, to lead a search party of one hundred thousand monkeys eastward. Their mission is to locate Sita and Ravana's dwelling.  The search area encompasses a vast region including mountains, forests, and riverbanks, specifically naming the Bhagirathi (Ganga), Sarayu, Kausiki (Kosi), Yamuna, Saraswati, Sindhu (Indus), and Sona rivers, along with the Mahi and Kalamahi regions.  Furthermore, they are to explore various territories including Brahmamala, Videha, Malawa, Kasi, Kosala, Magadha, Pundra, Anga, regions known for silk production and silver mines.  Sugriva also directs them to search Yavadwipa (Java), including its seven kingdoms and islands rich in gold and silver, and a mountain called Sigira.  He further specifies the search should extend to the  swiftly flowing Sona river, mountain streams, and forests, particularly noting areas where large demons are said to dwell.  The search party is ordered to continue to the sea inhabited by Siddhas and Caranas, then to a reddish sea called the Red Sea, where they may find a giant Salmali tree.

summary:

content: Sugriva continues his detailed instructions to Vinata, describing the lands and creatures his search party must investigate.  He directs them to explore settlements on coastal mountains and Mount Mandara, encountering various beings including those with elongated ears, iron-like faces, and swift single-footed beings.  They are to search the lands of powerful, undying beings, golden-hued Kiratas, and fearsome, fish-eating, aquatic Kiratas.  He emphasizes exploring areas accessible by land and boat.  The search should also encompass Yavadwipa (Java), its seven kingdoms, and nearby gold and silver islands.  Beyond Java, they are to explore Mount Sigira. The search party is ordered to scour mountain passes, forests, and waterfalls for Sita.  They must also investigate the shores of the swiftly flowing Sona river and surrounding forests.  The monkeys are then instructed to explore fearsome islands, including one within the Iksusamudra ocean where Mandehas, mountain-like ogres, hang upside down.  Finally, Sugriva describes a loud, fiery ocean where the cries of its inhabitants can be heard, and beyond it, at thirteen yojanas distance, a golden mountain known as Jatarupasila.

summary:
Canto XL
Pointing out in pursuance of Sri Rama’s command to one of his generals,
Vinata by name, the regions in the east which could be penetrated into
by monkeys, detailing the tracts worthy of note, and entrusting him
with the task of conducting a search for Sita in those regions,
Sugriva enjoins the general to return within a month
after the work, adding that anyone returning

content: Sugriva directs Vinata's search party towards a series of islands and oceans. First, the terrifying Iksudwipa islands within the roaring Iksusamudra ocean, where large, perpetually hungry demons snatch flying creatures with their shadows. Next, they must cross the loud, serpent-infested ocean to the Red Sea, where they will find the giant Kutasalmali tree and Garuda's jeweled abode. On an island within a wine-like ocean, mountain-like ogres called Mandehas hang upside down, falling into the water at sunrise, scorched by the sun and slain by holy incantations, only to revive and reattach themselves. Beyond lies the Ksiroda, the ocean of milk, with its pearly white waves, in the middle of which rises the white Mount Rsabha with its fragrant, blossoming trees. Atop Mount Rsabha, they will find the Sudargana lake, resplendent with gold-filamented lotuses and frequented by divine beings. Crossing the Ksiroda leads to an ocean that terrifies all beings, containing the immense Vadavamukha, a submarine fire born of a sage's wrath.  A golden palmyra tree marks the eastern boundary of this ocean, beyond which lies the golden Mount Uday, the source of the sunrise.  Its vast, sky-high summit forms a base for other mountains.  Adorned with golden trees, Mount Uday features the peak Saumanasa, where Vishnu once placed his foot during his cosmic strides.  The sun is most visible from Jambudwipa when ascending this peak. Before Mount Uday shines the island of Sudarsana, bringing light to all beings. Sugriva commands the search party to thoroughly examine this mountain's peaks, caves, and forests for Ravana and Sita. The eastern horizon glows red, illuminated by the brilliance of the golden mountain and the sun.

summary:
Canto XL
Pointing out in pursuance of Sri Rama’s command to one of his generals,
Vinata by name, the regions in the east which could be penetrated into
by monkeys, detailing the tracts worthy of note, and entrusting him

content: Sugriva continues his detailed instructions to Vinata's search party. He describes a series of geographical features they will encounter eastward.  First,  ogres hanging from a mountain fall into the water at sunrise, scorched and temporarily killed, only to revive and reattach themselves.  The party will then reach the milky Ksiroda Ocean, with its white mountain, Rsabha, featuring the beautiful Sudargana lake frequented by divine beings.  Beyond lies a terrifying freshwater ocean containing Vadavamukha, a vast submarine fire.  Thirteen *yojanas* north of this ocean's shore stands the golden Mount Jatarupasila, where the serpent god Ananta resides, marked by a golden palmyra tree.  East of this marker rises the immense golden Mount Uday, from which the sun rises.  Its peak Saumanasa, once touched by Vishnu, offers the clearest view of the sun.  Sugriva instructs them to search this mountain thoroughly for Ravana and Sita. Beyond Mount Uday lies an inaccessible, dark region.  Sugriva stresses the importance of returning within a month, setting a strict deadline with a severe penalty for tardiness. He promises them happiness and success upon Sita's recovery.

summary:
Canto XL
Pointing out in pursuance of Sri Rama’s command to one of his generals,
Vinata by name, the regions in the east which could be penetrated into

Sugriva enjoins the general to return within a month

after a month will be punished
content: Sugriva directs Vinata's search party to look for ascetics shining like the sun on a peak.  He points out the island of Sudarsana, explaining that light and vision depend on the sun shining on this eastern mountain.  They are to thoroughly search this mountain's peaks, caves, and forests for Ravana and Sita.  The eastern horizon glows red, illuminated by the golden mountain and the sun. Sugriva explains this mountain marks the boundary between the earthly and higher realms, the point where the sun rises.  He reiterates the importance of searching its summits, waterfalls, and caves for Sita. Beyond this mountain lies an inaccessible, dark region inhabited by Indra, lacking the sun and moon.  Sugriva instructs them to search all mountains, rivers, caves, and unspecified areas for Sita.  He emphasizes this is the furthest the monkeys can go, as the region beyond is unknown and limitless.  The party is ordered to return within a month after reaching the eastern mountain and locating Sita and Ravana's abode.  Sugriva sets a strict deadline, warning that anyone exceeding it faces execution.  He promises them happiness upon their successful return with Sita after thoroughly searching the eastern region.

summary:
Canto XLI
Specifying the different regions of the southern quarter and fixing a limit of
one month for their return, Sugriva dispatches Hanuman, Nila, Angada
and others to that quarter for finding out the whereabouts of Sita
content: Sugriva dispatched a group of skilled monkeys southward to search for Sita.  He appointed Angada, known for his strength, as their leader.  This group included Hanuman, Nila, Jambavan, and several others.  Sugriva instructed them to explore various challenging regions including the Vindhya mountains, the Narmada, Godavari, Krishnaveni, and Varada rivers, as well as the areas of Mekhala, Utkala, Dasarna, Avanti,  Vidarbha, Rstika, Mahisaka, Vanga, Kalinga, Kausika, and the Dandaka forest. He also directed them to search the Ayomukha mountain.  Sugriva informed them that this area was likely the domain of the powerful Ravana, and warned them of a dangerous ogress named Angaraka who resided in the southern ocean.  He urged them to thoroughly investigate these areas, resolving any uncertainties, and to continue searching elsewhere if Sita was not found.

summary:
Canto XLI
Specifying the different regions of the southern quarter and fixing a limit of
one month for their return, Sugriva dispatches Hanuman, Nila, Angada
and others to that quarter for finding out the whereabouts of Sita
content:
Sugriva instructs the search party to thoroughly explore a series of geographical landmarks in their quest to find Sita.  These include the Vindhya mountains, the Narmada, Godavari, Krishnaveni, and Varada rivers. He further directs them to search the regions of Mekhala, Utkala, and the cities of Dasarna, Abravanti, and Avanti.  Their search should also encompass Vidarbha, Rstika, Mahisaka, Vanga, Kalinga, Kausika, and the Dandaka forest, including the Godavari river section flowing through it.  The territories of Andhra, Pundra, Chola, Pandya, and Kerala are also to be investigated.

Sugriva then highlights the Ayomukha (Malaya) mountain, rich in minerals and covered with blossoming forests, and the Kaveri river. He mentions Sage Agastya, residing near the Malaya mountain, and instructs them to seek his permission before crossing the Tamraparni river. Beyond the river lies the golden gate of the Pandya city. Finally, he mentions Mahendra mountain, a place visited by Indra and inaccessible to humans, located near a large island that must be searched.  This island, with its golden and silver peaks, can only be seen by those who are grateful, compassionate, and faithful.  The city of Bhogavati, home to the serpent king Vasuki, should also be explored.

summary:
Canto XLI
Specifying the different regions of the southern quarter and fixing a limit of
one month for their return, Sugriva dispatches Hanuman, Nila, Angada
and others to that quarter for finding out the whereabouts of Sita
content: Sugriva directs the search party towards the sandalwood-covered Malaya mountain, where the clear Kaveri river flows.  They are to seek permission from the radiant Sage Agastya before crossing the alligator-infested Tamraparni river.  Beyond the river lies the ornate golden gate of the Pandya city.  He then describes Mahendra mountain, a place frequently visited by Indra and impossible for humans to reach, situated near a large island they must thoroughly search.  This island, with its peaks of gold and silver, is only visible to those who are virtuous.  Sugriva also urges them to explore the area around the serpent king Vasuki's city, Bhogavati, heavily guarded by dangerous snakes. He describes a challenging route past Sūryavan and Vaidyuta mountains, to Kunjara mountain where Agastya's magnificent golden abode stands.  This area also houses the heavily fortified city of Bhogavati.  He warns them of the Gandharvas guarding the area beyond and advises against venturing into the fearsome realm of the dead ruled by Yama, which is the limit of their southward search.  They are to return within a month if Sita is found, and the successful monkey will be richly rewarded.  Sugriva encourages them, reminding them of their strength and noble lineage, to put forth their best effort in finding Sita.

summary:
Canto XLI
Specifying the different regions of the southern quarter and fixing a limit of
one month for their return, Sugriva dispatches Hanuman, Nila, Angada
and others to that quarter for finding out the whereabouts of Sita
content: Continuing their search south, the monkeys are to respectfully acknowledge Mount Suryavan, reachable by a difficult fourteen-yojana path.  Beyond lies Mount Vaidyuta, abundant with desirable fruits and honey.  Further on is Mount Kunjara, home to a magnificent golden abode of Sage Agastya, built by Vishwakarma.  This mountain also houses the well-protected serpent city of Bhogavati, ruled by Vasuki. The monkeys are to carefully explore this city. They are also instructed to search nearby hidden areas.  Beyond this region lies Mount Rshabha, rich with various sandalwood trees, which they are forbidden to touch.  This area is guarded by the Rohita Gandharvas, led by five powerful chiefs.  Beyond Mount Rshabha lies a radiant realm inhabited by virtuous beings.  The monkeys are warned not to proceed past this point to the forbidding realm of Yama, shrouded in darkness.  They are to thoroughly search this designated area for Sita and return within a month.  The monkey who successfully locates Sita will be richly rewarded and held in highest regard by Sugriva.

summary:
Canto XLI
Specifying the different regions of the southern quarter and fixing a limit of
one month for their return, Sugriva dispatches Hanuman, Nila, Angada
and others to that quarter for finding out the whereabouts of Sita
content: Sugriva declares that whoever finds Sita will be dearer to him than life itself, even if they have committed past offenses. He urges the monkey chiefs, highlighting their immense strength, prowess, and noble lineage, to dedicate themselves to finding Sita, the daughter of a human king.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva instructs a group of monkeys, led by Susena, to search for Sita in the western region. He addresses Susena, Tara's father, and other prominent monkeys including Arcisman, nicknamed Marica, and his group, the Arcirmalyas.  Sugriva tasks them with exploring various locations including Saurastra, Candracitra, Bahlika, and cities and rural areas filled with specific trees like Punnaga, Bakula, and Uddalaka, as well as Ketaka shrubs. They are also instructed to search along westward flowing rivers, ascetic groves, forested mountains, deserts, and cliffs.  Their search should extend to the Arabian Sea, exploring its shores, including the hills and forests. They are to search in clusters of Ketaka and Tamala trees and coconut groves for Sita and Ravana's dwelling. The monkeys should also explore towns like Muravi, Jatapura, Avanti, and Angalepa, the Alaksita forest, and other kingdoms and towns, including the hundred-peaked, tree-covered Mount Somagiri, located at the confluence of the Indus River and the sea.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva continues to direct the monkey search parties westward.  He instructs them to explore the Arabian Sea, its shores, and the surrounding areas including the towns of Muravi and Jatapura, the cities of Avanti and Angalepa, and the Alaksita forest. He also mentions other kingdoms and towns, including Mount Somagiri where winged lions nest, carrying Timis, alligators, and elephants to their mountaintop lairs. The monkeys are warned to avoid conflict with the Gandharvas inhabiting the Pariyatra mountain, but to thoroughly explore the area, including the Vajra mountain and its caves. Finally, he points them towards Cakravan mountain, a volcanic island in the Arabian sea, where the celestial architect Viswakarma forged a discus.  The monkeys are to maintain their simian forms for safety and to prioritize their search for Sita and Ravana's dwelling.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva continues to guide the monkey search parties, emphasizing the importance of finding Sita. He assures them of safety as long as they maintain their simian forms. He directs them to explore a magnificent mountain called Vajra, near Pariyatra mountain, described as shimmering like a cat's-eye gem, solid as diamond, covered in vegetation, and incredibly tall.  Its numerous caves are to be thoroughly searched. He then points them towards Cakravan, a volcanic island where the divine architect Viswakarma crafted a discus, after defeating the demons Panchajana and Hayagriva. The monkeys are instructed to search this island, including its peaks and caves, for Sita and Ravana. Sugriva further directs the search to Varaha, a massive mountain with golden peaks, located in the vast ocean, and home to the city of Pragjyotisapura, where the demon Naraka resides. The monkeys are to search this area as well. He then describes another mountain beyond Varaha, made entirely of gold and adorned with waterfalls, where elephants, boars, lions, and tigers roam. Finally, he speaks of Mount Megha, where Indra was crowned king, and beyond that, a vast range of sixty thousand golden hills, shining like the rising sun and covered with blossoming golden trees, with Mount Meru standing tall amongst them, a mountain blessed by the sun god to turn those who seek refuge on it into gold.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva directs the monkey search parties beyond Mount Megha to a range of sixty thousand golden hills, shimmering like the rising sun and adorned with golden trees in full bloom.  At the center of these hills stands Mount Meru, a king of mountains blessed by the sun god. The blessing grants anyone who seeks refuge on Mount Meru a golden form.  Even gods, Gandharvas, and demons who reside there become devotees of the sun god and shine like gold.  Sugriva explains that at twilight, the gods gather at Mount Meru to worship the sun as it sets behind the western mountain.  The sun god’s journey to this distant mountain, a journey of ten thousand Yojanas, takes less than half an hour.  Atop the western mountain stands a magnificent palace complex, built by Viswakarma,  filled with palaces and lush trees teeming with birds. This is the abode of Varuna. Between Mount Meru and the western mountain stands a giant, ten-branched golden palmyra tree. Sugriva instructs the monkeys to search all these locations – the difficult-to-access lakes, the rivers, and the mountains – for Sita and Ravana. He also tells them to seek out the sage Merusavarni, who resides on Mount Meru and is as wise as Brahma. This sage, shining like the sun, should be approached respectfully for information about Sita.  Sugriva concludes by informing the monkeys that their search cannot extend beyond this sunlit region as the area beyond is unknown and without the sun's light.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva continues his instructions to the monkey search parties, telling them to locate the sage Merusavarni, who resides on Mount Meru. This sage, as bright as the sun and as wise as Brahma, should be treated with utmost respect and questioned about Sita's whereabouts.  Sugriva defines the boundaries of their search, explaining that the sun's light reaches only as far as the western mountain where Varuna resides. Beyond this point lies an unknown, sunless region inaccessible to the monkeys. He urges them to thoroughly explore the lands between Mount Meru and the western mountain, including the lakes, rivers, and mountains.  He sets a one-month time limit for the search and warns against exceeding it. Sugriva emphasizes the authority of his father-in-law, who will accompany the search party, and instructs them to obey his commands. He reminds the monkeys of their strength and resourcefulness, encouraging them to use their father-in-law's guidance effectively.  Sugriva stresses the importance of finding Sita, highlighting that their duty to Rama will only be fulfilled upon her safe return. He advises them to attend to any other matters that could aid in their primary mission, taking into account the appropriate time, place, and purpose.  Finally, the monkey troops, led by Susena, depart westward towards the region governed by Varuna.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva instructs Satabali, a powerful monkey leader, to search for Sita in the northern region.  Satabali is to take a large contingent of monkeys and advisors with him.  Sugriva emphasizes the importance of this mission, stating that finding Sita will repay the debt they owe to Rama for his kindness and ensure their lives have meaning.  He highlights the virtue of fulfilling a request, especially for a benefactor.  Sugriva then outlines the areas Satabali should search, including challenging terrains, and lists various kingdoms and regions, from the Mlecchas and Pulindas to the  Kurus and Madra, as well as the Kambojas, Yavanas, Sakas, and Daradas. Specific locations like Lodhra and Padmaka thickets, deodar groves, and the Soma hermitage are mentioned, along with the Kala mountain and its surrounding caves.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva continues his detailed instructions to Satabali, outlining further areas to search for Sita.  He directs him to explore the Himalayan region, specifically mentioning the lands of various groups, from the Mlecchas and Pulindas to the Surasenas, Prasthalas, Bharatas, Kurus, and Madra people. He also includes the Kambojas, Yavanas, Sakas, and Daradas in his list.  Satabali is to search the Lodhra and Padmaka thickets and deodar groves.  He should also visit the Soma hermitage, inhabited by gods and Gandharvas, and then proceed to the Kala mountain, searching its caves and offshoots.  Continuing beyond Kala mountain, Satabali is to search Sudarsana mountain, and then Devasakha, a haven for birds. He is to explore its thickets, waterfalls, and caves.  Sugriva then describes a vast, desolate expanse devoid of life, which Satabali must cross to reach Mount Kailasa, home to Kubera's magnificent palace.  He is to search the moonlit slopes and caves of Kailasa. Finally, Sugriva instructs him to cautiously explore the difficult-to-access cave of Kraufica mountain, inhabited by radiant sages, and to investigate its other caves, plateaus, peaks, caverns, and slopes as well.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva continues his meticulous instructions to Satabali, directing him to search the moonlit caves and slopes of a mountain for Ravana and Sita.  He cautions Satabali about the Kraufica mountain, advising vigilance while entering its difficult-to-access cave, inhabited by radiant, god-like sages. Sugriva emphasizes a thorough search of the surrounding caves, plateaus, peaks, and slopes of Kraufica. He then mentions the Manasa peak, a desolate place devoid of life, including gods, demons, and even birds.  Continuing, Sugriva instructs Satabali to explore Mainaka mountain, home to Maya's abode, and the surrounding area inhabited by Kinnara women.  He then directs Satabali to a hermitage of perfected ascetics of the Vaikhanasa and Valakhilya orders, suggesting a respectful inquiry about Sita.  Nearby, lies a golden lotus-covered Vaikhanasa lake, frequented by Kubera's elephant, Sarvabhauma. Beyond the lake stretches a vast, starless, sunless expanse, illuminated by the aura of self-realized sages.  Sugriva guides Satabali further, mentioning the Sailoda river, bordered by Kicaka bamboos, which help the sages cross.  Finally, he describes the land of the Uttara Kurus, blessed with numerous streams adorned with lotus leaves resembling cat's-eye jewels.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva describes a region beyond the Vaikhanasa lake, illuminated by the aura of ascetic sages.  The Sailoda river flows through this region, its banks lined with Kicaka bamboos that aid the sages in crossing.  This river marks the entrance to the land of the Uttara Kurus, a land of reward for those who have performed good deeds.  This land abounds with streams fed by lotus ponds filled with golden lotuses.  The streams themselves are rich with dark green lotus leaves, resembling cat's-eye jewels.  Lakes the color of the rising sun, adorned with red and gold lotuses, add to the beauty of the land. Blue lotuses with gem-like petals and golden filaments also thrive there. The rivers' sandy banks are scattered with pearls, precious gems, and gold, bordered by mountains of jewels and gold that shine like fire.  The trees in this land are always in bloom and laden with fruit, exuding a divine fragrance and offering delightful tastes and textures. They fulfill all desires. Some trees provide clothing of various shapes and sizes, and others offer ornaments of pearls and cat's-eye jewels suitable for both men and women.  Certain trees bear fruit enjoyable in all seasons, while others produce fruits resembling precious gems.  There are trees that offer comfortable beds with colorful covers and pleasing garlands.  Other trees provide luxurious drinks, various foods, and the company of accomplished and beautiful women.  The land is also home to Gandharvas, Kinnaras, Siddhas, Nagas, and Vidyadharas, all radiant beings who enjoy life with their companions.  Everyone in this land is said to have performed good deeds in their past lives, and they now live in a state of constant joy and abundance, surrounded by wealth, luxury, and youthful companions. The air is filled with the delightful sounds of music, singing, and laughter, creating a harmonious atmosphere that pleases all.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva continues his description of the lands beyond the Vaikhanasa lake, detailing a land of unimaginable abundance and pleasure.  Trees miraculously provide luxurious beds with ornate covers and fragrant garlands, along with delicious food and drinks.  Even beautiful and talented companions are provided for by the magical trees of this land.  Gandharvas, Kinnaras, Siddhas, Nagas, and Vidyadharas, all radiant beings, live there, enjoying life's pleasures with their companions.  Everyone in this land is said to have earned this reward through good deeds in past lives.  Music, singing, and laughter fill the air, and no one experiences unhappiness or engages in negativity.  Goodness flourishes in this land.  Sugriva then mentions a massive golden mountain called Somagiri, located beyond this land in the Northern Sea.  Visible even from the heavens of Indra and Brahma, this mountain illuminates the surrounding region with its own brilliance.  Divine beings like Vishnu, Shiva, and Brahma reside in this area.  Sugriva warns the monkeys not to venture beyond the Uttara Kurus, as the path is impassable even for gods. He advises them to return after sighting Somagiri.  He emphasizes that the region beyond is unknown and without limits. Sugriva urges the monkeys to thoroughly search the described areas, and even beyond, with the ultimate goal of finding Sita.  He stresses that finding Sita will please Rama greatly, and even more so, himself.  He promises to reward them handsomely for their success, ensuring they will enjoy lives of comfort, peace, and companionship with their loved ones.

summary: No summary available
content: Sugriva reiterates the importance of thoroughly searching all the described lands, and even those he hasn't mentioned, for Sita. He emphasizes that finding Sita will bring immense joy to Rama, and even more so to himself.  He promises to richly reward the monkeys for their successful search, granting them comfortable, peaceful lives alongside their loved ones, free from conflict, and capable of supporting others.

summary:
Canto XLIV
With a view to inspiring confidence in Sita, when found, Sri Rama hands
over to Hanuman his signet ring as a token of recognition while
sending him away to the south. Bowing down at Sri Rama’s
feet, Hanuman leaves his presence alongwith a
contingent of the Vanara forces
content: Sugriva expressed confidence in Hanuman's ability to find Sita, highlighting his strength and knowledge.  He praised Hanuman's unimpeded movement and speed, comparable to his father, the wind-god. Sugriva emphasized Hanuman's unique vigor and wisdom, urging him to devise a plan to locate Sita.  He further commended Hanuman's strength, intellect, prowess, adaptability, and political acumen. Recognizing Sugriva's faith in Hanuman, Rama reflected on their shared confidence in Hanuman's capabilities.  Overjoyed, Rama presented Hanuman with his signet ring as a token of identification for Sita, assuring him that it would prove his connection to Rama.  He expressed confidence in Hanuman's resolve, courage, and Sugriva's encouraging words.  Accepting the ring, Hanuman respectfully bowed to Rama and embarked on his mission.

summary:
Canto XLIV
With a view to inspiring confidence in Sita, when found, Sri Rama hands
over to Hanuman his signet ring as a token of recognition while
sending him away to the south. Bowing down at Sri Rama’s
feet, Hanuman leaves his presence alongwith a
contingent of the Vanara forces
content: Rama, overjoyed, presented Hanuman with his signet ring, a token for Sita to recognize him.  He assured Hanuman that the ring, inscribed with Rama's name, would serve as unmistakable proof of his connection to Rama.  Rama further expressed confidence in Hanuman's resolve, courage, and the encouraging words of Sugriva, believing they foretold success. Hanuman, accepting the ring with reverence, placed it on his head, bowed to Rama, and departed on his mission.  Leading a vast assembly of monkeys, the valiant Hanuman, son of the wind-god, was a striking figure, reminiscent of the moon shining brightly amidst the stars in a clear night sky.  Rama, entrusting Hanuman with the vital task, urged him to utilize his immense strength and valor to locate Sita, the daughter of Janaka.

summary:
Canto XLV

Bowing to the command of Sugriva and leading their respective bands,
Vinata and other monkey chiefs set forth towards the
quarter assigned to them by Sugriva
content: King Sugriva, the foremost of monkeys, addressed his assembled troops, urging them to thoroughly search their designated areas as instructed by him to fulfill Rama's objective.  The monkey chiefs, understanding the gravity of their mission, spread out like locusts across the land.  Meanwhile, Rama and Lakshmana remained on Mount Prasravana, awaiting news of Sita's whereabouts. Satabali hastened towards the north, Vinata towards the east, and Hanuman, accompanied by Tara and Angada, journeyed south.  Susena, a powerful warrior, headed west.  Sugriva, having dispatched his forces, felt content.  The monkey generals, roaring and shouting, marched with determination, vowing to find Sita and defeat Ravana. Some boasted of their individual strength and ability to retrieve Sita single-handedly, even from the underworld, claiming they could shatter mountains, split the earth, and churn the oceans.

summary:
Canto XLV

Bowing to the command of Sugriva and leading their respective bands,
Vinata and other monkey chiefs set forth towards the
quarter assigned to them by Sugriva
content: Some monkeys, puffed up with their strength and eager to impress Sugriva, boasted loudly about their abilities. One proclaimed he would single-handedly slay Ravana, retrieve Sita even from the underworld, and bring her back.  Another bragged he could demolish trees, shatter mountains, split the earth, and churn the oceans.  Yet another asserted that nothing could stop him, whether on land, in the sea, in the mountains, forests, or even the depths of the underworld. They made these pronouncements in front of Sugriva.

summary:
Canto XLVI
While Sugriva gave instructions to his various generals mentioning in detail
the places they were to visit in the course of their expedition in search of
Sita, Sri Rama was struck by the knowledge of world geography revealed
by Sugriva and inquired of the latter how he had come to acquire such
wonderful and accurate knowledge of geography. In reply to this
Sugriva tells him of the encounter of Vali with Dundubhi, which
led the former to conceive an inveterate hatred towards him
(Sugriva) who when pursued by Vali had to range from one
end of the world to another and this enabled him (Sugriva)
to gather a first-hand knowledge of all parts of the globe
content: Sugriva explains to Rama the source of his geographical knowledge.  He recounts how he once guarded the entrance of a cave while his brother Vali fought a demon inside. When blood filled the cave and Vali didn't emerge, Sugriva assumed the worst and sealed the cave.  He returned to Kiskindha and assumed leadership, but Vali eventually reappeared, having slain the demon.  Fearful, Sugriva relinquished his position, but Vali, enraged, chased him across the world. Sugriva describes seeing various rivers, forests, and cities as he fled, the earth appearing like a mirror and a firebrand due to his speed.  He eventually reached the eastern region, observing diverse trees, mountains, and lakes, including a mineral-rich mountain and the mythical Milk Ocean.

summary:
Canto XLVI
While Sugriva gave instructions to his various generals mentioning in detail
the places they were to visit in the course of their expedition in search of
Sita, Sri Rama was struck by the knowledge of world geography revealed
by Sugriva and inquired of the latter how he had come to acquire such
wonderful and accurate knowledge of geography. In reply to this
Sugriva tells him of the encounter of Vali with Dundubhi, which
led the former to conceive an inveterate hatred towards him
(Sugriva) who when pursued by Vali had to range from one
end of the world to another and this enabled him (Sugriva)
to gather a first-hand knowledge of all parts of the globe
content: Sugriva continues his story, describing his flight from Vali.  He ran so fast that the earth seemed like a blur, reflecting everything like a mirror and appearing like a firebrand.  He ran east, observing diverse trees, mountains, lakes, and even a mountain rich with minerals and the mythical Milk Ocean, home to celestial nymphs.  He turned back and then suddenly departed for the south, passing through the Vindhyan range with its sandalwood trees.  He saw Vali pursuing him again and again and continued fleeing west, taking note of the western mountain.  He then ran north, reaching the Himalayas, Mount Meru, and even the Northern Sea, still pursued by Vali.  Finally, Hanuman reminded Sugriva of a sage's curse on Vali, forbidding him from entering a certain hermitage.  They found refuge on Mount Rsyamuka, safe from Vali due to the curse.  Sugriva emphasizes that this flight allowed him to directly observe the entire world.

summary:
Canto XLVI
While Sugriva gave instructions to his various generals mentioning in detail
the places they were to visit in the course of their expedition in search of
Sita, Sri Rama was struck by the knowledge of world geography revealed
by Sugriva and inquired of the latter how he had come to acquire such
wonderful and accurate knowledge of geography. In reply to this
Sugriva tells him of the encounter of Vali with Dundubhi, which
led the former to conceive an inveterate hatred towards him
(Sugriva) who when pursued by Vali had to range from one
end of the world to another and this enabled him (Sugriva)
to gather a first-hand knowledge of all parts of the globe
content: Sugriva assures Rama that their stay on Mount Rsyamuka will be safe and peaceful. He then describes arriving at the mountain and establishing his residence there.  Vali, fearing a sage's curse, dared not approach the area.  Sugriva concludes by reiterating that his flight from Vali allowed him to personally witness the entire world before finding refuge in a cave on Mount Rsyamuka.

summary:
Canto XLVII
Even after scouring more than once the regions comprised in the quarter
assigned to them, Vinata and others fail to get any clue to the whereabouts
of Sita. Returning despondent even before a month, they bow down
to Sri Rama and Sugriva and report their inability to find out
sita. They feel convinced for many reasons that

Hanuman alone will be
content: Sugriva dispatched the foremost monkeys to search for Sita.  They thoroughly investigated lakes, riversides, lands, and cities, leaving no area unexplored. Following Sugriva's detailed instructions, the monkey leaders meticulously searched their assigned regions, including mountains, forests, and woodlands.  They searched throughout the day, gathering at night to rest. They found fruit-bearing trees in every region they explored and slept beneath them. After a month of searching, the monkey chiefs returned to Sugriva and Rama, empty-handed. Vinata, having explored the east, returned with his group.  Satabali, having searched the north, returned with his troop, fearful.  Susena, having searched the west, also returned to Sugriva. They approached Sugriva and Rama, bowing before them. They reported having searched all mountains, dense forests, rivers, and lands. They investigated every cave and bower. They searched for and eliminated large animals in difficult and uneven terrains, suspecting they might be Ravana in disguise. They emphasized that even the most inaccessible areas were searched repeatedly, yet Sita remained undiscovered.  They concluded that only Hanuman, with his noble character and lineage, was capable of finding her, as he had fortunately gone to the same region where Sita had been taken.

summary:
Canto XLVII
Even after scouring more than once the regions comprised in the quarter
assigned to them, Vinata and others fail to get any clue to the whereabouts
of Sita. Returning despondent even before a month, they bow down
to Sri Rama and Sugriva and report their inability to find out
sita. They feel convinced for many reasons that

Hanuman alone will be
content: The monkey chiefs reiterated that they had thoroughly searched all designated caves and expansive, vine-covered bowers.  They also confirmed having hunted and killed unusually large animals in treacherous and uneven terrains, suspecting they might be Ravana disguised.  Furthermore, they emphasized that even the most inaccessible areas were searched repeatedly.  Despite these extensive efforts, Sita remained undiscovered. They expressed their conviction that only Hanuman, with his noble character and lineage, possessed the ability to find her, as fate had placed him in the same region where Sita had been abducted.

summary:
Canto XLVIII
Accompanied by Angada and others, Hanuman seeks for Sita in the dense
forests and caves of the Vindhyan range. Making their way into a forest
whose trees had ceased to flower and bear fruits because of a curse
pronounced by Sage Kandu, overwhelmed with grief occasioned by
the loss of his son, the monkeys, who felt exhausted due to exertion,
behold a demon. As the latter swoops upon the monkeys with a
raised fist, Angada makes short work of him thinking him to
be Ravana. Feeling exhausted through peregrination in
the forest, the monkeys seek the foot of a tree
content: Hanuman, accompanied by Tara and Angada, journeyed to the region assigned by Sugriva.  They traveled a great distance, exploring the Vindhyan range's caves and dense forests, but found no trace of Sita.  The monkeys sustained themselves on roots and fruits, searching tirelessly through difficult terrain. This particular region was challenging, full of caves, dense forests, and desolate wilderness.  After exhausting that area, they moved on to another, equally challenging region. This new area was barren – the trees were fruitless and leafless, the rivers dry, and there were no animals to be found. The land had been cursed by the sage Kandu after the death of his young son.  It was in this cursed forest that the monkeys encountered a fearsome demon, who stood like a mountain and threatened them.

summary:
Canto XLVIII
Accompanied by Angada and others, Hanuman seeks for Sita in the dense
forests and caves of the Vindhyan range. Making their way into a forest
whose trees had ceased to flower and bear fruits because of a curse
pronounced by Sage Kandu, overwhelmed with grief occasioned by
the loss of his son, the monkeys, who felt exhausted due to exertion,
behold a demon. As the latter swoops upon the monkeys with a
raised fist, Angada makes short work of him thinking him to
be Ravana. Feeling exhausted through peregrination in
the forest, the monkeys seek the foot of a tree
content: The monkeys entered a cursed forest, barren and lifeless due to the sage Kandu's grief over his son's death.  There, they encountered a formidable demon.  Mistaking the demon for Ravana, Angada swiftly attacked and killed him.  Believing they had perhaps found and defeated Ravana,  the monkeys thoroughly searched the surrounding caves and mountains again, but still couldn't find Sita.  Exhausted and disheartened, they rested beneath a tree.

summary:
Canto XLVIII
Accompanied by Angada and others, Hanuman seeks for Sita in the dense
forests and caves of the Vindhyan range. Making their way into a forest
whose trees had ceased to flower and bear fruits because of a curse
pronounced by Sage Kandu, overwhelmed with grief occasioned by
the loss of his son, the monkeys, who felt exhausted due to exertion,
behold a demon. As the latter swoops upon the monkeys with a
raised fist, Angada makes short work of him thinking him to
be Ravana. Feeling exhausted through peregrination in
the forest, the monkeys seek the foot of a tree
content: Angada, enraged, swiftly attacked the oncoming demon who threatened him with a raised fist.  Mistaking him for Ravana, Angada struck the demon with his palm, felling him to the ground.  The monkeys, believing they had possibly defeated Ravana, once again thoroughly searched the surrounding caves and mountains.  Their search yielded no results, and, exhausted and disheartened, they gathered to rest beneath a tree.

summary:
Canto XLIX
Asking the monkeys who accompanied him to the south and who had begun
to feel diffident about their success to take note of the delay that was
taking place in carrying out the command of Sugriva and to beware of
Sugriva’s wrath, which was sure to follow in consequence of the
delay, Angada advises them to take heart and encourages them to
pursue their search vigorously. Encouraged by his enlivening
words, they sally forth once more to renew their search in
the cave of the Silver Mountain and, getting exhausted
after a strenuous search, repose awhile in a
grove and get ready to resume their efforts
content: Exhausted from their search, Angada rallied the disheartened monkeys.  He reminded them that they had thoroughly searched the woods, mountains, rivers, caves, and every other conceivable hiding place, yet Sita and Ravana remained elusive.  With Sugriva's impatience looming, Angada urged them to overcome their fatigue and despair and continue the search with renewed vigor, emphasizing that perseverance leads to success.  He warned them of Sugriva's harsh temper and Rama's disappointment should they fail.  Offering his words as advice, he invited alternative suggestions.  Gandhamadana affirmed Angada's wise words and encouraged the monkeys to follow his counsel. He proposed they revisit the mountains, caves, rocks, and forests specifically mentioned by Sugriva.  Reinvigorated, the monkeys rose and resumed their search in the dense Vindhyan forest, focusing on the southern region.

summary:
Canto XLIX
Asking the monkeys who accompanied him to the south and who had begun
to feel diffident about their success to take note of the delay that was
taking place in carrying out the command of Sugriva and to beware of
Sugriva’s wrath, which was sure to follow in consequence of the
delay, Angada advises them to take heart and encourages them to
pursue their search vigorously. Encouraged by his enlivening
words, they sally forth once more to renew their search in
the cave of the Silver Mountain and, getting exhausted
after a strenuous search, repose awhile in a
grove and get ready to resume their efforts
content: The monkeys, following Angada's advice, decided to thoroughly search the mountains, caves, rocks, and forests as instructed by Sugriva.  They began their search in the southern region, dense with the Vindhyan forest.  They scaled the Silver Mountain, searching its peaks, caverns, and groves of Lodhra and Saptaparna trees.  Despite their immense efforts, they grew tired and reached a summit without finding Sita.  They descended, continuing their search, and eventually rested at the foot of a tree, fatigued and discouraged. After a brief respite, they resolved to renew their search of the southern region, with Hanuman leading the expedition around the Vindhyan mountain range.

summary: No summary available
content: Canto L:  Driven by Sugriva's deadline, Hanuman, Angada, and their monkey troop exhaustively searched the Vindhya mountains for Sita.  The terrain, riddled with caves and dense forests, proved challenging.  Reaching the southwestern peak, they discovered a fragrant cave named Rksabila, guarded by a demon.  The monkeys, parched and weary, were encouraged by the sight of water birds emerging from within. Despite the cave's ominous darkness and the presence of various creatures, Hanuman, along with the other monkeys, ventured inside, hoping to find a source of water.

summary: No summary available
content: Driven by the need to find water, Hanuman, Angada, and the monkey troop entered a fragrant cave called Rksabila.  Despite the initial darkness, they pressed on, their movement and vision unimpeded.  After traveling a considerable distance within the cave, the monkeys, weary and parched, discovered a luminous grove. This wondrous place held trees of gold, adorned with blossoms and vibrant leaves, some resembling the rising sun and standing on platforms of gleaming gems.  Lotus plants of a striking blue hue, encircled by birds, added to the surreal beauty of the scene.

summary: No summary available
content: Entering a luminous grove within the cave, the monkeys, weary and thirsty, discovered a wondrous spectacle.  Golden trees, some resembling the rising sun and standing on gem platforms, bore vibrant blossoms and leaves.  Lotus plants of a striking blue hue added to the surreal beauty.  They also found pools of clear water surrounded by golden trees adorned with golden fish and large lotuses.  Palaces of gold and silver stood alongside mansions decorated with gems and pearls.  Fruit-laden trees buzzed with golden bees, and various honeys lay nearby.  Spacious couches and seats, piles of vessels made of gold, silver, and bell-metal, along with collections of aloe and sandalwood, filled the area.  Food, roots, fruits, conveyances, ethereal textiles, blankets, and deerskins were also abundant.  Heaps of gleaming gold lay scattered about.  Amidst this splendor, the monkeys encountered an ascetic woman clad in bark and black antelope skin, radiating an aura of glory.  Astonished, they paused, and Hanuman approached her, inquiring about her identity and the ownership of the cave.

summary: No summary available
content: Within the resplendent grove, the monkeys discovered lotus ponds filled with clear water, surrounded by golden trees shining like the morning sun, adorned with golden fish and large lotuses.  They also found palaces of gold and silver, and mansions decorated with gems and pearls.  Fruit-bearing trees, buzzing with golden bees, offered various honeys.  The cave contained spacious couches and seats inlaid with gems and gold, along with piles of gold, silver, and bell-metal vessels.  Aloe wood, sandalwood, food, roots, fruits, conveyances, fine textiles, blankets, and deerskins were plentiful.  Heaps of gleaming gold lay scattered throughout. Amidst this wealth, the monkeys encountered an ascetic woman, dressed in bark and black antelope skin, radiating an aura of peace.  Astonished, they paused, and Hanuman approached her, inquiring about her identity and to whom the cave belonged.

summary:
Canto LI
Telling the monkeys how the cave was brought into existence, the ascetic
woman, whose name was Swayamprabha, feeds the newcomers, the
monkeys, with fruits and roots etc., and, when they had taken
rest, inquires of them what brought them to that spot
content: Hanuman addressed Swayamprabha, the ascetic woman residing in the cave, explaining that his group, driven by hunger, thirst, and exhaustion, had stumbled into the darkness-shrouded cave.  Upon entering the large cavern, they were bewildered by the dazzling array of objects within, fearing it might be an illusion.  He questioned the ownership and origin of the golden trees, the food, the opulent palaces and mansions adorned with gold and gems, the fragrant blossoms, and the golden lotuses in the clear water, even wondering about the golden fish and turtles.  Hanuman implored Swayamprabha to explain these wonders to them, as they were ignorant of their nature. Swayamprabha began her explanation by revealing that a powerful magician named Maya had created the golden grove. She explained that Maya was formerly the architect of the demon chiefs and that he had created the magnificent palace through his skill and the boon he received from Brahma, which granted him the creative power and resources of Sage Sukra. Maya enjoyed this power and the grove for a time until Indra, angered by Maya's attachment to the nymph Hema, slew him.  Brahma then bestowed the grove and its luxuries upon Hema. Swayamprabha, daughter of Merusavarni, revealed that she guarded Hema's home as a favor to her friend, the skilled dancer and singer.  She then inquired about the monkeys' purpose in the grove and how they had found this hidden place, requesting they share their story after they had eaten and rested.

summary:
Canto LI
Telling the monkeys how the cave was brought into existence, the ascetic
woman, whose name was Swayamprabha, feeds the newcomers, the
monkeys, with fruits and roots etc., and, when they had taken
rest, inquires of them what brought them to that spot
content: Swayamprabha continued her explanation of the grove's origins.  Maya, the architect of the demons, created this magnificent palace after performing intense austerities for thousands of years. Brahma granted him a boon equivalent to the wealth of Sage Sukra, including creative power, resources, and architectural knowledge. Maya enjoyed this power and the grove for a time until Indra, enraged by Maya's attachment to the nymph Hema, killed him. Brahma subsequently gifted the grove and its luxuries to Hema. Swayamprabha, daughter of Merusavarni, explained that she safeguards Hema's home as a favor to her friend, a skilled dancer and singer. She then questioned the monkeys' purpose for being in the grove, asking why they journeyed through such challenging terrains, and how they discovered this hidden place.  She invited them to share their full story after they had eaten and refreshed themselves.

summary:
Canto LII
Narrating the whole story from the entry of Sri Rama into the forest round
about the Pampa lake down to the entry of the monkeys into the cave, and
complimenting Swayamprabha on the hospitality shown by her and
wishing to repay her hospitality, Hanuman inquires of her if he
can be of any service to her. Swayamprabha, however, declines
his offer saying that, being engaged in the practice of
virtue, she has no purpose to achieve through another
content: Hanuman, addressing Swayamprabha, recounted their journey. After resting and enjoying the fruits offered by her, Hanuman explained their mission.  He described how Rama, the noble king, comparable to Indra and Varuna, entered the Dandaka forest with his brother Lakshmana and wife Sita.  Sita was abducted from Janasthana by Ravana. Hanuman continued, explaining they served Sugriva, a valiant monkey king and friend of Rama.  Sugriva sent them, including Angada and other leading monkeys, to the southern region protected by Yama and inhabited by Sage Agastya. Their mission: to find Ravana and Sita.  Having searched the forest and facing hunger, they found a large, dark cave concealed by vegetation.  Birds emerged from the cave, wet and covered in pollen, suggesting water within.  Hanuman encouraged the other monkeys to enter, hoping to find water. They cautiously proceeded into the darkness, holding onto each other.

summary:
Canto LII
Narrating the whole story from the entry of Sri Rama into the forest round
about the Pampa lake down to the entry of the monkeys into the cave, and
complimenting Swayamprabha on the hospitality shown by her and
wishing to repay her hospitality, Hanuman inquires of her if he
can be of any service to her. Swayamprabha, however, declines
his offer saying that, being engaged in the practice of
virtue, she has no purpose to achieve through another
content: The monkeys, emerging from the cave wet and covered in pollen, confirmed Hanuman's suspicion of water within.  He urged them to enter, a sentiment shared by the group, eager to find relief.  Driven by their thirst and the urgency of their mission, they ventured deep into the dark cave, holding onto each other.  Hanuman reminded them of their purpose: to find Sita and Ravana.  He then addressed Swayamprabha, explaining their plight.  Hungry and weary, they had gratefully accepted her hospitality. In return, they offered their services.  Swayamprabha, pleased with their visit, declined their offer, stating her virtuous life required no assistance.  Hanuman, however, pressed the matter, explaining that they had exceeded the time allotted by Sugriva for their search and were now in danger.  He pleaded with her to help them escape the cave. He further emphasized their failed mission due to their entrapment, highlighting the importance of their task.

summary:
Canto LII
Narrating the whole story from the entry of Sri Rama into the forest round
about the Pampa lake down to the entry of the monkeys into the cave, and
complimenting Swayamprabha on the hospitality shown by her and
wishing to repay her hospitality, Hanuman inquires of her if he
can be of any service to her. Swayamprabha, however, declines
his offer saying that, being engaged in the practice of
virtue, she has no purpose to achieve through another
content: Hanuman, anxious about overstaying their mission deadline, implored Swayamprabha to help them escape the cave, emphasizing the importance of their task to find Sita and Ravana.  Swayamprabha agreed to help them, using her ascetic powers.  She instructed the monkeys to close their eyes, assuring them that this was necessary for their safe passage.  The monkeys complied, covering their eyes with their hands, eager to escape.  Swayamprabha swiftly transported them out of the cave. Reassuring the monkeys, she pointed out their surroundings – the Vindhya range and the Prasravana mountain on one side and the Indian Ocean on the other – and bid them farewell before returning to the cave.

summary:
Canto LIII
While the monkeys were feeling dejected on coming out of the cave to see the
trees covered with blossom, which led them to conclude that the latter half
of winter had approached, Prince Angada reminds them of the fierceness
of Sugriva and says that a fasting till death on their part will be
preferable to an ignominious death at the latter’s cruel hands. Another
monkey general, Tara, for his part suggests that they should
take shelter in Swayamprabha’s cave and thus escape the wrath
of Sugriva, who dare not enter the cave. The monkeys fall in
with the latter’s view and make up their mind to re-enter
the cave and take up their abode in it
content: The monkeys discovered the vast, roaring ocean.  Their allotted month to search for Sita had expired while exploring Maya's illusory mountain fortress.  Seeing the blossoming trees near the Vindhya mountains, they realized spring had arrived, and they had failed their mission.  Angada, the prince, addressed the anxious monkeys, reminding them they had left Kiskindha in the month of Aswina under Sugriva's command, and that time had now run out.  He praised their experience, loyalty, and strength, reminding them they had placed him in charge of the expedition.  He declared that since they had failed Sugriva's mission, death was certain, and proposed fasting until death as a preferable alternative to facing Sugriva's wrath, given the monkey king's fierce temper and newfound authority.

summary:
Canto LIII
While the monkeys were feeling dejected on coming out of the cave to see the
trees covered with blossom, which led them to conclude that the latter half
of winter had approached, Prince Angada reminds them of the fierceness
of Sugriva and says that a fasting till death on their part will be
preferable to an ignominious death at the latter’s cruel hands. Another
monkey general, Tara, for his part suggests that they should
take shelter in Swayamprabha’s cave and thus escape the wrath
of Sugriva, who dare not enter the cave. The monkeys fall in
with the latter’s view and make up their mind to re-enter
the cave and take up their abode in it
content: Facing certain death for failing to locate Sita, Angada, the prince, proposed fasting to death rather than facing Sugriva's wrath. He lamented his predicament, highlighting Sugriva's temper and his own prior conflict with the king.  The other monkeys agreed, fearing Sugriva's anger and Rama's potential indifference. They resolved to return to Sugriva only if they found Sita, otherwise, they would choose death. Tara, however, suggested returning to the well-provisioned, inaccessible cave, where they would be safe from everyone, including Sugriva, Rama, and even Indra.  Reassured by Tara and Angada, the monkeys agreed to this plan, seeking a way to avoid Sugriva's deadly punishment.

summary:
Canto LIII
While the monkeys were feeling dejected on coming out of the cave to see the
trees covered with blossom, which led them to conclude that the latter half
of winter had approached, Prince Angada reminds them of the fierceness
of Sugriva and says that a fasting till death on their part will be
preferable to an ignominious death at the latter’s cruel hands. Another
monkey general, Tara, for his part suggests that they should
take shelter in Swayamprabha’s cave and thus escape the wrath
of Sugriva, who dare not enter the cave. The monkeys fall in
with the latter’s view and make up their mind to re-enter
the cave and take up their abode in it
content: The monkeys, fearing Sugriva's wrath, decided to return to him only if they found Sita. Otherwise, they would choose death. Tara, addressing their fears, proposed returning to the magically fortified and inaccessible cave. He highlighted the cave's abundance of resources – flowers, water, food, and drink – and assured them of safety from everyone, including Indra, Rama, and Sugriva.  Hearing Tara and Angada's encouraging words, the monkeys agreed, eager to find a way to avoid Sugriva's punishment and survive.

summary:
Canto LIV
Seeking to divert the minds of the other monkeys from the rebellious note
struck by Tara, whose advice readily found favour with Angada and others,
Hanuman warns Angada of the dire consequences which were sure
to follow in case he chose to stay away from home and, assuring him
of Sugriva’s amiability and disarming all his fears about him,
tries to convince him that they had better return to Kiskindha
and report themselves to their king
content: Hanuman, observing Angada's inclination to follow Tara's advice, perceived a potential challenge to Sugriva's rule. He saw Angada as a formidable force, intelligent, strong, and growing in influence, akin to the waxing moon.  Recognizing the need to sway Angada back to Sugriva's side, Hanuman employed his eloquence to create discord among the monkeys.  He then attempted to intimidate Angada by highlighting the fickle nature of the monkey troop and their unlikely acceptance of his leadership without their families. Hanuman emphasized his own loyalty to Sugriva, along with that of Jambavan, Nila, and Suhotra, asserting they would not be swayed by Angada, whether through persuasion or force.  He cautioned Angada against challenging a stronger opponent like Sugriva, reminding him that even the seemingly impenetrable cave they sought refuge in could be easily destroyed by Lakshmana's powerful arrows, which were capable of shattering even mountains.

summary:
Canto LIV
Seeking to divert the minds of the other monkeys from the rebellious note
struck by Tara, whose advice readily found favour with Angada and others,
Hanuman warns Angada of the dire consequences which were sure
to follow in case he chose to stay away from home and, assuring him
of Sugriva’s amiability and disarming all his fears about him,
tries to convince him that they had better return to Kiskindha
and report themselves to their king
content: Hanuman continued his efforts to dissuade Angada from rebellion. He argued that a weaker individual should avoid conflict with a stronger one, advising Angada against opposing Sugriva.  He warned that Lakshmana could easily destroy the seemingly impenetrable cave with his powerful arrows, comparing their force to Indra's thunderbolt and emphasizing their ability to shatter even mountains.  He predicted that if Angada stayed, the other monkeys, driven by fear and concern for their families, would abandon him, leaving him isolated and vulnerable, even to the smallest threat. Hanuman reiterated that Lakshmana's arrows would be merciless.  Finally, he offered an alternative: return with him to Sugriva, submit, and be reinstated in Kiskindha.  He painted Sugriva as a virtuous, trustworthy ruler who cared for Angada's mother and lacked other heirs, further incentivizing Angada's return.

summary:
Canto LV
Sick of hearing the praises of Sugriva uttered by Hanuman and pointing out the

faults of Sugriva and denouncing him,

Angada proclaims to the monkeys his

decision to undertake a fast unto death and actually sits down to fast.
Condemning Sugriva with tears in their eyes and making the mountain
caverns resound with their tale of woe commencing from the appearance

of Sri Rama on the scene and
to observe a fast till death, the
content: Angada, responding to Hanuman's respectful words, declares Sugriva unfit to rule, citing his supposed lack of morality and stability. He accuses Sugriva of taking his elder brother's wife while he was still alive, and questions Sugriva's loyalty, recalling how he sealed the cave entrance while his brother Vali fought, and later forgot Rama's kindness.  Angada claims Sugriva's search for Sita was motivated by fear of Lakshmana, not righteousness.  He expresses distrust in Sugriva, believing he would eliminate Angada, a potential rival to the throne.  Angada, fearing imprisonment or worse, announces his decision to fast unto death rather than return to Kishkindha. He instructs the monkeys to convey his well-being to Sugriva, Rama, Lakshmana, and his mothers, Ruma and Tara, specifically asking them to comfort Tara.

summary:
Canto LV
Sick of hearing the praises of Sugriva uttered by Hanuman and pointing out the

faults of Sugriva and denouncing him,

Angada proclaims to the monkeys his

decision to undertake a fast unto death and actually sits down to fast.
Condemning Sugriva with tears in their eyes and making the mountain
caverns resound with their tale of woe commencing from the appearance

of Sri Rama on the scene and
to observe a fast till death, the
content: Angada declares he will fast unto death at that very spot rather than return to Kishkindha. He instructs the monkeys to inform Sugriva, Rama, Lakshmana, and his mothers, Ruma and Tara, of his decision and well-being, emphasizing the need to comfort Tara. He predicts Tara will be devastated by the news of his death.  After speaking, Angada sits down on the sacred grass, grief-stricken. The other monkeys, weeping and lamenting, join his fast, denouncing Sugriva and praising Vali.  They formally begin their fast by sipping water, facing east on the sacred grass.  These monkeys, contemplating their imminent deaths, recall the events leading to this crisis: Rama's exile, Dasaratha's death, the havoc in Janasthana, Sita's abduction, Jatayu's death, Vali's death, and Rama's anger.  As the mountain fills with the roaring of numerous monkeys resembling mountain peaks, the caves resonate with their cries, like the sky echoing with thunder.

summary:
Canto LVI
Praising Providence out of joy at the prospect of deriving sustenance for a
long time on seeing a band of monkeys even as it emerged from a cave of
the Vindhyan range on hearing their noise, the vulture king, Sampati (the
elder brother of Jatayu), expresses his determination to eat them all up
one after another. Depressed to hear the vow and declaring Jatayu,
who had met his death at the hands of Ravana in the service of
Sri Rama, while trying to deliver Sita from the former’s clutches,
as more fortunate than any of them, who were threatened
with death even before accomplishing the purpose of Sri
Rama, Angada reproaches Kaikeyi who was at the root
of the whole mischief. Distressed to hear these words
of Angada and telling Angada that he was the
elder brother of Jatayu, and at the same time
inquiring about the death of his younger
brother, Sampati requests Angada to help
him slide down from his high post
adding that, his wings having been
burnt, he was unable to fly
content: Sampati, Jatayu's brother, a powerful and long-lived vulture, emerged from a Vindhyan cave.  Seeing the monkeys gathered, he rejoiced, believing they were destined to be his long-awaited meal. He declared his intention to devour them one by one as they succumbed to starvation.  Overhearing this, a worried Angada remarked to Hanuman that this felt like a deliberate attack orchestrated by Yama, the god of death, disguised as Sampati, to thwart their search for Sita.  He lamented that Rama's mission and Sugriva's orders remained unfulfilled while a new crisis arose.  Angada praised Jatayu's selfless act of defending Sita, contrasting it with their own arduous, yet unsuccessful search.  He declared Jatayu fortunate to have died fighting Ravana, escaping the perceived tyranny of Sugriva, and achieving a blessed state. Angada concluded that the monkeys were now endangered due to Dasaratha's death, Jatayu's demise, and Sita's abduction.

him slide down from his high post
adding that, his wings having been
burnt, he was unable to fly
content: Sampati, lamenting his hunger, declared his intention to devour the monkeys. Angada, distressed, questioned their misfortune and praised Jatayu's noble sacrifice for Rama, contrasting it with their own struggles. He attributed their current plight to Dasaratha's death, Jatayu's demise, and Sita's abduction, blaming Kaikeyi's actions for their predicament.  Overhearing Angada's lament, Sampati, intrigued and moved, inquired about the details of his brother Jatayu's death, recalling him with fondness.  Unable to fly due to his burnt wings, he requested the monkeys' assistance in descending from the mountain, expressing his eagerness to learn about Jatayu, Dasaratha, and Rama.

brother, Sampati requests Angada to help
him slide down from his high post
adding that, his wings having been
burnt, he was unable to fly
content: Sampati, expressing gratitude for their assistance, stated his desire to descend from the mountain peak.  He shared his joy at hearing Jatayu's praises after such a long time, describing his younger brother as virtuous and valiant.  Eager to learn more, he asked the monkey leaders about Jatayu's demise, inquiring specifically about the circumstances of his death in Janasthana.  He also inquired about the fate of King Dasaratha, a friend of Jatayu and father of Rama, whom he described as beloved and respected.  Due to his sun-scorched wings, Sampati was unable to fly and therefore requested the monkeys' help in getting down.

summary:
Canto LVII

Having helped Sampati to climb down the mountain, Angada narrates
to him the whole story from the entry of Sri Rama into the
Dandaka forest to his own fasting
content: Angada, still grieving over Jatayu’s death, tried to reason with Sampati, who intended to eat the monkeys. Sampati’s words, though hoarse with sorrow, did not assuage the monkeys’ fear, as they remained suspicious of his motives.  The monkeys, already resigned to starvation, assumed Sampati would devour them. They reasoned that if they died this way, their purpose would be fulfilled, achieving a swift end.  Reaching this grim conclusion, the monkey leaders helped Sampati down from the mountain. Angada then began to narrate their story to Sampati. He spoke of his grandfather, the mighty monkey king Riksraja, and his two sons, Vali and Sugriva. He highlighted his father, Vali's, renowned strength and deeds.  Continuing his tale, Angada described how Rama, the noble son of Dasaratha and rightful king, entered the Dandaka forest with his brother Lakshmana and wife Sita, adhering to his father's wishes and the path of righteousness.  He then recounted Sita's abduction by Ravana and how Jatayu, a friend of Rama's father, witnessed the act and intervened. Despite his age and weakened state, Jatayu fought valiantly against Ravana, destroying his chariot and rescuing Sita, but ultimately perished in the battle.  Angada mourned Jatayu's death at the hands of the powerful Ravana, and how Rama performed his last rites. He further explained how Rama formed an alliance with his uncle, Sugriva, resulting in Vali’s death. After installing Sugriva as king, Rama tasked the monkeys with finding Sita. Despite their extensive search, they were unable to locate her, much like searching for sunlight in the darkness of night.

summary:
Canto LVII

Having helped Sampati to climb down the mountain, Angada narrates
to him the whole story from the entry of Sri Rama into the
Dandaka forest to his own fasting
content: Angada continued his story, explaining that Rama allied with his uncle, Sugriva, and killed Vali. Sugriva, who had been previously banished by Vali, was then installed as the new king of the monkeys.  Under Rama's direction, Angada and the other monkeys searched extensively for Sita, but their efforts proved fruitless. They thoroughly explored the Dandaka forest, even venturing into a hidden cavern built by the demon Maya.  Unfortunately, their month-long search, as stipulated by Sugriva, ended without success.  Having surpassed the allotted time, the monkeys, fearing Sugriva and Rama’s anger, resigned themselves to death by starvation.  They believed that even if they returned, their lives would be forfeit.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Sampati tells Angada how having once soared with his younger brother to the
neighbourhood of the solar orb in his eagerness to test their relative strength
and flying power, he had his own wings burnt in trying to save the wings
of Jatayu by spreading his own wings over them as a canopy, and
dropped on a peak of the Vindhyan range. Inquired by Angada if he
knew anything about the abduction of Sita, he acknowledged his
having seen a youthful lady being borne away by Ravana and
crying out ‘Rama, which now leads him to think that she was no
other than Sri Rama’s beloved spouse. Sampati further tells
Angada how, gifted as he was with an inordinately long
vision, he could see Lanka, the capital of Ravana, at a
distance of one hundred Yojanas, as well as the place
where Sita has been lodged. On his expressing a
desire to offer water to the spirit of his deceased
brother, the monkeys take him to the seashore and
bring him back on his having concluded the rite
content: Sampati, overcome with grief, revealed to the despairing monkeys that Jatayu, whom they spoke of as slain by Ravana, was his younger brother.  He lamented his inability to avenge his brother due to his old age and lack of wings.  Sampati recounted how he and Jatayu, in their youth, flew towards the sun.  Jatayu faltered near the sun's intense heat, and Sampati shielded him with his own wings, which were subsequently burnt. He fell upon the Vindhyas and lost track of his brother.  Hearing this, Angada asked Sampati if he knew Ravana’s whereabouts.  Sampati, though weakened, declared his intent to help Rama through his knowledge.  He claimed awareness of various realms and events, including conflicts between gods and demons, and pledged to assist in Rama’s quest despite his age and diminished strength.

Canto LVIII

Angada how, gifted as he was with an inordinately long
vision, he could see Lanka, the capital of Ravana, at a

where Sita has been lodged. On his expressing a
desire to offer water to the spirit of his deceased
brother, the monkeys take him to the seashore and
bring him back on his having concluded the rite
content: Sampati, despite his weakened state, vowed to aid Rama's quest using his knowledge.  He claimed awareness of celestial events and declared his intent to help Rama through words.  He recounted seeing Ravana abducting a beautiful, jeweled woman who cried out "Rama!" repeatedly. Her silken robe contrasted sharply with Ravana's dark complexion.  Sampati deduced that this woman must be Sita.  He then revealed Ravana's location: the island city of Lanka, a hundred yojanas away, built by Vishwakarma.  He described the city's opulent architecture and Sita’s imprisonment within Ravana's palace. Sampati urged the monkeys to travel swiftly to Lanka’s southern shore, assuring them of a safe return, and explained the varying flight heights of different birds, beginning with the lowest fliers.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Sampati tells Angada how having once soared with his younger brother to the
neighbourhood of the solar orb in his eagerness to test their relative strength
and flying power, he had his own wings burnt in trying to save the wings
of Jatayu by spreading his own wings over them as a canopy, and

where Sita has been lodged. On his expressing a

brother, the monkeys take him to the seashore and
bring him back on his having concluded the rite
content: Sampati advises the monkeys to travel to Lanka's southern shore, a hundred yojanas across the sea, where they will find Ravana.  He assures them of a safe return and details the varying flight heights of different birds, from the lowest (sparrows and grain-eaters) to the highest (Garuda). He mentions vultures, his own kind, are descended from Garuda's brother, Aruna.  Sampati declares his intent to avenge his brother Jatayu's death at Ravana's hands, an act facilitated by the monkeys' journey.  He claims powerful vision, inherited from his lineage, enabling him to see Lanka and Sita. Sampati further explains that vultures can see great distances due to their diet and ancestry.  He then urges the monkeys to find a way across the sea, promising their quest will be successful. Finally, Sampati requests to be taken to the seashore to perform a ritual offering of water to his deceased brother, Jatayu.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Sampati tells Angada how having once soared with his younger brother to the
neighbourhood of the solar orb in his eagerness to test their relative strength
and flying power, he had his own wings burnt in trying to save the wings
of Jatayu by spreading his own wings over them as a canopy, and
dropped on a peak of the Vindhyan range. Inquired by Angada if he
knew anything about the abduction of Sita, he acknowledged his
having seen a youthful lady being borne away by Ravana and

other than Sri Rama’s beloved spouse. Sampati further tells
Angada how, gifted as he was with an inordinately long
