where Sita has been lodged. On his expressing a
desire to offer water to the spirit of his deceased
brother, the monkeys take him to the seashore and
bring him back on his having concluded the rite
content: The mighty monkeys carried Sampati, whose wings had been burned, to the seashore. After Sampati performed a ritual offering of water to his deceased brother, the monkeys returned him to his home.  Having learned the location of Ravana and Sita, the monkeys rejoiced, energized by this crucial information.

summary:
Canto LIX
Interrogated by Jambavan as to how Sampati came to know about Sita’s,
abduction by Ravana, the vulture tells him that it was his son,
Suparswa, who related to him how, one day, while he was ranging
along the seashore in search of food, he had seen a young
lady, shining brightly as gold, being borne away by an
ogre, of whom he had come to know later on from

the words of an eminent sage that they were
no other than Sita and Ravana, respectively
content: Sampati, the king of vultures, relayed the information about Sita's abduction to the monkey leaders, who were relieved to hear the news.  Jambavan, the foremost of monkeys and bears, inquired about the details of Sita's abduction, including who witnessed it, who abducted her, and her current location. He questioned who would dare defy the might of Rama and Lakshmana's arrows. Sampati consoled the monkeys, who were eagerly awaiting news about Sita, and began his tale. He described how he fell on a remote mountain long ago, becoming old and frail. His son, Suparswa, cared for him by bringing him food.  One day, as Sampati was hungry, Suparswa returned empty-handed.  Suparswa explained that he had positioned himself near the Mahendra mountain to hunt for creatures from the sea. He then saw someone resembling a dark cloud carrying a woman as bright as the dawn. Suparswa intended to seize them for Sampati's meal but was asked to allow them passage.

summary:
Canto LIX
Interrogated by Jambavan as to how Sampati came to know about Sita’s,
abduction by Ravana, the vulture tells him that it was his son,
Suparswa, who related to him how, one day, while he was ranging
along the seashore in search of food, he had seen a young
lady, shining brightly as gold, being borne away by an
ogre, of whom he had come to know later on from

the words of an eminent sage that they were
no other than Sita and Ravana, respectively
content: Suparswa, positioned near Mahendra mountain, waited to hunt sea creatures for his father, Sampati. He spotted a figure, dark as kohl, carrying a woman radiant as the sunrise.  Intending to capture them, Suparswa was instead politely asked to let them pass. He honored the request, acknowledging the power of gentle words. The figure, radiating brilliance, swiftly departed. Afterward, airborne sages informed Suparswa that the woman was Sita and, fortunately, still alive. They also identified her abductor as Ravana. Suparswa watched the grieving Sita, stripped of her jewels and silken veil, desperately calling for Rama and Lakshmana. He relayed this news to his father, Sampati, who, despite his wingless state, felt compelled to help Rama.  Sampati expressed confidence in the monkey leaders, sent by Sugriva, highlighting their strength and wisdom. He declared that Rama's arrows, divinely crafted and fletched with buzzard feathers, possessed the power to protect or conquer the three worlds. Though Ravana was mighty, Sampati believed nothing was beyond the capabilities of the assembled monkey leaders.

summary:
Canto LIX
Interrogated by Jambavan as to how Sampati came to know about Sita’s,
abduction by Ravana, the vulture tells him that it was his son,
Suparswa, who related to him how, one day, while he was ranging
along the seashore in search of food, he had seen a young
lady, shining brightly as gold, being borne away by an
ogre, of whom he had come to know later on from

the words of an eminent sage that they were
no other than Sita and Ravana, respectively
content: Suparswa described Sita’s plight to his father, Sampati. He saw Sita, stripped of her jewels and fine silks, overwhelmed with grief and crying out for Rama and Lakshmana.  Even after hearing this, Sampati, being wingless, felt helpless.  However, he resolved to use his wisdom and knowledge to guide the monkey leaders sent by Sugriva.  Sampati reminded them of their strength, intelligence, and divine purpose. He emphasized the power of Rama and Lakshmana’s divinely crafted arrows, capable of protecting or conquering the three worlds. While acknowledging Ravana's might, Sampati expressed complete confidence in the monkeys' ability to overcome any obstacle and urged them to act swiftly and decisively in their search for Sita.

summary:
Canto LX
Having inspired confidence in the monkeys, Sampati proceeds to relate to
Angada and others, who longed to hear more about Sita, the story of
Sita’s abduction in greater detail. He tells them how, having fallen on
the Vindhyan range with his wings completely burnt by the scorching
sun, he swooned and regained consciousness only on the
seventh day, when he came to know that it was the Vindhyan
range on which he had fallen. Descending with difficulty
from the eminence, he made his way into the hermitage
of a sage named Nisakara, and bowed down to him.
Recognizing him to be Sampati, the sage inquired
as to how his wings had come to be burnt
content: Sampati, having performed his brother's last rites, addressed the assembled monkeys, urging them to listen attentively as he recounted how he learned of Sita's whereabouts. He described how, long ago, he fell upon the Vindhyan mountains, his wings burned by the sun.  He lay unconscious for six days, and upon waking, was disoriented.  Eventually, he recognized the landscape: the Vindhyan range by the southern sea, teeming with birds and riddled with caves.  He recalled a hermitage in that area, once home to the sage Nisakara, a place he and Jatayu had frequented. Sampati descended the mountain and made his way to the hermitage site, where fragrant breezes blew and trees overflowed with blossoms and fruit. He sought shelter under a tree, hoping to see Nisakara, and soon spotted the radiant sage returning from his bath, accompanied by a variety of animals.

summary:
Canto LX
Having inspired confidence in the monkeys, Sampati proceeds to relate to
Angada and others, who longed to hear more about Sita, the story of
Sita’s abduction in greater detail. He tells them how, having fallen on
the Vindhyan range with his wings completely burnt by the scorching
sun, he swooned and regained consciousness only on the
seventh day, when he came to know that it was the Vindhyan
range on which he had fallen. Descending with difficulty
from the eminence, he made his way into the hermitage
of a sage named Nisakara, and bowed down to him.
Recognizing him to be Sampati, the sage inquired
as to how his wings had come to be burnt
content: Sampati, seeking refuge near Nisakara's hermitage, found the area lush with blossoming, fruit-laden trees and filled with sweet-smelling breezes.  He rested beneath a tree, hoping to see the sage. Soon, Nisakara appeared, radiating light, returning from his bath.  A variety of animals, including bears, deer, tigers, lions, and snakes, surrounded him like devotees around a generous benefactor. As the sage reached his hermitage, the animals dispersed to their own homes.  Nisākara, noticing Sampati, re-emerged from his dwelling and inquired about Sampati’s condition, observing his missing feathers and burned wings, and recalling a time when Sampati and his brother, Jatayu, powerful shape-shifting vultures, would visit him.  He asked Sampati to explain his injuries, whether caused by illness, accident, or some punishment.

summary:
Canto LXI

Relating to Sage Nisakara the circumstances which led to his being burnt,
Sampati takes before the sage a vow to give up the ghost by

leaping from a
content: Sampati recounted to the sage Nisakara his rash pursuit of the sun.  He explained his difficulty in speaking, due to injuries and shame from his earlier defeat by Indra.  He and his brother Jatayu, fueled by pride, had vowed to follow the sun to its setting point.  From the sky, they saw cities the size of chariot wheels, heard music and ornaments jingling, and observed groups of women in red clothing.  Ascending further, the forest below resembled grass, mountains looked like pebbles, and rivers appeared as threads. The Himalayas, Vindhyas, and Mount Meru looked like elephants in a pond.  Overcome by exhaustion, fear, and confusion, they fainted.  Disoriented, they struggled to regain their senses, the sun appearing as large as the earth. Jatayu then descended without warning, prompting Sampati to follow.

summary:
Canto LXI

Relating to Sage Nisakara the circumstances which led to his being burnt,
Sampati takes before the sage a vow to give up the ghost by

leaping from a
content: Sampati's vision faltered, his mind struggling to comprehend the overwhelming sight of the sun. With immense effort, he refocused, the sun appearing as large as the earth itself.  Jatayu suddenly descended without warning, prompting Sampati to follow.  In the descent, Sampati shielded Jatayu with his own wings, saving his brother from being burned by the sun's intense heat.  Sampati, however, was not so fortunate; his own wings were severely burned. He believed Jatayu had fallen somewhere in Janasthana while he himself landed, unconscious and injured, on the Vindhya mountains.  Devastated by the loss of his rulership, his brother, his wings, and his strength, Sampati resolved to end his life by leaping from a mountain peak.

summary:
Canto LXII
Sampati communicates to the monkeys the prediction made by Sage
Nisakara, who could foresee future events by dint of his
intuitive perception, that descending as Sri Rama
elsewhere the Lord will dispatch monkeys in
quest of Sita to the Vindhyan range and
that Sampati will get back his wings
as soon as he tells the monkeys
the whereabouts of Sita
content: Sampati recounted his woes to the sage Nisakara.  The sage comforted him, prophesying that Sampati would regain his wings, sight, strength, and vitality.  Nisakara shared a prophecy about a king named Dasaratha and his exceptionally powerful son, Rama.  Rama, along with his brother Lakshmana and wife Sita, would be exiled to the forest.  There, the demon king Ravana would abduct Sita.  Though offered delicacies, the grieving Sita would refuse all food.  Indra would send her a divine dish, but Sita would offer it to her absent husband and brother-in-law.  Monkeys, acting as Rama's messengers, would eventually find Sampati, who was instructed to tell them Sita's location. The sage advised Sampati to remain where he was, patiently awaiting the restoration of his wings. Nisakara explained that by staying, Sampati would perform a great service by helping Rama find Sita and defeat the evil Ravana, benefiting everyone, including Sampati himself.  Although eager to see Rama and Lakshmana, Nisakara expressed his desire to soon depart from his physical body.

summary:
Canto LXII
Sampati communicates to the monkeys the prediction made by Sage
Nisakara, who could foresee future events by dint of his
intuitive perception, that descending as Sri Rama
elsewhere the Lord will dispatch monkeys in
quest of Sita to the Vindhyan range and
that Sampati will get back his wings
as soon as he tells the monkeys
the whereabouts of Sita
content: Nisakara advised Sampati against leaving, assuring him that his wings would be restored in due time.  He emphasized that by staying put, Sampati would perform a vital service to humanity by aiding Rama in locating Sita and vanquishing Ravana. This act would benefit everyone, including Sampati, the princes Rama and Lakshmana, Brahmins, sages, and even Indra.  Nisakara confessed his own longing to see Rama and Lakshmana, but declared his readiness to relinquish his physical body, having already attained spiritual enlightenment.

summary:
Canto LXIII
Even while Sampati was thus talking with the monkeys, a pair of beautiful
wings shoot forth on his sides. Feeling transported with joy at their
sight and showing them to the monkeys, nay, assuring them
that their purpose too will likewise be achieved, Sampati
soars to the sky in order to test the power of his
wings. Feeling encouraged by this, the monkeys
too resume their journey further south
content: Sampati recounts Sage Nisakara's prophecy about Rama sending monkeys to the Vindhya mountains to search for Sita, and how Sampati would regain his wings upon revealing Sita's location. He waited for centuries, enduring agony and doubt after Nisakara's passing.  Sampati recalls reprimanding his son Ravana for not rescuing Sita, especially given the distress it caused Rama and Lakshmana and out of respect for the late King Dasaratha.  As he spoke, Sampati's wings miraculously regrew. Overjoyed, he declared this a sign of the monkeys' success in their search. He then took flight, testing his newfound wings. Inspired by Sampati's words and the miraculous return of his wings, the monkeys continued their journey south, filled with renewed hope and determination to find Sita.

summary:
Canto LXIII
Even while Sampati was thus talking with the monkeys, a pair of beautiful
wings shoot forth on his sides. Feeling transported with joy at their
sight and showing them to the monkeys, nay, assuring them
that their purpose too will likewise be achieved, Sampati
soars to the sky in order to test the power of his
wings. Feeling encouraged by this, the monkeys
too resume their journey further south
content: Sampati, rebuking his son Ravana's inaction in rescuing Sita, lamented the distress caused to Rama, Lakshmana, and the late King Dasaratha. As he spoke, his wings miraculously returned.  Overjoyed, Sampati showed his vibrant, new wings to the monkeys, declaring their reappearance a sign of the monkeys' impending success.  He then soared into the sky, testing his regained ability to fly.  Inspired by this miraculous event and Sampati's encouraging words, the monkeys, reinvigorated and confident, continued their southward journey to find Sita.

summary:
Canto LXIV
On the monkey heroes getting despondent at the sight of the (Indian) ocean,
which they found difficult to cross, Angada inquires of them if
anyone of them was capable of leaping across the sea dividing
India from Lanka. When all keep mum, he encourages all
to proclaim each his own leaping capacity and prowess
content: Upon learning Sita's location from Sampati, the jubilant monkeys, strong as lions, journeyed towards the ocean, the gateway to Ravana's kingdom.  Arriving at the northern shore of the vast Indian Ocean, they were awestruck by its immensity. The ocean seemed to shift in nature, appearing calm in some parts, turbulent in others, and elsewhere, its waters rose like mountains.  The monkeys became disheartened, fearing the ocean's depths and the creatures within.  They questioned how their mission could possibly succeed. Witnessing their despair, Angada, their leader, reassured and encouraged them, emphasizing that giving in to fear would only lead to failure.  He reminded them of the importance of courage and determination when faced with a challenge.  As night passed, Angada again conferred with the elder monkeys, surrounded by the monkey army, a force as impressive as Indra's own. He questioned who among them possessed the strength and resolve to cross the formidable ocean and help Sugriva fulfill his promise to rescue Sita.

summary:
Canto LXIV
On the monkey heroes getting despondent at the sight of the (Indian) ocean,
which they found difficult to cross, Angada inquires of them if
anyone of them was capable of leaping across the sea dividing
India from Lanka. When all keep mum, he encourages all
to proclaim each his own leaping capacity and prowess
content: Angada, surrounded by the monkey army, addressed the esteemed elders and the assembled troops. He questioned who among them possessed the strength and courage to cross the vast ocean and fulfill their promise to Sugriva by rescuing Sita. He asked who could leap across the immense distance, alleviate their fears, and allow them to return home safely to their families. Angada further inquired who would enable them to joyfully reunite with Rama, Lakshmana, and Sugriva.  He implored anyone capable of this feat to step forward and declare their ability, offering a reassuring vow to the anxious assembly.  Met with silence, Angada reiterated his plea, reminding the monkeys of their renowned strength, lineage, and honored status, urging them to reveal their leaping capabilities.

summary: No summary available
content: The monkey leaders, prompted by Angada, each declared their leaping prowess. Gaja claimed a distance of ten yojanas, Gavaksha twenty, Sarabha thirty, and Rsabha forty.  Gandhamadana boasted of fifty yojanas, Mainda sixty, Dwivida seventy, and Susena eighty.  Jambavan, respectfully acknowledging his comrades, admitted his age had diminished his former strength, yet declared he could still leap ninety yojanas. He emphasized the importance of their mission, endorsed by Sugriva and Rama, and recalled his past glory of circumambulating Lord Trivikrama during Bali's sacrifice.

summary: No summary available
content: Jambavan declared his leaping capacity to be ninety yojanas, but reminded the monkeys that his strength had diminished with age. He recounted how, in his youth, he had circumambulated Lord Trivikrama during Bali's sacrifice.  Now, however, he felt his current capability was insufficient for the task at hand, the rescue of Sita. Angada responded respectfully, offering to leap one hundred yojanas, though uncertain of his return. Jambavan, however, dissuaded Angada, arguing that a leader should not be risked on such a perilous mission.  He emphasized Angada's importance to their cause, likening him to the protected mistress of a household and the essential root of their endeavor.  Jambavan affirmed that Angada, with his wisdom and valor, was the key to Sita’s rescue and that the monkeys relied upon him for success.

summary: No summary available
content:
Jambavan continued to emphasize Angada's importance to their mission.  He argued that Angada must be protected, like a cherished wife, because he is fundamental to their success, the very root of their endeavor.  Just as a tree thrives only when its roots are healthy, so too will their search for Sita succeed only with Angada's leadership. Jambavan reminded Angada of his wisdom and strength, declaring him the key to rescuing Sita, the one the monkeys relied upon.

Angada responded that if neither he nor any other leader stepped forward, they would be forced to fast until death.  He stressed the importance of Sugriva's command and the dire consequences of failing him.  Returning to Kishkindha without Sita would surely invite Sugriva's wrath and lead to their demise. Given these grave circumstances, Angada urged Jambavan, whose wisdom he greatly respected, to find a solution.  Jambavan reassured Angada that his purpose would not be thwarted. He declared he would appeal to someone capable of accomplishing their mission, and turned to Hanuman.

summary:
Canto LXVI
Reminding Hanuman of his descent from the loins of the wind-god
through Afijana and of the circumstances which led to his getting the
name of Hanuman and also how he received boons from Brahma
(the creator) and others, and glorifying him, Jambavan
encourages Hanuman to leap across the sea and thereby
deliver the monkeys from their wretched plight
content: Jambavan addressed the disheartened monkey army and Hanuman, highlighting Hanuman's knowledge and strength. He compared Hanuman's power and might to Sugriva, Rama, and Lakshmana, even likening him to the mighty Garuda, whom Jambavan had witnessed lifting giant serpents from the sea.  Jambavan emphasized the equivalence of Hanuman's arm strength to Garuda's wing strength, urging Hanuman to utilize his capabilities. He recounted the story of Anjana, a celestial nymph cursed to be reborn as a monkey princess.  Anjana, known for her beauty, married Kesari.  One day, while adorned in fine silks on a mountain peak, the wind-god stole her garment, revealing her form.  Captivated by her beauty, the wind-god embraced her.

summary:
Canto LXVI
Reminding Hanuman of his descent from the loins of the wind-god
through Afijana and of the circumstances which led to his getting the
name of Hanuman and also how he received boons from Brahma
(the creator) and others, and glorifying him, Jambavan
encourages Hanuman to leap across the sea and thereby
deliver the monkeys from their wretched plight
content: Jambavan continues his encouraging speech to Hanuman, recounting the story of Hanuman's birth. He describes how the wind-god became captivated by Anjana's beauty, removing her garment as she stood on a mountain peak.  He then embraced her, resulting in Hanuman's conception.  Jambavan emphasizes that Hanuman's birth was divinely ordained, with the wind-god foretelling his son's strength and intelligence. As a child, Hanuman, mistaking the rising sun for a fruit, leaped towards it, reaching an astonishing height.  His ascent was interrupted by Indra, who, angered by Hanuman's audacity, struck him with a thunderbolt, injuring his chin and giving him the name "Hanuman." The enraged wind-god then ceased his activity, causing turmoil in the three worlds until the gods, led by Brahma, pacified him.  Brahma then bestowed upon Hanuman the boon of invulnerability in battle.

content: Jambavan continues to inspire Hanuman, reminding him of the incident where Indra, enraged by Hanuman's attempt to reach the sun, struck him with a thunderbolt, fracturing his chin and giving him the name "Hanuman."  The wind-god, distressed by his son's injury, stopped his activity, throwing the three worlds into chaos.  The gods, realizing their mistake, appeased the wind-god.  Brahma then granted Hanuman the boon of invulnerability to weapons. Indra, impressed by Hanuman's resilience, bestowed upon him another boon – the power to choose the time of his own death. Jambavan emphasizes Hanuman’s divine lineage and inherent strength, comparing him to the wind-god in power and agility. He highlights Hanuman's past feats of strength, including circling the earth multiple times and gathering herbs for the gods. Jambavan urges Hanuman to display his prowess by leaping across the ocean, reminding him that the hopes of the entire monkey army rest on him.

content: Jambavan exhorts Hanuman to leap across the vast ocean, declaring that Hanuman's capacity for such a feat surpasses all other beings. He questions Hanuman's inaction while the other monkeys despair, urging him to demonstrate his immense speed and strength, recalling the legendary strides of Vishnu.  Inspired by Jambavan's words and confident in his own power, Hanuman expands his form, filling the monkey army with joy and anticipation.

summary: No summary available
content: Hanuman, preparing to leap across the ocean, grew in size, filling the monkeys with joy and awe. They watched him, amazed, as he expanded his form, much like creation watched Narayana as Trivikrama.  His strength and size increased as the elder monkeys praised him.  He whirled his tail, his form radiating excellence.  Like a lion stretching in a mountain cave, Hanuman yawned, his mouth bright like a fire.  Rising among the monkeys, his hair standing on end with excitement, he addressed the elders, proclaiming his lineage and power. He declared himself the son of the wind-god, equal to his father in leaping ability. He boasted of his strength, claiming he could circle Mount Meru a thousand times, push back the ocean with his arms, and cause it to overflow with the force of his legs. He even asserted he could outpace Garuda in flight and overtake the sun in its journey across the sky.

summary: No summary available
content: Hanuman continues to boast of his immense power and speed, assuring the monkeys of his ability to cross the ocean. He claims he could push back the ocean, cause it to overflow, and even circle Mount Meru thousands of times.  He asserts he could surpass Garuda, the king of birds, and even the sun in speed.  He proclaims his ability to return from the sun before sunset without touching the earth.  Hanuman further boasts he could dry up oceans, shatter mountains, and scatter clouds. He compares his passage through the sky to the Milky Way, visible to all, and declares that only Garuda or his father, the wind-god, could match his power. He envisions his leap as a lightning bolt across the sky and likens his form to Vishnu's Trivikrama form.  Confident in his ability to find Sita, he encourages the monkeys to rejoice, estimating his leap to span ten thousand Yojanas.

summary: No summary available
content: Hanuman, preparing for his immense leap, envisions himself crossing the ocean with the speed and power of Vishnu's Trivikrama form. He expresses certainty in finding Sita and reassures the monkey troop.  He reiterates his immense power, claiming he could even retrieve nectar from Indra or Brahma.  Jambavan, overjoyed by Hanuman's confidence and strength, encourages him, assuring him of the monkeys' support and prayers.  Hanuman, in turn, warns them of the immense force he's about to exert, describing how Mount Mahendra will bear the brunt of his launch.  He describes the mountain, abundant in flora, fauna and mythical creatures, which trembles under his pressure.  The mountain's creatures – elephants, deer, Gandharvas, Vidyadharas, and serpents – are startled and flee as rocks dislodge and water springs erupt.  Finally, Hanuman, focusing his mind and immense power, sets his sights on Lanka.

summary: No summary available
content: Hanuman, confident in his strength, declares his ability to uproot and move Lanka itself.  His bold proclamation amazes the assembled monkeys, who watch him intently. Jambavan, filled with joy and relief, praises Hanuman and assures him of their support and prayers. The monkeys, led by Jambavan, promise to perform rituals and prayers for Hanuman's success.  Hanuman, acknowledging their support, warns them of the immense force he is about to unleash upon Mount Mahendra in preparation for his leap. He describes the mountain's peaks as strong and capable of withstanding the force. The mountain, rich with diverse flora, fauna, and mythical beings, becomes Hanuman's launching pad.  As Hanuman prepares, Mount Mahendra groans under the pressure.  The mountain's varied inhabitants – including elephants, deer, Gandharvas, Vidyadharas, and serpents – are startled and flee.  Rocks dislodge, water springs erupt, and the mountain appears adorned with the hissing serpents half-emerged from their disturbed homes.  The scene is one of intense activity and upheaval as Hanuman gathers his immense power, focusing his mind and strength, ready to launch himself towards Lanka.

summary: No summary available
content: Hanuman, preparing for his monumental leap, caused Mount Mahendra to tremble and its inhabitants to flee. Abandoned by the rishis (sages) in fear, the mountain appeared desolate. With focused mind and immense power, the courageous Hanuman, renowned amongst the monkeys, mentally fixed his destination on Lanka, ready to commence his journey.

summary:
Canto I

Desirous of reaching Lanka in order to discover Sita, Hanuman takes a leap
from a peak of Mount Mahendra and honouring with the touch of his hand
Mount Mainaka, which rose from the bottom of the sea to provide rest
on its peak to Hanuman, encounters Surasa (mother of Nagas), sent
by the gods in the form of an ogress, who was waiting for Hanuman
with her mouth open to devour him. He enters her belly
assuming a minute form and comes out without killing her.
Further, he is met by another ogress, Simhika by name,
standing with her mouth wide open to gulp Hanuman.

He enters her mouth after assuming a minute form
and comes out after splitting her belly open and

killing her. Then contracting his body into a
minute form again in order to preclude
the attention of the ogres, he descends
on a mountain peak outside Lanka
content: Hanuman, determined to find Sita, prepared for a monumental leap across the ocean.  He stood atop Mount Mahendra, a figure of immense strength and resolve, resembling a powerful bull.  He moved with ease and confidence through the lush vegetation, startling the birds and unintentionally crushing plants and small animals under his massive frame.  Mount Mahendra, vibrant with colorful minerals and frequented by mythical beings, provided a stunning backdrop for Hanuman's preparations.  He paused to honor the sun god, Indra, the wind god, Brahma, and other divine beings before focusing his intent on his journey south.  Drawing power from the wind god, Hanuman grew in size, observed by his fellow monkeys with a mixture of awe and affection.  Finally, bracing himself for the immense leap, he pressed his hands and feet against the mountain, causing it to tremble and shed its blossoms.

content: As Hanuman braced himself, the trees on Mount Mahendra released a fragrant shower of blossoms, covering the mountainside in a vibrant floral display.  Under the immense pressure of Hanuman's grip, the mountain spurted water like a rutting elephant, and streams of gold, silver, and antimony flowed down its slopes.  Massive boulders, some containing realgar, tumbled down the mountain's flanks.  The creatures living within the mountain cried out in distress as their home was squeezed, filling the air with their frightened calls.  Snakes, enraged by the disturbance, bit at the falling rocks, their venom causing the stones to burst into flames and shatter.  Even the mountain's medicinal herbs couldn't neutralize the potent venom.  Ascetics, Vidyadharas, and their families, mistaking the tremors for an attack by powerful spirits, fled the mountain in fear.

content: The Vidyadharas, leaving their homes in the mountain, ascended into the sky.  They left behind their golden wine jugs, precious utensils, food, animal hides, and swords.  Adorned with garlands and red markings, the men flew upwards, their wives joining them, adorned with jewelry.  From the sky, they looked down upon Mount Mahendra.  They listened as sages, bards, and Siddhas, also gathered in the air, discussed Hanuman's impending leap across the ocean.  They spoke of his strength and purpose, to reach the distant shore for Rama and the monkeys.  The Vidyadharas then turned their attention to Hanuman, a towering figure radiating immense power.  He shook his body, his hair bristling like flames, and a roar erupted from him like thunder.  As he prepared to jump, he unfurled his massive tail, which resembled a serpent being clutched by Garuda.

content: Hanuman prepared for his immense leap. Bracing his powerful arms, which resembled iron clubs, against the mountain, he crouched, drawing in his arms, neck, and feet.  He gathered his strength, courage, and focus, looking upwards to assess the vast distance to Lanka.  Taking a deep breath, he steadied himself, his ears flattened against his head. Then, the mighty monkey, a true leader among his kind, declared to the others that he would fly with the speed of one of Rama's arrows towards Ravana's Lanka.  He vowed to find Sita, the daughter of Janaka, or else continue to the heavens themselves to search for her. If she was not found even there, he would capture Ravana and bring him back in chains. He swore to return successful, with Sita by his side, or to bring back Lanka itself, uprooted with Ravana still upon it.  With this declaration, the powerful Hanuman launched himself into the sky with tremendous speed and force, likening himself to the mighty Garuda. As he ascended, the trees of the mountain, their branches laden with flowers and agitated lapwings, were uprooted by the force of his leap and carried aloft with him into the clear sky.

content: Hanuman's powerful leap carried him swiftly towards Lanka.  The force of his departure uprooted trees, which momentarily followed him through the air as if bidding farewell to a departing loved one.  These trees, including sturdy sal trees, trailed behind him like a king's retinue.  Adorned with blossoms from the uprooted trees, the enormous monkey presented a striking image against the sky.  As he progressed, the trees, unable to maintain their airborne journey, fell into the sea.  The falling blossoms created a beautiful spectacle on the ocean's surface, resembling stars scattered across the sky. The fragrant, multicolored blossoms further enhanced Hanuman's appearance, making him look like a cloud illuminated by lightning.  As the flowers settled on the water's surface, the sea resembled the night sky filled with newly risen stars. With arms outstretched, Hanuman soared through the air, his limbs resembling serpents emerging from a mountain peak.  He gazed down at the vast ocean as if about to drink it dry and then upwards, as though eager to consume the sky itself.  His eyes, bright as lightning, blazed like twin fires atop a mountain as he followed the path of the wind towards his destination.

content: Hanuman's imposing form was striking as he journeyed. His large, round, reddish-brown eyes shone brightly like the sun and moon together.  His face, reflecting the color of his copper-colored nose, glowed like the sun at dusk.  His coiled tail streamed behind him as he flew, resembling Indra's banner raised high in the sky.  With his curled tail and gleaming white teeth, the intelligent Hanuman, son of the wind-god, shone like the sun surrounded by a halo. His deep red rump was vibrant, like a mountain of red chalk split open.  The wind rushing through Hanuman's armpits as he flew over the sea roared like thunder.  He resembled a meteor streaking across the northern sky, trailing a luminous tail.  Like a mighty elephant adorned with a decorative girth, Hanuman's tail wrapped around his waist as he soared. His body cast a deep shadow on the water below, making him appear like a ship sailing across the sea, propelled by the wind. The sea churned and became turbulent in his wake due to his immense speed and power. He crashed through the towering waves with his chest, as the combined force of the wind he created and the natural wind whipped the ocean into a frenzy.

content: Hanuman, a powerful monkey, surged forward, creating massive waves in the salty sea.  He sped across the ocean, seemingly counting the enormous swells that rose like the towering Mounts Meru and Mandara.  The water, propelled upwards by his immense speed, hung suspended in the air with the clouds, creating a breathtaking spectacle resembling a vast expanse of autumnal clouds. Whales, crocodiles, alligators, and turtles, normally hidden beneath the waves, were exposed by the churning water, their forms revealed like limbs uncovered by the removal of clothing. Serpents, witnessing Hanuman's swift passage through the sea, mistook him for Garuda, the majestic king of birds.  Hanuman's shadow, vast and elongated, stretched across the water, its beauty enhanced by his rapid movement.  Trailing behind him like a procession of white clouds against the sky, his shadow added to the spectacle. The mighty monkey, radiating immense energy, resembled a winged mountain as he traversed his aerial path. The sea beneath him was transformed into a trough by the force of his passage.  Like Garuda soaring through the sky, Hanuman flew through the air, appearing like a powerful wind scattering the clouds. He pulled along clouds of various hues – white, reddish, blue, and madder-colored – creating a vibrant display.  As he repeatedly entered and emerged from these cloud masses, he resembled the moon, alternately hidden and revealed.

content: Celestial beings showered Hanuman with flowers as he journeyed. The sun did not burn him, and a gentle breeze refreshed him as he flew, focused on his mission for Rama. Sages praised him, while gods and Gandharvas sang to sustain him on his arduous quest.  Nagas, Yakshas, and ogres marveled at Hanuman's strength and composure.  The ocean god, wanting to honor Rama's lineage, considered how to help Hanuman, fearing the monkey's exhaustion. He decided that Hanuman must rest and called upon Mainaka, a golden mountain submerged in the ocean, to rise and offer respite.  The ocean god reminded Mainaka of his purpose as a barrier against demons from the netherworld and urged the mountain to rise from the water.

content: Varuna, the ocean god, addressed Mainaka, the golden mountain submerged beneath the waves. He announced Hanuman's arrival, a powerful monkey dedicated to serving Rama.  Varuna emphasized the importance of assisting anyone connected to the revered lineage of Ikswaku, urging Mainaka to fulfill his duty and rise from the ocean to offer Hanuman rest. Mainaka was reminded of his purpose as a barrier against netherworld demons and encouraged to support Hanuman's vital mission.  Heeding Varuna's call, Mainaka emerged from the water, its peaks adorned with gold, trees, and creepers, shining brightly like the sun.  The mountain's sudden appearance, with its golden peaks inhabited by Kinnaras and serpents, transformed the sky, reflecting a golden hue.

content: Hanuman, encountering a mountain rising from the sea, initially perceived it as an obstacle.  He struck the mountain, but Mainaka, recognizing Hanuman's strength and purpose, rejoiced. Mainaka, assuming human form, offered Hanuman rest and refreshment upon his peak.  He explained that the ocean god, Varuna, requested him to honor Hanuman, who was on a mission for Rama. Mainaka reiterated that repaying a service is a fundamental principle, and he was extending hospitality on behalf of the ocean, grateful to Rama's ancestors. Varuna informed Mainaka that Hanuman was on an arduous, long journey and would benefit from rest.  Mainaka offered Hanuman a variety of appealing fruits, roots, and bulbs, encouraging him to refresh himself before continuing his mission.  He acknowledged Hanuman's renowned virtues and recognized him as a prominent figure among the vigorous and agile monkeys.

content: Mainaka, addressing Hanuman, emphasizes the importance of honoring guests, especially one as distinguished as Hanuman, son of the mighty wind-god Vayu.  He praises Hanuman's speed, equaled only by his father. Mainaka explains that by honoring Hanuman, he indirectly honors Vayu. He then recounts a story from Satyayuga, when mountains had wings. Their swift movement caused fear among gods and rishis.  Indra, angered by this disruption, severed the mountains' wings. Mainaka reveals that Vayu saved him from Indra's wrath by throwing him into the sea, thus preserving his form.  Grateful to Vayu, Mainaka feels indebted to his son, Hanuman. He sees Hanuman's arrival as an opportunity to repay this debt and urges Hanuman to accept his hospitality and rest. Hanuman, acknowledging Mainaka's kind words and offer, politely declines, explaining that his urgent mission prevents him from accepting the hospitality. He assures Mainaka that his goodwill is appreciated.

summary:
Canto I

Desirous of reaching Lanka in order to discover Sita, Hanuman takes a leap
from a peak of Mount Mahendra and honouring with the touch of his hand
Mount Mainaka, which rose from the bottom of the sea to provide rest
on its peak to Hanuman, encounters Surasa (mother of Nagas), sent
by the gods in the form of an ogress, who was waiting for Hanuman
with her mouth open to devour him. He enters her belly
assuming a minute form and comes out without killing her.
Further, he is met by another ogress, Simhika by name,
standing with her mouth wide open to gulp Hanuman.

He enters her mouth after assuming a minute form
and comes out after splitting her belly open and

killing her. Then contracting his body into a
minute form again in order to preclude
the attention of the ogres, he descends
on a mountain peak outside Lanka
content: Hanuman, feeling the urgency of his mission to find Sita, declined Mainaka's offer of rest.  He explained that his pledged word and the dwindling time compelled him to continue his journey.  Touching the mountain as a gesture of respect, Hanuman ascended into the sky.  Deities of the mountain and ocean honored him with blessings as he soared.  Leaving the mountain and ocean far behind, Hanuman flew higher, following the path of the wind, his father.  He continued his unsupported flight, looking down at the receding mountain.  The gods, Siddhas, and Rishis praised Hanuman's unwavering focus and dedication to his mission, particularly his resistance to the tempting offer of rest.  The gods present, including Indra, were pleased with Mainaka's generous hospitality towards Hanuman. Indra, deeply gratified, expressed his pleasure to Mainaka, granting him freedom from fear and praising his valuable assistance to Hanuman.  Indra acknowledged Hanuman's arduous journey undertaken for Rama's sake.  Mainaka rejoiced at Indra's satisfaction.

content: Hanuman, having quickly crossed the sea, encountered Surasa, the mother of Nagas.  The gods, Siddhas, and Rishis, desiring to test Hanuman's strength and resourcefulness, had summoned Surasa.  They instructed her to intercept Hanuman in the guise of a fearsome ogress.  Surasa, taking on a monstrous form, confronted Hanuman and declared that he was destined to be her meal.  She claimed a boon granted by Brahma allowed her to devour anyone who crossed her path.  Hanuman, unfazed, responded with a joyful countenance, explaining his mission to find Sita, who had been abducted by Ravana. He appealed to Surasa’s sense of duty, reminding her that she resided within Rama’s dominion and therefore should assist him.

content: Hanuman, determined to continue his journey, assured Surasa that he would willingly enter her mouth after completing his mission to find Sita and inform Rama.  Surasa, bound by a boon to devour anyone crossing her path, insisted he enter her mouth immediately.  To test Hanuman's strength, Surasa opened her enormous mouth. Hanuman, accepting the challenge, grew to an immense size, matching Surasa's ever-widening jaws in a continuous escalating contest of size and power.

content: Hanuman, displaying his intelligence, shrunk himself to the size of a thumb upon seeing Surasa's terrifying, gaping maw, which resembled a hellish abyss with its extended tongue.  He swiftly entered and exited her mouth.  Reappearing in the sky, the mighty Hanuman addressed Surasa, stating he had fulfilled her demand and now would proceed to find Sita.  Surasa, witnessing Hanuman emerge from her mouth like the moon escaping Rahu's grasp, reverted to her true form and granted him safe passage, urging him to reunite Sita with Rama.  Observers marveled at Hanuman's remarkable escape, praising his ingenuity and strength.  He then continued his journey over the vast ocean, flying with the speed of Garuda, the divine eagle, towards Lanka.

content: Hanuman, swift as Garuda, the divine eagle, soared through the celestial realm.  This airy pathway was refreshed by flowing waters and teeming with birds. Celestial musicians and dancers graced its expanse, alongside Airavata, Indra's magnificent elephant. Gleaming aerial chariots, drawn by lions, elephants, tigers, birds, and serpents, adorned the path.  Radiant beings, rewarded for past deeds, resided there, their forms as hard as diamonds, shining like fire. Agni, the fire god, traversed this path, carrying offerings to the deities. The sun, moon, planets, stars, and constellations illuminated the sky.  Sages, Gandharvas, Nagas, and Yakshas populated this ethereal region.  It was a pristine and boundless space frequented by Vishvavasu, the Gandharva king. Indra's elephants also journeyed there, along the serene orbits of the sun and moon, beneath a vast canopy created by Brahma for all living beings. Heroes ascended this path to the heavens, their valor earning them a place among the stars, amidst the celestial artists, the Vidyadharas.  Hanuman, like the wind god himself, pulled along masses of clouds, ranging in color from the deep hues of aloe wood to red, yellow, and white.

content: Hanuman, pulling along vast, colorful clouds, flew through the sky, sometimes disappearing within them, then reappearing like the moon during the monsoon season.  He was observed by all as he travelled.  As he moved through the air, a gigantic, shape-shifting ogress named Simhika spotted him.  She thought to herself, "This creature will finally satisfy my hunger after such a long wait!"  She then grabbed hold of his shadow. Hanuman, feeling his movement restricted, looked around to see what had impeded him.  He saw a monstrous figure emerging from the salty depths below. He recognized this grotesque creature as Simhika, the shadow-grabbing ogress Sugriva had warned him about.  Understanding the situation, the clever Hanuman grew to an immense size, like a massive rain cloud.

content: Hanuman continued his journey, growing larger as he flew. Simhika, a monstrous ogress, saw him and attacked, her mouth opening wide like a chasm.  The quick-witted Hanuman shrunk himself and entered her mouth. He then tore through her vital organs with his sharp nails and escaped. Simhika fell lifelessly into the sea. Other sky-dwelling beings witnessed this and praised Hanuman for his strength and ingenuity, encouraging him to continue his quest. Hanuman, honored by their praise, continued his journey through the air, soon spotting the distant shoreline.

Mount Mainaka, which rose from the bottom of the sea to provide rest

content: Hanuman, soaring through the air, observed an island adorned with diverse trees and the groves of Malaya mountain.  He also noted the surrounding sea, the coastal marshes, the trees within them, and the river mouths.  His own form, vast and cloud-like, filled the sky.  Concerned that his size might attract unwanted attention from ogres, the clever Hanuman shrank back to his normal size.  He then further reduced his form, much like Vishnu returning to his dwarf form after his three cosmic strides. Reaching the far shore, Hanuman, capable of many forms, considered his mission to find Sita and returned to his natural state.  He landed on a peak of Mount Lamba, rich with fruit trees and flowers.  Finally, spotting Lanka on the summit of Trikuta mountain, he descended there, startling the local animals with his powerful monkey form.  He had crossed the vast, monster-filled ocean and now stood on the shore, gazing at Lanka, a city as magnificent as Amaravati.

summary:
Canto X

Hanuman catches sight of Ravana reposing on an excellent couch decked
with various ornaments, himself adorned with jewels of every description.
Perceiving Mandodari graced with bright jewels and lying asleep on a
wonderful couch not far from her husband in the midst of dancing
girls lying in a disorderly state with diverse musical instruments
clasped to their bosom, and taking her to be Sita, Hanuman
gets enraptured and exhibits his joy through various
simian gestures such as kissing the end of his tail
content: Hanuman surveyed the opulent chamber, observing a crystal dais adorned with precious stones.  Upon the dais, he saw a lavish couch, crafted from cat’s-eye gems and ivory, embellished with gold and intricate paintings, and covered with luxurious bedding. Above, a white canopy, decorated with celestial wreaths, shone like the moon. Nearby, another magnificent couch, overlaid with gold and draped with Asoka flowers, was being fanned by attendants. It was perfumed with fragrant scents, covered with a fine bed and sheepskin, and further adorned with exquisite floral garlands.  Hanuman then saw Ravana, richly dressed and adorned with jewels, his skin gleaming with red sandalwood paste, asleep on the splendid couch.  The powerful, multi-armed Ravana, resembling Mount Mandara, rested after a night of revelry.  Disgusted by the sight of the sleeping Ravana, Hanuman recoiled.

with various ornaments, himself adorned with jewels of every description.
Perceiving Mandodari graced with bright jewels and lying asleep on a

girls lying in a disorderly state with diverse musical instruments

content: Hanuman, positioned on a lower level of the dais, intently observed the sleeping Ravana. The magnificent couch upon which Ravana rested resembled a grand mountain.  Hanuman studied Ravana's outstretched, gold-adorned arms, marked with scars from past battles, including encounters with Indra's elephant, Airavata, and weapons like Indra's thunderbolt and Vishnu's discus. The arms, muscular and strong, with well-formed features, lay upon the bed like slumbering serpents.  Covered in fragrant sandalwood paste and adorned with jewels, the arms showed signs of having been massaged.  Their very sight was once enough to terrify other beings. Hanuman compared the arms, resting on the couch, to large serpents asleep in a mountain cave. Ravana's powerful form, accentuated by these arms, resembled a mountain with two peaks.  The room filled with the potent aroma of Ravana's breath, a mixture of fruits, flowers, food, and drink.

Canto X

with various ornaments, himself adorned with jewels of every description.
Perceiving Mandodari graced with bright jewels and lying asleep on a

gets enraptured and exhibits his joy through various
simian gestures such as kissing the end of his tail
content: Ravana lay on a magnificent couch, adorned with pearls and jewels. His face gleamed with earrings, a golden diadem slightly askew. His broad chest, smeared with red sandalwood paste and a necklace, added to his regal appearance. He wore a white silken loincloth and a yellow upper garment. His breathing was heavy like a serpent's hiss, and his form resembled a sleeping elephant in the Ganges river.  Four golden lamps illuminated him like lightning flashing on a cloud.  His wives lay at his feet, their faces radiant as the moon, adorned with earrings and celestial garlands. Some women, skilled in music and dance, rested in Ravana's embrace, wearing exquisite jewelry. Their ears were decorated with gold earrings set with diamonds and cat's-eye gems, and they wore armlets.  The room, filled with their moon-like faces and beautiful earrings, sparkled like a starlit sky.  Exhausted from revelry and intimacy, Ravana's slender-waisted consorts slept in various positions. One woman, even in sleep, appeared graceful, her body adorned and poised as if in a dance.

summary:
Canto X

Hanuman catches sight of Ravana reposing on an excellent couch decked
with various ornaments, himself adorned with jewels of every description.
Perceiving Mandodari graced with bright jewels and lying asleep on a
wonderful couch not far from her husband in the midst of dancing
girls lying in a disorderly state with diverse musical instruments

content: Amidst the sprawling palace chambers, the scene was one of post-revelry slumber.  Numerous women lay asleep in various states of disarray.  One woman clutched her six-stringed vina, her form as delicate as a lotus flower adrift on a river current.  Another, dark-eyed, cradled a small drum as if it were a beloved child.  A third embraced a tambourine, her expression suggesting a long-awaited reunion with a lover.  Yet another, with lotus-like eyes, held her vina close, as though embracing her husband. A graceful woman, seemingly mid-dance even in her sleep, clutched a seven-stringed vina.  Another woman, adorned in gold, rested with a wooden tom-tom against her body. A slender-waisted woman lay with a tabor nestled in her arms, exhausted from the night's festivities. One woman held a drum, another slung across her back, like a mother with two children. A lotus-eyed woman, overcome by drink, slept deeply with an Adambara instrument in her arms.  Another, having overturned a water jug, lay like a spring garland sprinkled with water.  Finally, a woman with breasts like golden pitchers succumbed to sleep, her hands resting upon them.  Another, moon-faced and lotus-eyed, embraced a companion, both lost in drunken slumber.

summary:
Canto X

Hanuman catches sight of Ravana reposing on an excellent couch decked
with various ornaments, himself adorned with jewels of every description.
Perceiving Mandodari graced with bright jewels and lying asleep on a
wonderful couch not far from her husband in the midst of dancing
girls lying in a disorderly state with diverse musical instruments
clasped to their bosom, and taking her to be Sita, Hanuman
gets enraptured and exhibits his joy through various
simian gestures such as kissing the end of his tail
content: Amidst the sleeping figures, Hanuman observed a woman reclining on a separate, opulent couch.  This woman, Mandodari, Ravana's wife, possessed a radiant, golden complexion and wore elaborate jewelry set with pearls and gems. Her beauty and adornments illuminated the palace chamber.  Mistaking her for Sita due to her captivating appearance and youthful vibrancy, Hanuman was overjoyed.  He expressed his excitement through typical simian actions: clapping, kissing his tail, pacing, climbing, and jumping.

summary:
Canto XI
Banishing by recourse to reason the thought that the lady whom he had seen
was Sita, Hanuman searches for Sita once more in the gynaeceum as well
as in the banqueting hall and sees there a number of women lying
in various states, as well as drinking vessels. He is seized with
fear of having incurred the sin of gazing on others’
wives; but the thought that he had looked on them
with a lust-free mind eases his conscience
content: Hanuman, after dismissing the idea that the woman he saw was Sita, continued his search within the palace's private chambers and banquet hall.  He observed many women sleeping in various states, some with drinking vessels nearby. He felt a pang of guilt for looking upon other men's wives, but reminded himself that his gaze was pure, without lust.  In the banquet hall, he saw women exhausted from gambling, singing, dancing, and drinking. Some rested on musical instruments, others on luxurious beds.  Hanuman observed thousands of beautifully adorned women discussing each other’s beauty and the meaning of songs, demonstrating their understanding of etiquette and proper language, seemingly engrossed in pleasure.  He also saw groups of young women sleeping after engaging in conversation about one another's appearances. These women, like the others, were described as possessing refined manners and speech.  Amongst these women, Ravana stood out, much like a bull amongst cows or a large elephant surrounded by females.

Canto XI

was Sita, Hanuman searches for Sita once more in the gynaeceum as well
as in the banqueting hall and sees there a number of women lying
in various states, as well as drinking vessels. He is seized with
fear of having incurred the sin of gazing on others’
wives; but the thought that he had looked on them
with a lust-free mind eases his conscience
content: Hanuman, the mighty monkey, searched the opulent banquet hall within Ravana's palace.  The hall was filled with every imaginable luxury.  He observed various meats laid out – venison, buffalo, and boar.  Large golden platters held untouched peacock and fowl.  He also saw dishes of boar, rhinoceros, porcupine, deer, and peacock preserved with curds and spices.  There were also various goat and hare dishes, partially eaten buffalo, a type of fish, and prepared ram meat.  A variety of chutneys and drinks were available, along with easily consumed delicacies and seasoned snacks.  The floor, strewn with discarded jewelry, drinking vessels, and scattered fruit offerings, shimmered.  Couches and seats of gold and precious stones added to the hall's splendor, giving it a warm glow even without firelight. Various natural and prepared drinks lay beside expertly prepared meats, flavored with a range of ingredients.  Hanuman also noted beverages made from sugar, honey, flowers, and fruits, each infused with different aromatic powders.

Canto XI
Banishing by recourse to reason the thought that the lady whom he had seen
was Sita, Hanuman searches for Sita once more in the gynaeceum as well

with a lust-free mind eases his conscience
content: Hanuman continued his search of Ravana's palace, observing the banquet hall floor, which was charmingly decorated with scattered flowers and an array of gold and crystal vessels, along with other ornate containers.  The floor also held a collection of silver and gold pitchers filled with various fine drinks.  He saw numerous gold and jeweled vessels, some full of wine, others half-empty or completely drained.  Hanuman observed different areas where various foods and beverages were laid out, some partially consumed. He also came across the sleeping quarters where some beds were empty, while others were occupied by women sleeping alone or embracing one another. One woman, overcome by sleep, had even taken another's covering.  The gentle breaths of the sleeping women caused their garments and garlands to stir slightly.

Canto XI
Banishing by recourse to reason the thought that the lady whom he had seen
was Sita, Hanuman searches for Sita once more in the gynaeceum as well

wives; but the thought that he had looked on them
with a lust-free mind eases his conscience
content: Hanuman, searching Ravana's palace, encountered fragrant breezes carrying the scents of sandalwood, wine, flowers, and incense. He observed women of varying complexions within the palace, some asleep, their beauty resembling closed lotus flowers.  Despite a thorough search of the gynaeceum, he couldn't find Sita. Hanuman then became concerned about the impropriety of seeing other men's wives, especially in their vulnerable state of sleep. He reassured himself that his gaze was devoid of lustful intent, his mind focused solely on finding Sita.  He reasoned that observing Ravana's wives was unavoidable in his search for Sita, as women are typically found amongst other women.

summary:
Canto XI
Banishing by recourse to reason the thought that the lady whom he had seen
was Sita, Hanuman searches for Sita once more in the gynaeceum as well
as in the banqueting hall and sees there a number of women lying
in various states, as well as drinking vessels. He is seized with
fear of having incurred the sin of gazing on others’
wives; but the thought that he had looked on them
with a lust-free mind eases his conscience
content: Hanuman continued his search for Sita within Ravana's palace.  He logically deduced that one searches for something amongst its own kind, just as one wouldn't search for a missing woman amongst deer. With pure intentions, he thoroughly searched Ravana's entire gynaeceum but still couldn't find Sita.  Even amongst the celestial nymphs and daughters of gods, Gandharvas, and Nagas, Sita remained unseen.  Not finding her among the other women present, Hanuman quietly departed that area to continue his search elsewhere, diligently exploring every corner of the palace.

summary:
Canto XII
Having failed to discover Sita even after searching for her in the picture
gallery and other places, Hanuman suspects that she might have been
disposed of by Ravana and, thus meeting with frustration, falls into
the quagmire of despair. On second thought, however, he deprecates
despair as harmful and, falling back upon self-reliance,
resumes the search. But, failing to find her even on
searching for her all round, he becomes despondent again
content: Hanuman, eager to find Sita, searched the various buildings within Ravana's palace, including the arbors, picture galleries, and sleeping quarters, but she was nowhere to be found.  He began to despair, thinking that Sita must be dead since she wasn't visible despite his thorough search. He speculated that Ravana, known for his wicked deeds, might have harmed her, perhaps because of her unwavering virtue and commitment to her husband Rama. Another grim possibility crossed Hanuman's mind: perhaps Sita, upon seeing the grotesque and monstrous forms of Ravana's female attendants, had died of fright.  Hanuman worried about the consequences of his failure. He was concerned about facing Sugriva, the monkey king, and enduring his harsh punishment for failing to find Sita. He imagined the disappointment of the other monkeys, especially Jambavan and Angada, and dreaded having to explain his fruitless efforts. However, Hanuman rallied his spirits, reminding himself that perseverance is the key to success and happiness. Determined, he resolved to continue his search, focusing on areas of Ravana's domain he hadn't yet explored. He was committed to putting forth his utmost effort, clinging to hope and self-reliance.

summary:
Canto XII
Having failed to discover Sita even after searching for her in the picture
gallery and other places, Hanuman suspects that she might have been
disposed of by Ravana and, thus meeting with frustration, falls into
the quagmire of despair. On second thought, however, he deprecates
despair as harmful and, falling back upon self-reliance,
resumes the search. But, failing to find her even on
searching for her all round, he becomes despondent again
content: Hanuman continued his meticulous search for Sita within Ravana's palace. He thoroughly checked banquet halls, gardens, picture galleries, pleasure houses, alleys, and even seven-story buildings.  He also searched basements, sheds, and other outlying structures.  Leaving no stone unturned, he sprang up and down, opened and closed doors, entered and exited various spaces. Despite covering every nook and cranny of Ravana's inner chambers, Sita remained elusive. His search extended beyond the palace walls to the lanes, platforms, chasms, and lotus ponds. He encountered ogresses of various grotesque forms, beautiful Vidyadhara women, and Naga princesses whose faces shone like the full moon, some of whom had been captured by Ravana. Yet, Sita was not among them.  Disheartened by his fruitless efforts, Hanuman, son of the wind-god, succumbed to despair. He felt his efforts, and those of the monkey leaders, including his own perilous journey across the ocean, had been in vain.  Overwhelmed by grief, he descended from the aerial car and sank into deep contemplation.

summary:
Canto XIII
Coming out of the aerial car, Puspaka, and not finding Sita even on searching
for her on all sides, Hanuman concludes her to have been killed. Believing
that her untraceability, no matter whether it is reported to Srt Rama or not,
may lead to disastrous consequences, Hanuman decides not to return to
the mainland. Meanwhile, as he contemplates fasting till death or
suicide or the killing of Ravana, he catches sight of a grove
which he does not remember to have seen and before
proceeding to explore it mentally invokes the succour
of Rsis and gods for success in his undertaking
content: Hanuman, swift and agile, landed on the fortress wall from the aerial car, Puspaka. He searched Ravana's palace thoroughly but could not find Sita.  He lamented that despite repeated searches throughout Lanka – its pools, ponds, lakes, rivers, forests, and mountains – Sita remained elusive. Sampati had told him she was here, yet she was nowhere to be seen. Hanuman speculated on Sita's fate: perhaps she had fallen from Ravana's grasp mid-flight, or her heart gave way at the sight of the ocean while being carried away. Perhaps she perished from Ravana's forceful grip or fell into the sea while struggling to free herself.  Hanuman even considered the possibility that Sita, while protecting her virtue, was killed by Ravana or perhaps by his wives out of malice.

summary:
Canto XIII
Coming out of the aerial car, Puspaka, and not finding Sita even on searching
for her on all sides, Hanuman concludes her to have been killed. Believing
that her untraceability, no matter whether it is reported to Srt Rama or not,
may lead to disastrous consequences, Hanuman decides not to return to
the mainland. Meanwhile, as he contemplates fasting till death or
suicide or the killing of Ravana, he catches sight of a grove
which he does not remember to have seen and before
proceeding to explore it mentally invokes the succour
of Rsis and gods for success in his undertaking
content: Hanuman grieved, imagining Sita's fate. Perhaps while thinking of Rama, she perished. He pictured her crying out for Rama, Lakshmana, and Ayodhya before dying.  He considered that she might still be alive, imprisoned in Ravana's palace, weeping like a caged bird. He questioned how Sita, Rama's wife and a descendant of a noble lineage, could ever submit to Ravana. Hanuman realized that regardless of Sita's fate – whether hidden, lost at sea, or dead – the news would be devastating for Rama. He was torn, knowing that informing Rama might cause his death, but remaining silent would be a betrayal. Hanuman wrestled with this difficult decision, questioning the best course of action. He felt his mission would be a failure if he returned without Sita. His efforts to cross the ocean, infiltrate Lanka, and face the demons would be meaningless. He worried about what Sugriva, the monkeys, and Rama would say upon his return.  He feared that breaking the news of Sita's disappearance to Rama would lead to Rama's death from grief.

summary:
Canto XIII
Coming out of the aerial car, Puspaka, and not finding Sita even on searching
for her on all sides, Hanuman concludes her to have been killed. Believing
that her untraceability, no matter whether it is reported to Srt Rama or not,
may lead to disastrous consequences, Hanuman decides not to return to
the mainland. Meanwhile, as he contemplates fasting till death or
suicide or the killing of Ravana, he catches sight of a grove
which he does not remember to have seen and before
proceeding to explore it mentally invokes the succour
of Rsis and gods for success in his undertaking
content: Hanuman, distraught by Sita's apparent demise, considers the devastating consequences of her loss. He envisions a chain reaction of grief-stricken deaths: Lakshmana, consumed by devotion to Rama, would perish.  Bharata and Shatrughna, upon hearing of Rama and Lakshmana's deaths, would also succumb to sorrow.  Their mothers, Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi, would undoubtedly follow. Sugriva, loyal to Rama, would give up his life. Ruma, Sugriva's wife, overwhelmed by the loss of her husband, would also die. Tara, still mourning Vali, would perish as well.  Angada, losing his parents and Sugriva, would also end his life. The monkeys, devoted to their leader, would be driven to self-harm, some smiting their heads, while others would resort to suicide by poison, hanging, fire, fasting, or their own weapons. They would cease their playful activities in the forests and mountains. Devastated by Rama's loss, they would fall from mountaintops, along with their families and ministers.

summary:
Canto XIII
Coming out of the aerial car, Puspaka, and not finding Sita even on searching
for her on all sides, Hanuman concludes her to have been killed. Believing
that her untraceability, no matter whether it is reported to Srt Rama or not,
may lead to disastrous consequences, Hanuman decides not to return to
the mainland. Meanwhile, as he contemplates fasting till death or
suicide or the killing of Ravana, he catches sight of a grove
which he does not remember to have seen and before
proceeding to explore it mentally invokes the succour
of Rsis and gods for success in his undertaking
content: Overwhelmed by despair, Hanuman contemplates the repercussions of his perceived failure. He believes his return without Sita will bring devastation to the Ikshvaku dynasty and the monkey kingdom.  Determined not to face Sugriva without Sita, he resolves to remain in Lanka. He considers several options: living as a hermit, self-immolating on a funeral pyre, fasting to death, or drowning himself. He laments the apparent breaking of his glorious mission, a mission that had begun so promisingly.  Yet, amidst his despair, a glimmer of hope emerges. He reasons that while suicide presents many perils, persevering in life holds the possibility of eventual happiness and reunion. Thus, despite his profound grief, he chooses to continue his search for Sita.

for her on all sides, Hanuman concludes her to have been killed. Believing
that her untraceability, no matter whether it is reported to Srt Rama or not,
may lead to disastrous consequences, Hanuman decides not to return to
the mainland. Meanwhile, as he contemplates fasting till death or
suicide or the killing of Ravana, he catches sight of a grove
which he does not remember to have seen and before
proceeding to explore it mentally invokes the succour
of Rsis and gods for success in his undertaking
content: Hanuman, powerful and resolute, considered his options. He contemplated defeating Ravana, avenging Sita's abduction.  He even imagined capturing Ravana and presenting him to Rama.  Distressed by his inability to locate Sita, Hanuman resolved to continue searching Lanka. He feared Rama's reaction if he returned without her.  Determined to persevere, Hanuman chose to remain in Lanka, controlling his desires and actions, hoping to avoid causing further harm. He decided to explore a nearby grove of Asoka trees he hadn't yet searched, invoking the blessings of various deities for success in his quest. He prayed to Rama, Lakshmana, Sita, and various gods, including his father, the wind-god, before turning his attention towards the grove.

summary:
Canto XIII
Coming out of the aerial car, Puspaka, and not finding Sita even on searching
for her on all sides, Hanuman concludes her to have been killed. Believing
that her untraceability, no matter whether it is reported to Srt Rama or not,
may lead to disastrous consequences, Hanuman decides not to return to
the mainland. Meanwhile, as he contemplates fasting till death or
suicide or the killing of Ravana, he catches sight of a grove
which he does not remember to have seen and before
proceeding to explore it mentally invokes the succour
of Rsis and gods for success in his undertaking
content: Hanuman, son of the wind-god, approached the Asoka grove, mentally preparing himself for what he might find. He anticipated the grove to be heavily guarded by Ravana's forces, nestled within a dense forest.  He acknowledged the grove's sanctity, noting signs of careful cultivation. He observed that even the wind seemed to hold back within the grove, perhaps due to the presence of guards protecting the trees. To avoid detection by Ravana, Hanuman reduced his size.  He invoked the blessings of numerous deities – Brahma, the gods of fire, wind, and thunder, Varuna, the sun and moon gods, the Aswins, the wind deities, Vishnu, and any other unseen forces – for success in his mission. His ultimate desire was to find Sita and he yearned to see her face again, describing her features with admiration and concern for her current predicament under Ravana's cruel control. He lamented Sita's helplessness against the wicked Ravana, questioning if he would be able to find her that day.

summary:
Canto XIV
Leaping down to the enclosure of the Asoka grove and watching the loveliness
of the grove from the top of the wall, Hanuman enters the grove and,
leaping from tree to tree in the course of his search for Sita, catches
sight of an Asoka tree. Espying a stream running beside it and
expecting that Sita might turn up on the bank of the stream to
say her Sandhya prayers, he takes up his position on that
Asoka tree, hiding himself behind its leafy boughs
content: Hanuman, full of energy, jumped from the palace wall into the Asoka grove.  He was thrilled to see the blossoming trees – sal, asoka, bhavya, champaka, uddalaka, nagakesara, and mango trees, their fruits a vibrant red. The grove was lush with flowering climbers and buzzing with life, a vibrant tapestry of nature's beauty in the early spring.  He moved through the grove like an arrow, observing the diverse flora and fauna.  The grove was alive with the sounds of birds and the movement of deer, its reddish flowers glowing like the rising sun. The trees were laden with fruits and flowers, attracting cuckoos and bees. It was a place of natural beauty, delighting all creatures. As Hanuman searched for Sita, he disturbed the sleeping birds, causing a flurry of activity. The trees, shaken by the birds taking flight, showered colorful blossoms down on Hanuman, covering him in a fragrant blanket of petals.  He appeared as a vibrant mountain of flowers amidst the grove.  Seeing him move so swiftly amongst the trees, the creatures of the grove mistook him for the embodiment of Spring itself.

summary:
Canto XIV
Leaping down to the enclosure of the Asoka grove and watching the loveliness
of the grove from the top of the wall, Hanuman enters the grove and,
leaping from tree to tree in the course of his search for Sita, catches
sight of an Asoka tree. Espying a stream running beside it and
expecting that Sita might turn up on the bank of the stream to
say her Sandhya prayers, he takes up his position on that
Asoka tree, hiding himself behind its leafy boughs
content: Hanuman's frantic search for Sita continued within the Asoka grove. The ground, covered in fallen blossoms of various colors, resembled a richly adorned woman. As Hanuman, driven by urgency, moved swiftly through the trees, his actions caused a cascade of flowers and fruits to fall, stripping the trees bare. The trees, now devoid of their leaves, flowers, and fruits, appeared desolate, like gamblers who had lost everything. The once vibrant grove, now deserted by its bird population, seemed forlorn and uninviting due to Hanuman's disruptive passage. The grove, damaged by Hanuman's forceful movements, resembled a young woman whose appearance had been marred by rough treatment.  He broke the thick vines hanging from the trees as he searched, much like strong winds scatter rain clouds. Amidst this disarray, Hanuman noticed the opulent beauty of the grove's grounds, adorned with precious gems, silver, and gold.

summary:
Canto XIV
Leaping down to the enclosure of the Asoka grove and watching the loveliness
of the grove from the top of the wall, Hanuman enters the grove and,
leaping from tree to tree in the course of his search for Sita, catches
sight of an Asoka tree. Espying a stream running beside it and
expecting that Sita might turn up on the bank of the stream to
say her Sandhya prayers, he takes up his position on that
Asoka tree, hiding himself behind its leafy boughs
content:
Hanuman observed various ponds filled with pristine water, accessible by gem-encrusted steps.  Their sandy bottoms shimmered with pearls and corals and crystalline stones, while golden trees adorned their edges.  Water lilies and lotuses floated on the surface, attracting red geese and Cataka birds, their calls mingling with the sounds of swans and cranes.  Wide, pleasant streams, bearing sweet, nectar-like water, nourished the ponds.  The banks were shaded by trees, draped with flowering creepers, and surrounded by diverse shrubs, with openings in the foliage provided by Karavira trees.

He then spotted a towering mountain, its peak resembling a rain cloud. Its picturesque summit was surrounded by other peaks, its slopes dotted with caves and covered in a variety of trees, making it a truly beautiful sight.  From this mountain flowed a stream, reminiscent of a lover leaping from her beloved's embrace. The stream, adorned with overhanging trees, appeared like a distressed woman being held back by companions. Further along, the stream's flow was impeded by the trees' branches, creating the image of the lover returning to her beloved, reconciled.  Near the mountain, Hanuman also observed lotus ponds teeming with various bird species.

summary:
Canto XIV
Leaping down to the enclosure of the Asoka grove and watching the loveliness
of the grove from the top of the wall, Hanuman enters the grove and,
leaping from tree to tree in the course of his search for Sita, catches
sight of an Asoka tree. Espying a stream running beside it and
expecting that Sita might turn up on the bank of the stream to
say her Sandhya prayers, he takes up his position on that
Asoka tree, hiding himself behind its leafy boughs
content: Hanuman explored a meticulously crafted artificial pond, adorned with gem-inlaid steps and shimmering with pearl and coral sands.  Deer grazed amidst the surrounding thickets and grand mansions.  Trees laden with fruit and flowers provided ample shade, their branches like outstretched umbrellas.  Each tree stood on a raised platform, topped with a smaller golden platform. Amongst these, a striking golden Asoka tree, entwined with climbers and lush foliage, caught Hanuman's eye.  He also observed open fields, mountain springs, and other radiant golden trees. Their brilliance was so intense that Hanuman felt bathed in a golden glow. The wind rustled through the golden trees, creating a melodic sound like tinkling bells.  Hanuman, filled with anticipation, climbed the leafy Asoka tree, hoping to spot Sita from this vantage point.  He reasoned that, longing for Rama and consumed by sorrow, she might wander into view.  He noted the beauty of Ravana's Asoka grove, adorned with sandalwood, Campaka, and Bakula trees.  He then saw a picturesque lotus pond, attracting numerous birds, and surmised that Sita might visit it.

summary:
Canto XIV
Leaping down to the enclosure of the Asoka grove and watching the loveliness
of the grove from the top of the wall, Hanuman enters the grove and,
leaping from tree to tree in the course of his search for Sita, catches
sight of an Asoka tree. Espying a stream running beside it and
expecting that Sita might turn up on the bank of the stream to
say her Sandhya prayers, he takes up his position on that
Asoka tree, hiding himself behind its leafy boughs
content: Hanuman reasoned that Sita, accustomed to forest life, would eventually wander to this part of the grove.  He believed that, weakened by grief for Rama, she would seek the solace of the forest's beauty.  He further surmised that, given her fondness for nature, she would surely visit this area.  He recalled Sita's love for the forest's creatures and her inherent virtue.  He anticipated that she would come to the clear stream for her evening prayers. He observed that the beautiful Asoka grove was a fitting place for someone of Sita's grace and status, the esteemed wife of Rama.  He hoped that if she were still alive, Sita, whose face was as radiant as the moon, would come to the cool waters.  Thinking thus, Hanuman, hidden amongst the blossoms and dense leaves of a tree, searched the entire grove for her.

summary:
Canto XV

Casting his eyes all round while remaining perched on the top of that
SimSapa tree, Hanuman catches sight of Sita in a temple and
recognizes her by virtue of her characteristics and age
content: Hanuman, perched atop the Simsapa tree, scanned the surrounding area, searching for Sita.  His gaze swept over the grounds below, carefully examining the Ashoka grove as he sought the princess of Mithila.

summary:
Canto XV

Casting his eyes all round while remaining perched on the top of that
SimSapa tree, Hanuman catches sight of Sita in a temple and
recognizes her by virtue of her characteristics and age
content: Hanuman, surveying the opulent Ashoka grove, noted its resemblance to the celestial Nandana gardens.  Adorned with fragrant blossoms and sweet-smelling substances, the grove was alive with birds and animals and dotted with multi-storied buildings and sparkling pools.
