Lost and grieving on the Vindhyan mountains, the search party, despairing of finding Sita and fearing Sugriva's wrath for their failure, prepared to end their lives.  Having searched mountains, rivers, and waterfalls without success, they resolved to fast until death.  Angada lamented Sita's disappearance, Vali's death, and Jatayu's demise, mirroring the despair of the entire search party.  Just as they were about to perish, a large, powerful vulture named Sampati, Jatayu's older brother, appeared.  Hearing of Jatayu's death, Sampati demanded to know who killed him and where. Angada recounted Jatayu's valiant sacrifice while protecting Sita from a monstrous ogre. This news deeply saddened Sampati.

Hanuman continues his narrative to Sita, explaining how Sampati revealed her location in Ravana's abode.  This knowledge spurred the search party's renewed hope and journey from the Vindhyan mountains to the coast. Overjoyed at the prospect of finding Sita, the monkeys reached the seashore, but their joy turned to despair at the vastness of the ocean. Hanuman, however, displaying immense courage and power, leaped across the daunting distance of one hundred yojanas (eight hundred miles) to Lanka. He assures Sita that he saw her, grief-stricken, in Ravana's captivity. He then formally introduces himself as a messenger sent by Rama and conveys Rama and Lakshmana's well-being and dedication to her rescue. Finally, Hanuman expresses relief that his perilous journey across the ocean has borne fruit, as he is now able to confirm Sita's whereabouts and alleviate the monkey army's distress.

Hanuman assures Sita of Rama's imminent arrival after defeating Ravana and his army. He recounts his lineage, detailing how his father, Kesari, killed the demon Sambasadana at Gokarna mountain. He confirms his identity as Hanuman, son of the wind-god, known for his prowess.  He then reiterates his role as Rama's messenger and asks Sita if there's anything more he can do before departing.

summary:
Canto XXXVI
Hanuman delivers Sri Rama’s signet ring to Sita in order to strengthen her
confidence in him. Applauding Hanuman, Sita, who was rejoiced to
receive the token, inquires about the health of Sri Rama and others.
In order to appease the lady, who felt indignant at the thought that
S17 Rama did not care to come to her rescue so far, which led
her to suspect that he had ceased to love her, Hanuman
attributes his non-appearance to his ignorance about her
whereabouts. Describing Sri Rama’s desolation in order
to betoken his excessive love for her, he consoles
her with the assurance that Sri Rama is sparing
no pains to rescue her from her confinement
content: Hanuman, son of the wind-god, reassured Sita with kind words and presented Rama's ring as a token.  He told her that her suffering would soon end.  Overjoyed at seeing her husband's ring, Sita's face brightened.  Grateful and relieved, she praised Hanuman's bravery, intelligence, and strength, noting his fearless crossing of the vast ocean and his daring infiltration of Ravana's stronghold. She acknowledged that only someone of exceptional ability, tested and trusted by Rama, would be sent on such a perilous mission.  Relieved, Sita asked about Rama and Lakshmana's well-being.

summary:
Canto XXXVI
Hanuman delivers Sri Rama’s signet ring to Sita in order to strengthen her
confidence in him. Applauding Hanuman, Sita, who was rejoiced to
receive the token, inquires about the health of Sri Rama and others.
In order to appease the lady, who felt indignant at the thought that
S17 Rama did not care to come to her rescue so far, which led
her to suspect that he had ceased to love her, Hanuman
attributes his non-appearance to his ignorance about her
whereabouts. Describing Sri Rama’s desolation in order
to betoken his excessive love for her, he consoles
her with the assurance that Sri Rama is sparing
no pains to rescue her from her confinement
content: Sita, still anxious despite seeing Rama's ring, expresses a torrent of worried questions about Rama's well-being and his efforts to rescue her.  She wonders if he is consumed by anger at her abduction, and questions whether his grief is hindering his actions. She hopes he is attending to his duties as a king and warrior, strategically managing his allies and enemies, while still finding support from his friends. Sita further questions if Rama is seeking divine help and relying on his own strength.  She anxiously wonders if her prolonged absence has diminished his love for her and if he will indeed rescue her from her plight.  Expressing concern for Rama's emotional state, she hopes he isn't overwhelmed by despair.  Finally, Sita inquires about the welfare of Kausalya, Sumitra, and Bharata, and wonders if Bharata will send an army to her aid.

Canto XXXVI
Hanuman delivers Sri Rama’s signet ring to Sita in order to strengthen her
confidence in him. Applauding Hanuman, Sita, who was rejoiced to
receive the token, inquires about the health of Sri Rama and others.
In order to appease the lady, who felt indignant at the thought that
S17 Rama did not care to come to her rescue so far, which led
her to suspect that he had ceased to love her, Hanuman
attributes his non-appearance to his ignorance about her
whereabouts. Describing Sri Rama’s desolation in order
to betoken his excessive love for her, he consoles
her with the assurance that Sri Rama is sparing
no pains to rescue her from her confinement
content: Sita, worried about Rama's well-being and his efforts to rescue her, questions if Sugriva, the monkey king, will come to her aid with his army. She wonders if Lakshmana, skilled in warfare, will destroy the demons with his arrows.  She expresses her hope to see Ravana and his followers defeated by Rama's might.  Anxious about Rama's emotional state, Sita hopes his face, radiant as gold and fragrant as a lotus, hasn't withered from grief due to her absence.  She recalls Rama's steadfastness in upholding righteousness, even when renouncing his kingdom and accompanying her to the forest, and hopes he maintains that same resolve. Sita affirms that no one, not even his parents or any other relative, has received as much love from Rama as she has, and expresses her desire to live only as long as she hears news of him. Upon hearing Sita's heartfelt words, Hanuman, the courageous monkey messenger, respectfully prepares to respond to her concerns.  He reveals that Rama is unaware of Sita's location, which explains his delay in rescuing her, and assures her that Rama will immediately mobilize a vast army of monkeys and bears upon hearing his report. Hanuman confidently declares that Rama will overwhelm Lanka with his arrows and eliminate the demons.  He further asserts that nothing, not even death, gods, or powerful demons, can stop Rama from achieving his goal.

summary:
Canto XXXVI
Hanuman delivers Sri Rama’s signet ring to Sita in order to strengthen her
confidence in him. Applauding Hanuman, Sita, who was rejoiced to
receive the token, inquires about the health of Sri Rama and others.
In order to appease the lady, who felt indignant at the thought that
S17 Rama did not care to come to her rescue so far, which led
her to suspect that he had ceased to love her, Hanuman
attributes his non-appearance to his ignorance about her
whereabouts. Describing Sri Rama’s desolation in order
to betoken his excessive love for her, he consoles
her with the assurance that Sri Rama is sparing
no pains to rescue her from her confinement
content: Hanuman, attempting to comfort Sita, describes Rama's intense grief over her absence. He compares Rama's restlessness to a lion-wounded elephant, unable to find peace.  Hanuman swears by the sacred mountains and their resources that Sita will soon see Rama's face, beautiful as the full moon. He promises that she will see Rama, adorned with earrings, perched on Prasravana mountain, majestic as Indra upon his elephant.  To emphasize Rama's devotion and asceticism, Hanuman details his simple diet of fruits and wild rice, abstaining from meat and honey. He portrays Rama as so consumed by thoughts of Sita that he neglects even to brush away insects.  Hanuman emphasizes Rama's constant contemplation and grief, focused solely on Sita's plight, even in his sleep, where he calls out her name.  He describes Rama sighing at the sight of beautiful things, reminiscent of Sita, and addressing her with affectionate terms. Hanuman concludes by reiterating Rama's unwavering dedication to Sita's rescue, constantly invoking her name amidst his suffering. He emphasizes Rama's single-minded pursuit of her recovery.  Upon hearing this, Sita's initial grief over Rama's supposed indifference dissipates, replaced by sorrow for his suffering.  Her emotional state is likened to a partially clouded autumn night, reflecting both relief and lingering sadness.

Sita, deeply saddened by Hanuman's news of Rama's grief, responded with a mixture of hope and despair.  She acknowledged Hanuman's words about Rama's devotion as bittersweet.  Sita reflected on the inevitability of death, regardless of one's fortune, citing herself, Rama, and Lakshmana as examples of those suffering despite their different circumstances.  She questioned how Rama could overcome his immense sorrow, comparing it to a vast, inescapable ocean.  Sita wondered when Rama would finally see her again, after defeating the demons, killing Ravana, and destroying Lanka.  Urging swift action, she revealed the time limit imposed by Ravana – twelve months – and that only two months remained. Sita explained how Ravana, despite Vibhishana's pleas, refused to return her.  She then shared information from Vibhishana's daughter, Kala, about a respected demon elder named Avindhya who had warned Ravana of impending doom if Sita wasn't returned, advice which Ravana ignored.

Sita expressed hope that Rama's inherent virtues – perseverance, courage, kindness, gratitude, prowess, and energy – would enable him to rescue her. She recalled his strength, referencing his single-handed defeat of fourteen thousand demons in Janasthana, and affirmed her confidence in his resilience and power. Sita likened Rama's might to Indra's, emphasizing her unwavering belief in his capabilities.  She envisioned Rama, like a radiant sun with arrows as rays, vanquishing the demon army. Hanuman reassured the grieving Sita, promising to relay her message to Rama, who would then march to Lanka with a vast monkey and bear army.  Confident in his own strength, Hanuman offered to carry Sita back to Rama himself, boasting of his ability to transport even the entire city of Lanka.  He vowed to reunite her with Rama and Lakshmana that very day, comparing himself to a sacred fire carrying offerings to Indra.  Hanuman described Rama as resolute and powerful, awaiting her return, like Indra upon Mount Meru.

Hanuman urged Sita to mount his back, assuring her safe passage across the ocean to Rama. He promised their swift reunion, likening it to Rohini joining the moon god. Hanuman boasted that no one in Lanka could match his speed, guaranteeing her safe return just as he arrived. Sita, surprised by Hanuman's bold proposal, questioned how he, with his seemingly small stature, could carry her such a distance.  She considered his suggestion impulsive, typical of a monkey. Sita again expressed doubt about Hanuman's ability to carry her back to Rama. Hanuman, feeling slighted by Sita's skepticism about his strength and capabilities, resolved to reveal his true form to her. He then descended from the tree and began to grow in size, becoming as massive as Mount Meru or Mandara, radiating like a blazing fire, to demonstrate his power and instill confidence in Sita.

Hanuman, a formidable figure resembling a mountain, with a coppery face, strong teeth, and immense power, addressed Sita. He declared his ability to carry the entire city of Lanka, including its inhabitants, natural features, and structures, back to Rama.  He urged Sita to cast aside her doubts and allow him to reunite her with her husband and Lakshmana.  Sita, acknowledging Hanuman's strength and speed, questioned how any ordinary monkey could have reached Lanka.  While recognizing Hanuman's power, she expressed concerns about the propriety and safety of riding on his back across the ocean. She feared falling into the sea filled with dangerous creatures and becoming their prey. Additionally, she worried that her presence on Hanuman's back would arouse the suspicion of Ravana's forces, leading to pursuit.

Sita continues to express her doubts to Hanuman about his proposed plan. She argues that his being unarmed makes him vulnerable to the Rakshasas guarding Lanka, especially if she accompanies him.  She fears falling from his back during the ensuing fight, or being captured by the demons if they overpower him.  Even if Hanuman were to win, Sita points out the uncertainty of battle and the risk of her being killed or abducted in the chaos.  She further argues that even Hanuman’s success in killing all the Rakshasas might diminish Rama's glory. Finally, she stresses the importance of her own life to Rama, Lakshmana, Sugriva, and their families, warning that her capture or death would lead them to despair and possibly suicide.

Sita reaffirms her devotion to Rama, stating she wouldn't willingly touch anyone else. She clarifies that her contact with Ravana was forced upon her due to her helpless state. Sita expresses her desire for Rama to defeat Ravana and his forces in Lanka and then retrieve her. She praises Rama's valor and strength, asserting that no being, divine or demonic, can match him in battle. She vividly describes Rama's prowess, comparing him to a blazing fire and a powerful elephant, emphasizing his invincibility.  Finally, Sita implores Hanuman to swiftly bring Rama, Lakshmana, and the monkey army to Lanka, urging him to end her prolonged grief and bring her joy.

summary:
Canto XXXVIII
Asked by Hanuman for a token, Sita narrates the episode of a crow,
which occurred on the Citrakita mountain, and asks Hanuman to
repeat the story to Sri Rama by way of a token. She further sends
her greetings both to Srt Rama and Laksmana, conveys her
inquiry after their welfare and communicates to them her
resolve to survive only for a month after the expiry of the
time-limit prescribed by Ravana; and last of all, she
hands over to him her jewel for the head as a token
content: Hanuman, pleased with Sita's words, assured her that her virtuous and modest response was understandable, given her delicate nature and the vastness of the ocean. He reiterated that her refusal to be touched by any male other than Rama was commendable.  He promised to convey Sita's words and deeds to Rama.  Hanuman explained his offer arose from multiple motivations: a desire to please Rama, the difficulty of entering Lanka, the challenge of crossing the ocean, and his own ability to carry her.  His sole intention, driven by affection for Rama and devotion to Sita, was to reunite them. Failing that, he requested a token of their meeting for Rama.  Sita, deeply moved and tearful, then began to speak.

summary:
Canto XXXVIII
Asked by Hanuman for a token, Sita narrates the episode of a crow,
which occurred on the Citrakita mountain, and asks Hanuman to
repeat the story to Sri Rama by way of a token. She further sends
her greetings both to Srt Rama and Laksmana, conveys her
inquiry after their welfare and communicates to them her
resolve to survive only for a month after the expiry of the
time-limit prescribed by Ravana; and last of all, she
hands over to him her jewel for the head as a token
content: Sita recounts a specific incident to Hanuman as a message for Rama.  While living on the slopes of Citrakita mountain, near the Mandakini river, Sita was relaxing in a flower-filled grove after bathing. A crow began to peck at her. She initially drove it off, but it persisted.  In her struggle to keep the crow away, her skirt slipped, exposing her, a moment Rama witnessed and found amusing. Embarrassed and irritated, Sita sought comfort in Rama's arms.  Exhausted, Sita fell asleep in Rama's embrace, and he, in turn, fell asleep in hers. The persistent crow returned, attacking Sita's breasts as she awoke and rose from Rama's arms.  The crow attacked her again, drawing blood, which awakened Rama.

summary:
Canto XXXVIII
Asked by Hanuman for a token, Sita narrates the episode of a crow,
which occurred on the Citrakita mountain, and asks Hanuman to
repeat the story to Sri Rama by way of a token. She further sends
her greetings both to Srt Rama and Laksmana, conveys her
inquiry after their welfare and communicates to them her
resolve to survive only for a month after the expiry of the
time-limit prescribed by Ravana; and last of all, she
hands over to him her jewel for the head as a token
content: Rama, witnessing Sita distressed and bleeding, questioned who had dared to harm her. Looking around, he saw a crow, talons stained with blood. This crow, it's said, was Indra's son, swift as the wind.  Enraged, Rama took a blade of kusa grass and imbued it with Brahma's power.  The grass blazed like a wildfire and he hurled it at the crow.  The fiery blade pursued the crow relentlessly as it fled across the three worlds, seeking refuge. Abandoned by all, even his father, the crow returned to Rama, begging for mercy.  Though the crow deserved death, Rama, moved by its plea, spared its life. However, he declared that Brahma's weapon couldn't be nullified, and demanded a way to make it effective. The crow then sacrificed its right eye, and Rama, accepting this offering, spared its life.

summary:
Canto XXXVIII
Asked by Hanuman for a token, Sita narrates the episode of a crow,
which occurred on the Citrakita mountain, and asks Hanuman to
repeat the story to Sri Rama by way of a token. She further sends
her greetings both to Srt Rama and Laksmana, conveys her
inquiry after their welfare and communicates to them her
resolve to survive only for a month after the expiry of the
time-limit prescribed by Ravana; and last of all, she
hands over to him her jewel for the head as a token
content: Sita, addressing Rama as if he were present, questioned why he directed the divine Brahmastra at a mere crow for her sake, yet tolerated Ravana's abduction. She implored Rama, a man of immense strength and compassion, to remember her, his lonely wife. Sita acknowledged his unmatched power, comparing him to the vast ocean and Indra, and questioned why he hadn't unleashed his devastating weaponry against the Rakshasas.  She wondered why neither Rama nor Lakshmana, both warriors of immense strength and comparable to gods, had yet rescued her.  Sita lamented, believing her own past misdeeds were the cause of her current plight, preventing her valiant husband and brother-in-law from saving her.

summary:
Canto XXXVIII
Asked by Hanuman for a token, Sita narrates the episode of a crow,
which occurred on the Citrakita mountain, and asks Hanuman to
repeat the story to Sri Rama by way of a token. She further sends
her greetings both to Srt Rama and Laksmana, conveys her
inquiry after their welfare and communicates to them her
resolve to survive only for a month after the expiry of the
time-limit prescribed by Ravana; and last of all, she
hands over to him her jewel for the head as a token
content: Hanuman consoles the weeping Sita, assuring her that Rama is consumed by grief over her absence, and that Lakshmana shares in his sorrow.  He tells her that her discovery marks the beginning of the end of her suffering.  Hanuman promises that Rama and Lakshmana, driven by their desire to see her again, will destroy the Rakshasas. He further assures her that Rama will kill Ravana and his allies, and bring her back to his city.  Finally, Hanuman asks Sita for any message she wishes to convey to Rama, Lakshmana, Sugriva, or the assembled monkeys.

summary:
Canto XXXVIII
Asked by Hanuman for a token, Sita narrates the episode of a crow,
which occurred on the Citrakita mountain, and asks Hanuman to
repeat the story to Sri Rama by way of a token. She further sends
her greetings both to Srt Rama and Laksmana, conveys her
inquiry after their welfare and communicates to them her
resolve to survive only for a month after the expiry of the
time-limit prescribed by Ravana; and last of all, she
hands over to him her jewel for the head as a token
content: Sita, deeply grieved, instructs Hanuman to convey her heartfelt messages to Rama and Lakshmana.  She asks Hanuman to inquire after Rama's well-being, reminding him that Rama is Kausalya's son and a protector of the world. She requests Hanuman to offer Rama a respectful greeting.  Sita also asks Hanuman to convey similar sentiments and inquiries to Lakshmana, praising his virtues: his strength, devotion to Rama, and respectful treatment of her.  She describes Lakshmana's noble character, highlighting his selflessness in following Rama into exile and his unwavering support for his brother.  Finally, Sita urges Hanuman to deliver her messages effectively, specifically asking him to address Lakshmana in a manner that might alleviate her suffering. She emphasizes Hanuman's critical role in facilitating Rama's action to rescue her.  Sita then tells Hanuman to inform Rama that she can only endure her captivity for one more month.

summary:
Canto XXXVIII
Asked by Hanuman for a token, Sita narrates the episode of a crow,
which occurred on the Citrakita mountain, and asks Hanuman to
repeat the story to Sri Rama by way of a token. She further sends
her greetings both to Srt Rama and Laksmana, conveys her
inquiry after their welfare and communicates to them her
resolve to survive only for a month after the expiry of the
time-limit prescribed by Ravana; and last of all, she
hands over to him her jewel for the head as a token
content: Sita solemnly affirms to Hanuman that she cannot endure captivity for more than a month.  She implores Hanuman to rescue her from Ravana's cruel confinement, comparing her plight to that of Goddess Earth when she was trapped in the netherworld and rescued by Vishnu in his boar incarnation.  Sita then gives Hanuman her head jewel to take back to Rama as a token. Hanuman accepts the invaluable jewel and places it on his finger, as his arm, even in its smaller form, is too large for it. He then respectfully circumambulates Sita and stands beside her, bowing low.  Overjoyed at having found Sita, Hanuman thinks longingly of Rama and Lakshmana.  He feels a sense of relief and happiness, akin to someone rescued from a precarious situation on a mountain, as he prepares to return with the precious jewel, which Sita had preserved through her ascetic practices, unnoticed by Ravana's guards.

summary:
Canto XXXIX
Sita asks Hanuman, when the latter is about to depart with the jewel for her
head, to apprise Sri Rama and Laksmana of her own welfare and urge them
to rescue her. When Sita expresses her misgivings about the capacity of
the heroes on Sri Rama’s side to cross the sea, Hanuman expatiates
on the might of the monkey heroes and assures her of their ability
to cross it, and in the end consoles her by promising to bring
Sri Rama and Laksmana on his back across the sea in any case
content: Sita, after giving Hanuman her jewel, tells him that Rama will recognize it.  She urges Hanuman to consider how best to expedite her rescue, emphasizing his unique ability to accomplish this task and imploring him to end her suffering.  Hanuman, agreeing to her request, prepares to depart.  As he does, a tearful Sita asks him to assure Rama, Lakshmana, Sugriva, his ministers, and the elder monkeys of her well-being.  She implores Hanuman to convey her plight to Rama in a way that ensures her survival and encourages him to act swiftly to deliver her from her sorrow.  She believes that her message, delivered by Hanuman, will strengthen Rama’s resolve to rescue her.

summary:
Canto XXXIX
Sita asks Hanuman, when the latter is about to depart with the jewel for her
head, to apprise Sri Rama and Laksmana of her own welfare and urge them
to rescue her. When Sita expresses her misgivings about the capacity of
the heroes on Sri Rama’s side to cross the sea, Hanuman expatiates
on the might of the monkey heroes and assures her of their ability
to cross it, and in the end consoles her by promising to bring
Sri Rama and Laksmana on his back across the sea in any case
content: Hanuman reassured Sita that Rama, accompanied by his monkey and bear army, would swiftly arrive to vanquish her enemies and end her sorrow.  He declared Rama’s unmatched prowess in battle, stating that no being, mortal, demon, or god, could withstand his arrows. Hanuman even asserted Rama's ability to defeat powerful deities like the sun-god, Indra, and Yama if necessary, especially for Sita's sake. He emphasized Rama's capability to conquer the entire earth and assured Sita of his certain victory in her cause.  Deeply moved by Hanuman's sincere and eloquent words, Sita expressed her gratitude and respect. As Hanuman prepared to leave, Sita, gazing at him with affection, requested him to consider staying for a day to rest before departing, expressing that his presence provided her with a brief respite from her immense grief.  She confessed her fear for her survival if there was any doubt about his return, stating that his absence would intensify her suffering.

summary:
Canto XXXIX
Sita asks Hanuman, when the latter is about to depart with the jewel for her
head, to apprise Sri Rama and Laksmana of her own welfare and urge them
to rescue her. When Sita expresses her misgivings about the capacity of
the heroes on Sri Rama’s side to cross the sea, Hanuman expatiates
on the might of the monkey heroes and assures her of their ability
to cross it, and in the end consoles her by promising to bring
Sri Rama and Laksmana on his back across the sea in any case
content: Sita expressed her doubt to Hanuman about how the monkey and bear army, including Rama and Lakshmana, could possibly cross the vast and impassable ocean. She pointed out that only three beings – Garuda, the wind-god, and Hanuman himself – possessed such a capability.  She questioned Hanuman, whom she considered wise and resourceful, about his plan to overcome this seemingly insurmountable obstacle.  Sita acknowledged Hanuman's individual strength and ability to rescue her single-handedly but argued that such a feat would glorify Hanuman, not Rama.  She believed a truly fitting victory would involve Rama leading his army across the ocean, defeating Ravana in battle with their support, and triumphantly returning to his kingdom with her. Sita urged Hanuman to devise a plan that would allow Rama to display his valor and achieve this worthy victory. Upon hearing Sita's thoughtful and reasoned plea, Hanuman prepared to offer his final response.  He reassured her that Sugriva, the truthful leader of the monkey and bear army, was fully committed to her rescue and, accompanied by a massive force, would soon arrive to defeat the demons.

summary:
Canto XXXIX
Sita asks Hanuman, when the latter is about to depart with the jewel for her
head, to apprise Sri Rama and Laksmana of her own welfare and urge them
to rescue her. When Sita expresses her misgivings about the capacity of
the heroes on Sri Rama’s side to cross the sea, Hanuman expatiates
on the might of the monkey heroes and assures her of their ability
to cross it, and in the end consoles her by promising to bring
Sri Rama and Laksmana on his back across the sea in any case
content: Hanuman reassured Sita about the strength and capabilities of the monkey army. He described them as valiant, courageous, and incredibly strong, capable of leaping vast distances at will and never tiring, even during arduous tasks. They obeyed Sugriva's every command and had proven their ability by repeatedly circling the entire earth, including its oceans and mountains. Hanuman emphasized that the army included monkeys as powerful as him, some even stronger, and none were weak. He argued that if he could reach Lanka, the rest of the army, especially its strongest members, could certainly do so as well. He urged Sita to abandon her grief and assured her the monkey leaders could reach Lanka in a single leap. Furthermore, he promised to personally carry Rama and Lakshmana on his back to her.  He foretold their imminent arrival, comparing them to the rising sun and moon, and predicted their swift victory over Lanka. Hanuman assured Sita that Rama would defeat Ravana and his forces and return her safely to Ayodhya. He encouraged her to be patient and strong, promising her a reunion with Rama as surely as the moon rejoins its constellation Rohini, and the ultimate demise of Ravana at Rama's hands.

summary:
Canto XXXIX
Sita asks Hanuman, when the latter is about to depart with the jewel for her
head, to apprise Sri Rama and Laksmana of her own welfare and urge them
to rescue her. When Sita expresses her misgivings about the capacity of
the heroes on Sri Rama’s side to cross the sea, Hanuman expatiates
on the might of the monkey heroes and assures her of their ability
to cross it, and in the end consoles her by promising to bring
Sri Rama and Laksmana on his back across the sea in any case
content: Hanuman consoles Sita, telling her she will soon see Rama and Lakshmana at Lanka's gates. He describes the monkey army that will accompany them, comparing them to lions, tigers, and elephants in their strength and appearance.  He emphasizes their numbers and the noise they will make upon arrival. Hanuman acknowledges Rama's suffering due to their separation, comparing it to a wounded elephant.  He urges Sita not to weep or fear, assuring her she will be reunited with Rama, just as Indra was reunited with his wife, Sachi.  Hanuman reminds Sita of Rama and Lakshmana's power and their role as her protectors.  He promises that her stay in Lanka will be short and that Rama's arrival is imminent, asking her to be patient until he can inform Rama of her situation.

Hanuman, having stealthily entered Lanka, bypassed the city's defenses and ventured deep within.  He moved through the streets, observing the opulent architecture adorned with various decorative patterns and vibrant flowers.  The city resonated with the sounds of music, laughter, and the activities of its inhabitants.  From the grand houses of the Rakshasas, Hanuman heard the sounds of women's ornaments, footsteps, and the boisterous challenges of warriors engaged in contests of strength and skill.

Hanuman observed a diverse array of Rakshasas within Lanka. Some were engaged in religious practices, chanting mantras or studying scriptures. Others were stationed throughout the city, some openly proclaiming their loyalty to Ravana, while others were disguised as ascetics or holy men, carrying various symbolic items.  Hanuman also noted spies of different shapes, sizes, and complexions, armed with an assortment of weapons, from traditional bows and swords to more unusual implements like javelins and tree branches.  Many wore elaborate clothing and jewelry, moving freely throughout the city.

Hanuman observed a heavily fortified central garrison of one hundred thousand Rakshasas stationed near the royal palace.  He then saw Ravana's magnificent palace atop Mount Trikuta, surrounded by moats filled with lotuses.  The palace, reminiscent of paradise with its delightful sounds, was bustling with horses, chariots, aerial cars, and adorned elephants.  The area was further populated with birds and deer, and guarded by numerous powerful Yatudhanas.  Hanuman finally entered Ravana's opulent inner chambers, decorated with gold, pearls, gems, and fragrant with sandalwood and agallochum.

summary:
Canto XL
Asking Hanuman, who was ready to depart, to remind Sri Rama of his
having once painted a decorative mark on her cheek with red realgar,
and also of how he threw a reed at the crow assailing her, destroying
his right eye thereby, as further tokens of Hanuman having met
her, and urging him to apprise Sri Rama of her wretched
plight, Sita grants him leave to depart with her blessings
content: Sita, relieved by Hanuman's assurances, spoke to him, sharing her plight and asking for his help.  She expressed her joy at his kind words, comparing it to the relief of a parched field receiving rain. She urged Hanuman to arrange for Rama to embrace her, as she was wasting away from grief.  As proof of his meeting with her, Sita asked Hanuman to remind Rama of two incidents: the time Rama playfully applied a red cosmetic mark to her cheek and when he shot down a crow that was bothering her, injuring its eye. She questioned how Rama could bear her captivity among the Rakshasas. Sita then showed Hanuman a treasured jewel and instructed him to give it to Rama.  She declared her intention to survive only one more month, enduring the harsh treatment and cruel words of the Rakshasas only for Rama's sake.  She feared she wouldn't live even a moment longer if she heard Rama was delaying his rescue.  Upon hearing Sita’s tearful plea, Hanuman prepared to respond.

summary:
Canto XL
Asking Hanuman, who was ready to depart, to remind Sri Rama of his
having once painted a decorative mark on her cheek with red realgar,
and also of how he threw a reed at the crow assailing her, destroying
his right eye thereby, as further tokens of Hanuman having met
her, and urging him to apprise Sri Rama of her wretched
plight, Sita grants him leave to depart with her blessings
content: Hanuman reassured Sita that Rama and Lakshmana were consumed by grief over her absence.  He promised her that her suffering would soon end.  He declared that Rama and Lakshmana, fueled by their determination to reunite with her, would destroy Lanka.  Hanuman asked Sita for a token to give to Rama as proof of their encounter. Sita confirmed that the jewel she had already given him would suffice.  As Hanuman prepared to leave, Sita, overcome with emotion, asked him to convey her well-being to Rama, Lakshmana, Sugriva, and their ministers. She implored Hanuman to ensure Rama rescued her from her misery and to describe to him the depth of her sorrow and the constant threats she faced from the Rakshasas.  Finally, she wished Hanuman a safe journey.

summary:
Canto XL
Asking Hanuman, who was ready to depart, to remind Sri Rama of his
having once painted a decorative mark on her cheek with red realgar,
and also of how he threw a reed at the crow assailing her, destroying
his right eye thereby, as further tokens of Hanuman having met
her, and urging him to apprise Sri Rama of her wretched
plight, Sita grants him leave to depart with her blessings
content: Hanuman, his mission of meeting Sita successful, felt a surge of joy.  Knowing that the most crucial part of his task was complete, and only a small portion remained, he turned his thoughts northward, preparing for his departure.

summary:
Canto XLI
Accounting his purpose not fully accomplished till he had ascertained the
strength of the ogres, even though he had received the message of
Sita, and making up his mind to lay waste the royal
pleasure-garden as a means of ascertaining
the strength of the enemy, Hanuman
proceeds to do it
content: Hanuman, having received Sita's message, considered his mission incomplete.  To gauge the enemy's strength, he decided to destroy Ravana's pleasure garden.  He reasoned that negotiation, gifts, or sowing dissent wouldn't work against the power-drunk Rakshasas. Only a display of strength would be effective.  He believed that by defeating their strongest warriors, the Rakshasas might adopt a more submissive stance.  Hanuman aimed to accomplish this secondary objective without jeopardizing his primary mission of locating Sita. He emphasized resourcefulness and adaptability in achieving any goal.  Returning to Sugriva without assessing the enemy's strength and formulating a plan would be a failure.  He questioned how this seemingly simple visit had escalated and how it would lead to a confrontation with Ravana, revealing both their strengths. He planned to confront Ravana, his ministers, army, and charioteer, discern their intentions, assess their power, and then return.  Hanuman observed the beauty of Ravana's garden, comparable to Nandana, the celestial garden, filled with diverse trees and creepers. He resolved to destroy it like fire consuming a forest, provoking Ravana's wrath and inciting a large-scale battle involving chariots, elephants, and weapons.

summary:
Canto XLI
Accounting his purpose not fully accomplished till he had ascertained the
strength of the ogres, even though he had received the message of
Sita, and making up his mind to lay waste the royal
pleasure-garden as a means of ascertaining
the strength of the enemy, Hanuman
proceeds to do it
content: Hanuman declared his intent to confront Ravana's forces, confident in his ability to defeat them and return to Sugriva.  He began his assault on the pleasure garden, uprooting trees with furious strength. The once beautiful garden, filled with diverse flora and fauna, was quickly ravaged by Hanuman.  Trees were uprooted, ponds muddied, and hills flattened, leaving the garden unsightly. The sounds of distressed birds filled the air as their habitat was destroyed. The ravaged landscape resembled a forest devastated by wildfire, with broken branches and scattered leaves.  Arbors, animal enclosures, and grottos were demolished, leaving the garden in ruins. The Ashoka creepers, once a symbol of joy, now appeared disheveled and mournful, reflecting the destruction wrought by Hanuman.  Having inflicted significant damage, Hanuman positioned himself at the garden's entrance, ready to face Ravana's army and further demonstrate his power.

summary:
Canto XLII
Perceiving the devastation of the royal pleasance at the hands of Hanuman,
the ogresses keeping watch over Sita asked her who he was. On Sita’s
pleading her ignorance in the matter, some of them hurriedly seek
the presence of Ravana and report the matter to him. Hanuman
makes short work of a company of ogres known by
the name of Kinkaras, despatched by Ravana.
Thereupon Ravana sends Prahasta’s
son to punish the intruder
content: The denizens of Lanka were gripped by terror as birds shrieked and trees crashed to the ground.  Panicked animals and birds added to the cacophony, while ominous signs appeared.  The ogresses, awakened by the commotion, discovered their grove ravaged and a colossal monkey standing before them. Hanuman, radiating strength and courage, grew to an even more imposing size, terrifying the ogresses.  They immediately questioned Sita, demanding to know the monkey's identity, origin, and purpose. Sita, claiming ignorance about the shapeshifting ogres, insisted they were better equipped to understand the situation. Fearful herself, she admitted to being just as terrified and assumed the monkey was another ogre in disguise. Upon hearing Sita's response, the ogresses scattered – some remaining frozen in place, while others rushed to inform Ravana of the alarming intrusion.  They described the newcomer as a monstrous and formidable monkey.

summary:
Canto XLII
Perceiving the devastation of the royal pleasance at the hands of Hanuman,
the ogresses keeping watch over Sita asked her who he was. On Sita’s
pleading her ignorance in the matter, some of them hurriedly seek
the presence of Ravana and report the matter to him. Hanuman
makes short work of a company of ogres known by
the name of Kinkaras, despatched by Ravana.
Thereupon Ravana sends Prahasta’s
son to punish the intruder
content: The ogresses reported to Ravana that a fearsome monkey of immense size and strength was in the Asoka grove, having spoken with Sita. Despite their questioning, Sita feigned ignorance of the monkey's identity, suggesting he could be a messenger from Indra, Kubera, or even Rama. The ogresses emphasized the monkey's destructive rampage through the pleasance, noting, however, that the area where Sita resided, including the Shingshapa tree under which she sat, remained untouched. They urged Ravana to punish the monkey for daring to speak to Sita, emphasizing the audacity of approaching someone Ravana considered his own.  Enraged by this news, Ravana's eyes blazed with fury and tears streamed down his face.  He immediately dispatched a group of his strongest warriors, the Kinkaras, to subdue Hanuman.

summary:
Canto XLII
Perceiving the devastation of the royal pleasance at the hands of Hanuman,
the ogresses keeping watch over Sita asked her who he was. On Sita’s
pleading her ignorance in the matter, some of them hurriedly seek
the presence of Ravana and report the matter to him. Hanuman
makes short work of a company of ogres known by
the name of Kinkaras, despatched by Ravana.
Thereupon Ravana sends Prahasta’s
son to punish the intruder
content: Hanuman, perched on the archway, was met by eighty thousand of Ravana's formidable Kinkara warriors. These large, fierce ogres, armed with iron mallets, clubs, maces, and gleaming arrows, charged towards Hanuman like moths drawn to a flame.  They surrounded him, brandishing clubs, spears, pikes, lances, and javelins.  In response, Hanuman, radiating power and resembling a mountain, slammed his tail against the ground with a resounding roar.  Growing to an immense size, he swung his tail with such force that the sound filled Lanka, causing birds to plummet from the sky.  He then proclaimed his allegiance to Rama, Lakshmana, and Sugriva, declaring himself Rama's servant and a destroyer of enemy armies.  Hanuman boasted that not even a thousand Ravanas could withstand his might, and vowed to destroy Lanka, see Sita, and return successfully, despite the ogres' presence.

summary:
Canto XLII
Perceiving the devastation of the royal pleasance at the hands of Hanuman,
the ogresses keeping watch over Sita asked her who he was. On Sita’s
pleading her ignorance in the matter, some of them hurriedly seek
the presence of Ravana and report the matter to him. Hanuman
makes short work of a company of ogres known by
the name of Kinkaras, despatched by Ravana.
Thereupon Ravana sends Prahasta’s
son to punish the intruder
content: Hanuman, looming large like a storm cloud, frightened the Kinkara warriors with his powerful roar.  Despite knowing he served Rama, the ogres attacked him with a barrage of weapons.  Unfazed, Hanuman grabbed an iron bar from the archway and began to decimate the ogres, wielding it with the deadly precision of Garuda striking a serpent.  Soaring through the air, he continued his assault, resembling Indra vanquishing demons.  After defeating the Kinkaras, the valiant Hanuman stood triumphantly at the gate, eager for further combat.  Some surviving ogres, witnessing the carnage from a safe distance, reported the Kinkaras' defeat to Ravana.  Enraged, Ravana dispatched his formidable warrior, Jambumali, son of Prahasta, to confront Hanuman.

summary:
Canto XLIII
Having disposed of the Kinkaras sent by Ravana and making up
his mind to demolish the sanctuary sacred to the guardian deity
of ogres, Hanuman climbs it up and, killing the guards posted
there, exhibits his prowess by uttering the name of Sri
Rama in a thundering voice. Nay, uprooting a pillar
of the sanctuary, he brandishes it and burns the
sanctuary with the fire produced thereby
content: Hanuman, having decimated Ravana's guards, turned his attention to the ogre's sanctuary.  He decided to destroy the imposing structure. With a powerful leap, he ascended the sanctuary, which towered like a mountain peak.  His radiant form shone like a second sun.  From his vantage point atop the sanctuary, Hanuman, formidable and unyielding, began its demolition.  The sound of collapsing stone echoed through Lanka, startling birds and causing the sanctuary guards to fall senseless.  Hanuman roared a challenge, proclaiming his allegiance to Rama and his intent to destroy Lanka.  He declared his invincibility, boasting that even a thousand Ravanas could not withstand his might. After laying waste to the city and conveying his respects to Sita, he vowed to return triumphant.  His powerful roar instilled fear in the hearts of the ogres.

summary:
Canto XLIII
Having disposed of the Kinkaras sent by Ravana and making up
his mind to demolish the sanctuary sacred to the guardian deity
of ogres, Hanuman climbs it up and, killing the guards posted
there, exhibits his prowess by uttering the name of Sri
Rama in a thundering voice. Nay, uprooting a pillar
of the sanctuary, he brandishes it and burns the
sanctuary with the fire produced thereby
content: Hanuman, facing an onslaught from one hundred of Ravana's sanctuary guards, found himself surrounded. The guards attacked with a variety of weapons – maces, clubs, and arrows. Undeterred, Hanuman reacted with fury.  He ripped a gold-adorned pillar from the sanctuary and, with immense strength, whirled it around, igniting a fire that quickly engulfed the structure.  After defeating the guards, Hanuman, soaring in the air, proclaimed his power and the vastness of Sugriva's monkey army, which was searching the world for Sita.  He described the monkeys' immense strength, some equal to ten elephants, others ten times stronger, and still others with the might of a thousand elephants. Some possessed the strength of a flood, others the strength of the wind, and still others immeasurable power.

summary:
Canto XLIII
Having disposed of the Kinkaras sent by Ravana and making up
his mind to demolish the sanctuary sacred to the guardian deity
of ogres, Hanuman climbs it up and, killing the guards posted
there, exhibits his prowess by uttering the name of Sri
Rama in a thundering voice. Nay, uprooting a pillar
of the sanctuary, he brandishes it and burns the
sanctuary with the fire produced thereby
content: Hanuman, having decimated the sanctuary's guards, issued a powerful warning to the remaining ogres. He proclaimed that Sugriva, leading an immense army of monkeys numbering in the hundreds, thousands, millions, and even tens of millions, was coming to Lanka.  He declared that Sugriva possessed the power to destroy them all, and that neither the city of Lanka, nor its inhabitants, nor Ravana himself would survive the coming conflict, especially given Ravana's foolish provocation of the mighty Rama.

Jambumali, son of Prahasta, armed with a powerful bow and shining arrows, advanced on Ravana's orders. His appearance was fearsome: adorned in crimson, with red garland and gleaming earrings. The twang of his bow resonated like thunder and lightning, filling the air. Hanuman, observing Jambumali's arrival in a donkey-drawn chariot, roared in anticipation of the battle.  Jambumali attacked, piercing Hanuman with arrows aimed at his mouth, head, and arms.  Despite the wounds, Hanuman's face, stained with blood, was striking, resembling a lotus flower kissed by the sun or sprinkled with nectar. Angered by the attack, Hanuman seized a large rock and hurled it at Jambumali, who shattered it with his arrows. Undeterred, Hanuman uprooted a massive sal tree, preparing to wield it as a weapon.

Jambumali showered Hanuman with arrows, piercing his arms, chest, and torso.  Despite the assault, Hanuman, enraged, grabbed a large sal tree and began to swing it. Jambumali countered by cutting the tree to pieces with his arrows.  Undeterred, Hanuman picked up an iron club and, with furious strength, hurled it at Jambumali. The blow was devastating.  Jambumali's body was so utterly destroyed that nothing remained identifiable – no head, arms, knees, nor even his chariot.  Hearing of Jambumali’s death, Ravana, eyes burning with rage, ordered his minister's sons, known for their strength and skill, to attack Hanuman.

Hanuman, after defeating the king's ministers, returned to his perch on the archway.  Ravana then sent out the seven sons of his chief minister, accompanied by a large, well-armed army.  These warriors were highly skilled and eager to prove themselves in battle.  They rode in ornate chariots decorated with gold, banners, and flags, creating a thunderous noise. Upon learning of the earlier defeat, the warriors' mothers and families were overcome with grief and fear.  The sons of the chief minister, adorned with gold ornaments, charged towards Hanuman on the archway.  They fired a barrage of arrows, likened to a monsoon downpour.  Hanuman, momentarily obscured by the shower of arrows,  skillfully evaded the projectiles and the charging chariots, moving through the sky like the wind-god amidst the clouds. With a mighty roar that terrified Ravana's army, Hanuman attacked the ogres, striking them down with his hands, feet, fists, and nails.

Hanuman's power overwhelmed the sons of Ravana's chief minister. Some were crushed, others simply fell from the sound of his roar.  The remaining army, terrified, scattered in all directions. Elephants trumpeted, horses fell, and chariots were destroyed, their banners and canopies scattered across the battlefield.  Blood flowed like rivers, and the cries of Lanka's inhabitants filled the air.  Victorious, Hanuman, with his immense strength and courage, returned to his position on the archway, ready to face any other challengers.

summary:
Canto XLVI

Having killed five more generals sent by Ravana, Hanuman
returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove
content: Ravana, upon learning of the deaths of his chief minister's sons at the hands of Hanuman, devised a new plan.  He ordered five powerful generals – Virupaksa, Yupaksa, Durdhara, Praghasa, and Bhasakarna – to capture Hanuman.  Ravana instructed them to proceed with a large army of horses, chariots, and elephants, emphasizing the importance of choosing their actions wisely based on the time and place.  Ravana expressed his doubt that Hanuman was merely a monkey, suspecting him to be a powerful being, possibly sent by Indra to destroy them. Recalling his army's past victories against gods, demons, and other celestial beings, Ravana cautioned his generals not to underestimate Hanuman's strength and urged them to bring him captive.

summary:
Canto XLVI

Having killed five more generals sent by Ravana, Hanuman
returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove
content: Ravana, doubting Hanuman's true nature and strength, dispatched five powerful generals – Virupaksa, Yupaksa, Durdhara, Praghasa, and Bhasakarna – to capture him.  He reminded them of their army's past victories but cautioned against underestimating Hanuman.  Ravana stressed the importance of strategic action and self-preservation, acknowledging the unpredictable nature of battle. The generals, energized by their orders, set off with their troops and diverse weaponry.  They found Hanuman radiating a brilliant light, possessing immense strength, agility, and intelligence. They immediately attacked him with various weapons.  Durdhara struck Hanuman's head with five arrows, which had little effect and merely resembled lily petals adorning him.  Enraged, Hanuman leaped skyward with a mighty roar that echoed in all directions.

summary:
Canto XLVI

Having killed five more generals sent by Ravana, Hanuman
returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove
content: Hanuman continued to fend off Ravana's generals.  Durdhara attacked Hanuman with a barrage of arrows, but Hanuman deflected them with his roar, much like a wind dispersing a monsoon cloud.  Annoyed, Hanuman grew in size and landed on Durdhara's chariot with tremendous force, destroying it and killing the general.  Seeing this, Virupaksa and Yupaksa attacked Hanuman in the air with clubs, but Hanuman countered their attack and descended swiftly to the ground.  He then uprooted a sal tree and used it to kill both Virupaksa and Yupaksa.  Upon witnessing the deaths of these three generals, Praghasa and Bhasakarna approached Hanuman, one armed with a spear and the other with a dart, and attacked him.

summary:
Canto XLVI

Having killed five more generals sent by Ravana, Hanuman
returns again to the archway of the Asoka grove
content: Hanuman, wounded but enraged, retaliated fiercely against Ravana's forces. He uprooted a mountain peak, complete with its flora and fauna, and used it to crush the generals Virupaksa and Yupaksa.  With the five generals defeated, Hanuman proceeded to decimate the remaining army. He employed a unique battle tactic, using their own resources against them – horses against horses, elephants against elephants, warriors against warriors, and chariots against chariots. The battlefield became littered with the bodies of fallen soldiers, animals, and broken chariots, blocking all pathways.  Victorious, Hanuman stood on the archway, poised and ready for the next challenge, much like time itself awaiting the destruction of all beings.

summary:
Canto XLVII

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet
Hanuman in combat, is killed
content: Hanuman's devastating attack left Ravana's five generals and their armies destroyed.  Ravana turned his attention to his son, Prince Aksa, who eagerly awaited battle.  Inspired by his father's gaze, Aksa, armed with a magnificent bow, sprang forward, ready for combat. He mounted a radiant chariot, adorned with gold, and charged towards Hanuman. This chariot, earned through rigorous austerities, was a formidable war machine, equipped with flags, a jeweled ensign, swift horses, and an array of weapons.  Aksa, a warrior of divine prowess, filled the battlefield with the sounds of his advancing army. He approached Hanuman, who stood defiantly on an archway.  Hanuman, poised for battle, was surprised by the young prince's challenge.  Aksa, confident and strong, considered Hanuman's power and his own might.  Fueled by anger at Hanuman's strength, Aksa firmly positioned himself and shot three arrows at Hanuman, provoking him to fight.  Aksa, adorned with gold ornaments, saw Hanuman's pride and readiness for battle and prepared to engage him in a duel that promised to be legendary, causing awe among gods and demons alike.

summary:
Canto XLVII

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet
Hanuman in combat, is killed
content: The earth trembled, the sun dimmed, the wind stilled, and the very mountain shook as Hanuman and Aksa clashed.  Aksa, a skilled archer, shot three arrows, tipped with gold and resembling venomous serpents, into Hanuman's head.  Wounded and bleeding, Hanuman shone like the rising sun, the arrows in his forehead resembling its rays.  Seeing Aksa's magnificent chariot and weapons raised in challenge, Hanuman, the monkey general, rejoiced and grew in size, his fury burning like the sun. He glared at Aksa, his eyes blazing with anger.  Aksa, likened to a storm cloud, rained arrows upon Hanuman, who stood firm like a mountain.  Hanuman roared with delight at the challenge. Aksa, fueled by youthful pride, advanced on Hanuman, who watched him with a mixture of awe and apprehension. As Aksa's arrows found their mark, Hanuman roared, a sound like thunder, and stretched his limbs, preparing to retaliate. Aksa pursued the airborne Hanuman, showering him with arrows.  Hanuman, swift and agile, dodged the missiles, moving through the air like the wind. He paused, observing Aksa's skill and determination with respect, and considered his next move.

summary:
Canto XLVII

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet
Hanuman in combat, is killed
content: Hanuman, wounded by Aksa's arrows, considered his opponent's skill. He observed Aksa's youthful prowess and the power he wielded, acknowledging that such strength could shake even gods and demons.  Realizing the growing threat, Hanuman decided he could not afford to underestimate Aksa.  He swiftly killed Aksa's eight chariot horses, causing the chariot to crash.  Aksa leaped from the wreckage, but Hanuman caught him by the legs, spun him around, and threw him to the ground with devastating force, leaving him broken and lifeless.

summary:
Canto XLVII

Prince Aksa, son of Ravana, who came to meet
Hanuman in combat, is killed
content: Hanuman, after slamming Aksa to the ground, instilled fear in Ravana.  The observing sages, celestial beings (Yakshas and Nagas), and gods led by Indra, watched in astonishment as Hanuman killed the prince.  The valiant Hanuman, having defeated Aksa, who rivaled Indra's son in battle and whose eyes were reddened, then returned to his position on the archway, awaiting another chance to vanquish more of Ravana's forces.

summary:
Canto XLVIII
Dispatched by Ravana, Indrajit, Ravana’s eldest son, marches against
Hanuman. On his shafts being rendered ineffective by Hanuman
through his extreme agility, Indrajit takes him captive by discharging
the unfailing missile presided over by Brahma (the creator).
Even though capable of rendering it ineffective, Hanuman
yields to its influence, eager as he was to meet
Ravana, to whose presence he is led by his son

Talrsterafetarcar
content: Ravana, grieving yet composed, commands his powerful son, Indrajit, to subdue Hanuman.  He praises Indrajit's skill with weapons, recalling his past victories against gods and demons.  Ravana emphasizes Indrajit's resilience, intelligence, and strategic mind, asserting that his son's power rivals his own. He laments the loss of his army, including his kinsmen and generals, emphasizing that his hope now rests solely with Indrajit.  Ravana urges Indrajit to assess Hanuman's strength and his own, advising against conventional warfare and the use of brute force. He suggests a more cunning approach, instructing Indrajit to use his unique abilities and the potent bow to capture Hanuman unharmed.

summary:
Canto XLVIII
Dispatched by Ravana, Indrajit, Ravana’s eldest son, marches against
Hanuman. On his shafts being rendered ineffective by Hanuman
through his extreme agility, Indrajit takes him captive by discharging
the unfailing missile presided over by Brahma (the creator).
Even though capable of rendering it ineffective, Hanuman
yields to its influence, eager as he was to meet
Ravana, to whose presence he is led by his son

Talrsterafetarcar
content: Indrajit's departure is fraught with Ravana's conflicted emotions, acknowledging the inherent danger yet recognizing the necessity aligned with royal duty and warrior ethos.  He emphasizes the importance of Indrajit's knowledge and strategic prowess for achieving victory.  Indrajit, resolute and determined, receives his father's words and prepares for battle, circumambulating Ravana as a mark of respect before departing.  Honored by his peers, the formidable warrior, radiating power and eagerness for combat, advances towards Hanuman.  The scene is further intensified by ominous signs – darkening skies and the unsettling howls of wild creatures.  A sense of anticipation builds with the arrival of supernatural beings and the excited cries of birds, foreshadowing the impending clash.  Hanuman, emboldened by the sight of Indrajit's approaching chariot, roars defiantly and grows to an immense size, ready to face the challenge.

summary:
Canto XLVIII
Dispatched by Ravana, Indrajit, Ravana’s eldest son, marches against
Hanuman. On his shafts being rendered ineffective by Hanuman
through his extreme agility, Indrajit takes him captive by discharging
the unfailing missile presided over by Brahma (the creator).
Even though capable of rendering it ineffective, Hanuman
yields to its influence, eager as he was to meet
Ravana, to whose presence he is led by his son

Talrsterafetarcar
content: Indrajit, riding a celestial chariot and armed with a powerful bow, readied himself for battle against Hanuman.  The two formidable warriors, filled with intense rivalry, clashed like legendary adversaries. Hanuman, immense and strong, skillfully evaded Indrajit's arrows, moving with the swiftness of his father, the wind-god.  Indrajit, a skilled archer, unleashed a barrage of sharp, swift arrows. Hanuman, agile and defiant, leaped and dodged, evading the projectiles.  The two warriors, equal in their prowess, engaged in a captivating battle.  Despite Indrajit's relentless attack, Hanuman remained unharmed.  Frustrated by his inability to defeat Hanuman, Indrajit contemplated how to capture the seemingly invincible monkey. He then decided to employ the Brahmastra, a powerful weapon presided over by the creator god Brahma, against Hanuman.

summary:
Canto XLVIII
Dispatched by Ravana, Indrajit, Ravana’s eldest son, marches against
Hanuman. On his shafts being rendered ineffective by Hanuman
through his extreme agility, Indrajit takes him captive by discharging
the unfailing missile presided over by Brahma (the creator).
Even though capable of rendering it ineffective, Hanuman
yields to its influence, eager as he was to meet
Ravana, to whose presence he is led by his son

Talrsterafetarcar
content: Indrajit, realizing Hanuman's invulnerability, cleverly used the Brahmastra, a divine missile, to bind him.  Hanuman, though unaffected by the missile due to a boon, recognized its power and strategically chose to honor its binding. He reflected on Brahma's favor and accepted his temporary captivity. Confident in his divine protection from Brahma, Indra, and his father, the wind-god, Hanuman saw this capture as an opportunity to meet Ravana.  He allowed himself to be bound by the Rakshasas, feigning distress, furthering his plan to encounter Ravana. The Rakshasas, seeing Hanuman immobile, bound him with ropes.  Hanuman welcomed this, certain that his capture would pique Ravana's curiosity and lead to a meeting.  Ironically, the ropes released Hanuman from the Brahmastra's bind, as the divine weapon could not coexist with another form of restraint.

summary:
Canto XLVIII
Dispatched by Ravana, Indrajit, Ravana’s eldest son, marches against
Hanuman. On his shafts being rendered ineffective by Hanuman
through his extreme agility, Indrajit takes him captive by discharging
the unfailing missile presided over by Brahma (the creator).
Even though capable of rendering it ineffective, Hanuman
yields to its influence, eager as he was to meet
Ravana, to whose presence he is led by his son

Talrsterafetarcar
content: Indrajit, seeing Hanuman bound with ordinary ropes, realized the Brahmastra's effect had been neutralized. He lamented that the Rakshasas' ignorance of the missile's workings had ruined his plan.  Hanuman, though free, continued to feign helplessness as the Rakshasas dragged him to Ravana. The Rakshasas, brutal in their handling, struck Hanuman as they brought him before their king. Indrajit presented the seemingly bound Hanuman to Ravana and his court.  The Rakshasas, curious and enraged, questioned Hanuman's identity and purpose, some even suggesting violence.  Hanuman, amidst the chaos, observed Ravana's court, the king's attendants, and the opulent surroundings.  He saw Ravana, radiating power and fury, observing him intently.  Ravana, in turn, saw Hanuman being dragged before him and ordered his advisors to question the captive monkey.

summary:
Canto XLVIII
Dispatched by Ravana, Indrajit, Ravana’s eldest son, marches against
Hanuman. On his shafts being rendered ineffective by Hanuman
through his extreme agility, Indrajit takes him captive by discharging
the unfailing missile presided over by Brahma (the creator).
Even though capable of rendering it ineffective, Hanuman
yields to its influence, eager as he was to meet
Ravana, to whose presence he is led by his son

Talrsterafetarcar
content: Hanuman, questioned about his mission, purpose, and objective, declared himself a messenger sent by Sugriva, the monkey king.

Hanuman, awestruck by Ravana's magnificence, observed the king's opulent attire and powerful presence.  Ravana wore a gold crown studded with pearls, adorned with exquisite jewelry, and dressed in fine silk robes, decorated with red sandalwood paste and intricate designs. His twenty piercing red eyes, sharp teeth, and protruding lips gave him a formidable appearance.  With ten heads, he resembled a majestic mountain, his twenty bejeweled arms like five-headed serpents.  He sat upon a magnificent crystal throne, inlaid with precious stones.  Attendants surrounded him, while his four chief advisors, Durdhara, Prahasta, Mahaparsva, and Nikumbha, sat nearby, offering counsel.  Hanuman noted that despite Ravana's impressive appearance, his unrighteousness ultimately diminished his power.

Hanuman observed Ravana, the powerful king of the ogres, seated high upon a throne, like a storm cloud atop a mountain peak. Though surrounded by intimidating ogres, Hanuman was filled with awe.  He marveled at Ravana's shining presence, noting his impressive charm, composure, courage, and splendor.  Hanuman reflected that if Ravana's actions were righteous, he would be a formidable protector, even for the gods themselves. However, Ravana's cruel deeds instilled fear in all beings, and Hanuman recognized his capacity for immense destruction.

summary:
Canto V
Even though seeing ogres and ogresses of various grades and orders while

the moon was ascending the heavens, Hanuman gives way to
anxiety on his not being able to find Sita
content: Hanuman, observing the full moon ascend to its zenith, saw it cast its brilliant light across the earth.  The moon, resembling a majestic white bull, illuminated the world, dispelling darkness and seemingly minimizing opportunities for wrongdoing.  Its radiant glow, akin to the splendor seen on Mount Mandara, the sea, and the lotus flower, shone brightly, reminiscent of a swan in a silver cage, a lion in its mountain den, or a hero atop an elephant. The fully formed orb, complete with its shadowed hare-like figure, radiated a pure, clear light, having shed the vestiges of frost and cold.  Its brilliance intensified, much like a beast reaching a rocky summit, an elephant entering a forest, or a king returning to his kingdom.  However, even amidst this beautiful moonlit night,  the darkness fostered an increase in the ogres' predatory activities.  As music drifted through the air and virtuous women slept peacefully beside their husbands, the ogres began to stir, their behavior growing increasingly erratic and violent.  Hanuman observed their opulent mansions filled with chariots, horses, and golden seats, witnessing the ogres' boisterous revelry, their boasts, and their aggressive posturing toward one another.  They engaged in physical displays of strength, adorned themselves in various attire, and practiced their archery skills.

summary:
Canto V
Even though seeing ogres and ogresses of various grades and orders while

the moon was ascending the heavens, Hanuman gives way to
anxiety on his not being able to find Sita
content: Hanuman surveyed the opulent city of Lanka. He observed women engaged in various activities: some applied fragrant sandalwood paste, others slept peacefully, some laughed merrily, while others, perhaps angered by lovers' quarrels, seethed.  The city resonated with the trumpeting of majestic elephants and the presence of noble individuals like Vibhishana. Yet, amidst this bustling activity, Hanuman also noted the presence of formidable warriors, their restless energy reminiscent of coiled serpents.  He saw the city's inhabitants, intelligent and eloquent, devoted to their pursuits and adorned in diverse, elegant attire. Hanuman was pleased by the sight of these graceful individuals, many possessing admirable virtues, even though some bore physical imperfections.  He then observed the women of Lanka, elegantly dressed and adorned, their hearts pure and dignified, their affections divided between their beloved and fine drinks. Some women, radiant with beauty and virtue, embraced their husbands in the intimacy of night. Others, held in high esteem by their husbands, sat comfortably, their devotion evident. Hanuman noted the varied appearances of the women, some clad in shimmering gold, others pale with longing for absent husbands. He saw joyous women, adorned with flowers, reunited with their beloved. Their faces shone like the moon, their eyes sparkled, and their ornaments glittered.  Despite this panorama of life in Lanka, Hanuman could not find Sita, the noble princess descended from a virtuous lineage, her beauty akin to a blossoming flower.  His search continued.

summary:
Canto V
Even though seeing ogres and ogresses of various grades and orders while

the moon was ascending the heavens, Hanuman gives way to
anxiety on his not being able to find Sita
content: Hanuman, despite seeing many within Lanka, failed to find Sita, the devoted wife of Rama.  Sita, steadfast in her love for Rama, remained ever present in his thoughts.  Now, suffering in captivity, her once adorned throat was choked with tears of sorrow. Her former radiance, once comparable to a dancing peahen, was now dimmed, obscured like a crescent moon behind clouds or a gold streak dulled by dust.  The pain of her absence, a wound upon Rama's heart, was mirrored in her own suffering, visible like a scar. Even as she was carried to Lanka, her inherent nobility shone through, like a streak of cloud against the wind.  Hanuman, not finding her, was overcome with sorrow.

summary:
Canto L
Commanded by Ravana, Prahasta inquires of Hanuman as to who he was as
also his motive in devastating the royal pleasance and killing the ogres.
In reply Hanuman says that he destroyed the grove in order that he
might be taken captive and dragged to the presence of Ravana,
whom he was eager to see, and was compelled in self-defence to
kill those who stood in his way. In the end he declares himself
to be a messenger of Sri Rama and adds that, though protected

against the missile presided over by Brahma, he submitted

to its influence only in order to be able to see Ravana
content: Ravana, enraged by Hanuman's presence and destruction, questioned his identity and motives.  Unsure of Hanuman's true nature, Ravana wondered if he was a divine being like Nandi or a powerful demon in disguise.  He commanded Prahasta to question Hanuman. Prahasta, attempting to reassure Hanuman, inquired about his origins and purpose, suggesting he could be a messenger from Indra, Kubera, Yama, Varuna, or even Vishnu, observing that Hanuman's power seemed to exceed that of a typical monkey.  Prahasta promised Hanuman freedom if he revealed the truth, but warned of dire consequences if he lied.

summary:
Canto L
Commanded by Ravana, Prahasta inquires of Hanuman as to who he was as
also his motive in devastating the royal pleasance and killing the ogres.
In reply Hanuman says that he destroyed the grove in order that he
might be taken captive and dragged to the presence of Ravana,
whom he was eager to see, and was compelled in self-defence to
kill those who stood in his way. In the end he declares himself
to be a messenger of Sri Rama and adds that, though protected

against the missile presided over by Brahma, he submitted

to its influence only in order to be able to see Ravana
content: Hanuman, questioned about his motives, declared he was neither a messenger of the gods nor allied with them. He identified himself as a monkey, explaining his destruction of the grove was a deliberate act to gain an audience with Ravana. He fought the ogres in self-defense, claiming to be immune to their weapons. Hanuman revealed he willingly submitted to Brahma's missile to meet Ravana. He stated he was brought before Ravana as a captive despite being freed from the missile's effect. Finally, Hanuman identified himself as Sri Rama's messenger, urging Ravana to listen to his message.

summary:
Canto LI
Narrating the story of Sri Rama from the latter’s entry into the forest, to his
own sight of Sita being borne away by Ravana through the air over the
Rsyamika mountain, and celebrating the glory of Sri Rama, Hanuman
points out to Ravana that if he longed to survive he should
restore Sita to Sri Rama and that he should be prepared for the
worst if, on the other hand, he chooses not to part with her
content: Hanuman, possessing great strength, calmly addressed the powerful, ten-headed Ravana. He conveyed a message from Sugriva, the monkey king, inquiring about Ravana's well-being. Hanuman then relayed Sugriva's message, advising Ravana on a course of action beneficial for both his present and future life.  He recounted the story of King Dasaratha, a benevolent ruler, whose eldest son, Rama, was exiled to the Dandaka Forest with his wife Sita and brother Lakshmana.  Sita, daughter of King Janaka, was lost in the Janasthana forest. While searching for her, Rama and Lakshmana met Sugriva on Mount Rsyamuka.  Sugriva promised to help Rama find Sita, and in return, Rama pledged to restore Sugriva's kingship. Rama fulfilled his promise by slaying Vali and reinstating Sugriva as king of the monkeys and bears.  Hanuman reminded Ravana of his prior acquaintance with Vali, the foremost of monkeys, whom Rama killed with a single arrow. Now, Sugriva, eager to locate Sita and honor his promise, has sent search parties in all directions.

summary:
Canto LI
Narrating the story of Sri Rama from the latter’s entry into the forest, to his
own sight of Sita being borne away by Ravana through the air over the
Rsyamika mountain, and celebrating the glory of Sri Rama, Hanuman
points out to Ravana that if he longed to survive he should
restore Sita to Sri Rama and that he should be prepared for the
worst if, on the other hand, he chooses not to part with her
content: Hanuman continues his message to Ravana, emphasizing the vast scale of the search for Sita.  Countless monkeys are scouring the earth, sky, and even the underworld. These monkey warriors possess incredible speed and strength, some rivaling Garuda and the wind itself.  Hanuman identifies himself as the son of the wind-god and reveals he crossed the vast ocean to Lanka, driven by his determination to find Sita. He states that he has indeed located Sita in Ravana's garden.  Hanuman urges Ravana to consider the consequences of his actions. He appeals to Ravana's wisdom, advising him against keeping another man's wife, reminding him of the principles of righteousness. He cautions Ravana against actions that are harmful and destructive.  Hanuman warns Ravana of the power of Rama and Lakshmana, questioning who could withstand their wrath. He asserts that no one can offend Rama and expect to remain unscathed.  Hanuman implores Ravana to return Sita to Rama, presenting this as a course of action that would benefit him in the long run.  He reiterates that he has found the grieving Sita, comparing her to a dangerous serpent within Ravana's home, warning that she is too powerful for Ravana to control, just as poisoned food cannot be digested.

summary:
Canto LI
Narrating the story of Sri Rama from the latter’s entry into the forest, to his
own sight of Sita being borne away by Ravana through the air over the
Rsyamika mountain, and celebrating the glory of Sri Rama, Hanuman
points out to Ravana that if he longed to survive he should
restore Sita to Sri Rama and that he should be prepared for the
worst if, on the other hand, he chooses not to part with her
content: Hanuman continues his warning to Ravana, stressing the gravity of stealing Sita.  He reminds Ravana of the spiritual merits and long life earned through austerities, which are now at risk.  Ravana's immunity to death, even from gods and demons, stems from these austerities, yet he jeopardizes it all.  Hanuman highlights the difference in nature between Rama and Sugriva, who are not divine beings but human and monkey respectively, questioning how Ravana can possibly hope to survive against them.  He points out that good deeds cannot undo the consequences of unrighteous acts, and that Ravana will inevitably face the repercussions of his actions. Ravana has already enjoyed the benefits of his past virtues, but now the negative consequences of his current misdeed, the abduction of Sita, are imminent. Hanuman urges Ravana to remember the defeats of the Janasthana ogres and Vali, and to consider the alliance between Rama and Sugriva as a sign of the forces aligned against him.  Hanuman asserts his own power, claiming he could single-handedly destroy Lanka, but clarifies that Rama's intention is not mere destruction.  He reveals Rama's vow to annihilate those responsible for harming Sita, emphasizing that even Indra could not escape the consequences of wronging Rama.  Hanuman then likens Sita to Kalaratri, the goddess of destruction, warning that she represents doom for Lanka.  He describes Sita as a noose of death around Ravana's neck and advises him to prioritize his own safety by returning her.  Hanuman concludes with a vivid image of Lanka, already consumed by Sita's moral power and ignited by Rama's anger.

summary:
Canto LI
Narrating the story of Sri Rama from the latter’s entry into the forest, to his
own sight of Sita being borne away by Ravana through the air over the
Rsyamika mountain, and celebrating the glory of Sri Rama, Hanuman
points out to Ravana that if he longed to survive he should
restore Sita to Sri Rama and that he should be prepared for the
worst if, on the other hand, he chooses not to part with her
content: Hanuman implores Ravana to reconsider his actions, urging him not to destroy his kingdom, family, and possessions for the sake of keeping Sita.  He emphasizes the truthfulness of his words, particularly as a messenger of Rama. Hanuman then declares Rama's immense power, capable of destroying and recreating the entire universe. He asserts that no being, be it god, demon, or any other creature, can withstand Rama's might in battle.  He reminds Ravana of Rama's divine status and the futility of opposing him, highlighting that even powerful deities like Brahma, Rudra, and Indra would be unable to stand against him.  Finally, the verses describe Ravana's furious reaction to Hanuman's bold words, ordering the monkey's death.

summary:
Canto LII
Provoked by the harsh words of Hanuman, Ravana orders him to be put
to death. Vibhisana, however, exhorts the latter to desist from
this dastardly act, pointing out that the killing of an
envoy is forbidden by the Sastras
content: Ravana, enraged by Hanuman's defiant words, ordered his execution. Vibhisana, however, disapproved of this command and contemplated the impending, unjust act. Recognizing the gravity of the situation, he carefully considered his duty.  Vibhisana, eloquent and wise, then implored his elder brother Ravana to reconsider. He argued against harming Hanuman, emphasizing that executing a messenger is a transgression against dharma (righteousness) and against the conduct of noble rulers. He reminded Ravana of his wisdom and knowledge of royal duties, urging him to distinguish between right and wrong. Vibhisana pointed out the futility of scriptural knowledge if overcome by anger and advised Ravana to carefully determine a just punishment, rather than resorting to immediate execution.  However, Ravana, furious upon hearing Vibhisana's plea, retorted that killing the wicked is not a sin and declared his intention to slay Hanuman. Vibhisana, recognizing the flaws in Ravana's reasoning, prepared to offer a further, wiser response.

summary:
Canto LII
Provoked by the harsh words of Hanuman, Ravana orders him to be put
to death. Vibhisana, however, exhorts the latter to desist from
this dastardly act, pointing out that the killing of an
envoy is forbidden by the Sastras
content: Vibhisana continued his impassioned plea to Ravana, emphasizing that messengers should never be harmed, regardless of their message. He argued that numerous alternative punishments existed, such as mutilation, flogging, shaving the head, or branding, but death was never an option.  He appealed to Ravana's wisdom and knowledge of dharma (righteousness), questioning how someone so learned could be swayed by anger.
