Vibhisana lauded Ravana's knowledge of scripture and his prowess, reminding him of his victories against gods and demons. He asserted that no one dared defy Ravana, highlighting the monkey's insignificance and suggesting that punishment should be directed at those who sent him, not the messenger himself. Vibhisana further argued that killing Hanuman would prevent any further communication, effectively cutting off a valuable source of information.  He concluded by suggesting that instead of focusing on Hanuman, Ravana should direct his attention towards the gods, especially Indra.  Finally, Vibhisana pointed out that Hanuman's death would eliminate the catalyst for war with Rama and Lakshmana, implying it would be strategically unwise.

summary:
Canto LII
Provoked by the harsh words of Hanuman, Ravana orders him to be put
to death. Vibhisana, however, exhorts the latter to desist from
this dastardly act, pointing out that the killing of an
envoy is forbidden by the Sastras
content: Vibhisana continues to advise Ravana against killing Hanuman. He argues that it is unbecoming of Ravana, whose power surpasses even the gods and demons, to engage in such a trivial act of violence against the monkey envoy. Instead, he suggests that Ravana should dispatch some of his best warriors to capture Rama and Lakshmana, showcasing his dominance to his enemies. Ravana, surprisingly, takes his brother's counsel to heart.

summary:
Canto LIII
Wrapping up the tail of Hanuman in rags and soaking it in oil, and then
lighting it up, the ogres, as urged by Ravana, take Hanuman round the city
to the accompaniment of beat of drums. Hearing this news from the lips
of the ogresses, Sita prays to the god of fire on oath to see that
Hanuman remains unscathed. Climbing up the gate and shedding
the fire by attenuating his body, and then resuming vast

proportions and seizing the iron bar of the gate,

Hanuman kills with it the ogres guarding the gate
content: Hanuman, captured by Ravana's forces, is punished by having his tail set on fire.  Vibhishana advises against killing Hanuman, suggesting a less severe punishment.  Ravana orders Hanuman's tail to be wrapped in rags, soaked in oil, and lit. As the ogres carry out the order, Hanuman grows in size.  He then uses his burning tail as a weapon, striking the ogres. Despite being bound, Hanuman considers breaking free and fighting back, but also reflects on his duty to his master, Rama.  He reasons that if his capture serves Rama's purpose, the ogres will not face retribution for their actions.

summary:
Canto LIII
Wrapping up the tail of Hanuman in rags and soaking it in oil, and then
lighting it up, the ogres, as urged by Ravana, take Hanuman round the city
to the accompaniment of beat of drums. Hearing this news from the lips
of the ogresses, Sita prays to the god of fire on oath to see that
Hanuman remains unscathed. Climbing up the gate and shedding
the fire by attenuating his body, and then resuming vast

proportions and seizing the iron bar of the gate,

Hanuman kills with it the ogres guarding the gate
content: Hanuman, though capable of defeating all the ogres in battle, chooses to endure his captivity for Rama's sake.  He also notes that being paraded around Lanka allows him a second opportunity to observe the city's fortifications, which he hadn't been able to examine thoroughly during the night.

summary:
Canto LIII
Wrapping up the tail of Hanuman in rags and soaking it in oil, and then
lighting it up, the ogres, as urged by Ravana, take Hanuman round the city
to the accompaniment of beat of drums. Hearing this news from the lips
of the ogresses, Sita prays to the god of fire on oath to see that
Hanuman remains unscathed. Climbing up the gate and shedding
the fire by attenuating his body, and then resuming vast

proportions and seizing the iron bar of the gate,

Hanuman kills with it the ogres guarding the gate
content: Hanuman, resolved to complete his reconnaissance of Lanka, willingly allows the Rakshasas to bind and parade him through the city.  His captors, making a spectacle of him, announce his supposed crimes with conches and drums as they drag him through streets and squares.  The curious citizens of Lanka – men, women, and children – flock to see the captured monkey.  As Hanuman’s tail is set alight, the news reaches Sita, relayed by Rakshasa women. Distressed by the news of Hanuman’s plight, Sita prays to Agni, the god of fire, invoking her devotion to Rama and her past austerities, to protect Hanuman from the flames.

summary:
Canto LIII
Wrapping up the tail of Hanuman in rags and soaking it in oil, and then
lighting it up, the ogres, as urged by Ravana, take Hanuman round the city
to the accompaniment of beat of drums. Hearing this news from the lips
of the ogresses, Sita prays to the god of fire on oath to see that
Hanuman remains unscathed. Climbing up the gate and shedding
the fire by attenuating his body, and then resuming vast

proportions and seizing the iron bar of the gate,

Hanuman kills with it the ogres guarding the gate
content: Sita, anguished by Hanuman's predicament, fervently prayed to Agni, the fire god, for his protection. She appealed to Agni's compassion for her, invoking her virtuous conduct and unwavering devotion to Rama. She hoped that her past austerities and her husband's inherent righteousness would move Agni to spare Hanuman.  Miraculously, the flames began to burn gently, their heat replaced by a comforting coolness.  Even the wind, Hanuman’s divine parent, blew cold, soothing Sita’s worries.  Hanuman himself marveled at the fire's inability to harm him. He pondered this strange phenomenon, observing the intense flames that brought no pain, only a sensation akin to ice.  He reasoned that this must be due to Rama's divine influence, the same power that had manifested the miraculous mountain during his ocean crossing. Recalling the respect shown to Rama by both the ocean and Mount Mainaka, Hanuman concluded that Agni, too, must be demonstrating reverence by its gentle touch. He also attributed his safety to Sita's compassion, Rama's inherent power, and his father Vayu's friendship with Agni.

summary:
Canto LIII
Wrapping up the tail of Hanuman in rags and soaking it in oil, and then
lighting it up, the ogres, as urged by Ravana, take Hanuman round the city
to the accompaniment of beat of drums. Hearing this news from the lips
of the ogresses, Sita prays to the god of fire on oath to see that
Hanuman remains unscathed. Climbing up the gate and shedding
the fire by attenuating his body, and then resuming vast

proportions and seizing the iron bar of the gate,

Hanuman kills with it the ogres guarding the gate
content: Hanuman, briefly pondering his capture, questioned how such a powerful being like himself could be bound by the ogres. Determined to retaliate, he broke free from his bonds, leaping into the air with a mighty roar. He reached the towering city gate, now deserted by the ogre guards. Shrinking his form to a minuscule size, he slipped free of his restraints and then returned to his colossal, mountain-like stature.  Spotting an iron-studded club at the gate, he wielded it to vanquish the remaining guards.  Surrounded by the fiery glow emanating from his burning tail, Hanuman, the fierce warrior, surveyed Lanka, radiating a brilliance akin to the sun.

summary:
Canto LIV
Making up his mind to set fire to the city of Lanka, which was the only
work left for him to do, and coming out into the open, Hanuman
burns the entire city barring the abode of Vibhisana.
Astonished to see the city burning, the gods and
Gandharvas (celestial musicians) are
filled with supreme joy
content: Hanuman, having successfully found Sita, considered his next move.  He wondered what else he could do to further weaken his enemies, the Rakshasas.  He had already damaged the Ashok Vatika grove, killed several Rakshasas, and diminished their army. Destroying Lanka's citadel remained his final objective. He reasoned that demolishing the fort would make his earlier efforts, including crossing the ocean and vandalizing the grove, worthwhile. It would also ensure the success of his mission to find Sita. Hanuman then decided to use the fire on his tail, which burned his enemies but didn't harm him, to set Lanka ablaze.  He began systematically setting fire to the buildings, moving quickly from rooftop to rooftop.  Undeterred, he surveyed the mansions and gardens as he spread the flames.  With great force, he set fire to the homes of prominent Rakshasas, including Prahasta, Mahaparswa, Vajradamshtra, Suka, Sarana, Indrajit, Jambumali, and Sumali.

summary:
Canto LIV
Making up his mind to set fire to the city of Lanka, which was the only
work left for him to do, and coming out into the open, Hanuman
burns the entire city barring the abode of Vibhisana.
Astonished to see the city burning, the gods and
Gandharvas (celestial musicians) are
filled with supreme joy
content: Hanuman continued his fiery rampage through Lanka, specifically targeting the homes of prominent Rakshasa figures.  He set ablaze the residences of Rasmiketu, SuryaSatru, Hraswakarna, Damstra, Romasa, Yuddhonmatta, Matta, Dhwajagriva, Vidyujjihva, Ghora, Hastimukha, Karala, Visala, Sonitaksa, Kumbhakarana, Makaraksa, Narantaka, Kumbha, Nikumbha, Yajnasatru, and Brahmasatru. Notably, he spared the house of Vibhishana.  Hanuman then proceeded to burn the opulent mansions of wealthy Rakshasas, consuming their riches. Finally, he reached the magnificent palace of Ravana, which was adorned with jewels and resembled the majestic mountains Meru and Mandara.  There, Hanuman unleashed the fire from his tail, and with a roar like a doomsday cloud, the flames spread rapidly, fueled by the wind, engulfing Ravana's palace in a destructive inferno.

summary:
Canto LIV
Making up his mind to set fire to the city of Lanka, which was the only
work left for him to do, and coming out into the open, Hanuman
burns the entire city barring the abode of Vibhisana.
Astonished to see the city burning, the gods and
Gandharvas (celestial musicians) are
filled with supreme joy
content: Hanuman, empowered by the wind, spread the fire throughout Lanka.  The grand mansions, adorned with gold, gems, and pearls, crumbled and fell like the celestial homes of Siddhas.  Panic-stricken Rakshasas ran to save their homes, their wealth and pride lost.  They cried out that the fire god himself had come in the form of a monkey.  Women and children fell from the burning buildings.  Some women, engulfed in flames, appeared like lightning against the smoke.  Hanuman watched as molten gold, silver, diamonds, corals, and cat’s-eye gems flowed from the burning structures. He felt no fatigue, just as fire is never sated by consuming wood and grass.  Similarly, the earth received the bodies of the slain Rakshasas. The inferno, sparked by Hanuman, raged like the fire that destroyed the mythical city of Tripura.  It climbed Mount Trikuta, spreading its flames like a doomsday fire, fueled by the bodies of the Rakshasas, and reaching towards the heavens. The flames, bright as a million suns, filled the air with thunderous roars.

summary:
Canto LIV
Making up his mind to set fire to the city of Lanka, which was the only
work left for him to do, and coming out into the open, Hanuman
burns the entire city barring the abode of Vibhisana.
Astonished to see the city burning, the gods and
Gandharvas (celestial musicians) are
filled with supreme joy
content: The fire, intensely bright and scarlet, spread rapidly towards the sky, creating massive columns of dark smoke that resembled blue lotuses. The Rakshasas, witnessing this terrifying spectacle, believed Hanuman to be a god – Indra, Yama, Varuna, the fire god within Shiva’s third eye, Surya, Kubera, Chandra, or even Kala, the Time-Spirit himself. Some wondered if it was Brahma, disguised as a monkey, unleashing his wrath upon them. Others speculated if it was Vishnu's divine energy, taking the form of a monkey to destroy the Rakshasas.  The Rakshasas gathered before Ravana, lamenting the destruction of their city, their homes, and the loss of life. Lanka, once vibrant, was now consumed by flames, its warriors defeated and scattered. Hanuman, having set the city ablaze, stood amidst the devastation, witnessing the scene of destruction and chaos.  He had destroyed the Asoka grove, defeated powerful Rakshasas in battle, and set fire to the magnificent buildings of Lanka. Having completed his task, he turned his thoughts towards Rama.  The gods, witnessing Hanuman's extraordinary feat of strength and speed, rejoiced and praised the valiant son of the wind god.

summary:
Canto LIV
Making up his mind to set fire to the city of Lanka, which was the only
work left for him to do, and coming out into the open, Hanuman
burns the entire city barring the abode of Vibhisana.
Astonished to see the city burning, the gods and
Gandharvas (celestial musicians) are
filled with supreme joy
content: Hanuman, having completed his mission of setting Lanka ablaze, was a sight to behold. The gods, celestial musicians, and other divine beings rejoiced at this extraordinary feat.  With immense power and radiance, Hanuman stood atop a building, flames emanating from his tail, resembling the sun with its aura. After inflicting significant damage upon Lanka, he extinguished his fiery tail in the ocean. The gods, along with celestial sages and seers, marveled at the sight of Lanka reduced to ashes, some even mistaking Hanuman for the fire of cosmic destruction.

summary:
Canto LV
Seeing the whole of Lanka reduced to ashes and presuming Sita too
to have shared its lot, Hanuman begins to reproach himself and
gets overwhelmed with grief. In the meantime, he hears
of her safety from the lips of Siddhas, Caranas
(celestial bards) and others and feels comforted
content: Hanuman, observing the burning city of Lanka and its panicked inhabitants, was overcome with worry and self-reproach. He lamented his impulsive act of setting the city ablaze, recognizing the destructive nature of unchecked anger.  He reflected on the virtue of controlling one's temper, comparing it to extinguishing a fire with water.  Hanuman condemned his anger, acknowledging that it can lead even the wisest to commit terrible deeds, from harming elders to insulting the pious.  He further berated himself for his lack of foresight, fearing that his actions had inadvertently caused Sita's death and undermined Rama's mission.  Consumed by guilt, Hanuman believed he had destroyed the very purpose of his journey and considered ending his own life.

summary:
Canto LV
Seeing the whole of Lanka reduced to ashes and presuming Sita too
to have shared its lot, Hanuman begins to reproach himself and
gets overwhelmed with grief. In the meantime, he hears
of her safety from the lips of Siddhas, Caranas
(celestial bards) and others and feels comforted
content: Overwhelmed by despair, Hanuman considers ending his life by fire, water, or at the hands of sea creatures. He believes he cannot face Sugriva, Rama, or Lakshmana, having ruined their mission through his rash act of burning Lanka. Hanuman laments his impulsive anger, a characteristic of his simian nature, known throughout the three worlds. He blames his uncontrolled rage for Sita's presumed demise, even though he was capable of protecting her. Hanuman predicts a cascade of tragic consequences:  Rama and Lakshmana perishing due to Sita’s death, followed by Sugriva and his kin, and then the virtuous Bharata and Shatrughna succumbing to grief. Ultimately, he foresees the destruction of the righteous Ikshvaku dynasty and widespread suffering throughout the world. He believes he has lost all merit and become an instrument of global destruction. However, amidst his despair, Hanuman recalls positive omens and considers that Sita, protected by her own virtue and Rama's power, may have survived the fire, just as he himself did. He reasons that fire cannot harm fire, and Sita's purity and Rama's strength would shield her from the flames.

summary:
Canto LV
Seeing the whole of Lanka reduced to ashes and presuming Sita too
to have shared its lot, Hanuman begins to reproach himself and
gets overwhelmed with grief. In the meantime, he hears
of her safety from the lips of Siddhas, Caranas
(celestial bards) and others and feels comforted
content: Hanuman, reflecting on Sita's virtue, questioned how the fire could harm someone so revered by Rama and his brothers. He recalled how the fire hadn't even burned his own tail and wondered if Sita's purity might have protected her.  Remembering the miraculous appearance of Mainaka mountain amidst the sea, Hanuman reasoned that Sita's devotion and truthful nature would shield her from the flames. He overheard celestial bards marveling at the extent of the fire's destruction across Lanka, yet confirming that Sita remained unharmed.  This news filled Hanuman with immense joy and relief, reaffirming his earlier hopes based on positive omens and Sita's inherent strength.  Knowing Sita was safe, Hanuman, his mission successful, decided to return to Rama.

summary:
Canto LVI

Hanuman sees Sita once more and bidding adieu to her,
takes a leap across the sea
content: Hanuman reassured Sita of her safety. As he prepared to leave, Sita, deeply concerned, urged him to stay, believing his presence lessened her immeasurable grief. She worried about his safe return and the immense challenge of crossing the ocean, doubting even Sugriva and his army's ability to achieve this feat.  She acknowledged Hanuman, Garuda, and the wind-god as the only beings capable of such a crossing.  Sita questioned Hanuman, an expert in action, about his plan to overcome this seemingly insurmountable obstacle. While acknowledging Hanuman's individual capability to rescue her, Sita expressed her desire for Rama to achieve this victory, leading his forces to conquer Lanka and reclaim her, an act she deemed truly worthy of him.

summary:
Canto LVI

Hanuman sees Sita once more and bidding adieu to her,
takes a leap across the sea
content: Hanuman advised Sita to remain courageous so that Rama, known for his valor, could showcase his true prowess.  He then reassured Sita that Sugriva, the leader of the monkeys and bears, was committed to her rescue.  A vast army was being assembled and would arrive soon, led by Sugriva.  Furthermore, Rama and Lakshmana would join the battle and destroy Lanka with their arrows.  Hanuman confidently predicted Rama's victory over Ravana and his forces, promising Sita's safe return to Ayodhya. He urged her to be patient and have faith, assuring her that she would witness Rama's triumph over Ravana.  He compared their reunion to that of Rohini and the moon-god, emphasizing its certainty.  Finally, Hanuman reiterated Rama's imminent arrival with his monkey and bear allies, promising the end of her sorrow.  Having thus comforted Sita, Hanuman prepared to depart.

summary:
Canto LVI

Hanuman sees Sita once more and bidding adieu to her,
takes a leap across the sea
content: Hanuman, having demonstrated his immense strength by defeating powerful demons and making his presence felt throughout Lanka, instilled confidence in Sita.  He then prepared to return across the ocean.  Driven by a powerful desire to rejoin Rama, Hanuman, the mightiest of monkeys, ascended Mount Arista.  The mountain's description emphasizes its grandeur and natural beauty:  its peaks shrouded in clouds, adorned with diverse trees and vibrant blossoms, echoing with the sounds of waterfalls and teeming with wildlife.  As Hanuman climbed, eager to return with news of Sita, the mountain groaned under his immense weight, its rocks crumbling beneath his feet.  He then surveyed the vast ocean, preparing for his tremendous leap.

summary:
Canto LVI

Hanuman sees Sita once more and bidding adieu to her,
takes a leap across the sea
content: As Hanuman prepared for his leap, his immense power caused widespread disruption on Mount Arista. The trees, laden with blossoms, were uprooted and thrown to the ground as if struck by lightning.  Lions roared, their cries echoing through the sky as the mountain pressed in upon their caves.  The Vidyadhara women, their clothing disheveled and ornaments scattered, fled in terror.  Large, venomous snakes with fiery tongues were crushed beneath the pressure.  The Kinnaras, Nagas, Gandharvas, Yaksas, and Vidyadharas abandoned the mountain, taking refuge in the sky as the peak itself began to sink under Hanuman's weight.  The once towering mountain, ten yojanas wide and thirty yojanas high, became level with the earth.  With the ocean before him, its shores lashed by waves, Hanuman launched himself into the air, ready to cross the vast expanse of water.

Hanuman, powerful and swift, crossed the sky like a winged mountain, touching Mainaka mountain on his way.  He roared with triumphant joy upon seeing Mahendra mountain, signaling his successful return to the waiting monkeys. Jambavan reassured the startled monkeys that Hanuman's roar indicated his mission's success.  Landing on Mahendra mountain, Hanuman greeted Jambavan and the others and briefly recounted his discovery of Sita.  Eager to hear the full story, Prince Angada and the other monkeys settled down on a large flat rock. Continuing his journey, Hanuman soared through the sky, which seemed like a vast ocean, with celestial bodies resembling aquatic features: constellations like water lilies and swans, the moon a white lily, the sun a waterfowl, and clouds like duckweed. He moved with immense speed, seemingly consuming the sky itself, brushing against the moon, and parting the clouds.  The sky displayed clouds of various hues - white, pink, blue, reddish-brown, green, and dark. Hanuman, frequently disappearing and reappearing within the clouds, resembled the waxing and waning moon, his white clothing adding to the illusion. He continued his flight, tearing through the cloud formations like a powerful bird.

Hanuman, having vanquished powerful demons and made his name known throughout Lanka, caused chaos and grief for Ravana. After meeting Sita and defeating Ravana's strongest warriors, Hanuman, full of energy, crossed the sea again, touching Mainaka mountain on his way.  Like an arrow shot from a bow, he approached Mahendra mountain.  He roared like thunder, filling the sky with the sound.  Reaching the area where his fellow monkeys awaited, Hanuman, eager to see them, let out a great cry and waved his tail, his roar shaking the sky and sun.  The monkeys on the northern shore, waiting anxiously for Hanuman’s return, heard the whooshing sound of his powerful flight, like a massive wind-driven cloud.  Hearing Hanuman’s thunderous roar, the monkeys, distressed with worry, became filled with anticipation and joy at his return. Jambavan, overjoyed, then addressed the waiting monkeys.

Hanuman's triumphant return was evident in his powerful roar, causing the waiting monkeys to leap with joy and anticipation.  They scrambled from tree to tree, eager to greet their hero.  Standing on branches, they waved their garments in celebration. Hanuman, with a roar like wind escaping a mountain cavern, descended from the sky like a storm cloud towards Mahendra mountain. The monkeys, awestruck, watched him land with the grace of a mountain settling upon the earth, near a refreshing spring.  Overjoyed, they surrounded Hanuman, their faces beaming with happiness. They brought him gifts of fruits and roots, while some roared with delight and others brought branches for him to rest upon.

Hanuman, the mighty monkey, respectfully greeted the elders, including Jambavan and Prince Angada.  Honored by them and the other monkeys, Hanuman shared the news that he had found Sita. He sat down with Angada and described Sita's condition: she was residing in the Asoka grove, guarded by fearsome ogresses, longing for Rama.  Wearing a single braid, she was weakened by fasting, her hair matted and dusty, her body thin.  This news brought immense joy to the monkeys.  Some roared, some cheered, some bellowed.  They waved their tails, embraced Hanuman, and celebrated his return. Angada praised Hanuman's unmatched courage and valor in crossing the vast sea and returning safely, calling him their savior.

Hanuman's successful mission brought immense relief and joy to the monkey army.  Jambavan, Angada, Hanuman, and the other monkeys gathered on the mountain rocks.  Eager to hear the details of Hanuman's journey across the ocean and his encounter with Sita and Ravana in Lanka, the monkeys respectfully gathered around Angada and Hanuman, their faces turned towards Hanuman in anticipation. Angada, adorned with armlets, sat among the throng like Indra among the gods.  The scene was filled with joy and excitement, the mountain peak seeming to glow with the reflected happiness of the assembled monkeys.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Hanuman, having returned from Lanka, rejoices with the other monkeys atop Mount Mahendra. Jambavan asks Hanuman to recount his journey and his encounter with Sita.  He inquires about Sita's well-being, Ravana's treatment of her, how Hanuman located her, and her response. Jambavan also seeks Hanuman's advice on what to report to Rama and Lakshmana upon their arrival in Lanka.  Prompted by Jambavan, Hanuman begins his narration, describing his leap across the ocean from Mount Mahendra.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: During his flight to Lanka, Hanuman encountered a golden mountain, Mainaka, obstructing his path.  He initially prepared to destroy the mountain but Mainaka addressed him, revealing himself as a friend of Hanuman's father, the wind-god. Mainaka explained that he and other mountains once had wings and roamed freely, causing disruption.  Indra, however, clipped their wings. Mainaka was saved by Hanuman's father.  He urged Hanuman to assist Rama, whom he described as a righteous and powerful warrior. After this exchange, Hanuman continued his journey, and Mainaka, both in his human and mountain form, disappeared.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Hanuman, having left Mainaka, continued his journey to Lanka at great speed.  He then encountered the serpentine goddess Surasa in the middle of the ocean. She declared that he was destined to be her food, a claim supported by the gods.  Hanuman, appearing respectful and slightly afraid, explained his mission to find Sita, wife of Rama, who had been kidnapped by Ravana. He promised to return to Surasa after completing his task for Rama.  Surasa, however, challenged him, stating no one escapes her.  Hanuman, initially ten yojanas (eighty miles) wide, increased his size by half.  Surasa widened her mouth even further to consume him.  In response, Hanuman shrunk himself to the size of a thumb, entered her mouth, and promptly exited.  Surasa, impressed by his cleverness, then reverted to her true form and addressed Hanuman.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Surasa, a fearsome ogress, challenged Hanuman, demanding he become her meal.  Hanuman, clever and respectful, agreed but then grew larger than her gaping maw.  As Surasa expanded her mouth further, Hanuman shrunk to the size of a thumb, zipped through her mouth, and then escaped into the sky. Defeated, Surasa acknowledged Hanuman's wit and blessed his journey to find Sita.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Hanuman, having defeated Surasa, heard voices from the sky celebrating his victory over the demoness Simhika.  Remembering his mission to find Sita, he continued his journey and reached the shores of Lanka.  Unseen by the fearsome ogre guards, Hanuman entered the city at sunset. A powerful, dark-skinned female entity confronted him.  Hanuman quickly defeated her.  She then revealed herself as the spirit of Lanka and prophesied Hanuman's victory over all the ogres. Despite searching throughout the night, even within Ravana's palace, Hanuman couldn't find Sita. Dejected, he then spotted a walled garden filled with Ashoka trees.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Hanuman, climbing a tree, saw a grove of plantain trees and then spotted Sita.  She appeared thin and pale from fasting, wearing a single garment, her hair dusty.  Grief and worry were evident.  She was surrounded by grotesque, cruel ogresses, like a deer cornered by tigers.  Sita, with her doe-like eyes, wore a single braid, lay on the bare ground, and looked as fragile as a lotus plant in winter.  Rejected by Ravana and despairing, she seemed resigned to death. Hanuman watched her from his perch in the tree.  Suddenly, he heard loud voices, the clinking of jewelry, and other commotion coming from Ravana's quarters. Alarmed, Hanuman shrank his size and hid amongst the thick leaves.  Ravana then arrived with his wives at the place where Sita was held captive.  Upon seeing the ogre king, Sita instinctively drew her knees up and covered herself with her arms.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Ravana, approaching the captive Sita, prostrated himself and implored her to favor him.  Sita, terrified and desperate, found no solace in his presence.  Enraged by his advances, she retorted that he dared address her, the wife of the mighty Rama and daughter-in-law of King Dasaratha, with such a disrespectful proposal. She questioned his courage, highlighting his cowardly act of abducting her in Rama's absence.  She declared Rama's superiority, stating that Ravana wasn't even fit to be his servant.  Infuriated by her defiance, Ravana, eyes blazing with anger, raised his fist to strike her.  The women attendants cried out in alarm.  Mandodari, Ravana's wife, intervened, restraining him and attempting to pacify his anger.  She urged him to enjoy her company instead of pursuing Sita, suggesting that Sita was no better than herself or any of the other celestial women available to him.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: After Ravana's forceful exit, the grotesque ogresses guarding Sita threatened her with harsh words.  Sita, however, dismissed their intimidation, their shouts and threats meaningless to her. The ogresses, frustrated by Sita's unwavering resolve, reported her death wish to Ravana.  Exhausted and defeated, the guards fell asleep. While they slept, Sita, deeply grieved and loyal to Rama, lamented her plight.  Among the sleeping ogresses, Trijata awoke and urged the others not to harm Sita. Trijata recounted a terrifying dream that foretold the ogres' destruction and Rama's victory, suggesting that Sita alone could protect them from Rama's wrath.  Trijata proposed that they seek Sita's favor, believing that such a dream indicated Sita's imminent release from suffering and that showing respect to her would be beneficial.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Hanuman, encouraged by Trijata's dream foretelling Rama's victory and the ogresses' reliance on Sita for protection, felt renewed hope.  He devised a plan to speak with Sita. He began by praising Rama's lineage, prompting a tearful Sita to question his identity and purpose. Hanuman revealed himself as Sugriva's emissary, sent by Rama with a ring as a token.  Upon recognizing the ring, Sita expressed her desire to be rescued by Rama. Hanuman then respectfully requested a token from Sita to bring back to Rama, and she offered him a jewel.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Sita gave Hanuman a jewel and tearfully shared details known only to Rama. Hanuman bowed to Sita and circumambulated her, intending to return. Sita urged him to quickly relay her plight to Rama and Lakshmana, emphasizing her dwindling hope and limited time.  Angered by Sita's desperation, Hanuman grew to an immense size and began destroying Ravana's pleasure garden.  The ogresses, witnessing the devastation and Hanuman's power, alerted Ravana, urging him to swiftly punish the monkey before he could escape.  Ravana, in turn, dispatched his formidable Kinkara warriors.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Hanuman recounted his escalating conflict with Ravana's forces. He single-handedly defeated eighty thousand ogres armed with various weapons. The surviving few reported the devastating loss to Ravana.  Enraged, Hanuman then destroyed a sacred sanctuary, killing one hundred more ogres.  Ravana responded by sending Jambumali and his followers, but Hanuman swiftly defeated them as well.  Next, Ravana deployed his minister's sons and an infantry regiment, who also met their demise at Hanuman's hands.  Finally, Ravana sent his mighty son Aksa and other ogres into battle.  Hanuman overpowered Aksa, seizing him by the feet, whirling him around, and then crushing him.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Hanuman, captured after his destructive rampage, was brought before Ravana. Indrajit, Ravana's son, had subdued Hanuman using Brahma's weapon after witnessing Hanuman's overwhelming power.  Hanuman then explained to Ravana he was a messenger from Rama and Sugriva, seeking Sita and offering counsel from Sugriva in the spirit of righteousness and mutual benefit. He further detailed his alliance with Rama while residing on Mount Rsyamuka.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Hanuman recounts to Jambavan and the assembled monkeys how he conveyed his message to Ravana.  He reminded Ravana that Sita had been kidnapped and that he should return her. He explained Rama's alliance with Sugriva, forged after Rama aided Sugriva in reclaiming his kingdom from Vali.  Hanuman stressed that he was acting as Sugriva's envoy, offering Ravana a chance to return Sita peacefully before facing the might of the monkey army.  Ravana, enraged by Hanuman's words, ordered his execution. However, Vibhisana, Ravana's wise brother, intervened, reminding Ravana that killing a messenger, especially one who had committed no offense, violated all codes of conduct.  He argued that even for a serious transgression, the prescribed punishment for a messenger was mutilation, not death.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Ravana, advised by Vibhisana against killing Hanuman, instead ordered his tail to be set on fire.  Hanuman's tail was wrapped in cloth and set alight.  Despite being bound and struck by the Rakshasas, Hanuman felt no pain, focused as he was on observing the city.  He was paraded through Lanka's streets with his burning tail. Hanuman then shrunk his size, broke free, and grabbed an iron bar, attacking the Rakshasas.  Using his flaming tail, he set fire to the city.  Believing Sita to have perished in the flames, Hanuman despaired, thinking he had ruined Rama's mission. He then overheard celestial bards proclaiming that Sita was still alive, sparking a renewed sense of hope and prompting him to reconsider the situation.

summary:
Canto LVIII
Enquired by Jambavan, seated alongwith the monkey hordes, Hanuman
narrates in extenso how he saw Mount Mainaka in the course of
his journey to Lanka, reached Lanka, beheld Janaka’s
daughter and returned to Mount Mahendra
content: Hanuman recounts how several auspicious signs convinced him that Sita remained unharmed despite the fire engulfing Lanka. He felt no pain from his burning tail, his heart was filled with joy, and sweet fragrances wafted through the air.  He also recalled past experiences where such omens proved true. Coupled with Sita's known devotion to Rama and the pronouncements of all-seeing celestial beings, Hanuman was certain of Sita's survival.  He confirms having seen Sita again and receiving her permission to depart. After reaching Mount Arista in Lanka, Hanuman began his journey back, eager to rejoin his companions.  He traveled the celestial path used by the wind, sun, moon, and other divine beings before finally finding them. He attributes his success to Rama's grace, the monkey's strength, and Sugriva's guidance.  Hanuman concludes his narrative, urging the others to complete any remaining tasks.

summary:
Canto LIX
Picturing to his fellow monkeys the said plight of Sita, and feeling that
it behoved them all to see Sri Rama only after recovering Sita
from Lanka, nay, recounting the past heroic exploits of
Jambavan and others, Hanuman incites them all to
have recourse to the arbitrament of war
content: Hanuman, addressing the assembled monkeys, recounts Sita's unwavering virtue and resilience, emphasizing her ability to endure hardship and her potential for righteous anger. He notes that even Ravana, powerful as he is, could not diminish her through his touch.  Hanuman declares his confidence in his ability to single-handedly defeat Ravana and destroy Lanka, but emphasizes the greater potential for victory with the combined strength of the monkey army. He expresses his determination to vanquish Ravana, his army, his sons, and his brothers in battle, even countering Indrajit's formidable arsenal of divine missiles.  He urges the monkeys to join him in reclaiming Sita and returning her to Rama and Lakshmana.

summary:
Canto LIX
Picturing to his fellow monkeys the said plight of Sita, and feeling that
it behoved them all to see Sri Rama only after recovering Sita
from Lanka, nay, recounting the past heroic exploits of
Jambavan and others, Hanuman incites them all to
have recourse to the arbitrament of war
content: Hanuman continues to rally the monkey army, boasting of their collective strength and his own prowess. He asserts that his power, if unleashed with their approval, could devastate even the gods, let alone Ravana's demonic forces.  He emphasizes his restraint, awaiting their consent before unleashing his full potential.  Hanuman then extols the unwavering strength of Jambavan, declaring that even the mightiest forces of nature could not shake him in battle. He further praises Angada, Nila, Mainda, and Dwivida, highlighting their individual capabilities and asserting their unmatched power against any opponent.  Recounting his exploits in Lanka, Hanuman describes how he single-handedly ravaged the city and boldly proclaimed Rama's name throughout its streets, declaring his own servitude to the Kosalan king.  Finally, he paints a poignant picture of Sita's plight, emphasizing her sorrow, fragility, and confinement within Ravana's Asoka grove.

summary:
Canto LIX
Picturing to his fellow monkeys the said plight of Sita, and feeling that
it behoved them all to see Sri Rama only after recovering Sita
from Lanka, nay, recounting the past heroic exploits of
Jambavan and others, Hanuman incites them all to
have recourse to the arbitrament of war
content: Hanuman continues his impassioned plea to the monkey army, describing Sita's dire circumstances in Lanka. He portrays her as a virtuous and loyal wife, imprisoned against her will and suffering greatly from separation from Rama.  He emphasizes her physical and emotional distress, highlighting her thinness, pallor, and constant grief. Despite her ordeal, she remains resolute in her devotion to Rama, refusing any interaction with Ravana. Hanuman stresses Sita's unwavering hope and joy upon learning of the alliance between Rama and Sugriva.  He concludes by urging the monkeys to devise a plan to rescue her, suggesting that Ravana's power, though substantial, will ultimately be his undoing due to the sin he committed by abducting Sita.  Rama, Hanuman implies, will be the instrument of Ravana's divinely ordained destruction.

Hanuman, possessing immense strength and the ability to change his form, swiftly explored Lanka. He arrived at Ravana's magnificent palace, its walls gleaming like the sun and guarded by fierce sentinels.  The palace was adorned with silver artwork, gold-decorated archways, and beautiful enclosures.  Elephant riders, brave warriors, and resilient horses attended the palace, along with elaborate chariots decorated with animal skins, ivory, gold, silver, and ringing bells.  The palace interior was filled with precious stones, grand seating, chariot sheds, and warrior quarters.  The grounds teemed with various captivating animals and birds.  Disciplined guards and other ogres protected the palace, which was also inhabited by numerous beautiful women.

Hanuman surveyed Ravana's opulent palace, bustling with activity and filled with the sounds of jewelry, music, and the sea.  The palace, fragrant with sandalwood and crowded with prominent figures, housed luxurious chambers, precious stones, and chariots.  Hanuman, undeterred, explored the palace grounds, observing the gardens and the residences of Ravana's kin and allies, including Prahasta, Mahaparswa, Kumbhakarna, and Vibhisana, as well as  Mahodara, Virupaksa, Vidyujjihva, Vidyunmali, Vajradamstra, Suka, Sarana, Indrajit, Jambumali, and Sumali.

Hanuman continued his exploration of Ravana's palace, leaping from rooftop to rooftop. He surveyed the residences of numerous Rakshasa warriors, including Rasmiketu, Suryasatru, Vajrakaya, Dhumraksa, Sampati, Vidyudripa, Bhima, Ghana, Vighana, Sukanabha, Cakra, Satha, Kapata, Hraswakarna, Damstra, Lomaśa, Yuddhonmatta, Matta, Dhwajagriva, Sadi, Vidyujjihva, Dwijihva, Hastimukha, Karala, and Sonitaksa, noting their wealth and possessions.  After completing this comprehensive survey, Hanuman returned to Ravana's main palace. There, he observed the ogresses guarding Ravana's chambers and the various Rakshasa guards armed with diverse weapons. He also noted the presence of numerous tethered horses, both red and white.

Hanuman observed within Ravana's palace highly trained elephants, comparable to Indra's Airavata, their trumpeting echoing like thunder. The palace itself was adorned with gold, shimmering like the morning sun, and filled with palanquins, ornate buildings, pleasure houses, and pens for peacocks.  It was decorated with flagstaffs and overflowing with precious stones and treasures, reminiscent of Kubera's opulent abode.  The palace glittered with the brilliance of gems and Ravana's own splendor. Hanuman also saw golden couches, seats, and vessels, and noted the palace floors were damp with spilled wines and liquors. The air was filled with the sounds of anklets, girdles, clapping, and drums, creating a constant din within the multitude of mansions, which were filled with women and surrounded by spacious enclosures.

Angada, son of Vali, declared that the mighty monkeys Mainda and Dwivida, fueled by a boon of invincibility from Brahma, grew arrogant and defeated the gods.  He asserted that even he alone could destroy Lanka and kill Ravana, and with the other monkeys' help, it would be even easier.  He questioned the point of returning without Sita, reminding everyone of Hanuman's strength and the monkeys' prowess.  He urged them to conquer Lanka, kill Ravana, retrieve Sita, and return victorious. He reasoned that since Hanuman had already vanquished the ogre's forces, there was no reason to delay. They should simply retrieve Sita and return her to Rama and Lakshmana, sparing the other monkeys a difficult journey across the ocean.

Angada declared that the monkey army should proceed to Lanka, defeat Ravana and his forces, and then return to Rama, Lakshmana, and Sugriva with Sita.  Jambavan, however, advised against this course of action. He reminded Angada that their mission was only to search for Sita, not to retrieve her.  He argued that Rama had publicly vowed to personally win back Sita, and taking her back now would invalidate Rama's vow and render Hanuman's efforts meaningless. Jambavan suggested they return to Rama and Sugriva and inform them of Hanuman’s success in locating Sita.  He then gently rebuked Angada, suggesting he align his plan with Rama’s original intent.

summary:
Canto LXI
Set out from the Mahendra mountain in the direction of Kiskindha, the
monkeys halt at Madhuvana, a grove guarded by the monkey Dadhimukha,
and most beloved of Sugriva, which falls on their way. When the
monkeys began to enjoy the fruits of the grove with the
permission of Prince Angada, Dadhimukha, comes in
their way and is lacerated with their claws and teeth
content: The monkey troop, led by Angada and Hanuman, heeded Jambavan's counsel and set off from the Mahendra mountain towards Kiskindha. Filled with joy and purpose, they soared through the air, their immense forms resembling mountains or rutting elephants, their might acknowledged even by celestial beings.  Their mission was clear: to serve Rama and achieve his goals.  They were eager to share the good news of their alliance and prepared for any battle that might come. They soon reached a lush grove, Madhuvana, reminiscent of the celestial Nandana garden. This protected grove, beloved by all, belonged to Sugriva and was guarded by his maternal uncle, the valiant Dadhimukha.  The monkeys were eager to enjoy the grove's bounty and requested Angada's permission to partake of the honey.  After consulting with the elders, including Jambavan, Angada granted them permission.

summary:
Canto LXI
Set out from the Mahendra mountain in the direction of Kiskindha, the
monkeys halt at Madhuvana, a grove guarded by the monkey Dadhimukha,
and most beloved of Sugriva, which falls on their way. When the
monkeys began to enjoy the fruits of the grove with the
permission of Prince Angada, Dadhimukha, comes in
their way and is lacerated with their claws and teeth
content: Allowed to enjoy the fruits of Madhuvana by Angada, the monkeys reveled in the grove's abundance, becoming intoxicated and boisterous.  Their revelry included singing, dancing, laughing, and some even bowing down in reverence, while others fell, ran, leaped, or simply raved. Some monkeys supported each other, while others argued or chased one another through the trees.  The grove became a scene of chaotic merriment, with monkeys swinging through the trees and behaving erratically.  Observing the escalating chaos and destruction of the grove, Dadhimukha, Sugriva's appointed guardian, attempted to intervene.  He scolded the unruly monkeys, attempting to reason with some and physically restraining others.  However, the intoxicated monkeys, unrestrained and disrespectful of Dadhimukha's authority, began to physically assault him, dragging and clawing at him. They continued to consume the grove's resources, leaving it bare.

summary:
Canto LXII
Having entered Madhuvana and partaken of honey, as permitted by Hanuman,
and singing and dancing like drunken people, the monkeys were forbidden
by those guarding the grove and they belaboured the latter. Repulsed by
them, the guards reported the matter to their leader, Dadhimukha,
who appeared on the scene with his entourage and on remonstrating
with the monkeys was thrashed by Angada, who dashed
him to the ground. The guards headed by Dadhimukha
then move en masse to Kiskindha to report the
matter to Sugriva and bow down at the latter’s feet
content: Hanuman encouraged the monkeys to enjoy the honey in Madhuvana without worry, assuring them of his protection.  Angada supported Hanuman's directive, emphasizing the importance of following the words of one who has successfully completed his mission.  The monkeys enthusiastically agreed, expressing their excitement and approval. They then entered Madhuvana, overpowering the guards.  Driven by Angada's permission and inspired by tales of Sita's presence, the monkeys indulged in the honey and fruits.  Some drank large quantities of honey, others ate the honeycombs, and some discarded them after consuming the honey.  Intoxicated by the honey, some monkeys playfully threw wax at each other, while others clung to tree branches or lay down on leaves, overcome by the effects of the honey.

summary:
Canto LXII
Having entered Madhuvana and partaken of honey, as permitted by Hanuman,
and singing and dancing like drunken people, the monkeys were forbidden
by those guarding the grove and they belaboured the latter. Repulsed by
them, the guards reported the matter to their leader, Dadhimukha,
who appeared on the scene with his entourage and on remonstrating
with the monkeys was thrashed by Angada, who dashed
him to the ground. The guards headed by Dadhimukha
then move en masse to Kiskindha to report the
matter to Sugriva and bow down at the latter’s feet
content: The monkeys, intoxicated by the honey, engaged in various antics.  Some quarreled and stumbled, while others roared and hooted playfully.  Some lay on the ground, others laughed, and some inexplicably wept.  Their actions were erratic and contradictory.  The guards of Madhuvana, overwhelmed by the monkeys' boisterous behavior, were forced to flee.  They were manhandled and tossed around by the unruly monkeys. The frightened guards reported the incident to their leader, Dadhimukha, describing the monkeys' destructive revelry and their own mistreatment.  Angered by this report, Dadhimukha set out with his entourage to confront the monkeys.  He uprooted a large tree, positioning himself amidst his followers, and advanced towards Madhuvana.  The monkeys, armed with rocks, trees, and stones, furiously charged towards the approaching guards, ready for a confrontation.  Seeing Dadhimukha's anger, Hanuman and the other powerful monkeys rushed forward to meet the challenge.

summary:
Canto LXII
Having entered Madhuvana and partaken of honey, as permitted by Hanuman,
and singing and dancing like drunken people, the monkeys were forbidden
by those guarding the grove and they belaboured the latter. Repulsed by
them, the guards reported the matter to their leader, Dadhimukha,
who appeared on the scene with his entourage and on remonstrating
with the monkeys was thrashed by Angada, who dashed
him to the ground. The guards headed by Dadhimukha
then move en masse to Kiskindha to report the
matter to Sugriva and bow down at the latter’s feet
content: Angada, enraged, forcefully seized Dadhimukha.  Ignoring familial ties, Angada threw Dadhimukha to the ground and began to roughly drag him.  Badly injured and bleeding, Dadhimukha briefly lost consciousness.  When the monkeys released him, Dadhimukha retreated and instructed his subordinates to return with him to Sugriva and Rama. He intended to report the monkeys' misconduct, confident that Sugriva would punish them for their disrespect of the sacred Madhuvana.  Dadhimukha believed his anger would be satisfied only when the monkeys were punished for defying the royal decree. After issuing these commands, Dadhimukha and his guards immediately departed for Kiskindha, arriving almost instantly at the location of Sugriva, Rama, and Lakshmana. He descended from the sky to address them.

summary:
Canto LXII
Having entered Madhuvana and partaken of honey, as permitted by Hanuman,
and singing and dancing like drunken people, the monkeys were forbidden
by those guarding the grove and they belaboured the latter. Repulsed by
them, the guards reported the matter to their leader, Dadhimukha,
who appeared on the scene with his entourage and on remonstrating
with the monkeys was thrashed by Angada, who dashed
him to the ground. The guards headed by Dadhimukha
then move en masse to Kiskindha to report the
matter to Sugriva and bow down at the latter’s feet
content: Dadhimukha, the leader of the Madhuvana guards, descended from the sky accompanied by his retinue.  He appeared distressed, his face sorrowful, and his hands clasped above his head in a gesture of supplication.  He immediately prostrated himself before Sugriva, bowing his head to touch Sugriva's feet.

summary:
Canto LXIII
Having heard from the mouth of Dadhimukha the story of Madhuvana
having been laid waste by the monkeys, Sugriva concludes from
the jubilation of the monkeys that Sita has been traced out.
Comforting Dadhimukha, he asks him to send Angada
and others without delay
content: Sugriva, the leader of the monkeys, was alarmed to see Dadhimukha fall at his feet.  He urged Dadhimukha to rise and reassured him, promising safety and requesting him to speak the truth about what troubled him.  Sugriva inquired about the state of Madhuvana, eager for news.  Reassured by Sugriva's kind words, Dadhimukha explained that the grove, which had never been harmed under the rule of Sugriva's father, Sugriva himself, or even Vali, had been devastated by the monkeys.  He recounted how he and the other guards had tried to intervene, but the monkeys disregarded them, continuing to eat the fruit and honey.  Even when the guards attempted to stop the monkeys’ destruction, they were met with defiance and anger.  Some monkeys not only consumed the honey but also discarded what remained.  The situation escalated, with the guards being assaulted and driven away by the aggressive monkey invaders.  Dadhimukha lamented the beating of the valiant monkey guards and the complete destruction of Madhuvana under Sugriva's rule.

summary:
Canto LXIII
Having heard from the mouth of Dadhimukha the story of Madhuvana
having been laid waste by the monkeys, Sugriva concludes from
the jubilation of the monkeys that Sita has been traced out.
Comforting Dadhimukha, he asks him to send Angada
and others without delay
content: Sugriva deduces that Sita has been found, based on the monkeys' joyous devastation of Madhuvana.  He explains to Lakshmana that the grove's destruction could only mean the monkeys had succeeded in their mission. He points out the guards were overpowered, even Dadhimukha, and reminds Lakshmana that he himself appointed Dadhimukha as guardian.  Sugriva confidently declares Sita has been found by Hanuman, emphasizing Hanuman's unique abilities, intelligence, strength, and knowledge.  He highlights Hanuman's crucial role, along with Angada and Jambavan, in leading the monkey army. He confirms the reports of Madhuvana's destruction by the returning monkeys, led by Angada, after their search of the southern region. Sugriva notes the grove, previously untouchable, was ravaged upon the monkeys' return from Lanka.  He adds that the grove was not merely damaged, but completely consumed, and the guards were overpowered and assaulted.

summary:
Canto LXIII
Having heard from the mouth of Dadhimukha the story of Madhuvana
having been laid waste by the monkeys, Sugriva concludes from
the jubilation of the monkeys that Sita has been traced out.
Comforting Dadhimukha, he asks him to send Angada
and others without delay
content: Sugriva introduces Dadhimukha, the grove's guard, highlighting his eloquence and renowned strength. He tells Lakshmana that Sita must have been found because the monkeys are joyfully feasting in Madhuvana. He reasons that such renowned warriors wouldn't dare touch the sacred grove unless they had succeeded in their mission.  Hearing this news, Rama and Lakshmana are overjoyed.  Sugriva, also delighted, tells Dadhimukha that he's pleased the grove has been enjoyed by the successful monkeys, adding that their actions, though destructive, are understandable given their accomplishment. He instructs Dadhimukha to quickly return and guard the grove, and to immediately send Hanuman and the other monkeys back so he can hear about their success in finding Sita, alongside Rama and Lakshmana. Seeing Rama and Lakshmana's joy, and sensing the imminent success of his plan, Sugriva feels immense happiness.

summary:
Canto LXIV
Returning to Madhuvana as commanded by Sugriva, and craving the
forgiveness of Angada, Dadhimukha submits to the latter that
he is urgently summoned by Sugriva. Set out in the
company of Hanuman and others, and approaching
Sugriva, Prince Angada apprises Sit Rama, after
saluting him, of the discovery of Sita
and other events
content: Dadhimukha, following Sugriva's orders, respectfully greeted Rama, Lakshmana, and Sugriva.  He then swiftly returned to Madhuvana, landing in the forest and finding the previously unruly monkey leaders now subdued and weakened.  Approaching Angada with respect, Dadhimukha urged him not to resent the guards' earlier actions, explaining they acted out of ignorance. He invited Angada and the others to partake of the honey, reminding Angada of his status as crown prince and rightful owner of the grove.

summary:
Canto LXIV
Returning to Madhuvana as commanded by Sugriva, and craving the
forgiveness of Angada, Dadhimukha submits to the latter that
he is urgently summoned by Sugriva. Set out in the
company of Hanuman and others, and approaching
Sugriva, Prince Angada apprises Sit Rama, after
saluting him, of the discovery of Sita
and other events
content: Dadhimukha urged Angada to forgive the guards' prior hostility, attributing it to ignorance. He emphasized Sugriva's current authority, comparing it to Vali's former rule, and reminded Angada of his own high status. Dadhimukha relayed Sugriva's joyful reaction to the news of Angada's return and his command to send everyone to him. Hearing this, Angada addressed the monkeys, acknowledging that Rama likely knew of their arrival.  He deduced this from Dadhimukha's cheerful demeanor and declared they should not delay their return now that their task was complete. He urged the monkeys to return to Kiskindha since they had enjoyed the honey and fulfilled their purpose.  Angada humbly stated his reliance on the monkeys' collective decision regarding his actions, despite his position as crown prince. He added he couldn't order them, as they'd done their duty, and it would be wrong to exert his authority.  The monkeys, pleased with Angada's words, responded even as he spoke. They praised his humility, contrasting it with the typical arrogance of those in power.

summary:
Canto LXIV
Returning to Madhuvana as commanded by Sugriva, and craving the
forgiveness of Angada, Dadhimukha submits to the latter that
he is urgently summoned by Sugriva. Set out in the
company of Hanuman and others, and approaching
Sugriva, Prince Angada apprises Sit Rama, after
saluting him, of the discovery of Sita
and other events
content: Angada's humble speech is praised by the monkeys, who declare their eagerness to return to Sugriva. They affirm their obedience to Angada, stating they won't move without his command.  Angada agrees, and the powerful monkeys, led by Angada and Hanuman, leap into the air. They fill the sky like projectiles, roaring like a wind-driven storm.  Upon reaching Sugriva, Angada reassures a grieving Rama, confirming Sita's discovery and suggesting their success is evident in their timely return and Angada's joyful demeanor.  He reasons that Angada wouldn't approach Sugriva if they had failed, and even if he had, his demeanor would reflect defeat, which it clearly doesn't.  Sugriva adds that the monkeys wouldn't have dared destroy Madhuvana without finding Sita first.

summary:
Canto LXIV
Returning to Madhuvana as commanded by Sugriva, and craving the
forgiveness of Angada, Dadhimukha submits to the latter that
he is urgently summoned by Sugriva. Set out in the
company of Hanuman and others, and approaching
Sugriva, Prince Angada apprises Sit Rama, after
saluting him, of the discovery of Sita
and other events
content: Sugriva consoles Rama, assuring him that Sita has been found by Hanuman.  He emphasizes Hanuman's unique capabilities, intelligence, strength, and scholarship, highlighting that with leaders like Jambavan and Angada, and an advisor like Hanuman, the mission couldn't have failed.  Sugriva observes the monkeys' joyous return and the destruction of Madhuvana as further proof of their success. He hears their celebratory cries echoing their triumph.  Overjoyed, Sugriva stretches and curls his tail as Angada and Hanuman, along with the eager monkeys, approach Rama.  Angada and his company descend near Rama and Sugriva.  Hanuman respectfully informs Rama that Sita is safe and loyal.  Rama and Lakshmana are overjoyed by the news.  Lakshmana looks at the joyful Sugriva with respect and love, while Rama regards Hanuman with great affection and esteem.

Hanuman, along with other monkeys including Angada and Sugriva, respectfully approached Rama and Lakshmana to deliver news about Sita. They reported her confinement in Ravana's garden, guarded by ogresses, and relayed her unwavering devotion to Rama.  They also informed Rama of Ravana's ultimatum for Sita. Upon hearing that Sita was alive, Rama inquired about her current location and her feelings towards him.  The monkeys, acknowledging Rama's question, urged Hanuman, who had firsthand knowledge of Sita's situation, to provide a detailed account.

Hanuman, bowing respectfully towards the south where Sita was held captive, recounted to Rama how he found her. He described giving Sita Rama's ring and then shared details of her plight.  Sita was found in Ravana's garden, Lanka, closely guarded by monstrous ogresses.  She was grieving, wearing a single braid as a sign of mourning, sleeping on the bare ground, and refusing any comfort or association with Ravana.  Hanuman reassured her, gradually building her confidence by speaking of Rama's glory and his alliance with Sugriva.  Learning of this alliance brought Sita some joy. She remained utterly devoted to Rama.

Hanuman continued his report to Rama, explaining that Sita, deeply devoted to Rama and practicing asceticism, was found in Ravana's garden. She confirmed her identity by referencing the incident with the crow at Chitrakuta. Sita urged Hanuman to relay every detail of her plight to Rama.  She also gave him a jewel to return to Rama, reminding him of the mark he made on her forehead with realgar, and explaining that looking at this jewel brought her comfort.  Sita declared she could only endure another month of captivity.  Hanuman described Sita as emaciated and fearful, like a trapped doe.  Having delivered this message and the jewel, Hanuman concluded his report and urged Rama to devise a plan to cross the ocean.

summary:
Canto LXVI

Pressing to his bosom the jewel for the head sent by Sita and piteously wailing
in many ways, Sri Rama urges Hanuman to repeat the message of Sita
content: Overwhelmed with grief, Rama clutches Sita's jewel and weeps with Lakshmana.  He tells Sugriva that the jewel, a gift from Janaka to Sita, evokes the same longing as a cow feels for her calf. Rama explains the jewel's history, a gift from Indra to Janaka, and how seeing it is like seeing both his father-in-law and Sita.  He believes that the jewel's presence signifies Sita's survival.  Rama implores Hanuman to repeat Sita's message.  He laments to Lakshmana the pain of seeing the jewel without Sita and declares he cannot live without her.  Rama urges Hanuman to take him to where Sita is held captive, anxious about her safety among the fearsome Rakshasas.

summary:
Canto LXVI

Pressing to his bosom the jewel for the head sent by Sita and piteously wailing
in many ways, Sri Rama urges Hanuman to repeat the message of Sita
content: Rama, holding Sita's jewel, continues to grieve. He remarks that Sita's face, usually bright like the autumn moon, is now dimmed, as if by clouds.  Desperate for news, he begs Hanuman to repeat Sita's message, saying it sustains him like medicine.  He asks again what Sita, his beloved wife, beautiful and sweet-spoken, said.  Separated from him and facing immense hardship, Rama wonders how Sita endures.

summary:
Canto LXVII
Hanuman reproduces at length the incident connected with a crow that
took place during the sojourn of Sita with Sri Rama at Citrakiita,
related by her by way of a token, and further describes her
piteous lament and the way in which she was
comforted by Hanuman
content: Hanuman recounts Sita's story to Rama. Sita told Rama about an incident at Chitrakuta.  While Rama slept, a crow pecked Sita's breast.  She awoke Rama, and the crow attacked again.  Seeing Sita's injury, Rama, enraged, questioned her.  He spotted the blood-stained crow, identified as Jayanta, son of Indra.  Rama, furious, took a blade of kusa grass and imbued it with Brahma's power to punish the crow.

summary:
Canto LXVII
Hanuman reproduces at length the incident connected with a crow that
took place during the sojourn of Sita with Sri Rama at Citrakiita,
related by her by way of a token, and further describes her
piteous lament and the way in which she was
comforted by Hanuman
content: Sita continued her story to Hanuman, describing how she threw a blazing blade of kusa grass at the crow. This flaming projectile pursued the terrified crow, who, abandoned by the gods, fruitlessly searched the three worlds for refuge from Rama's wrath.

summary:
Canto LXVII
Hanuman reproduces at length the incident connected with a crow that
took place during the sojourn of Sita with Sri Rama at Citrakiita,
related by her by way of a token, and further describes her
piteous lament and the way in which she was
comforted by Hanuman
content: Sita recounted to Hanuman how a crow had harassed her at Citrakuta.  She attacked the crow, which then begged Rama for mercy.  Despite being capable of destroying the crow, Rama, known for his compassion, only injured its eye.  Sita questioned why Rama, so skilled in combat, did not use his power against the demons who had abducted her. She lamented her fate, wondering if her own past actions were responsible for her current plight. She expressed her anguish that both Rama and Lakshmana, powerful warriors, seemed unable to protect her.  Hanuman, witnessing Sita's distress, reassured her that Rama was consumed by grief due to their separation, and that Lakshmana shared his brother's sorrow.  He emphasized that now, having found her, it was not the time for despair.

summary:
Canto LXVII
Hanuman reproduces at length the incident connected with a crow that
took place during the sojourn of Sita with Sri Rama at Citrakiita,
related by her by way of a token, and further describes her
piteous lament and the way in which she was
comforted by Hanuman
content: Hanuman tells Rama that Sita, despite her sorrow, expressed hope that Rama and Lakshmana would destroy Ravana and his army and rescue her.  Sita gave Hanuman a jewel to take back to Rama as a token of recognition.  As Hanuman prepared to depart, Sita, overwhelmed with grief, envied Hanuman's opportunity to see Rama and Lakshmana.  Hanuman offered to carry Sita back to Rama, but she refused, stating it would be improper for her to do so.

summary:
Canto LXVII
Hanuman reproduces at length the incident connected with a crow that
took place during the sojourn of Sita with Sri Rama at Citrakiita,
related by her by way of a token, and further describes her
piteous lament and the way in which she was
comforted by Hanuman
content: Hanuman recounts Sita's words to Rama, detailing her distress when Ravana abducted her. Despite her helplessness against Ravana's aggression, she urges Hanuman to return to Rama and Lakshmana.  She implores Hanuman to convey her well-being to the princes, Sugriva, and his ministers.  Sita asks Hanuman to deliver her message in a manner that will compel Rama to rescue her from her misery.  She also asks Hanuman to inform Rama of her profound sorrow and the threats she endures from the ogres.  Finally, Hanuman assures Rama that Sita, though distressed, remains resolute and steadfast in her devotion.

Hanuman recounted to Rama how Sita initially doubted a monkey's ability to cross the vast ocean.  Sita urged Hanuman to convey to Rama the urgency of her situation, suggesting he either persuade Rama to swiftly defeat Ravana and rescue her or, if necessary, rest and return the following day. Sita expressed fear for her safety upon Hanuman's departure, anticipating renewed grief.  She voiced her significant doubt about how any monkey, even with Hanuman's assistance, or even Rama and Lakshmana themselves, could possibly cross the formidable ocean.  Sita acknowledged the unique capability of only Garuda, the wind-god, and Hanuman to achieve such a feat.  She then implored Hanuman, whom she recognized as resourceful, to propose a solution, suggesting that while he was capable of rescuing her single-handedly, Rama's triumph over Ravana with his army would bring him greater glory.

Hanuman reassured Sita, explaining that Rama would not resort to the same deceitful tactics Ravana used.  A triumphant, open victory over Ravana and his forces in Lanka would be a more fitting way for Rama to reclaim her.  Hanuman emphasized that he was working towards such a glorious outcome. He highlighted Sugriva's commitment to Sita's rescue and the vast, powerful monkey army at their disposal. This army, he described, was incredibly strong, fast, and capable, having circumnavigated the earth. He pointed out that while there were monkeys as strong or stronger than himself, it was customary for the younger ones to be sent on missions like his. Hanuman urged Sita to abandon her despair, promising a swift arrival of the monkey army and envisioning Rama and Lakshmana, carried on his back, coming for her like the rising sun and moon.

Hanuman painted a vivid picture of Rama's imminent arrival for Sita. He described Rama, lion-like in his strength, arriving at Lanka's gates with Lakshmana.  He spoke of the monkey army, comparing their strength and fierceness to lions, tigers, and elephants, and their numbers to mountains and clouds, soon to fill Lanka with their roars from the Trikuta mountain.  He reassured Sita that she would witness Rama's coronation in Ayodhya upon their return from exile. Hanuman's comforting and encouraging words brought solace to Sita, easing her grief and despair.

summary:
Canto VII

The poet incidentally draws a pen-picture of Ravana’s palace and his
aerial car known by the name of Puspaka
content: Hanuman observed Ravana's palace, a magnificent structure adorned with gold and cat's-eye lattices, teeming with birds and resembling a massive cloud formation illuminated by lightning.  He noted various apartments filled with conches, bows, and other weapons, as well as spacious attics atop the towering mansions.  Hanuman saw opulent mansions, revered by gods and demons alike, flawless and acquired from Kubera. These mansions, strategically arranged and seemingly built by the renowned architect Maya, surpassed all others in excellence.  He then beheld Ravana's splendid palace, radiant like gold and a towering cloud, a fitting residence for the powerful king.  This palace, a celestial paradise on earth, glittered with countless jewels and blossoms, resembling a pollen-covered mountain peak.  Illuminated by the presence of women, it shone like a cloud lit by lightning, a magnificent aerial chariot soaring through the sky, drawn by majestic swans.  Within the palace, Hanuman saw an aerial car, adorned with precious stones and resembling a vibrant, multi-hued cloud.  It was reminiscent of a mountain peak, rich with minerals, under a starlit sky.  The platform for boarding the aerial car featured artificial hills of gems and gold, covered with artificial trees bearing artificial flowers, complete with filaments and petals.  White mansions, pools adorned with flowers, lotuses, and charming groves with lakes were also present. Hanuman observed the magnificent Puspaka, a vast aerial car named after flowers, capable of long journeys and radiating the brilliance of precious stones.  It was considered among the finest of aerial cars, fit for gods.  The car was decorated with birds crafted from cat's-eye gems, silver, and coral.  Serpents made of jewels and beautifully formed horses, resembling noble steeds, were also present.

summary:
Canto VII

The poet incidentally draws a pen-picture of Ravana’s palace and his
aerial car known by the name of Puspaka
content: Hanuman examined the aerial car, Puspaka, noting the sculpted birds with beaks and wings adorned with coral and gold flowers.  These birds appeared to have playfully drawn in their wings and resembled companions of Cupid.  Within the car, artificial elephants with ornate trunks offered lotus petals to a statue of Lakshmi, the goddess of fortune.  Hanuman marveled at the car's splendor, comparing it to a beautiful mountain with caverns and a fragrant tree in springtime.  Despite being able to move freely within Ravana's well-guarded city, Hanuman was distressed at not finding Sita, the respected daughter of King Janaka.  Even with his disciplined mind and keen observation, he could not locate her.

summary:
Canto VIII
A further description of the aerial car, Puspaka
content: Hanuman, son of the wind-god, discovered a magnificent aerial car within the palace.  This vehicle, adorned with gems and intricate gold latticework, was a marvel of craftsmanship.  It was a creation of Viswakarma, the celestial architect, and shone brilliantly as it rested in the sky. Every detail of the car reflected meticulous effort and was inlaid with precious stones.  Its unique features surpassed even those found in the abodes of the gods. Acquired by Ravana through dedicated asceticism and focus, the Puspaka car responded to its master's thoughts, traveling swiftly and remaining inaccessible to the unworthy. Its construction involved specific designs and materials gathered from various locations.  This remarkable vehicle was reserved for virtuous individuals of great merit and renown, offering them unparalleled comfort and speed. Its distinct form, adorned with numerous compartments, resembled a mountain peak with multiple summits, shining brightly like the autumnal moon.  Carried by a multitude of swift, powerful, and wide-eyed spirits who roamed the night sky, the Puspaka car, decorated with spring flowers, presented a captivating sight, surpassing even the beauty of the spring season itself.

Hanuman, searching for Sita, surveyed Ravana's opulent palace from the Puspaka vimana. The vast, heavily guarded compound housed numerous structures, including the main palace, which was filled with Ravana's wives and captive princesses.  The palace's splendor rivaled that of Kubera, Yama, and Varuna.  Within the complex, Hanuman observed another magnificent edifice,  the celestial chariot Puspaka, originally crafted for Brahma and later won by Ravana from Kubera.

Hanuman, from his vantage point on the magnificent Puspaka vimana, observed a grand palace within Ravana's compound.  This structure, gleaming with gold and silver pillars, was adorned with intricate carvings and resembled the celestial mountains Meru and Mandara.  Numerous chambers and pavilions, radiant like the sun and fire, surrounded the main building.  Viswakarma, the divine architect, had crafted this edifice with golden stairways and exquisite platforms of sapphire and emerald.  Lattice windows of gold and crystal added to its splendor. The floors, inlaid with coral, precious gems, and pearls, shimmered like the rising sun.  The air was filled with the fragrant aroma of sandalwood and other sweet scents, further enhancing the palace's allure. This captivating aroma seemed to beckon Hanuman closer.  He then discovered a lavish hall, Ravana's favorite, adorned with gem-studded stairways and gold latticework. Crystal floors inlaid with ivory, pearls, diamonds, coral, silver, and gold created a dazzling effect.  Tall, ornate pillars, resembling outstretched wings, gave the impression that the hall was about to ascend to heaven. A large carpet depicting mountains, trees, and rivers covered the floor, while murals showcased various lands and dwellings.  The sounds of exotic birds and the lingering scent of celestial perfumes filled the air.  This hall, used by Ravana, was decorated with tapestries and perfumed with incense.  It was a place of radiant beauty, captivating the senses and dispelling all sorrow. Like a nurturing mother, this hall offered delights to all of Hanuman's senses.  He wondered if he had stumbled upon paradise, the abode of the gods, or even the realm of Brahma, the ultimate achievement.

Hanuman observed the golden lamps within Ravana's chamber, their light seemingly dimmed by Ravana's own splendor, as if in quiet contemplation. The chamber glowed with the combined radiance of the lamps, Ravana's presence, and the gleam of ornaments.  Hanuman then saw numerous women reclining on a carpet, adorned in colorful garments and garlands. They lay asleep, overcome by drink and slumber after a night of revelry.  The sleeping women, bedecked in silent jewelry, resembled a serene lotus pond, with swans and bees resting peacefully. Their closed eyes and lips, fragrant like lotuses, seemed like blossoms that had opened at nightfall and closed again with the coming of dawn.  Hanuman likened their faces to lotuses, attracting the attention of admirers, much like flowers draw bees.  The chamber, illuminated by these women, shone like a clear autumn sky filled with stars.  Ravana, surrounded by them, appeared like the moon amidst the stars. Hanuman mused that these women might be the fallen stars, gathered together in this place.

Within Ravana's chamber, the sleeping women resembled a scattered constellation of stars. Their beauty and grace shone brightly, yet their finery was disarrayed from their revelries.
